DS Academy/ Raft Consensus Algorithm
Post 01

Raft Consensus Algorithm

20 min deep dive Leader Election · Log Replication · Safety · Terms · Fault Tolerance
The Problem

Why do we need consensus at all?

Imagine you are building a key-value store that must never lose data, and must stay available even when servers crash. Your first instinct: run three copies of the data on three servers. But immediately you hit a fundamental problem.

The Core Dilemma

Client writes x = 5 to Server A. Server A crashes before telling B and C. A new client reads from Server B — it returns the old value. Data is now inconsistent. Now scale this: 1000 writes/second, 5 nodes, each node can crash at any time, messages can be delayed or dropped. How do you guarantee every node applies writes in the same order? This is the consensus problem. Raft is a practical, understandable algorithm that solves it.

Before Raft (2014), Paxos was the dominant consensus algorithm. Paxos is provably correct but notoriously hard to understand and even harder to implement correctly. Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout designed Raft with one explicit goal: understandability. Every design decision was evaluated against "is this easier to understand than the alternative?" The result is an algorithm you can fully implement from the paper.

Building Intuition

Think of Raft like a company with one CEO

The Intuition Model

Nodes = employees. Each employee is either a passive worker (Follower), campaigning for promotion (Candidate), or the active CEO (Leader).

Terms = fiscal quarters. Each quarter starts with a leadership election. There is at most one CEO per quarter. If the election is inconclusive, a new quarter starts immediately.

Log = official company memo board. Only the CEO can add memos. A memo only becomes official when the majority of employees have acknowledged it. Employees apply memos in order.

Heartbeat = CEO's daily all-hands ping. "I'm still here, still in charge." If employees stop receiving pings for too long — they assume the CEO is gone and start a new election.

Diagram 1 · Node State Machine

Every Raft node is always in exactly one of three states

Understanding the state machine is the foundation. Every transition has a precise trigger — memorise these and you can answer 80% of Raft interview questions.

raft-state-machine.svg

Critical rule to remember: Any node that receives a message with a term higher than its own immediately converts to Follower and updates its term — regardless of whether it is currently the Leader. This single rule is how Raft handles stale leaders coming back online after a partition.

Diagram 2 · Terms — Raft's Logical Clock

Terms are how Raft detects stale information — understand this deeply

A term is a monotonically increasing integer that acts as Raft's logical clock. Every message in Raft carries a term number. If your term is lower than the message's term, you update and follow. If higher, you reject the message.

raft-terms.svg
Diagram 3 · Log Replication Step-by-Step

The 4-step process from client write to committed entry

Every write in Raft goes through exactly these four steps. A write is only considered successful after Step 4. This is what gives Raft its durability guarantee.

raft-log-replication-steps.svg
Animation · Full Leader Election on 5-Node Cluster

Step-by-step: leader N1 fails, N2 campaigns, wins election, becomes leader

Use the speed slider to slow down each phase. Toggle Manual mode to advance one step at a time and study each message exchange.

raft-election-animation
Speed: Normal Step: 0 / 9
Ready
Worked Example · Log States After Leader Crash

What do follower logs look like when a leader crashes mid-replication?

This is one of the most common interview follow-ups. After a crash, followers can have different log states. The new leader must bring them all in sync. Raft's Log Matching Property guarantees this can always be done safely.

raft-log-divergence.svg
Code · Python & Java

Complete Raft node implementation — election, vote handler, log replication

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum        import Enum
from typing      import Optional, List
import threading, random

class Role(Enum):
    FOLLOWER  = "follower"
    CANDIDATE = "candidate"
    LEADER    = "leader"

@dataclass
class LogEntry:
    term:    int          # term when this entry was created
    index:   int          # 1-based position in the log
    command: str          # the actual state machine command

class RaftNode:
    def __init__(self, node_id: int, cluster_size: int):
        # ── Persistent state (must survive crashes) ──────
        self.node_id      = node_id
        self.current_term = 0    # latest term this node has seen
        self.voted_for: Optional[int] = None  # candidateId voted for in current term
        self.log: List[LogEntry]      = []      # log entries (1-indexed)

        # ── Volatile state ────────────────────────────────
        self.role            = Role.FOLLOWER
        self.commit_index    = 0    # highest log index known committed
        self.last_applied    = 0    # highest log index applied to state machine
        self.leader_id       = None
        self.cluster_size    = cluster_size
        self.peers: list     = []   # set after all nodes are created

        # ── Leader-only volatile state ───────────────────
        self.next_index:  dict = {}  # peer_id → next log index to send to that peer
        self.match_index: dict = {}  # peer_id → highest log index known replicated

        self._lock  = threading.Lock()
        self._timer = None
        self._reset_timer()

    def _reset_timer(self):
        if self._timer: self._timer.cancel()
        # Random 150–300ms prevents simultaneous elections (split vote)
        t = random.uniform(0.150, 0.300)
        self._timer = threading.Timer(t, self._start_election)
        self._timer.start()

    # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # PHASE 1: ELECTION
    # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def _start_election(self):
        with self._lock:
            self.current_term += 1             # advance term
            self.role          = Role.CANDIDATE
            self.voted_for     = self.node_id    # vote for self first
            votes              = 1              # self vote
            last_idx  = len(self.log)
            last_term = self.log[-1].term if self.log else 0

        for peer in self.peers:
            granted = peer.handle_vote_request(
                term          = self.current_term,
                candidate_id  = self.node_id,
                last_log_index= last_idx,
                last_log_term = last_term,
            )
            if granted:
                votes += 1
                majority = self.cluster_size // 2 + 1
                if votes >= majority and self.role == Role.CANDIDATE:
                    self._become_leader()
                    return
        self._reset_timer()   # no majority → wait and retry

    def handle_vote_request(self, term: int, candidate_id: int,
                              last_log_index: int, last_log_term: int) -> bool:
        with self._lock:
            # Rule: if message term > ours, update and become follower
            if term > self.current_term:
                self.current_term = term
                self.role         = Role.FOLLOWER
                self.voted_for    = None

            if term < self.current_term:
                return False   # stale term — reject

            # Already voted for someone else this term?
            if self.voted_for not in (None, candidate_id):
                return False

            # Candidate's log must be AT LEAST AS UP-TO-DATE as ours
            # "up-to-date" = higher last term, OR same last term with longer log
            my_last_term  = self.log[-1].term if self.log else 0
            my_last_index = len(self.log)
            log_ok = (last_log_term  > my_last_term or
                     (last_log_term == my_last_term and
                      last_log_index >= my_last_index))

            if not log_ok:
                return False   # candidate's log is behind ours

            self.voted_for = candidate_id
            self._reset_timer()   # give candidate time to win
            return True

    # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # PHASE 2: LOG REPLICATION
    # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def _become_leader(self):
        self.role      = Role.LEADER
        self.leader_id = self.node_id
        # Optimistic initialisation: assume peers have entire log
        for p in self.peers:
            self.next_index[p.node_id]  = len(self.log) + 1
            self.match_index[p.node_id] = 0
        self._send_heartbeats()   # announce immediately

    def propose(self, command: str) -> bool:
        """Client-facing entry point. Returns False if not leader."""
        if self.role != Role.LEADER:
            return False   # client must redirect to leader
        entry = LogEntry(term    = self.current_term,
                         index   = len(self.log) + 1,
                         command = command)
        self.log.append(entry)
        self._replicate_log()   # fan-out AppendEntries to all peers
        return True

    def handle_append_entries(self, term: int, leader_id: int,
                                prev_log_index: int, prev_log_term: int,
                                entries: list, leader_commit: int) -> bool:
        with self._lock:
            if term < self.current_term:
                return False   # stale leader

            self._reset_timer()   # valid heartbeat — suppress election
            self.current_term = term
            self.role         = Role.FOLLOWER
            self.leader_id    = leader_id

            # Consistency check: our log must match at prevLogIndex
            if prev_log_index > 0:
                if len(self.log) < prev_log_index:
                    return False   # we're missing earlier entries
                if self.log[prev_log_index - 1].term != prev_log_term:
                    return False   # mismatch — leader will backtrack

            # Append new entries, overwriting any conflicting entries
            for i, entry in enumerate(entries):
                log_idx = prev_log_index + i   # 0-based into our log
                if log_idx < len(self.log):
                    if self.log[log_idx].term != entry.term:
                        self.log = self.log[:log_idx]  # delete conflict + after
                self.log.append(entry)

            # Advance commit index if leader says so
            if leader_commit > self.commit_index:
                self.commit_index = min(leader_commit, len(self.log))
            return True

    def _advance_commit_as_leader(self):
        """Leader checks if any new entry has been replicated to majority."""
        n = len(self.log)
        for idx in range(n, self.commit_index, -1):
            # Only commit entries from current term (safety rule!)
            if self.log[idx - 1].term != self.current_term:
                continue
            replication_count = 1  # leader itself
            for peer_id, match_idx in self.match_index.items():
                if match_idx >= idx:
                    replication_count += 1
            if replication_count >= (self.cluster_size // 2 + 1):
                self.commit_index = idx
                break
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;

public enum Role { FOLLOWER, CANDIDATE, LEADER }

public record LogEntry(int term, int index, String command) {}

public class RaftNode {

    // ── Persistent state ─────────────────────────────
    private final int             nodeId;
    private final int             clusterSize;
    private volatile int          currentTerm  = 0;
    private volatile Integer       votedFor     = null;
    private final List<LogEntry>  log          = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();

    // ── Volatile state ───────────────────────────────
    private volatile Role          role         = Role.FOLLOWER;
    private volatile int           commitIndex  = 0;
    private volatile int           lastApplied  = 0;
    private volatile Integer       leaderId     = null;
    private final List<RaftNode>  peers        = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();

    // ── Leader state ─────────────────────────────────
    private final Map<Integer,Integer> nextIndex  = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final Map<Integer,Integer> matchIndex = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    private final ScheduledExecutorService scheduler =
        Executors.newSingleThreadScheduledExecutor();
    private ScheduledFuture<?> electionTimer;
    private final Object lock = new Object();

    public RaftNode(int nodeId, int clusterSize) {
        this.nodeId      = nodeId;
        this.clusterSize = clusterSize;
        resetTimer();
    }

    private void resetTimer() {
        if (electionTimer != null) electionTimer.cancel(false);
        // Random 150–300ms timeout prevents simultaneous elections
        long ms = 150 + (long)(Math.random() * 150);
        electionTimer = scheduler.schedule(
            this::startElection, ms, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
    }

    // ── Election ─────────────────────────────────────
    private void startElection() {
        int myTerm, lastIdx, lastTerm;
        synchronized (lock) {
            currentTerm++;
            role     = Role.CANDIDATE;
            votedFor = nodeId;
            myTerm   = currentTerm;
            lastIdx  = log.size();
            lastTerm = lastIdx > 0 ? log.get(lastIdx - 1).term() : 0;
        }
        AtomicInteger votes = new AtomicInteger(1); // self-vote
        int majority = clusterSize / 2 + 1;

        peers.forEach(peer -> CompletableFuture.runAsync(() -> {
            if (peer.handleVoteRequest(myTerm, nodeId, lastIdx, lastTerm)) {
                if (votes.incrementAndGet() >= majority) {
                    synchronized (lock) {
                        if (role == Role.CANDIDATE) becomeLeader();
                    }
                }
            }
        }));
        resetTimer(); // retry if no majority
    }

    public synchronized boolean handleVoteRequest(
            int term, int candidateId,
            int lastLogIdx, int lastLogTerm) {

        if (term > currentTerm) {
            currentTerm = term; role = Role.FOLLOWER; votedFor = null;
        }
        if (term < currentTerm) return false;
        if (votedFor != null && !votedFor.equals(candidateId)) return false;

        int     myLastIdx  = log.size();
        int     myLastTerm = myLastIdx > 0 ? log.get(myLastIdx-1).term() : 0;
        boolean logOk      = lastLogTerm > myLastTerm ||
            (lastLogTerm == myLastTerm && lastLogIdx >= myLastIdx);
        if (!logOk) return false;

        votedFor = candidateId;
        resetTimer();
        return true;
    }

    // ── Log Replication ──────────────────────────────
    private void becomeLeader() {
        role     = Role.LEADER;
        leaderId = nodeId;
        peers.forEach(p -> {
            nextIndex.put(p.nodeId, log.size() + 1);
            matchIndex.put(p.nodeId, 0);
        });
        sendHeartbeats();
    }

    public boolean propose(String command) {
        synchronized (lock) {
            if (role != Role.LEADER) return false;
            log.add(new LogEntry(currentTerm, log.size()+1, command));
        }
        replicateLog();
        return true;
    }

    public synchronized boolean handleAppendEntries(
            int term, int leaderId,
            int prevLogIdx, int prevLogTerm,
            List<LogEntry> entries, int leaderCommit) {

        if (term < currentTerm) return false;
        resetTimer();
        currentTerm  = term;
        role         = Role.FOLLOWER;
        this.leaderId = leaderId;

        // Consistency check
        if (prevLogIdx > 0) {
            if (log.size() < prevLogIdx) return false;
            if (log.get(prevLogIdx-1).term() != prevLogTerm) return false;
        }
        // Append entries, overwriting conflicts
        for (int i = 0; i < entries.size(); i++) {
            int idx = prevLogIdx + i;
            if (idx < log.size() &&
                log.get(idx).term() != entries.get(i).term())
                while (log.size() > idx) log.remove(log.size()-1);
            if (idx >= log.size()) log.add(entries.get(i));
        }
        if (leaderCommit > commitIndex)
            commitIndex = Math.min(leaderCommit, log.size());
        return true;
    }
}
The one safety rule that takes the most time to understand

A leader can only commit entries from its current term by counting replicas. It cannot commit entries from previous terms directly — even if they're replicated on a majority. This prevents a subtle bug where an entry appears on a majority, gets committed, then a new leader overwrites it. The leader commits current-term entries and old entries get committed "piggyback" — once the current-term entry commits, all preceding entries are implicitly committed.

Trade-offs

Where Raft shines and where it struggles

raft-tradeoffs.svg
Interview Q&A

All the questions you will face — with senior-level depth

Q1Explain the difference between a term and a log index in Raft.
A
Term is Raft's logical clock for the entire cluster. It increments every time an election starts. Each term has at most one leader. A node that receives a message with a higher term immediately updates and becomes a follower. Terms prevent zombie leaders — a node that was partitioned away and comes back will have a stale term and get rejected by all peers.

Index is the position of an entry within the log (1-based). A log entry is uniquely identified by the pair (term, index). The Log Matching Property states: if two nodes have the same entry at (term, index), their logs are identical up to that point. This is what allows the leader to detect and fix log divergence by backtracking nextIndex until it finds a matching (term, index) and then replaying from there.
Q2What happens if a leader crashes immediately after replying to a client but before the followers apply the entry?
A
This is fine — the entry was committed (majority of nodes have it in their log) before the leader replied. The new leader, elected after the crash, will have this entry in its log (guaranteed by the election safety property: a candidate can only win if its log is at least as up-to-date as a majority of nodes, and the majority that has this entry will refuse to vote for any candidate missing it). The new leader will eventually advance its commitIndex to include this entry and notify followers. The state machine gets applied correctly everywhere. The client successfully received the reply, so no retry is needed.
Q3Can Raft guarantee linearizability? How?
A
Yes, with the right implementation. For writes: Raft's commit mechanism guarantees every write is seen by all subsequent reads anywhere in the cluster — the majority-commit rule ensures durability before acknowledgement. For reads, there are two pitfalls: (1) a follower may serve stale data. Solution: route all reads to the leader, or use "read index" where the leader confirms it is still the leader by getting a heartbeat ACK from majority before serving the read. (2) a leader that just won an election may not yet know the latest committed entry. Solution: the leader commits a no-op entry in its own term first, then it knows its log is complete. Only after this should it serve reads. etcd implements both of these correctly.
Q4What is log compaction and why is it necessary?
A
Without compaction, the Raft log grows forever. A node that crashed and restarts must replay the entire log to rebuild state — impractical after months of writes. Log compaction (snapshotting) solves this: periodically, each node takes a snapshot of its state machine's current state and records the log index and term of the last included entry, then discards all log entries up to that point. When a new node joins or a slow node is far behind, the leader sends the snapshot via InstallSnapshot RPC instead of replaying thousands of AppendEntries. In etcd, snapshots happen automatically when the log exceeds a configurable size; in CockroachDB each Raft group manages its own compaction.
Q5How does Raft handle a split vote, and why can't it loop forever?
A
A split vote occurs when multiple candidates split votes among themselves and none achieves majority. All candidates time out, increment their term, and start a new election. Raft prevents indefinite loops via randomised election timeouts: each node picks a random timeout in the range 150–300ms. With high probability, one node fires before the others, gets enough votes before competing candidates even start, and wins. The key insight: the randomisation doesn't need to be perfect — it just needs to be good enough that ties are rare, and in practice they resolve in 1–2 rounds. In a 5-node cluster the probability of a split vote in round k drops geometrically with k.
Q6Why can a leader only commit entries from its current term?
A
This is the most subtle safety rule in Raft. Suppose a leader in Term 4 has an uncommitted entry from Term 2 that appears on 3 of 5 nodes. It might seem safe to commit it — majority has it. But here's the bug: a new election can happen with a candidate that doesn't have the Term 4 leader's log entries but does have a Term 3 entry that wins it majority votes. That new leader would then overwrite the Term 2 entry that the previous leader thought was committed. The fix: the leader can only commit an entry from the current term. Once a current-term entry is committed, all preceding entries are committed implicitly (because the log matching property ensures they are present on any node that has the current-term entry). This is why a freshly elected leader immediately proposes a no-op entry — it needs to commit something in its current term before it can safely advance commitIndex.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "would you use Raft for a new distributed system?" say: "Yes for the consensus layer, with deliberate trade-off decisions. In a single datacenter with up to 7 nodes per Raft group, Raft gives you strong consistency, understandable failure modes, and excellent tooling (etcd is production-grade). For higher write throughput I'd partition data into multiple Raft groups — this is exactly what CockroachDB, TiKV, and YugabyteDB do. For multi-region I'd evaluate geo-aware quorums (place the leader in the region with most writes) or consider Paxos variants with flexible quorums that can tolerate cross-region latency more gracefully. The single-leader bottleneck is real but manageable; the strong consistency guarantee is often worth it for coordination services, metadata stores, and configuration management."

Post 02

Consistent Hashing

25 min deep dive Modulo Hashing Problem · Ring Hash · Virtual Nodes · Replication · Real-World Systems
The Problem

Why naive hashing destroys distributed caches

You have a cache cluster of 3 servers. You receive a key "user:42". The obvious approach: compute a hash and use modulo to pick the server. hash("user:42") % 3 = 1 → goes to Server 1. Simple and fast. Now your traffic triples — you add a fourth server. Suddenly hash("user:42") % 4 = 2 → Server 2. Every key that used to go to Server 1 now goes somewhere else. Your entire cache is invalidated. Every request misses. Your database gets hammered with 100% of traffic at once.

The Core Problem with key % n

When n changes — you add or remove a server — nearly all keys remap. Specifically, only 1/n keys stay on the same server. The other (n-1)/n keys move. For a 3→4 server change, roughly 75% of all keys remap. For a cache cluster with millions of keys, this is catastrophic: every remapped key is a cache miss that hits your database simultaneously — a thundering herd.

Consistent hashing solves this by fundamentally changing the mapping: instead of mapping keys to servers by index, we map both keys and servers onto the same abstract circular space — a "ring" of integers from 0 to 2³²-1. Adding or removing a server affects only the keys that were on that server's immediate predecessor arc — roughly 1/n of total keys. The other (n-1)/n keys are completely undisturbed.

Building Intuition

The clock-face mental model — before we see any math

The Clock-Face Analogy

Imagine a clock face — not 12 hours, but numbered 0 to 100 continuously around the circle. You place three servers at positions 20, 50, and 80 on this dial. Now you want to store a key with hash value 35. You put your finger at 35, then sweep clockwise until you hit a server. The first server you reach is the one at position 50. That is your server for this key.

Now add a 4th server at position 65. Only keys between 50 and 65 (those that were going to Server@80 by sweeping clockwise past 65) need to move — everything else is untouched. You moved at most 1/4 of your keys.

Remove Server@50. Keys between 20 and 50 now sweep to Server@80 instead. Again, only ~1/n keys affected.

Diagram 1 · The Modulo Problem — why 75% of keys remap

Adding 1 server with key % n breaks almost every mapping

modulo-problem.svg
Diagram 2 · The Consistent Hash Ring — complete anatomy

Both keys AND servers live on the same circular number line

The ring is the integers 0 to 2³²-1 (about 4 billion positions) arranged in a circle. A hash function (like MD5 or SHA-1 truncated) maps both server names and cache keys into this space. The rule is simple: a key belongs to the first server clockwise from its position. This "first clockwise" rule is what makes additions and removals local — they only affect a contiguous arc on the ring.

consistent-hash-ring-anatomy.svg
Animation · Adding Server S4 — step-by-step key remapping

Watch exactly which keys move and which stay — with speed control

ch-add-server-animation
Ready
Virtual Nodes (vnodes) — fixing uneven load distribution

3 physical servers, each with 3 virtual positions on the ring

Basic consistent hashing has a problem: with only 3 servers, each server owns about 1/3 of the ring, but in practice the arcs are rarely exactly equal — one server may own 40% of the ring, another 25%. This is load imbalance. Worse, when a server is removed, all its keys pile onto a single successor.

Virtual nodes solve this: each physical server is placed at multiple positions on the ring using different hash seeds. hash("S1#1"), hash("S1#2"), hash("S1#3") each give a different ring position for S1. Now S1 owns several small arcs spread around the ring. Load is much more even, and when S1 fails, its load is distributed across all remaining servers (not just one).

virtual-nodes.svg
How many virtual nodes?

Production systems typically use 100–200 virtual nodes per physical server. Amazon DynamoDB uses the concept to spread token ranges, Cassandra defaults to 256 vnodes per node (configurable with num_tokens). More vnodes = better balance but more metadata to store and more coordination overhead during membership changes. 150 vnodes per node is a common sweet spot.

Replication — storing N copies on N clockwise successors

How DynamoDB uses the ring for replication, not just routing

Consistent hashing + replication: when a key hashes to position P, store it on the N next clockwise servers (called the preference list). If N=3, the key lives on the coordinator plus the next 2 clockwise physical nodes. This gives fault tolerance: the key survives 2 server failures. DynamoDB uses N=3 by default. Cassandra calls this the replication factor.

replication-ring.svg
Trade-offs

When consistent hashing shines — and where it struggles

trade-offs-table
Code · Python & Java — complete implementation

Full consistent hashing implementation with virtual nodes

PYTHON
import hashlib
import bisect
from typing import Optional

class ConsistentHashRing:
    """
    Consistent hashing ring with virtual nodes.

    Each physical server is represented by `replicas` virtual
    nodes on the ring.  A key maps to the first virtual node
    whose position is ≥ hash(key) (clockwise walk).
    """

    def __init__(self, replicas: int = 150):
        self.replicas   = replicas           # virtual nodes per server
        self._ring: dict[int, str] = {}      # pos → server_name
        self._sorted_keys: list[int] = []    # sorted list of positions

    # ── internal hash ──────────────────────────────────────────
    def _hash(self, key: str) -> int:
        # MD5 then take first 4 bytes as uint32
        h = hashlib.md5(key.encode()).digest()
        return int.from_bytes(h[:4], 'big')

    # ── add a server ───────────────────────────────────────────
    def add_server(self, server: str) -> None:
        for i in range(self.replicas):
            vnode_key = f"{server}#{i}"          # e.g. "server1#42"
            pos = self._hash(vnode_key)
            self._ring[pos] = server
            bisect.insort(self._sorted_keys, pos)  # maintain sorted order

    # ── remove a server ────────────────────────────────────────
    def remove_server(self, server: str) -> None:
        for i in range(self.replicas):
            vnode_key = f"{server}#{i}"
            pos = self._hash(vnode_key)
            if pos in self._ring:
                del self._ring[pos]
                idx = bisect.bisect_left(self._sorted_keys, pos)
                self._sorted_keys.pop(idx)

    # ── find server for a key ──────────────────────────────────
    def get_server(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        if not self._ring:
            return None
        pos = self._hash(key)
        # bisect_right gives index of first position > pos
        # wrap around with modulo (the ring wraps)
        idx = bisect.bisect_right(self._sorted_keys, pos) % len(self._sorted_keys)
        ring_pos = self._sorted_keys[idx]
        return self._ring[ring_pos]

    # ── replication: N servers for a key ──────────────────────
    def get_servers(self, key: str, n: int = 3) -> list[str]:
        """Return the N clockwise distinct physical servers for key."""
        if not self._ring:
            return []
        pos = self._hash(key)
        idx = bisect.bisect_right(self._sorted_keys, pos) % len(self._sorted_keys)
        servers_seen: list[str] = []
        servers_set: set[str] = set()
        for _ in range(len(self._sorted_keys)):
            ring_pos = self._sorted_keys[idx % len(self._sorted_keys)]
            server = self._ring[ring_pos]
            if server not in servers_set:
                servers_seen.append(server)
                servers_set.add(server)
                if len(servers_seen) == n:
                    break
            idx += 1
        return servers_seen

# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
ring = ConsistentHashRing(replicas=150)
for s in ["server1", "server2", "server3"]:
    ring.add_server(s)

# Measure distribution
from collections import Counter
counts = Counter(ring.get_server(f"key:{i}") for i in range(10_000))
for server, count in sorted(counts.items()):
    print(f"{server}: {count} keys ({count/100:.1f}%)")
# → server1: 3321 keys (33.2%)
# → server2: 3298 keys (33.0%)
# → server3: 3381 keys (33.8%)

# Show remapping on scale-out
before = {f"key:{i}": ring.get_server(f"key:{i}") for i in range(10_000)}
ring.add_server("server4")
after  = {f"key:{i}": ring.get_server(f"key:{i}") for i in range(10_000)}
moved  = sum(1 for k in before if before[k] != after[k])
print(f"Keys moved: {moved}/10000 ({moved/100:.1f}%)")
# → Keys moved: ~2500/10000 (25.0%)  ← only 1/4, not 75%!

# Replication: get 3 servers for a key
servers = ring.get_servers("user:42", n=3)
print(f"Preference list for user:42: {servers}")
# → ['server2', 'server4', 'server1']
JAVA
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;
import java.security.MessageDigest;
import java.util.*;

public class ConsistentHashRing {
    private final int replicas;         // virtual nodes per server
    private final TreeMap<Long, String> ring = new TreeMap<>();

    public ConsistentHashRing(int replicas) {
        this.replicas = replicas;
    }

    // ── internal hash → unsigned 32-bit ──────────────────────
    private long hash(String key) {
        try {
            MessageDigest md = MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5");
            byte[] digest = md.digest(key.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8));
            long h = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++)
                h = (h << 8) | (digest[i] & 0xFFL);
            return h & 0xFFFFFFFFL;   // unsigned 32-bit
        } catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); }
    }

    // ── add server ────────────────────────────────────────────
    public void addServer(String server) {
        for (int i = 0; i < replicas; i++)
            ring.put(hash(server + "#" + i), server);
    }

    // ── remove server ─────────────────────────────────────────
    public void removeServer(String server) {
        for (int i = 0; i < replicas; i++)
            ring.remove(hash(server + "#" + i));
    }

    // ── get single server for key ─────────────────────────────
    public String getServer(String key) {
        if (ring.isEmpty()) return null;
        long pos = hash(key);
        // ceilingEntry: first entry with key ≥ pos (clockwise walk)
        Map.Entry<Long, String> entry = ring.ceilingEntry(pos);
        if (entry == null)
            entry = ring.firstEntry();  // wrap around the ring
        return entry.getValue();
    }

    // ── get N servers (preference list for replication) ───────
    public List<String> getServers(String key, int n) {
        if (ring.isEmpty()) return Collections.emptyList();
        long pos = hash(key);
        Set<String> seen = new LinkedHashSet<>();

        // Walk clockwise from pos, collect distinct physical servers
        NavigableMap<Long, String> tail = ring.tailMap(pos, true);
        for (String s : tail.values()) {
            seen.add(s);
            if (seen.size() == n) return new ArrayList<>(seen);
        }
        // wrap: continue from ring start
        for (String s : ring.values()) {
            seen.add(s);
            if (seen.size() == n) break;
        }
        return new ArrayList<>(seen);
    }

    // ── demo ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ConsistentHashRing ring = new ConsistentHashRing(150);
        for (String s : new String[]{"server1","server2","server3"})
            ring.addServer(s);

        // Distribution check
        Map<String,Integer> counts = new HashMap<>();
        for (int i=0; i<10_000; i++) {
            String sv = ring.getServer("key:"+i);
            counts.merge(sv, 1, Integer::sum);
        }
        counts.forEach((k,v) ->
            System.out.printf("%s: %d (%.1f%%)%n", k, v, v/100.0));

        // Remapping ratio after adding server4
        Map<String,String> before = new HashMap<>();
        for (int i=0; i<10_000; i++)
            before.put("key:"+i, ring.getServer("key:"+i));
        ring.addServer("server4");
        long moved = before.entrySet().stream()
            .filter(e -> !e.getValue().equals(ring.getServer(e.getKey())))
            .count();
        System.out.printf("Moved: %d/10000 (%.1f%%)%n", moved, moved/100.0);
        // → Moved: ~2500/10000 (25.0%)

        // Preference list for replication
        System.out.println(ring.getServers("user:42", 3));
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about consistent hashing

QWhy is key % n bad for distributed caches — give me the exact math.
A
With n servers, changing to n+1 servers means a key at position k moves from server k%n to k%(n+1). These are equal only when n divides k with the same remainder in both, which happens approximately 1/(n+1) of the time. So about n/(n+1) of all keys remap — for 3→4 servers that is 75%. Consistent hashing reduces this to exactly 1/(n+1) remapping on average because only the new server's arc (of size ~1/n) is affected.
QWhat is a virtual node and why do you need 150 of them?
A
A virtual node is a hash ring position that maps back to a physical server. One physical server has multiple virtual nodes. With 3 physical servers and no vnodes, each server owns one contiguous arc — those arcs vary in size due to hash function non-uniformity, causing load imbalance. With 150 vnodes per server, you have 450 positions on the ring interleaved, and by the law of large numbers the total arc owned by each server converges to ~1/3. 150 is the empirical sweet spot: enough for good balance (standard deviation of load <10%), not so many that ring membership changes are expensive. Cassandra defaults to 256.
QHow does DynamoDB use consistent hashing?
A
DynamoDB hashes partition keys (not sort keys) onto a ring. Each partition key maps to a coordinator node, and the item is replicated on the next N-1 clockwise physical nodes (N=3 by default). Reads and writes use a quorum: W+R > N. For eventual consistency R=1 (fast), for strong consistency R=2 (ensures at least one up-to-date replica). DynamoDB also uses token ranges (a range of ring positions) rather than individual vnodes, and manages token assignment centrally to ensure balance across Availability Zones.
QA single key is getting 100× the traffic of average. How do you fix it with consistent hashing in place?
A
Consistent hashing maps one key to one server — it doesn't help with a hot key. Solutions: (1) Key sharding: append a random suffix 0–9 to create user:celebrity:0 through user:celebrity:9, which distribute across 10 different ring positions. Reads must fan out to all 10 and aggregate. (2) Local caching: cache the hot key in each application server's in-process cache for a short TTL (1–5 seconds). (3) Read replicas: route hot-key reads to a dedicated read replica pool. The root issue is that hashing provides distribution across keys but not distribution of one key across servers.
QWhen a server crashes, how does consistent hashing decide who gets its keys without coordination?
A
Each client independently computes get_server(key) — when the dead server's virtual nodes are gone from the ring (either the client detects failure via health check, or the routing layer removes the server), the next clockwise virtual node becomes the first CW node for any key that was on the dead server. All clients, running the same algorithm with the same ring state, independently route to the same replacement server. This is the key property: consistent hashing is deterministic given the same ring state — no coordinator, no consensus needed for routing decisions.
Senior-Level Power Answer

If asked "design the sharding layer for a distributed cache", say: "I'd use consistent hashing with virtual nodes. Each cache server gets 150 virtual positions on a 2³² ring using SHA-1 truncated to 32 bits. Adding a node steals ~1/n keys from existing nodes; removing migrates its keys to the next clockwise nodes. For replication I'd use a preference list of N=3 clockwise distinct physical nodes per key, with R=2/W=2 quorum. To handle hot keys I'd add a local in-process LRU cache with a 2-second TTL for keys above a frequency threshold. I'd implement the ring as a sorted array (or Java TreeMap) — lookups are O(log n) for 150×servers positions, which is very fast. For the failure detection feeding node removals, I'd use a gossip protocol or rely on the load balancer's health check."

Post 03

CAP Theorem

25 min deep dive Consistency · Availability · Partition Tolerance · CP vs AP · PACELC · Real Systems
The Problem

Why you cannot have everything in a distributed system

You're building a distributed database that runs across three servers. A client writes balance = $1000 to Server A. One millisecond later, the network cable between A and B is severed. Now a second client reads balance from Server B. What do you return?

If you return the stale $800 (old value), you are available but inconsistent. If you refuse to answer until you can confirm the latest value, you are consistent but unavailable. You cannot do both. This dilemma, formalised by Eric Brewer in 2000 and proved by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002, is the CAP Theorem.

CAP Theorem — Formal Statement

In any distributed system that experiences a network partition (messages between nodes are lost or delayed), you must choose one of: Consistency (every read returns the most recent write or an error) OR Availability (every request receives a response — not necessarily the most recent data). You cannot guarantee both simultaneously during a partition.

The crucial nuance that trips up most engineers: Partition Tolerance is not optional. Networks fail. Packets drop. Data centers get split. Any distributed system deployed in reality must tolerate partitions — so the CAP choice is really a binary: when a partition occurs, do you sacrifice consistency or availability? This makes CAP a CP/AP choice, not a three-way trade-off.

Building Intuition — from first principles

Why you cannot have C and A during a partition — the proof in plain English

The Two-Node Proof (Intuition)

Suppose you have two nodes N1 and N2, and a partition occurs — they cannot communicate. A client writes x=1 to N1. Another client then reads x from N2.

For Availability: N2 must respond. It doesn't know about the write to N1 (partition). So it must either respond with the old value (stale = inconsistent) or refuse (not available).

For Consistency: N2 must return the latest value. But it can't reach N1 to get it. So it must either wait (not available) or refuse with an error (not available).

Conclusion: You cannot have both. Pick one. Real systems pick their trade-off upfront and design around it.

Diagram 1 · What C, A, and P actually mean — precise definitions

Most engineers misdefine at least one of the three — get these exact

cap-definitions.svg
Diagram 2 · CP vs AP — where real systems sit and why

Every major database has made this choice — know where they land

cp-vs-ap-systems.svg
Animation · Network Partition — CP blocks, AP serves stale

Step-by-step: same partition, two different system choices, two different outcomes

cap-partition-animation
Ready
PACELC — the extension CAP doesn't tell you

Even without a partition, you still trade off latency vs consistency

CAP only describes behaviour during a partition. But partitions are rare. What about normal operation? Daniel Abadi proposed PACELC in 2012: if Partition (P), choose between Availability (A) and Consistency (C); Else (E, no partition), choose between Latency (L) and Consistency (C).

This is often more relevant in practice. A system might be CP (sacrifices A during partitions) but also EL (sacrifices C for low latency in normal operation). DynamoDB is PA/EL — prioritises availability during partitions and low latency normally. CockroachDB is PC/EC — consistent in both cases (but with higher latency).

pacelc-table.svg
Trade-offs — when to choose CP vs AP

The business context determines the right choice

CP — choose when wrong data = disaster

Financial transactions (debit/credit must be consistent), inventory management (can't sell the same item twice), distributed locking (must never have two lock holders), leader election (must have exactly one leader), configuration management (all services must see the same config). In all these cases, returning an error is safer than returning a stale value.

AP — choose when availability is revenue

Shopping carts (stale cart is fine, empty cart loses revenue), social media feeds (slightly stale is imperceptible), DNS resolution (stale IP is better than NXDOMAIN), product catalogue (stale price is OK), user profile views (stale bio is fine), IoT sensor readings (approximate is fine). In all these cases, a partial/stale response is far better than an error.

The nuance: most systems are not purely CP or AP

DynamoDB lets you choose per-request: ConsistentRead=false (AP, lower latency, cheaper) or ConsistentRead=true (CP, costs 2× read units, slightly slower). Cassandra lets you choose consistency per operation: QUORUM (CP-like), ONE (AP-like). This is called tunable consistency and is the practical answer to the CP/AP dilemma for most applications.

Code · Python & Java — implementing CP and AP behaviour

Simulate a two-node partition and observe CP vs AP responses

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Dict
import time, threading

# ── Node state ─────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Node:
    node_id: str
    data: Dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
    _partitioned: bool = False

    def partition(self):
        """Simulate network partition — disconnect from peers."""
        self._partitioned = True

    def heal(self):
        self._partitioned = False

    def can_reach_peer(self) -> bool:
        return not self._partitioned


# ── CP Node (sacrifice availability) ───────────────────────
class CPNode(Node):
    """
    Consistent/Partition-tolerant.
    Refuses reads/writes if it cannot confirm quorum.
    """
    def write(self, key: str, value: int, peer: 'Node') -> bool:
        if not self.can_reach_peer():
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"[CP] Write rejected: cannot replicate to peer (partition). "
                f"Refusing write to maintain consistency."
            )
        self.data[key] = value
        peer.data[key] = value   # synchronous replication
        return True

    def read(self, key: str, peer: 'Node') -> Optional[int]:
        if not self.can_reach_peer():
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"[CP] Read rejected: cannot verify latest value (partition)."
            )
        return self.data.get(key)


# ── AP Node (sacrifice consistency) ────────────────────────
class APNode(Node):
    """
    Available/Partition-tolerant.
    Always responds, even if data may be stale.
    Queues writes for replay after partition heals.
    """
    write_queue: list = field(default_factory=list)

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.write_queue = []

    def write(self, key: str, value: int, peer: 'Node') -> bool:
        self.data[key] = value          # always accept locally
        if not self.can_reach_peer():
            self.write_queue.append((key, value))
            print(f"[AP] Accepted write locally. Queued for peer sync.")
        else:
            peer.data[key] = value      # replicate immediately if healthy
        return True                     # always returns success

    def read(self, key: str, peer: 'Node') -> Optional[int]:
        # Always responds with local value — may be stale
        val = self.data.get(key)
        if not self.can_reach_peer():
            print(f"[AP] Serving potentially stale value: {key}={val}")
        return val

    def sync_after_heal(self, peer: 'Node'):
        """Replay queued writes after partition heals (last-write-wins)."""
        for key, value in self.write_queue:
            peer.data[key] = value
            print(f"[AP] Synced queued write: {key}={value}")
        self.write_queue.clear()


# ── Simulation ─────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=== CP System ===")
cp_a = CPNode("A", data={"balance": 800})
cp_b = CPNode("B", data={"balance": 800})

cp_a.partition()                         # network split!
try:
    cp_a.write("balance", 1000, cp_b)    # should fail
except RuntimeError as e:
    print(e)
    # [CP] Write rejected: cannot replicate to peer (partition).
try:
    cp_b.read("balance", cp_a)
except RuntimeError as e:
    print(e)
    # [CP] Read rejected: cannot verify latest value (partition).

print("\n=== AP System ===")
ap_a = APNode("A", data={"balance": 800})
ap_b = APNode("B", data={"balance": 800})

ap_a.partition()                         # network split!
ap_a.write("balance", 1000, ap_b)
# → [AP] Accepted write locally. Queued for peer sync.
stale = ap_b.read("balance", ap_a)
print(f"B reads: {stale}")          # → B reads: 800 (stale!)

ap_a.heal()
ap_a.sync_after_heal(ap_b)           # repair after partition heals
# → [AP] Synced queued write: balance=1000
fresh = ap_b.read("balance", ap_a)
print(f"B reads after heal: {fresh}") # → B reads after heal: 1000
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicBoolean;

// ── Base Node ──────────────────────────────────────────────
abstract class Node {
    protected final String id;
    protected final Map<String, Integer> data = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    protected final AtomicBoolean partitioned = new AtomicBoolean(false);

    public Node(String id) { this.id = id; }
    public void partition() { partitioned.set(true); }
    public void heal()      { partitioned.set(false); }

    public abstract boolean write(String key, int value, Node peer);
    public abstract Optional<Integer> read(String key, Node peer);
}

// ── CP Node ────────────────────────────────────────────────
class CPNode extends Node {
    public CPNode(String id) { super(id); }

    @Override
    public boolean write(String key, int value, Node peer) {
        if (partitioned.get())
            throw new IllegalStateException(
                "[CP] Write rejected: partition — cannot guarantee consistency.");
        data.put(key, value);
        peer.data.put(key, value);   // sync replication
        return true;
    }

    @Override
    public Optional<Integer> read(String key, Node peer) {
        if (partitioned.get())
            throw new IllegalStateException(
                "[CP] Read rejected: cannot verify latest value.");
        return Optional.ofNullable(data.get(key));
    }
}

// ── AP Node ────────────────────────────────────────────────
class APNode extends Node {
    private final Queue<Map.Entry<String,Integer>> writeQueue
        = new ConcurrentLinkedQueue<>();

    public APNode(String id) { super(id); }

    @Override
    public boolean write(String key, int value, Node peer) {
        data.put(key, value);   // always accept locally
        if (partitioned.get()) {
            writeQueue.add(Map.entry(key, value));
            System.out.println("[AP] Accepted locally, queued for sync.");
        } else {
            peer.data.put(key, value);
        }
        return true;   // always succeeds
    }

    @Override
    public Optional<Integer> read(String key, Node peer) {
        if (partitioned.get())
            System.out.println("[AP] Serving potentially stale: " + key);
        return Optional.ofNullable(data.get(key));
    }

    public void syncAfterHeal(Node peer) {
        while (!writeQueue.isEmpty()) {
            var e = writeQueue.poll();
            peer.data.put(e.getKey(), e.getValue());
            System.out.printf("[AP] Synced: %s=%d%n",
                e.getKey(), e.getValue());
        }
    }
}

// ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
public class CAPDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== CP System ===");
        CPNode cpA = new CPNode("A"); cpA.data.put("balance", 800);
        CPNode cpB = new CPNode("B"); cpB.data.put("balance", 800);
        cpA.partition();
        try { cpA.write("balance", 1000, cpB); }
        catch (IllegalStateException e) { System.out.println(e.getMessage()); }
        try { cpB.read("balance", cpA); }
        catch (IllegalStateException e) { System.out.println(e.getMessage()); }

        System.out.println("\n=== AP System ===");
        APNode apA = new APNode("A"); apA.data.put("balance", 800);
        APNode apB = new APNode("B"); apB.data.put("balance", 800);
        apA.partition();
        apA.write("balance", 1000, apB);
        System.out.println("B reads: " + apB.read("balance", apA).orElse(-1));
        // → B reads: 800  (stale)
        apA.heal();
        apA.syncAfterHeal(apB);
        System.out.println("B after heal: " + apB.read("balance", apA).orElse(-1));
        // → B after heal: 1000
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about CAP Theorem

QWhy is "CA" (Consistent + Available) not a valid choice for a distributed system?
A
CA means you guarantee both consistency and availability — but only if you also assume no partitions ever occur. In a single-node system this is trivially true. In a multi-node distributed system, network partitions are inevitable: cables break, switches fail, GC pauses cause nodes to appear dead. You cannot control or eliminate partitions in a real distributed system. So saying "we're CA" means "we shut down when a partition occurs" — which is not a distributed system, just a single-node system with replicas that go offline. Real distributed systems must be either CP or AP.
QCassandra is typically called an AP system. But you can configure it to behave like CP. How?
A
Cassandra has tunable consistency per operation. With replication factor N=3, if you set WRITE QUORUM (requires 2/3 nodes to ack) and READ QUORUM (reads from 2/3 nodes), then W+R=4 > N=3, which guarantees you always read a value at least one node received the write — this is linearisable, CP behaviour. At CONSISTENCY ONE for reads, R=1 < 2, which cannot guarantee freshness — AP behaviour. The trade-off: QUORUM halves your write throughput and increases latency (must wait for majority). ONE is fast but potentially stale.
QWhat is the difference between CAP consistency and ACID consistency?
A
They are completely different concepts that share the word "consistency" — a source of massive confusion. CAP consistency = linearisability: reads always return the most recent committed write across all replicas. It's about distributed replication behaviour during network failures. ACID consistency = database invariants: a transaction must bring the database from one valid state to another (e.g. the sum of all account balances must always equal X). It's about data integrity rules within a single transaction. You can have ACID consistency without CAP consistency (a single-node database). You can have CAP consistency without ACID consistency (a key-value store that replicates synchronously but has no transaction support).
QWhat is PACELC and why is it a more useful model than CAP in practice?
A
CAP only describes behaviour during a partition. Partitions are rare in practice — most of the time your network is healthy. But there's still a trade-off in normal operation: if you require strong consistency (every read confirms with all replicas before returning), latency increases. PACELC formalises this: "if Partition (P): choose between Availability (A) and Consistency (C). Else (E): choose between Latency (L) and Consistency (C)." DynamoDB is PA/EL — AP during partitions, optimises for latency normally. CockroachDB is PC/EC — consistent in both cases. PACELC is more useful because it describes everyday trade-offs, not just rare failure scenarios.
QIs MongoDB CP or AP? Trick question — explain.
A
It depends on write concern configuration. With w:1 (default): write is acknowledged after the primary writes, before replication — this is AP. The primary can accept writes during a partition and replicas diverge. With w:majority: write blocks until a majority of replica set members acknowledge — CP. Writes are refused if quorum is unavailable. Reads also have this duality: readPreference: secondary can return stale data (AP), readPreference: primary with readConcern: majority is linearisable (CP). The answer to MongoDB's CAP position is: "it's tunable — choose per operation." This is the correct nuanced answer in any senior interview.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "is X database CP or AP", the senior answer is: "That framing is incomplete. CAP only describes partition-time behaviour, and most databases are tunable. The better question is: what consistency level does your application need per operation? For account balances and inventory I'd use CP behaviour — write with quorum, read with quorum, accept the latency cost. For shopping carts and feeds I'd use AP behaviour — write locally, replicate async, accept occasional staleness. The really important model is PACELC: you're always trading consistency against latency even without partitions, and that's the everyday trade-off that matters more than the rare partition scenario."

Post 04

Two-Phase Commit (2PC)

25 min deep dive Prepare · Commit · Coordinator Failure · Blocking Problem · 3PC · Sagas
The Problem

How do you atomically commit a transaction across multiple databases?

You're building an e-commerce checkout. When a user pays, three things must happen atomically: (1) debit $150 from the payments database, (2) decrement stock by 1 in the inventory database, (3) create an order record in the orders database. Either all three happen, or none do. You cannot have a world where the money is debited but the order never gets created.

Within a single database, this is trivial: wrap it in a transaction with BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK. But these are three separate databases, potentially on different servers in different data centres. There is no shared transaction manager. How do you achieve atomicity across them?

The Core Challenge: Distributed Atomicity

Single-node transactions are easy because the commit is a local operation — the database either writes or doesn't. Distributed transactions are hard because you need every participant to commit or every participant to roll back. Any asymmetry — one commits, one rolls back — leaves data permanently inconsistent with no automatic repair. Two-Phase Commit is the classic protocol for coordinating this decision.

Building Intuition — the wedding vow analogy

2PC is exactly how a wedding ceremony works

The Wedding Analogy

Phase 1 (Prepare = "Do you take this person?"): The officiant (coordinator) asks each participant "are you prepared to commit?" Each participant privately checks: "Is my data valid? Do I have all locks? Can I guarantee I will commit if asked?" If yes, they say "YES" and write this vote to a durable log. This is a promise they cannot break — once they vote YES, they must commit if asked.

Phase 2 (Commit/Abort = "I now pronounce you..."): Only after ALL participants vote YES does the coordinator make the global decision. If even one votes NO (or doesn't respond), the coordinator sends ABORT to everyone. If all vote YES, the coordinator sends COMMIT to everyone.

The problem: What if the officiant collapses between phases? All participants have said "YES" (they are locked and waiting), but they never hear the final verdict. They are stuck — they cannot commit without the coordinator's COMMIT, they cannot abort without the coordinator's ABORT. This is the blocking problem.

Diagram 1 · 2PC Happy Path — complete message flow

All participants vote YES → coordinator commits → everyone commits

2pc-happy-path.svg
Diagram 2 · The Blocking Problem — coordinator crashes after PREPARE

Participants voted YES, hold locks, and now they are stuck forever

This is the fundamental flaw of 2PC. After a participant votes YES, it enters the prepared state: it has acquired all locks, written its intent to the WAL, and is waiting. It cannot unilaterally commit (it might conflict with other participants) and it cannot unilaterally abort (it promised YES). If the coordinator crashes now — before broadcasting its Phase 2 decision — the participants are blocked indefinitely.

2pc-blocking-problem.svg
Animation · 2PC Full Flow — prepare, vote, crash, block

Step-by-step: happy path then coordinator crash — watch participants freeze

2pc-animation
Ready
Solutions — 3PC, Paxos Commit, and Sagas

How the industry works around 2PC's blocking problem

2pc-alternatives.svg
Trade-offs

2PC in the real world — when to use it and when to avoid it

When 2PC is appropriate

Financial double-entry bookkeeping (debit one account, credit another — must be atomic), inventory reservation (reserve stock + create order), distributed lock acquisition (all participants must hold the lock or none do). In these cases, the blocking risk is acceptable because the alternative — inconsistency — is catastrophic.

The blocking problem in practice

Most production systems using 2PC (e.g. PostgreSQL + foreign data wrappers, XA transactions in Java EE) handle coordinator failure by: (1) writing the decision to a durable log before Phase 2, (2) on restart, the coordinator reads its log and replays Phase 2 to any participants that didn't ACK. The blocking window is the downtime of the coordinator — typically seconds to minutes with good monitoring. For most OLTP applications this is acceptable.

When to avoid 2PC

Microservices architecture (services should be independently deployable, 2PC creates tight coupling), high-throughput systems (2PC holds locks for the full round-trip duration — often 50-200ms in multi-DC), or when eventual consistency is acceptable (shopping carts, social feeds). In these cases, use Sagas with compensating transactions or event-driven eventual consistency.

Code · Python & Java — complete 2PC implementation

Working coordinator and participant with crash simulation

PYTHON
from enum import Enum, auto
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional
import random

class Vote(Enum):
    YES  = auto()
    NO   = auto()

class TxnState(Enum):
    IDLE       = auto()
    PREPARED   = auto()  # voted YES, awaiting Phase 2
    COMMITTED  = auto()
    ABORTED    = auto()

# ── Participant ────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Participant:
    name: str
    fail_on_prepare: bool = False  # inject fault
    state: TxnState = TxnState.IDLE
    pending_value: Optional[int] = None
    committed_value: Optional[int] = None

    def prepare(self, value: int) -> Vote:
        if self.fail_on_prepare:
            print(f"  [{self.name}] prepare() — VOTING NO (simulated failure)")
            return Vote.NO

        # Check constraints, acquire locks, write to WAL
        self.pending_value = value
        self.state = TxnState.PREPARED
        print(f"  [{self.name}] prepare({value}) — acquired lock, wrote WAL → VOTE YES")
        return Vote.YES

    def commit(self):
        if self.state != TxnState.PREPARED:
            raise RuntimeError(f"{self.name}: commit() called but not in PREPARED state")
        self.committed_value = self.pending_value
        self.state = TxnState.COMMITTED
        print(f"  [{self.name}] commit() — applied value={self.committed_value}, released lock")

    def abort(self):
        self.pending_value = None
        self.state = TxnState.ABORTED
        print(f"  [{self.name}] abort() — rolled back, released lock")


# ── Coordinator ────────────────────────────────────────────
class Coordinator:
    def __init__(self, participants: List[Participant], crash_after_decide=False):
        self.participants    = participants
        self.crash_after_decide = crash_after_decide
        self._log: list      = []   # durable decision log

    def _write_log(self, entry: str):
        self._log.append(entry)
        print(f"  [Coordinator] LOG: {entry}")

    def execute(self, values: list[int]) -> bool:
        # ── PHASE 1: Prepare ────────────────────────────────
        print("\n── Phase 1: PREPARE ──")
        votes = []
        for p, v in zip(self.participants, values):
            vote = p.prepare(v)
            votes.append(vote)

        # ── Decision ─────────────────────────────────────────
        decision = "COMMIT" if all(v == Vote.YES for v in votes) else "ABORT"
        self._write_log(f"Decision: {decision}")   # MUST persist before Phase 2

        if self.crash_after_decide:
            print("  [Coordinator] ⚡ CRASH! Participants are now BLOCKED.")
            print("  [Coordinator] They hold locks and await Phase 2 that never comes.")
            return False

        # ── PHASE 2: Commit or Abort ─────────────────────────
        print(f"\n── Phase 2: {decision} ──")
        if decision == "COMMIT":
            for p in self.participants: p.commit()
        else:
            for p in self.participants: p.abort()
        return decision == "COMMIT"

    def recover(self):
        """Coordinator restarts — replay last log entry to unblock participants."""
        if not self._log:
            print("  [Coordinator] No log entries — nothing to recover")
            return
        last = self._log[-1]
        print(f"\n── Recovery: replaying '{last}' ──")
        if "COMMIT" in last:
            for p in self.participants:
                if p.state == TxnState.PREPARED:
                    p.commit()
        elif "ABORT" in last:
            for p in self.participants:
                if p.state in (TxnState.PREPARED, TxnState.IDLE):
                    p.abort()


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=== Happy Path ===")
pa = Participant("Payments DB")
pb = Participant("Inventory DB")
coord = Coordinator([pa, pb])
coord.execute([-150, -1])   # debit $150, decrement stock

print("\n=== One Participant Fails ===")
pa2 = Participant("Payments DB")
pb2 = Participant("Inventory DB", fail_on_prepare=True)
coord2 = Coordinator([pa2, pb2])
coord2.execute([-150, -1])   # Inventory votes NO → ABORT

print("\n=== Coordinator Crash + Recovery ===")
pa3 = Participant("Payments DB")
pb3 = Participant("Inventory DB")
coord3 = Coordinator([pa3, pb3], crash_after_decide=True)
coord3.execute([-150, -1])   # Crash! Participants blocked.
coord3.recover()            # Restart and replay.
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicReference;

enum Vote      { YES, NO }
enum TxnState  { IDLE, PREPARED, COMMITTED, ABORTED }

// ── Participant ────────────────────────────────────────────
class Participant {
    private final String name;
    private final boolean failOnPrepare;
    private TxnState state = TxnState.IDLE;
    private Integer pending;
    private Integer committed;

    public Participant(String name, boolean failOnPrepare) {
        this.name = name;
        this.failOnPrepare = failOnPrepare;
    }

    public Vote prepare(int value) {
        if (failOnPrepare) {
            System.out.printf("  [%s] prepare() — VOTING NO%n", name);
            return Vote.NO;
        }
        pending = value;
        state = TxnState.PREPARED;
        System.out.printf("  [%s] prepare(%d) — locked, WAL written → YES%n", name, value);
        return Vote.YES;
    }

    public void commit() {
        if (state != TxnState.PREPARED)
            throw new IllegalStateException(name + ": not in PREPARED state");
        committed = pending;
        state = TxnState.COMMITTED;
        System.out.printf("  [%s] commit() — applied %d, lock released%n", name, committed);
    }

    public void abort() {
        pending = null;
        state = TxnState.ABORTED;
        System.out.printf("  [%s] abort() — rolled back, lock released%n", name);
    }

    public TxnState getState() { return state; }
}

// ── Coordinator ────────────────────────────────────────────
class Coordinator {
    private final List<Participant> participants;
    private final boolean crashAfterDecide;
    private final List<String> log = new ArrayList<>();

    public Coordinator(List<Participant> participants, boolean crashAfterDecide) {
        this.participants = participants;
        this.crashAfterDecide = crashAfterDecide;
    }

    private void writeLog(String entry) {
        log.add(entry);
        System.out.printf("  [Coordinator] LOG: %s%n", entry);
    }

    public boolean execute(int... values) {
        System.out.println("\n── Phase 1: PREPARE ──");
        List<Vote> votes = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < participants.size(); i++)
            votes.add(participants.get(i).prepare(values[i]));

        String decision = votes.stream().allMatch(v -> v == Vote.YES)
            ? "COMMIT" : "ABORT";
        writeLog("Decision: " + decision);

        if (crashAfterDecide) {
            System.out.println("  [Coordinator] ⚡ CRASH! Participants BLOCKED.");
            return false;
        }

        System.out.printf("%n── Phase 2: %s ──%n", decision);
        if (decision.equals("COMMIT"))
            participants.forEach(Participant::commit);
        else
            participants.forEach(Participant::abort);
        return decision.equals("COMMIT");
    }

    public void recover() {
        if (log.isEmpty()) return;
        String last = log.get(log.size() - 1);
        System.out.printf("%n── Recovery: replaying '%s' ──%n", last);
        if (last.contains("COMMIT"))
            participants.stream()
                .filter(p -> p.getState() == TxnState.PREPARED)
                .forEach(Participant::commit);
        else
            participants.stream()
                .filter(p -> p.getState() != TxnState.COMMITTED)
                .forEach(Participant::abort);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("=== Happy Path ===");
        var c1 = new Coordinator(List.of(
            new Participant("Payments", false),
            new Participant("Inventory", false)), false);
        c1.execute(-150, -1);

        System.out.println("\n=== Participant Fails ===");
        var c2 = new Coordinator(List.of(
            new Participant("Payments", false),
            new Participant("Inventory", true)), false);
        c2.execute(-150, -1);

        System.out.println("\n=== Coordinator Crash + Recovery ===");
        var pa = new Participant("Payments",  false);
        var pb = new Participant("Inventory", false);
        var c3 = new Coordinator(List.of(pa, pb), true);
        c3.execute(-150, -1);
        c3.recover();
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about 2PC

QExplain the blocking problem in 2PC and why it's hard to solve.
A
After a participant votes YES in Phase 1, it enters a prepared state: it has acquired locks and written its intent to a durable log, but it cannot make a unilateral decision. It cannot commit alone (it might violate atomicity if another participant aborted) and it cannot abort alone (it promised YES). If the coordinator crashes now, the participant is stuck holding locks indefinitely — this is the blocking problem. It's hard to solve because the participant has no way to know what the coordinator decided without contacting the coordinator (or at least a quorum that knows the decision). 3PC attempts to solve this by adding a pre-commit phase but doesn't handle network partitions safely. The production solution is to replicate the coordinator itself via Raft/Paxos (as Spanner and CockroachDB do) so coordinator failure triggers a fast leader election rather than blocking.
QWhy is 2PC considered an anti-pattern in microservices?
A
Three reasons: (1) Tight coupling — 2PC requires all participant services to expose a prepare/commit interface and be available simultaneously. In microservices, services should be independently deployable and loosely coupled. (2) Lock duration — 2PC holds locks for the full network round trip (potentially 100s of milliseconds or seconds). In high-throughput microservices, this creates latency spikes and cascading timeouts. (3) Failure modes — the blocking problem means one slow or crashed service can freeze transactions system-wide. The microservices alternative is the Saga pattern: each service executes a local transaction and publishes an event; on failure, compensating transactions run in reverse. Sagas have no blocking, no coordinator, and are loosely coupled — at the cost of no true isolation.
QWhat exactly does a participant write to its WAL during prepare, and why?
A
During prepare, the participant writes a prepare record to its Write-Ahead Log containing: the transaction ID, the operation to perform (e.g. SQL statements or key-value changes), and the vote (YES). This record is fsync'd to durable storage before voting YES. This durability guarantee is what makes the YES vote a binding promise: even if the participant crashes immediately after voting, when it restarts it reads the WAL, sees the prepare record, re-enters the prepared state, and waits for the coordinator's Phase 2 message. Without this WAL write, a crash-then-restart participant would have no memory of its promise, breaking atomicity.
QWhat is an XA transaction? How does it relate to 2PC?
A
XA is the X/Open standard defining the interface between a transaction manager (coordinator) and resource managers (participants, e.g. databases, message queues). It defines three calls: xa_start (begin branch), xa_prepare (Phase 1), xa_commit/xa_rollback (Phase 2). Most production databases support XA: PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle. Java EE / Jakarta EE application servers (Wildfly, WebLogic) act as the transaction manager. Spring's JtaTransactionManager wraps XA. The XA protocol is 2PC — the standard just provides a language-neutral interface so different databases can participate in the same 2PC transaction.
QGoogle Spanner says it uses 2PC but without the blocking problem. How?
A
Spanner uses 2PC but solves the coordinator single-point-of-failure by running the coordinator itself on a Paxos group. The coordinator's decision log is replicated to 2f+1 coordinator nodes. If the coordinator leader crashes, Paxos elects a new leader in milliseconds, and the new leader reads the replicated log and resumes Phase 2. Participants never block for more than one Paxos election timeout (~10ms in Spanner). Additionally, Spanner uses TrueTime (atomic clocks + GPS) to assign commit timestamps with bounded uncertainty, enabling external consistency (linearisability across globally distributed transactions) without a single global lock.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you handle distributed transactions in a new microservices system?", say: "I'd avoid 2PC in microservices. The blocking problem, tight coupling, and lock duration make it unsuitable for independently deployable services. Instead I'd use the Saga pattern with choreography-based or orchestration-based coordination. Each service executes a local ACID transaction and either publishes an event (choreography) or replies to an orchestrator (orchestration). On failure, compensating transactions run in reverse — for example, a 'cancel payment' event compensates a 'charge card' event. The trade-off is that Sagas have no isolation guarantee — a concurrent transaction can see intermediate state — so I'd handle this with semantic locks or idempotency keys. For cases that truly require atomicity with no tolerance for inconsistency — like double-entry bookkeeping — I'd keep those operations in a single ACID database and design the service boundary to not straddle transactions."

Post 05

Gossip Protocol

25 min deep dive Epidemic Spread · O(log N) Convergence · Failure Detection · Anti-Entropy · SWIM · Cassandra
The Problem

How does information spread reliably across hundreds of nodes with no central coordinator?

You have a 200-node Cassandra cluster. A node joins, leaves, or dies. Every other node needs to know about this membership change to route requests correctly. How do you broadcast this information?

The naive approach: have one "master" node broadcast to all 199 others. Problems: (1) the master is a single point of failure — if it crashes, nodes never hear about membership changes, (2) the master's network bandwidth scales as O(N) — at 1000 nodes it becomes a bottleneck, (3) the master must know about all nodes — itself a coordination problem.

The Core Insight

Gossip protocols solve this by mimicking how rumours spread in a social network: each node periodically picks a small number of random peers and exchanges state with them. No node knows the whole cluster. No coordinator exists. Yet the information reaches every node in O(log N) rounds — the same way a rumour spreads through a school. At 1000 nodes, that's about 10 rounds. Each round takes ~1 second. Convergence in ~10 seconds, with no central server and no O(N) bandwidth anywhere.

Building Intuition — from rumours to distributed systems

Why O(log N) is almost magical — the doubling argument

The Doubling Argument

Start with 1 infected node out of N=1024. Each round, every infected node gossips to k random peers (typically k=2). After round 1: ~3 nodes know. After round 2: ~7. After round 3: ~15. The count roughly doubles each round (minus re-infections of already-infected nodes). After log₂(N) = 10 rounds, all 1024 nodes know.

This is the same mathematics as binary search running backwards — each step halves the unknowing population. The key insight: you don't need O(N) message-sends to reach N nodes, you only need O(N log N) total messages spread across O(log N) rounds, with each node only ever sending to k peers per round.

The exact convergence probability is: after t rounds with k peers per round, the probability that a specific node has NOT received the update is approximately (1 - 1/N)^(k·t). For practical purposes, with k=2 and t = 3·log₂(N), this probability is negligible.

Diagram 1 · Gossip Round Mechanics — exactly what happens each cycle

Every gossip interval (e.g. 1 second) each node does exactly this

gossip-round-mechanics.svg
Diagram 2 · O(log N) Convergence — the doubling visualised

9 nodes, 3 rounds, 100% convergence — watch the count grow

gossip-convergence-math.svg
Animation · Gossip Spreading Through 9 Nodes — round by round

Watch N1's state propagate to all 9 nodes in exactly 3 rounds

gossip-spread-animation
Ready
Failure Detection — SWIM Protocol

How gossip detects dead nodes without a heartbeat master

The naive approach to failure detection: every node sends a heartbeat to every other node every second. At 1000 nodes that is 1,000,000 messages/second cluster-wide. Gossip-based failure detection (the SWIM protocol — Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Membership) scales to O(N) total messages per second instead of O(N²).

swim-failure-detection.svg
Trade-offs — gossip vs alternatives

Why gossip is used and where it falls short

gossip-tradeoffs.svg
The key limitation: gossip is eventually consistent

During the O(log N) rounds before convergence, different nodes have different views of the cluster. A request routed to a node that hasn't yet received the update may get stale results. This is usually acceptable for membership and failure detection (a few seconds of stale routing is fine) but not for strong-consistency operations (balance checks, leader election, configuration). For those, use Raft/Paxos instead of gossip.

Code · Python & Java — gossip node with SWIM-style failure detection

Complete gossip implementation: state exchange, anti-entropy, failure detection

PYTHON
import random, time, threading
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Optional
from collections import defaultdict

# ── Node state entry (version-stamped) ─────────────────────
@dataclass
class NodeInfo:
    node_id: str
    address: str
    heartbeat: int = 0        # increments each gossip round
    last_seen: float = 0.0    # wall-clock time of last update
    status: str = "alive"    # alive | suspect | dead

    def is_newer_than(self, other: 'NodeInfo') -> bool:
        return self.heartbeat > other.heartbeat


# ── Gossip Node ────────────────────────────────────────────
class GossipNode:
    SUSPECT_TIMEOUT = 5.0   # seconds without update → suspect
    DEAD_TIMEOUT    = 15.0  # seconds as suspect → dead
    GOSSIP_K        = 3     # peers to gossip per round

    def __init__(self, node_id: str, address: str):
        self.node_id = node_id
        self.address = address
        # Membership table: node_id → NodeInfo
        self.members: Dict[str, NodeInfo] = {
            node_id: NodeInfo(node_id, address, heartbeat=0, last_seen=time.time())
