        }
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    # ── Each gossip round ──────────────────────────────────────
    def gossip_round(self, all_nodes: List['GossipNode']) -> None:
        # 1. Increment own heartbeat
        with self._lock:
            self.members[self.node_id].heartbeat += 1
            self.members[self.node_id].last_seen  = time.time()

        # 2. Pick k random peers (exclude self and suspected-dead)
        alive_peers = [
            n for n in all_nodes
            if n.node_id != self.node_id
            and self.members.get(n.node_id, NodeInfo(n.node_id,'')).status != "dead"
        ]
        targets = random.sample(alive_peers, min(self.GOSSIP_K, len(alive_peers)))

        # 3. Send SYN (our full member table) to each target
        for target in targets:
            self._send_syn(target)

        # 4. Run failure detection on local membership table
        self._detect_failures()

    def _send_syn(self, target: 'GossipNode') -> None:
        # In real systems this is an RPC. Here: direct call.
        with self._lock:
            digest = dict(self.members)   # send full table (or digest for large clusters)
        target._receive_syn(digest)

    def _receive_syn(self, remote_members: Dict[str, NodeInfo]) -> None:
        """Merge remote state into local table — keep higher heartbeat version."""
        with self._lock:
            for node_id, remote_info in remote_members.items():
                local = self.members.get(node_id)
                if local is None or remote_info.is_newer_than(local):
                    # Remote has newer info — adopt it
                    self.members[node_id] = NodeInfo(
                        node_id      = remote_info.node_id,
                        address      = remote_info.address,
                        heartbeat    = remote_info.heartbeat,
                        last_seen    = time.time(),
                        status       = remote_info.status
                    )

    def _detect_failures(self) -> None:
        now = time.time()
        with self._lock:
            for nid, info in self.members.items():
                if nid == self.node_id: continue
                age = now - info.last_seen
                if   age > self.DEAD_TIMEOUT:
                    info.status = "dead"
                elif age > self.SUSPECT_TIMEOUT:
                    info.status = "suspect"

    def membership_table(self) -> str:
        rows = []
        with self._lock:
            for nid, info in sorted(self.members.items()):
                rows.append(f"  {nid}: hb={info.heartbeat:3d} status={info.status}")
        return "\n".join(rows)


# ── Simulation ─────────────────────────────────────────────
nodes = [GossipNode(f"N{i}", f"10.0.0.{i}:8000") for i in range(1, 10)]

# Bootstrap: share initial membership
for n in nodes:
    for peer in nodes:
        n.members.setdefault(peer.node_id,
            NodeInfo(peer.node_id, peer.address, heartbeat=0, last_seen=time.time()))

# Simulate 4 gossip rounds
for rnd in range(4):
    print(f"\n=== Round {rnd} ===")
    for node in nodes:
        node.gossip_round(nodes)

# Show final N1 membership table
print("\nN1 membership table after 4 rounds:")
print(nodes[0].membership_table())
# All 9 nodes visible, heartbeats converged

# Simulate N5 dying
print("\n--- N5 dies (stops gossiping) ---")
alive_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n.node_id != "N5"]
for n in alive_nodes:
    n.members["N5"].last_seen -= 20   # simulate stale heartbeat
for n in alive_nodes:
    n._detect_failures()
print(f"N1 sees N5 as: {nodes[0].members['N5'].status}")
# → N1 sees N5 as: dead
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;

// ── Node state entry ───────────────────────────────────────
record NodeInfo(String nodeId, String address,
                  int heartbeat, long lastSeenMs, String status) {
    boolean isNewerThan(NodeInfo other) {
        return this.heartbeat > other.heartbeat;
    }
    NodeInfo withStatus(String s) {
        return new NodeInfo(nodeId, address, heartbeat, lastSeenMs, s);
    }
    NodeInfo withHeartbeat(int hb, long ts) {
        return new NodeInfo(nodeId, address, hb, ts, status);
    }
}

// ── Gossip Node ────────────────────────────────────────────
class GossipNode {
    private static final int  GOSSIP_K         = 3;
    private static final long SUSPECT_TIMEOUT  = 5_000L;
    private static final long DEAD_TIMEOUT     = 15_000L;

    final String nodeId;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, NodeInfo> members
        = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final Random rng = new Random();

    public GossipNode(String nodeId, String address) {
        this.nodeId = nodeId;
        members.put(nodeId,
            new NodeInfo(nodeId, address, 0, System.currentTimeMillis(), "alive"));
    }

    // ── One gossip round ──────────────────────────────────────
    public void gossipRound(List<GossipNode> allNodes) {
        // 1. Bump own heartbeat
        NodeInfo self = members.get(nodeId);
        members.put(nodeId, self.withHeartbeat(
            self.heartbeat() + 1, System.currentTimeMillis()));

        // 2. Pick k random live peers
        List<GossipNode> peers = allNodes.stream()
            .filter(n -> !n.nodeId.equals(nodeId))
            .filter(n -> {
                NodeInfo ni = members.get(n.nodeId);
                return ni == null || !"dead".equals(ni.status());
            })
            .toList();
        Collections.shuffle(peers, rng);
        peers.subList(0, Math.min(GOSSIP_K, peers.size()))
             .forEach(this::sendSyn);

        detectFailures();
    }

    private void sendSyn(GossipNode target) {
        target.receiveSyn(new HashMap<>(members));
    }

    public void receiveSyn(Map<String, NodeInfo> remote) {
        long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
        remote.forEach((id, remoteInfo) ->
            members.merge(id, remoteInfo, (local, r) ->
                r.isNewerThan(local)
                    ? new NodeInfo(r.nodeId(), r.address(),
                                    r.heartbeat(), now, r.status())
                    : local));
    }

    private void detectFailures() {
        long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
        members.forEach((id, info) -> {
            if (id.equals(nodeId)) return;
            long age = now - info.lastSeenMs();
            if      (age > DEAD_TIMEOUT)    members.put(id, info.withStatus("dead"));
            else if (age > SUSPECT_TIMEOUT) members.put(id, info.withStatus("suspect"));
        });
    }

    public void printTable() {
        members.entrySet().stream()
            .sorted(Map.Entry.comparingByKey())
            .forEach(e -> {
                NodeInfo i = e.getValue();
                System.out.printf("  %s: hb=%3d status=%s%n",
                    i.nodeId(), i.heartbeat(), i.status());
            });
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        List<GossipNode> nodes = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 1; i <= 9; i++)
            nodes.add(new GossipNode("N"+i, "10.0.0."+i));

        // Bootstrap: seed all nodes into each other's membership tables
        long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
        nodes.forEach(n -> nodes.forEach(peer ->
            n.members.putIfAbsent(peer.nodeId,
                new NodeInfo(peer.nodeId, peer.nodeId, 0, now, "alive"))));

        // 4 gossip rounds
        for (int r = 0; r < 4; r++) {
            System.out.println("\n=== Round " + r + " ===");
            nodes.forEach(n -> n.gossipRound(nodes));
        }
        System.out.println("\nN1 membership table:");
        nodes.get(0).printTable();

        // Simulate N5 failure (make its last_seen stale)
        System.out.println("\n--- N5 dies ---");
        nodes.stream().filter(n -> !n.nodeId.equals("N5")).forEach(n -> {
            NodeInfo ni = n.members.get("N5");
            if (ni != null)
                n.members.put("N5", new NodeInfo(
                    ni.nodeId(), ni.address(), ni.heartbeat(),
                    now - 20_000L, ni.status()));
            n.detectFailures();
        });
        System.out.println("N1 sees N5: " + nodes.get(0).members.get("N5").status());
        // → N1 sees N5: dead
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Gossip Protocol

QWhy does gossip converge in O(log N) rounds? Derive it intuitively.
A
Start with 1 infected node. Each round, every infected node gossips to k random peers. If no re-infections occurred, the count would double: 1 → k+1 → (k+1)² → ... but re-infections do occur and slow it slightly. Formally: after round t, the expected fraction of infected nodes is 1 - (1 - 1/N)^(k·t), which approaches 1 when k·t ≫ ln(N). For k=2, we need t ≈ ln(N)/2 rounds = O(log N). For N=1024: about 5–10 rounds at 1 second per round = ~10 seconds. The key insight: each round roughly doubles the infected count, and doubling N times starting from 1 takes log₂(N) doublings. This is the same intuition as binary search in reverse.
QHow does Cassandra use gossip? What exactly is exchanged?
A
Cassandra's gossip runs every second. Each node maintains an endpoint state table mapping every node to a map of ApplicationState keys (STATUS, LOAD, SCHEMA, DC, RACK, TOKENS, etc.) each with a version number. Each gossip round: (1) node picks up to 3 random live peers, (2) sends them a SYN containing a digest (node → max version seen), (3) receiver sends back an ACK with the delta (states newer than the digest), (4) initiator sends ACK2 if it needs anything the receiver has. This three-way exchange (SYN/ACK/ACK2) ensures both sides converge. Cassandra also gossips to one seed node per round to help new nodes bootstrap membership.
QWhat is the SWIM protocol and how does it improve on naive heartbeating?
A
SWIM (Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Membership) has two components. (1) Failure detection: instead of each node heartbeating all others (O(N²)), each node picks one random target per round and pings it. If no ACK: ask k random third-party nodes to ping the target on your behalf (indirect probing). If none ACK: mark as suspect, then dead after timeout. This gives O(N) total messages/round. (2) Dissemination: failure/join/leave events are piggybacked onto gossip messages — no separate broadcast. The key benefit over naive heartbeating: constant bandwidth per node regardless of cluster size. HashiCorp's Memberlist (used in Consul, Serf) is a production SWIM implementation.
QWhat is anti-entropy in gossip and how does it differ from the main gossip loop?
A
The main gossip loop propagates new state changes (delta gossip — only sends what's new). Anti-entropy is a background reconciliation process that periodically exchanges full state between pairs of nodes to catch any missed updates — for example, if a node was partitioned for a while and missed several rounds of gossip. Cassandra runs anti-entropy via Merkle tree-based read repair and periodic repair operations. The distinction matters for correctness: the main gossip loop is probabilistic and can miss updates in adverse network conditions; anti-entropy provides a backstop guarantee that eventually all nodes converge to the same state. Without anti-entropy, a node that misses K consecutive gossip rounds may have a permanent gap.
QYou have a 5000-node cluster. An operator updates a configuration key. How long until all nodes have it, and what bandwidth does it cost?
A
Convergence time: log₂(5000) ≈ 12.3 rounds. At 1 round/second that's ~13 seconds. Bandwidth: each node sends k=3 gossip messages per round, each message carries the update payload (say 1KB). Total messages per round = 3×5000 = 15,000. Total bandwidth = 15,000 × 1KB = ~15MB/round cluster-wide. Per-node bandwidth = 3KB/second outbound — negligible. Compare to centralised broadcast: the master would send 5000 messages of 1KB = 5MB in one burst from a single node. That 5MB burst is fine at 5000 nodes, but at 50,000 nodes it becomes a bottleneck (50MB burst). Gossip's O(N log N) total messages scale gracefully; broadcast's O(N) from a single sender does not.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you implement cluster membership for a new distributed database?", say: "I'd use a gossip-based protocol similar to Cassandra's. Each node maintains a membership table with version-stamped entries. Every second, each node gossips its full table (or a digest + delta) to k=3 random peers, merging by taking the higher version on each entry. For failure detection I'd use SWIM: direct ping + indirect probe via k helpers before marking a node as suspect, then dead after a timeout. This gives O(N log N) total messages per round and O(log N) convergence — for a 1000-node cluster, convergence in ~10 seconds with negligible bandwidth overhead per node. For bootstrapping, new nodes contact one or more seed nodes which provide an initial membership view. For strong consistency operations like schema changes, I'd use a separate Raft group rather than relying on the eventually-consistent gossip layer."

Post 06

Write-Ahead Log (WAL)

25 min deep dive Durability · Crash Recovery · ARIES · LSN · Checkpoints · fsync · Replication
The Problem

How does a database guarantee your committed write survives a crash?

You run UPDATE accounts SET balance = 1000 WHERE id = 42 and the database returns "OK". Your process immediately continues. One millisecond later the server loses power. When it reboots, is your write there or not?

If the database writes directly to its heap files (data pages on disk), the answer is "maybe". Page writes are not atomic — a crash mid-write leaves a torn page. Even if the write completed, the OS buffers writes in its page cache; without an explicit fsync, data may sit in memory for seconds before hitting disk.

The Core Guarantee the WAL Provides

A Write-Ahead Log makes one simple but powerful promise: before any data page is modified, the intent is first written and fsynced to the log. On crash recovery, the database replays the log to reconstruct a consistent state. This gives you the "D" in ACID — Durability — with minimal performance cost: sequential log writes are far faster than random data page writes.

The WAL is not just about crash recovery — it is also the foundation of streaming replication (PostgreSQL ships WAL records to standbys), CDC (Debezium reads Postgres WAL), point-in-time recovery, and logical replication. Understanding the WAL unlocks understanding of every database's internals.

Building Intuition — the accounting ledger analogy

Think of it like a bank's paper ledger before updating the balance sheet

The Ledger Analogy

A bank teller never erases and rewrites the balance sheet directly. Instead, they first write every transaction in a sequential ledger book: "10:04am — Deposit $200 to account #42". Only after the ledger is committed do they update the actual balance sheet. If the office burns down mid-update, the balance sheet is destroyed — but auditors can reconstruct the exact state by replaying the ledger from the last known good snapshot.

The WAL is the ledger. Data pages are the balance sheet. The checkpoint is the periodic "snapshot of balances as of today". Recovery is "replay everything in the ledger since the last snapshot".

The "write-ahead" rule is strict: you must write the log record to durable storage before you write the data page. This ordering guarantee is what makes recovery possible. If you reversed it — write the page, then the log — a crash after the page write but before the log write would leave an unlogged change that the database doesn't know how to undo.

Diagram 1 · WAL Record Structure — anatomy of a log entry

Every log record has these fields — know them for interviews

wal-record-structure.svg
Diagram 2 · The Write Path — WAL first, then buffer pool

The exact sequence of events for every committed write

wal-write-path.svg
Animation · WAL Write → Crash → ARIES Recovery (3 phases)

Step-by-step: write txn-A and txn-B, crash, then recover via Analysis→Redo→Undo

wal-crash-recovery-animation
Ready
Checkpoints — bounding recovery time

Without checkpoints you'd replay the entire WAL from day one on every restart

The WAL grows forever. On crash recovery, ARIES needs a starting point for the REDO phase. Without checkpoints, it would scan the entire WAL from the beginning — potentially terabytes. Checkpoints solve this: periodically the database flushes all dirty buffer pool pages to disk, writes a checkpoint record to the WAL, and records the LSN. On recovery, ARIES only needs to scan backward to the last checkpoint LSN to find dirty pages, then forward from there for REDO.

PostgreSQL's checkpoint_completion_target

PostgreSQL's checkpoint writes dirty pages spread over checkpoint_completion_target × checkpoint_timeout (default: 0.9 × 5min = 4.5 minutes) to avoid I/O spikes. The max_wal_size setting controls how much WAL can accumulate before forcing a checkpoint — larger values mean faster normal operation but longer recovery after a crash. Default is 1GB. Production databases set this to 4–16GB depending on acceptable recovery window.

WAL Beyond Recovery — streaming replication and CDC

The WAL is the single source of truth for everything that happened

wal-uses.svg
Trade-offs — WAL tuning decisions that affect performance

The three knobs engineers get wrong in production

synchronous_commit = off (PostgreSQL) — dangerous speedup

Setting synchronous_commit = off makes PostgreSQL return "committed" to the client before the WAL fsync completes. This eliminates the fsync latency (~1-5ms) giving a massive throughput boost — but means up to wal_writer_delay (default 200ms) of committed transactions can be lost on crash. Not data corruption — the database remains consistent — but recent commits disappear. Appropriate for: analytics inserts, logging, non-critical data. Never for: financial transactions, user registrations, anything where data loss is unacceptable.

fsync = on vs WAL on SSD

Each WAL fsync is a ~1-5ms operation on spinning disk, or ~50-200μs on NVMe SSD. A PostgreSQL write bottleneck is often purely fsync throughput. Solutions: (1) move WAL to a dedicated NVMe drive, (2) group commits — batch multiple transactions' fsync into one (PostgreSQL does this automatically), (3) use a storage system that provides durable write acknowledgement without full fsync (like AWS io2 with provisioned IOPS).

wal_level controls what goes into the WAL

wal_level = minimal: crash recovery only, smallest WAL size. wal_level = replica: adds info needed for streaming replication. wal_level = logical: adds row-level change data needed for CDC (Debezium) and logical replication. Each level adds size overhead. Logical is 20-40% larger than replica. Choose based on what you need: just HA → replica. CDC/multi-master → logical.

Code · Python & Java — WAL implementation and ARIES recovery

Full WAL with append, checkpoint, and crash recovery simulation

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Optional
from enum import Enum, auto

class RecordType(Enum):
    BEGIN    = auto()
    UPDATE   = auto()
    COMMIT   = auto()
    ABORT    = auto()
    CLR      = auto()       # Compensation Log Record (written during UNDO)
    CHECKPOINT = auto()

@dataclass
class WALRecord:
    lsn:       int
    prev_lsn:  Optional[int]    # previous LSN for this transaction
    txn_id:    str
    rec_type:  RecordType
    page_id:   Optional[str]  = None
    before:    Optional[int]  = None  # for UNDO
    after:     Optional[int]  = None  # for REDO


# ── Write-Ahead Log ────────────────────────────────────────
class WAL:
    def __init__(self):
        self.records: List[WALRecord] = []
        self._lsn_counter = 0
        self._txn_last_lsn: Dict[str, int] = {}   # txn_id → last LSN

    def append(self, txn_id: str, rec_type: RecordType,
               page_id=None, before=None, after=None) -> int:
        self._lsn_counter += 1
        lsn = self._lsn_counter
        prev = self._txn_last_lsn.get(txn_id)
        record = WALRecord(lsn=lsn, prev_lsn=prev, txn_id=txn_id,
                           rec_type=rec_type, page_id=page_id,
                           before=before, after=after)
        self.records.append(record)
        self._txn_last_lsn[txn_id] = lsn
        # In real systems: fsync here before returning to caller
        print(f"  WAL LSN:{lsn:3d} [{txn_id}] {rec_type.name:10s}"
              + (f" page={page_id} {before}→{after}" if page_id else ""))
        return lsn


# ── Buffer Pool (simulated) ────────────────────────────────
class BufferPool:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pages: Dict[str, int] = {}  # page_id → value

    def write(self, page_id: str, value: int):
        self.pages[page_id] = value      # dirty — not yet on disk

    def read(self, page_id: str) -> Optional[int]:
        return self.pages.get(page_id)


# ── ARIES Recovery ─────────────────────────────────────────
class ARIESRecovery:
    def recover(self, wal: WAL, buffer: BufferPool) -> None:
        print("\n=== ARIES RECOVERY ===")

        # Phase 1: ANALYSIS — identify winners and losers
        print("\n--- Phase 1: ANALYSIS ---")
        committed: set = set()
        started:   set = set()
        for r in wal.records:
            if   r.rec_type == RecordType.BEGIN:  started.add(r.txn_id)
            elif r.rec_type == RecordType.COMMIT: committed.add(r.txn_id)
        losers = started - committed
        print(f"  Winners (committed): {committed}")
        print(f"  Losers  (no commit): {losers}")

        # Phase 2: REDO — replay all records forward to reach crash state
        print("\n--- Phase 2: REDO ---")
        for r in wal.records:
            if r.rec_type == RecordType.UPDATE and r.page_id:
                buffer.write(r.page_id, r.after)
                print(f"  Redo LSN:{r.lsn} [{r.txn_id}] {r.page_id}={r.after}")

        # Phase 3: UNDO — roll back all loser transactions
        print("\n--- Phase 3: UNDO ---")
        for r in reversed(wal.records):
            if r.txn_id in losers and r.rec_type == RecordType.UPDATE:
                buffer.write(r.page_id, r.before)   # restore before image
                # Write CLR to WAL so we don't undo again if we crash mid-undo
                wal.append(r.txn_id, RecordType.CLR,
                           page_id=r.page_id, after=r.before)
                print(f"  Undo LSN:{r.lsn} [{r.txn_id}] {r.page_id}={r.before}")

        print("\nFinal buffer state:")
        for p, v in sorted(buffer.pages.items()):
            print(f"  {p} = {v}")


# ── Simulation ─────────────────────────────────────────────
wal = WAL()
buf = BufferPool()
buf.pages = {'x': 800, 'y': 3}   # initial state on disk

print("=== Normal Operation ===")
# txn-A: commits successfully
wal.append('txn-A', RecordType.BEGIN)
wal.append('txn-A', RecordType.UPDATE, page_id='x', before=800, after=1000)
buf.write('x', 1000)
wal.append('txn-A', RecordType.COMMIT)

# txn-B: started, modified y, but CRASH before COMMIT
wal.append('txn-B', RecordType.BEGIN)
wal.append('txn-B', RecordType.UPDATE, page_id='y', before=3, after=7)
buf.write('y', 7)
print("  ⚡ CRASH! txn-B never committed")

# Simulate restart — buffer pool is lost (RAM volatile)
buf.pages.clear()
buf.pages = {'x': 800, 'y': 3}  # reloaded from disk (pre-crash checkpointed state)

ARIESRecovery().recover(wal, buf)
# Expected: x=1000 (txn-A committed), y=3 (txn-B rolled back)
JAVA
import java.util.*;

enum RecordType { BEGIN, UPDATE, COMMIT, ABORT, CLR, CHECKPOINT }

record WALRecord(int lsn, int prevLsn, String txnId, RecordType type,
                   String pageId, Integer before, Integer after) {}

// ── WAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class WAL {
    final List<WALRecord> records = new ArrayList<>();
    private int lsnCounter = 0;
    private final Map<String, Integer> txnLastLsn = new HashMap<>();

    public int append(String txnId, RecordType type,
                        String pageId, Integer before, Integer after) {
        int lsn  = ++lsnCounter;
        int prev = txnLastLsn.getOrDefault(txnId, 0);
        records.add(new WALRecord(lsn, prev, txnId, type, pageId, before, after));
        txnLastLsn.put(txnId, lsn);
        // In production: fsync here before returning
        String extra = pageId != null ? " page="+pageId+" "+before+"→"+after : "";
        System.out.printf("  WAL LSN:%3d [%s] %-10s%s%n", lsn, txnId, type, extra);
        return lsn;
    }
    public int append(String txnId, RecordType type) {
        return append(txnId, type, null, null, null);
    }
}

// ── Buffer Pool ────────────────────────────────────────────
class BufferPool {
    final Map<String, Integer> pages = new HashMap<>();
    public void write(String pageId, int val) { pages.put(pageId, val); }
    public Integer read(String pageId) { return pages.get(pageId); }
}

// ── ARIES Recovery ─────────────────────────────────────────
class ARIESRecovery {
    public void recover(WAL wal, BufferPool buf) {
        System.out.println("\n=== ARIES RECOVERY ===");

        // Phase 1: Analysis
        System.out.println("\n--- Phase 1: ANALYSIS ---");
        Set<String> committed = new HashSet<>(), started = new HashSet<>();
        for (WALRecord r : wal.records) {
            if      (r.type() == RecordType.BEGIN)  started.add(r.txnId());
            else if (r.type() == RecordType.COMMIT) committed.add(r.txnId());
        }
        Set<String> losers = new HashSet<>(started);
        losers.removeAll(committed);
        System.out.println("  Winners: " + committed);
        System.out.println("  Losers:  " + losers);

        // Phase 2: Redo — replay all forward
        System.out.println("\n--- Phase 2: REDO ---");
        for (WALRecord r : wal.records) {
            if (r.type() == RecordType.UPDATE && r.pageId() != null) {
                buf.write(r.pageId(), r.after());
                System.out.printf("  Redo LSN:%d [%s] %s=%d%n",
                    r.lsn(), r.txnId(), r.pageId(), r.after());
            }
        }

        // Phase 3: Undo — roll back losers in reverse
        System.out.println("\n--- Phase 3: UNDO ---");
        List<WALRecord> reversed = new ArrayList<>(wal.records);
        Collections.reverse(reversed);
        for (WALRecord r : reversed) {
            if (losers.contains(r.txnId()) && r.type() == RecordType.UPDATE) {
                buf.write(r.pageId(), r.before());
                wal.append(r.txnId(), RecordType.CLR,   // write CLR
                    r.pageId(), r.after(), r.before());
                System.out.printf("  Undo [%s] %s=%d%n",
                    r.txnId(), r.pageId(), r.before());
            }
        }

        System.out.println("\nFinal state:");
        buf.pages.entrySet().stream()
            .sorted(Map.Entry.comparingByKey())
            .forEach(e -> System.out.printf("  %s = %d%n", e.getKey(), e.getValue()));
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        WAL wal = new WAL(); BufferPool buf = new BufferPool();
        buf.pages.put("x", 800); buf.pages.put("y", 3);
        System.out.println("=== Normal Operation ===");
        wal.append("txn-A", RecordType.BEGIN);
        wal.append("txn-A", RecordType.UPDATE, "x", 800, 1000);
        buf.write("x", 1000);
        wal.append("txn-A", RecordType.COMMIT);
        wal.append("txn-B", RecordType.BEGIN);
        wal.append("txn-B", RecordType.UPDATE, "y", 3, 7);
        buf.write("y", 7);
        System.out.println("  ⚡ CRASH — txn-B never committed");
        buf.pages.put("x", 800); buf.pages.put("y", 3); // reload from disk snapshot
        new ARIESRecovery().recover(wal, buf);
        // → x=1000, y=3
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about the Write-Ahead Log

QWhy must the WAL record be fsynced before the data page? What breaks if you reverse the order?
A
The WAL-first rule exists to guarantee recoverability. If you write the data page first and then crash before writing the WAL record, you have a change on disk with no record of what the before-image was. You cannot undo it — the database is permanently inconsistent. If you write the WAL record first and crash before writing the data page, that's fine: recovery reads the WAL record, sees the after-image, and replays it (REDO phase). The WAL record contains everything needed to reconstruct the change. The data page is redundant to the WAL — the WAL is the authoritative record, the data page is an optimisation (to avoid replaying the entire WAL on every read).
QWalk me through ARIES recovery phases for this scenario: txn-A committed, txn-B was active when crash occurred.
A
Phase 1 — Analysis: scan WAL forward from last checkpoint. Build a table of all active transactions at crash time. txn-A has a COMMIT record → winner. txn-B has BEGIN and UPDATE but no COMMIT → loser. Also build the dirty page table (pages modified after last checkpoint). Phase 2 — REDO: replay ALL WAL records forward (including losers) to restore the exact buffer pool state at the time of crash. This re-applies txn-A's changes (x=1000) and txn-B's changes (y=7) to buffer pool. Phase 3 — UNDO: iterate losers backward through the WAL using the prevLSN chain. For each UPDATE by a loser, apply the before-image to buffer pool and write a CLR (Compensation Log Record) to WAL. txn-B's y=7 is undone back to y=3. Write CLRs so we don't re-undo if we crash again mid-recovery. Result: x=1000 (txn-A's committed change persists), y=3 (txn-B rolled back).
QWhat is a CLR (Compensation Log Record) and why is it needed?
A
A CLR is written to the WAL during the UNDO phase of recovery for each operation that is being undone. It records the undo action itself, making the undo operation idempotent. If the database crashes again during recovery (mid-UNDO), and recovery restarts, it will re-run REDO (which will replay the CLR, re-applying the undo), then UNDO again — but this time it sees the CLR and knows not to undo that operation again. Without CLRs, crashing mid-undo could result in an operation being undone twice, which would corrupt data. CLRs are "never undone" records — UNDO skips them, only REDO applies them.
QHow does PostgreSQL use the WAL for streaming replication?
A
PostgreSQL's WAL is a continuous stream of 8KB WAL segments. A WAL sender process on the primary reads WAL records as they are written and streams them to WAL receiver processes on standbys over a persistent TCP connection. The standby's WAL receiver writes incoming records to its local WAL files and passes them to a startup process that replays them against the standby's data files — identically to crash recovery REDO. The standby continuously tracks its replay LSN and reports it to the primary. The primary uses this to calculate replication lag (primary LSN − standby replay LSN). With synchronous_commit = remote_write or remote_apply, the primary waits for the standby to acknowledge receipt/replay before returning OK to the client — synchronous replication at the cost of cross-network latency per write.
QA developer wants to speed up batch inserts — they set synchronous_commit = off. Explain the exact risk.
A
With synchronous_commit = off, PostgreSQL returns OK to the client before the WAL records are fsynced to disk. The WAL writer still flushes periodically (every wal_writer_delay, default 200ms). If the server crashes within that 200ms window, the committed transactions that were in the WAL buffer but not yet fsynced are lost — even though the client received OK. Crucially: the database never becomes inconsistent — it never exposes partial writes. The only risk is losing the most recent 0–200ms of committed data. This is analogous to TCP window acknowledgement — you might lose the in-flight data if the connection drops. Acceptable for: log ingestion, analytics, temporary data. Unacceptable for: financial records, user signups, anything with an audit trail requirement.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "walk me through what happens when PostgreSQL crashes and restarts", say: "On startup, PostgreSQL checks whether the previous shutdown was clean by looking at the pg_control file. If not, it enters crash recovery. It finds the last checkpoint record in the WAL to determine the REDO start point. Phase 1 Analysis: scan forward from checkpoint, building a dirty page table and transaction table — identifying committed and active transactions. Phase 2 Redo: replay all WAL records forward from the REDO start LSN, re-applying every change to bring the buffer pool to the exact state at crash time — including uncommitted changes. Phase 3 Undo: for each active (uncommitted) transaction, traverse its WAL records backward via prevLSN chain, applying before-images and writing CLRs. When all undo is complete, write a new checkpoint, and the database opens for connections. For typical OLTP workloads this takes 1–30 seconds; for large WAL accumulation (checkpoint_completion_target too high) it could take minutes, which is why tuning max_wal_size and checkpoint_timeout matters."

Post 07

Vector Clocks

25 min deep dive Causality · Happens-Before · Conflict Detection · Lamport Clocks · Version Vectors · DynamoDB
The Problem

How do you know which of two writes happened first in a distributed system?

In a distributed system, there is no global clock. Two nodes can both write to the same key at the same time — but "same time" is meaningless when each node has its own local clock (which may drift by milliseconds or seconds). Even if you add NTP synchronisation, clock skew of a few hundred microseconds is enough to make "which write came first?" ambiguous.

Consider: Node A writes shopping_cart = ["shoes"] at 14:00:00.001. Node B writes shopping_cart = ["jacket"] at 14:00:00.000. Node B's timestamp is earlier — but what if Node B's clock is 10ms behind? In reality, A wrote first. You cannot trust wall-clock timestamps to establish ordering in distributed systems.

The Core Problem: Causality Without Clocks

What you actually need is not "which happened earlier in wall-clock time" but "which event could have influenced the other". This is the happens-before (→) relation, introduced by Leslie Lamport in 1978. If event A happened-before event B (A → B), then A could causally influence B. If neither A → B nor B → A, they are concurrent — neither could have influenced the other, and we have a genuine conflict to resolve. Vector clocks track this precisely.

Building Intuition — Lamport clocks first, then why they're not enough

Step 1: Lamport clocks. Step 2: Why they fail. Step 3: Vector clocks fix it.

Lamport Clocks — the foundation

Each node maintains a single integer counter. Rules: (1) On any local event: increment by 1. (2) On sending a message: attach the current counter. (3) On receiving a message: clock = max(local, received) + 1.

Lamport's guarantee: If A → B (A happened-before B), then timestamp(A) < timestamp(B). But the reverse is NOT true — if timestamp(A) < timestamp(B), you cannot conclude A → B. Two concurrent events may have any ordering of Lamport timestamps.

The failure case: Node A sends a message at Lamport time 5. Node B, independently, does a local write at Lamport time 8. Is B → A? No — they're concurrent. But B's timestamp is higher. Lamport clocks cannot tell you "these events are concurrent".

Vector Clocks — one counter per node

Instead of one integer, each node maintains a vector of integers — one per node in the system. [A:2, B:1, C:0] means "I know about 2 events from A, 1 from B, 0 from C". Rules: (1) On local event: increment own slot. (2) On send: attach full vector. (3) On receive: vector[i] = max(local[i], received[i]) for all i, then increment own slot.

Comparison: Vector clock V1 < V2 (V1 happened-before V2) iff every component of V1 ≤ corresponding component of V2, and at least one is strictly less. If neither V1 < V2 nor V2 < V1 — they're concurrent. This is the key capability Lamport clocks lack.

Diagram 1 · Happens-Before vs Concurrent — the two outcomes

Visualising what vector clocks actually detect

happens-before-vs-concurrent.svg
Diagram 2 · Three-Node Vector Clock — complete message flow

Events, messages, merges — the full picture on a timeline

vector-clock-full-flow.svg
Animation · Vector Clock Merge — step-by-step with speed control

Watch the merge rule in action: max(local, received) + increment own

vc-animation
Ready
Version Vectors — vector clocks in key-value stores

How DynamoDB and Riak use vector clocks to detect conflicts

Vector clocks track causality per-event. Version vectors are a closely related concept applied per-key: each replica maintains a version vector for a key's value. When a client writes a key, it increments the vector slot for the replica it wrote to. When replicas sync, they compare version vectors to decide whether one value supersedes the other or whether they conflict.

DynamoDB's approach — siblings and last-write-wins

DynamoDB tracks context (a compact version vector) with each item. On write: the client includes the context of the value it read. If the coordinator sees the write context ≥ the stored context, it's a causal successor — safe to overwrite. If the write was concurrent (the client never read the latest value), it's a conflict. DynamoDB defaults to last-write-wins (by timestamp) for conflicts — simple but can silently discard a concurrent write. For shopping carts and other merge-friendly data types, you'd use a CRDT instead.

The "sibling explosion" problem in Riak

Riak stores ALL conflicting values as "siblings" and returns them all to the client, which must merge them. This is correct but operationally painful: with high write concurrency and no explicit merging, siblings accumulate. A key can end up with dozens of sibling values. Production Riak deployments require explicit conflict resolution logic or CRDTs (which merge automatically). Vector clock sizes also grow over time as clients come and go — Riak periodically prunes old entries.

Trade-offs — vector clocks vs alternatives

Choosing the right causality mechanism for your system

vc-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — complete vector clock implementation

VectorClock class with tick, merge, send, receive, and conflict detection

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Tuple
from enum import Enum, auto
from copy import deepcopy

class Relation(Enum):
    BEFORE     = auto()   # self → other (self happened before other)
    AFTER      = auto()   # other → self (other happened before self)
    CONCURRENT = auto()   # neither → conflict!
    EQUAL      = auto()   # identical vectors

class VectorClock:
    def __init__(self, node_id: str, nodes: list[str]):
        self.node_id = node_id
        # initialise all slots to 0
        self.clock: Dict[str, int] = {n: 0 for n in nodes}

    def tick(self) -> Dict[str, int]:
        """Increment own slot — local event or before sending."""
        self.clock[self.node_id] += 1
        return self.snapshot()

    def merge(self, received: Dict[str, int]) -> None:
        """On receive: merge remote clock then tick own slot."""
        for node, ts in received.items():
            self.clock[node] = max(self.clock.get(node, 0), ts)
        self.tick()   # count the receive event itself

    def snapshot(self) -> Dict[str, int]:
        return deepcopy(self.clock)

    def compare(self, v1: Dict[str, int], v2: Dict[str, int]) -> Relation:
        """Compare two snapshots. Returns their causal relationship."""
        all_nodes = set(v1.keys()) | set(v2.keys())
        v1_leq = all(v1.get(n, 0) <= v2.get(n, 0) for n in all_nodes)
        v2_leq = all(v2.get(n, 0) <= v1.get(n, 0) for n in all_nodes)
        if v1_leq and v2_leq: return Relation.EQUAL
        if v1_leq:             return Relation.BEFORE    # v1 → v2
        if v2_leq:             return Relation.AFTER     # v2 → v1
        return Relation.CONCURRENT                        # conflict!


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
nodes = ['A', 'B', 'C']
vc_a = VectorClock('A', nodes)
vc_b = VectorClock('B', nodes)
vc_c = VectorClock('C', nodes)

# a1: A local event
snap_a1 = vc_a.tick()
print(f"a1: A={snap_a1}")       # {'A':1,'B':0,'C':0}

# b1: B local event (concurrent with a1)
snap_b1 = vc_b.tick()
print(f"b1: B={snap_b1}")       # {'A':0,'B':1,'C':0}

# A sends to B (b2: B receives a1 and merges)
vc_b.merge(snap_a1)           # max({A:1,B:0},{A:0,B:1})={A:1,B:1} → tick B → {A:1,B:2}
snap_b2 = vc_b.snapshot()
print(f"b2: B={snap_b2}")       # {'A':1,'B':2,'C':0}

# C local event (concurrent with everything so far)
snap_c1 = vc_c.tick()
print(f"c1: C={snap_c1}")       # {'A':0,'B':0,'C':1}

# B sends to C (c2: C receives b2 and merges)
vc_c.merge(snap_b2)
snap_c2 = vc_c.snapshot()
print(f"c2: C={snap_c2}")       # {'A':1,'B':2,'C':2}

# Now compare causal relationships
vc = VectorClock('A', nodes)   # just for compare method
print("\nCausal relationships:")
print(f"a1 vs b2: {vc.compare(snap_a1, snap_b2)}")
# → BEFORE (a1 causally precedes b2)
print(f"a1 vs b1: {vc.compare(snap_a1, snap_b1)}")
# → CONCURRENT (no message between them!)
print(f"a1 vs c2: {vc.compare(snap_a1, snap_c2)}")
# → BEFORE (a1 influence reached c2 via b2)
print(f"c1 vs b2: {vc.compare(snap_c1, snap_b2)}")
# → CONCURRENT (c1 and b2 have no causal link)
JAVA
import java.util.*;

enum Relation { BEFORE, AFTER, CONCURRENT, EQUAL }

class VectorClock {
    private final String nodeId;
    private final Map<String, Integer> clock = new LinkedHashMap<>();

    public VectorClock(String nodeId, List<String> nodes) {
        this.nodeId = nodeId;
        nodes.forEach(n -> clock.put(n, 0));
    }

    /** Local event or before send: increment own slot. */
    public Map<String, Integer> tick() {
        clock.merge(nodeId, 1, Integer::sum);
        return snapshot();
    }

    /** On receive: merge remote clock then tick own slot. */
    public void merge(Map<String, Integer> received) {
        received.forEach((node, ts) ->
            clock.merge(node, ts, Math::max));
        tick();
    }

    public Map<String, Integer> snapshot() {
        return new LinkedHashMap<>(clock);
    }

    /** Compare two clock snapshots for causal relationship. */
    public static Relation compare(
            Map<String,Integer> v1, Map<String,Integer> v2) {
        Set<String> keys = new HashSet<>(v1.keySet());
        keys.addAll(v2.keySet());

        boolean v1LeqV2 = keys.stream()
            .allMatch(k -> v1.getOrDefault(k, 0) <= v2.getOrDefault(k, 0));
        boolean v2LeqV1 = keys.stream()
            .allMatch(k -> v2.getOrDefault(k, 0) <= v1.getOrDefault(k, 0));

        if (v1LeqV2 && v2LeqV1) return Relation.EQUAL;
        if (v1LeqV2)            return Relation.BEFORE;
        if (v2LeqV1)            return Relation.AFTER;
        return                           Relation.CONCURRENT;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> nodes = List.of("A", "B", "C");
        VectorClock vcA = new VectorClock("A", nodes);
        VectorClock vcB = new VectorClock("B", nodes);
        VectorClock vcC = new VectorClock("C", nodes);

        // a1: A local event
        var snapA1 = vcA.tick();
        System.out.println("a1: A=" + snapA1);   // {A=1, B=0, C=0}

        // b1: B local event (concurrent with a1)
        var snapB1 = vcB.tick();
        System.out.println("b1: B=" + snapB1);   // {A=0, B=1, C=0}

        // A sends to B: merge a1 into vcB → b2
        vcB.merge(snapA1);
        var snapB2 = vcB.snapshot();
        System.out.println("b2: B=" + snapB2);   // {A=1, B=2, C=0}

        // c1: C independent local event
        var snapC1 = vcC.tick();
        System.out.println("c1: C=" + snapC1);   // {A=0, B=0, C=1}

        // B sends to C: merge b2 into vcC → c2
        vcC.merge(snapB2);
        var snapC2 = vcC.snapshot();
        System.out.println("c2: C=" + snapC2);   // {A=1, B=2, C=2}

        System.out.println("\nCausal relationships:");
        System.out.println("a1 vs b2: " + compare(snapA1, snapB2));
        // → BEFORE
        System.out.println("a1 vs b1: " + compare(snapA1, snapB1));
        // → CONCURRENT
        System.out.println("a1 vs c2: " + compare(snapA1, snapC2));
        // → BEFORE
        System.out.println("c1 vs b2: " + compare(snapC1, snapB2));
        // → CONCURRENT
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Vector Clocks

QWhat is the happens-before relation? How do vector clocks implement it?
A
The happens-before relation (→), defined by Lamport, captures causality: event A happens-before B if (1) A and B are on the same node and A occurred before B in that node's local execution, or (2) A is the send of a message and B is the receipt of that same message, or (3) transitivity: if A → C and C → B, then A → B. Vector clocks implement this by assigning each event a vector timestamp. V1 → V2 iff every component of V1 ≤ the corresponding component of V2, and at least one component is strictly less. This works because when a message is sent with the sender's vector, the receiver's merge ensures all events that causally preceded the send are reflected in the receiver's new clock — precisely capturing the transitive happens-before chains.
QWhy can't you use Lamport timestamps to detect concurrent events?
A
Lamport clocks provide a one-directional implication: A → B guarantees timestamp(A) < timestamp(B). But the converse is false — timestamp(A) < timestamp(B) does NOT imply A → B. Two concurrent events on different nodes can have any ordering of Lamport timestamps depending on how their clocks happened to be at the time. So if you see timestamp(A)=5 and timestamp(B)=8, you cannot conclude A → B; they could be concurrent. Vector clocks solve this by tracking per-node counters: the only way V1 ≤ V2 in all components is if V1's node knew about all events that produced V2's clock values, which is only possible through a causal message chain.
QHow does DynamoDB use vector clocks (or version vectors) to handle concurrent writes?
A
DynamoDB uses a context token (an opaque version vector) returned with every read. When a client writes, it passes back the context token of the value it read. The coordinator compares the write's context with the stored context: if the write's context ≥ stored (dominates or equals), this write causally succeeds the stored value — safe to overwrite. If neither dominates (concurrent write — the client wrote without reading the latest), DynamoDB applies last-write-wins using a server-side timestamp. Note: DynamoDB actually simplified away true vector clocks in later versions (2012 blog post), replacing them with a simple timestamp-based approach for most use cases, because sibling accumulation and vector clock pruning complexity was a maintenance burden.
QWhat is a Hybrid Logical Clock (HLC) and how does it improve on vector clocks?
A
Hybrid Logical Clocks (Kulkarni et al., 2014) combine physical time (wall clock) with a logical counter to get the best of both: (1) HLC tracks causality like a Lamport clock — if A → B, then hlc(A) < hlc(B), (2) HLC stays close to physical time — it's always within a bounded skew of NTP time, (3) HLC fits in a single 64-bit value (unlike O(N) vector clocks). The format: hlc = (physical_ms, logical) where physical_ms is NTP time and logical is a counter reset when physical advances. On send/receive: take max(my_physical, received_physical) and increment logical only if physical didn't advance. CockroachDB and YugabyteDB use HLCs for multi-version concurrency control. The limitation: HLC can still have causally unrelated events with the same timestamp if two independent events occur at the same millisecond.
QA shopping cart is stored across two replicas. A user adds "shoes" from their phone (replica A) while simultaneously adding "jacket" from their laptop (replica B). How do you merge these?
A
This is the classic vector clock conflict scenario. The two writes have concurrent vector clocks (neither dominates the other). You have three options: (1) Last-write-wins (LWW): compare timestamps, keep the latest. Result: you lose one item silently. Bad UX. (2) Siblings + client merge: return both versions to the client with their vector clocks; client must merge them. Riak does this. Result: client gets ["shoes"] and ["jacket"] and must combine them to ["shoes","jacket"]. Correct but requires client logic. (3) CRDT (G-Set or OR-Set): model the cart as a grow-only set or observed-remove set, which merges automatically by union. The OR-Set correctly handles add-then-remove concurrency. This is why Amazon's original DynamoDB paper described using CRDTs for shopping carts — you get automatic, correct merging without surfacing conflicts to the client.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you handle concurrent writes in a multi-master database?", say: "I'd use version vectors per key. Each replica maintains a version vector for every key it stores. On write, the replica increments its own slot and stores the new vector with the value. When replicas sync, I compare version vectors: if one dominates (all components ≥), the dominating one wins. If neither dominates (concurrent writes), I have a true conflict to resolve. For conflict resolution: if the data type supports automatic merging (counters → add, sets → union, last-writer-wins registers), I'd use CRDTs. For arbitrary data types, I'd surface siblings to the application and require explicit merge logic — trading operational complexity for correctness. For global ordering I'd use Hybrid Logical Clocks rather than vector clocks, since they provide O(1) size and stay within bounded physical time while still tracking causality — used in production by CockroachDB and YugabyteDB."

Post 08

Bloom Filters

25 min deep dive Probabilistic Set · False Positives · No False Negatives · Hash Functions · LSM Trees · Cassandra
The Problem

How do you answer "is this key in the database?" without reading the database?

You have an LSM-tree database (like Cassandra, RocksDB, or LevelDB) with millions of keys spread across hundreds of SSTables on disk. A read request arrives for key user:99999. To find it, you'd have to search through potentially dozens of SSTables — each requiring disk I/O. But most of the time the key doesn't exist in most SSTables. You're doing expensive disk reads to confirm absence.

More broadly: "is this element in this set?" is one of the most common operations in computing. If your set has 100 million elements and you want to answer this in microseconds using only a few kilobytes of memory, a hash set won't work (it needs O(N) memory). You need something probabilistic.

The Core Trade-off

A Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that answers "is X in the set?" with two possible answers: "Definitely NOT in the set" (100% certain — no false negatives) or "Probably in the set" (might be a false positive). For use cases where a false positive just means you do an unnecessary disk read (but a false negative would miss data), this trade-off is perfect. You pay a small probability of wasted work in exchange for eliminating most unnecessary disk I/Os.

Building Intuition — from a single hash to multiple hashes

Why one hash function isn't enough — and why k hash functions work

Step 1: Single hash

Start simple: a bit array of size m, all zeros. To insert "cat": compute hash("cat") mod m = 5, set bit[5] = 1. To query "cat": check bit[5] — if 1, probably present. If 0, definitely absent. Problem: any key that happens to hash to 5 will also return "probably present" — a high false positive rate when the array gets even 10% full.

Step 2: k hash functions reduce false positive rate dramatically

Use k different hash functions. To insert "cat": compute h₁("cat")=1, h₂("cat")=5, h₃("cat")=9 → set bits 1, 5, and 9. To query "dog": check bits at h₁("dog"), h₂("dog"), h₃("dog"). Only if ALL k bits are 1 do you say "probably present". If ANY bit is 0 → definitely absent. The probability that all k bits happen to be set by other elements decreases exponentially with k — giving you a much lower false positive rate for the same memory cost.

Diagram 1 · Bloom Filter Anatomy — bit array, k hash functions

A 16-bit array with k=3 hash functions — complete insert and query walkthrough

bloom-filter-anatomy.svg
Animation · Insert "cat", "dog" → Query "fish" (FP) and "bird" (TN)

Watch bits flip, hash arrows appear, false positive exposed — step by step

bloom-filter-animation
Ready
Real-World Use — Bloom filters in LSM-tree databases

How Cassandra and RocksDB use per-SSTable Bloom filters to eliminate disk I/O

Every SSTable in Cassandra, RocksDB, and LevelDB has its own Bloom filter stored in the file header. The filter is loaded into memory when the SSTable opens. On a read request for key K, before doing any disk I/O on an SSTable, the database checks that SSTable's Bloom filter. If it says "definitely not present", skip the SSTable entirely. Only SSTables where the filter says "possibly present" get searched.

The numbers in production

A Cassandra SSTable with 1 million rows uses a Bloom filter of about 1.2MB (10 bits/row × 1M rows / 8 bits per byte). Loaded in memory, this filter lets you skip 99% of unnecessary SSTable searches with a ~1% false positive rate. RocksDB uses per-level Bloom filters with configurable bloom_bits_per_key (default 10). For a key that doesn't exist anywhere, every SSTable's filter must say "possibly present" for you to search it — the probability of this happening across all SSTables drops to essentially zero, making non-existent key lookups nearly instant.

Trade-offs — choosing parameters and knowing the limits

The three design decisions engineers must make

You cannot delete from a standard Bloom filter

Setting a bit to 0 during delete would break other elements that share that bit. Solution: Counting Bloom filter — use a counter per position instead of a single bit. Increment on insert, decrement on delete. This uses ~4× more memory. Alternative: when deletes are common, rebuild the filter periodically from scratch. Cassandra's compaction process rebuilds Bloom filters when it merges SSTables, so deletion (via tombstones) naturally gets cleaned up.

Sizing a Bloom filter: the formula you need

Given a target false positive rate p and expected number of elements n: required bits m = -n × ln(p) / (ln 2)². For p=1%: m ≈ 9.6 × n bits. For p=0.1%: m ≈ 14.4 × n bits. Optimal number of hash functions: k = (m/n) × ln 2 ≈ 0.693 × m/n. At p=1% and optimal k, you need about 7 hash functions. These hash functions are typically derived from two independent hash functions using the formula h_i(x) = h₁(x) + i × h₂(x) — so you only need 2 real hash evaluations, then linear combinations give you k functions for free.

When NOT to use a Bloom filter

Bloom filters are wrong when: (1) You need exact membership (security applications — a false positive could let an attacker in), (2) Your set changes frequently with many deletes (use a cuckoo filter instead — supports deletes and has better cache performance), (3) The set is small enough to fit in a hash set (overhead not worth it below ~10K elements), (4) You need to enumerate members of the set (Bloom filters don't support iteration). The alternative for security-sensitive exact membership: perfect hash tables or explicit sets.

Code · Python & Java — Bloom filter with configurable FP rate

Complete implementation: size from FP rate, k hash functions, bit array

PYTHON
import math
import hashlib
from bitarray import bitarray   # pip install bitarray

class BloomFilter:
    """
    Space-efficient probabilistic set with configurable FP rate.

    Parameters:
      n (int): expected number of elements
      fp_rate (float): desired false positive rate (e.g. 0.01 = 1%)
    """

    def __init__(self, n: int, fp_rate: float = 0.01):
        # Optimal bit array size: m = -n*ln(p) / (ln2)^2
        self.m = math.ceil(-n * math.log(fp_rate) / (math.log(2) ** 2))
        # Optimal number of hash functions: k = (m/n) * ln2
        self.k = math.ceil((self.m / n) * math.log(2))
        self.bits = bitarray(self.m)
        self.bits.setall(0)
        self._n_inserted = 0
        print(f"BloomFilter: m={self.m} bits ({self.m/8/1024:.1f} KB), k={self.k} hash funcs")
        print(f"  Target FP rate={fp_rate:.1%} for n={n} elements")

    def _hashes(self, item: str) -> list[int]:
        """
        Generate k hash positions using double hashing:
          h_i(x) = (h1(x) + i * h2(x)) % m
        Only 2 real hash evaluations — rest are linear combinations.
        """
        data = item.encode()
        h1 = int(hashlib.md5(data).hexdigest(), 16) % self.m
        h2 = int(hashlib.sha1(data).hexdigest(), 16) % self.m
        return [(h1 + i * h2) % self.m for i in range(self.k)]

    def add(self, item: str) -> None:
        for pos in self._hashes(item):
            self.bits[pos] = 1
        self._n_inserted += 1

    def __contains__(self, item: str) -> bool:
        """
        Returns:
          False → DEFINITELY NOT in set (no false negatives)
          True  → PROBABLY in set (may be false positive)
        """
        return all(self.bits[pos] for pos in self._hashes(item))

    def actual_fp_rate(self) -> float:
        """Estimate actual FP rate given current fill level."""
        bits_set = self.bits.count()
        fill = bits_set / self.m
        return fill ** self.k


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
bf = BloomFilter(n=1_000_000, fp_rate=0.01)
# → BloomFilter: m=9585059 bits (1172.5 KB), k=7 hash funcs

# Insert 1 million keys
for i in range(1_000_000):
    bf.add(f"user:{i}")

# Test membership
print(f"\n'user:42' in filter:    {('user:42' in bf)}")     # True
print(f"'user:999999' in filter: {('user:999999' in bf)}")  # True
print(f"'user:9999999' in filter: {('user:9999999' in bf)}") # False (not inserted)

# Measure actual FP rate
fp_count = 0
test_n = 100_000
for i in range(1_000_000, 1_000_000 + test_n):  # not inserted
    if f"user:{i}" in bf:
        fp_count += 1
print(f"\nActual FP rate: {fp_count/test_n:.2%}")  # ≈ 1%
print(f"Memory: {bf.m // 8 // 1024} KB  vs  hash set: ~{1_000_000 * 50 // 1024} KB")
JAVA
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;
import java.security.MessageDigest;
import java.util.BitSet;

public class BloomFilter {
    private final int    m;      // bit array size
    private final int    k;      // number of hash functions
    private final BitSet bits;

    /**
     * @param n       expected number of elements
     * @param fpRate  desired false positive rate (e.g. 0.01 = 1%)
     */
    public BloomFilter(int n, double fpRate) {
        // m = -n * ln(p) / (ln2)^2
        this.m = (int) Math.ceil(-n * Math.log(fpRate) /
                                    Math.pow(Math.log(2), 2));
        // k = (m/n) * ln2
        this.k = (int) Math.ceil(((double) m / n) * Math.log(2));
        this.bits = new BitSet(m);
        System.out.printf("BloomFilter: m=%d bits (%.1f KB), k=%d%n",
            m, m / 8.0 / 1024, k);
    }

    // Double hashing: h_i(x) = (h1 + i * h2) % m
    private long[] hashes(String item) {
        byte[] data = item.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
        long[] h = new long[k];
        try {
            long h1 = bytesToLong(MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5").digest(data));
            long h2 = bytesToLong(MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-1").digest(data));
            for (int i = 0; i < k; i++)
                h[i] = Math.abs((h1 + (long)i * h2) % m);
        } catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); }
        return h;
    }

    private long bytesToLong(byte[] b) {
        long v = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < Math.min(b.length, 8); i++)
            v = (v << 8) | (b[i] & 0xFFL);
        return v;
    }

    public void add(String item) {
        for (long pos : hashes(item)) bits.set((int) pos);
    }

    /**
     * @return false → DEFINITELY NOT in set
     *         true  → PROBABLY in set (may be false positive)
     */
    public boolean mightContain(String item) {
        for (long pos : hashes(item))
            if (!bits.get((int) pos)) return false;
        return true;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        BloomFilter bf = new BloomFilter(1_000_000, 0.01);

        // Insert 1M keys
        for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) bf.add("user:" + i);

        System.out.println("user:42 present: "       + bf.mightContain("user:42"));
        System.out.println("user:9999999 present: " + bf.mightContain("user:9999999"));
        // → false (not inserted)

        // Measure actual FP rate
        int fp = 0, tests = 100_000;
        for (int i = 1_000_000; i < 1_000_000 + tests; i++)
            if (bf.mightContain("user:" + i)) fp++;
        System.out.printf("Actual FP rate: %.2f%%%n", (double)fp/tests*100);
        // → ~1%
        System.out.printf("Memory: %d KB vs HashSet ~%d KB%n",
            bf.bits.size()/8/1024, 1_000_000*50/1024);
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Bloom Filters

QWhy can a Bloom filter have false positives but not false negatives?
A
False negatives are impossible by construction: when you insert element X, you set k specific bits. Those bits can never go back to 0 (standard Bloom filter only supports insert). So if X was inserted, all its k bits are guaranteed to be 1 forever. When you query X, you check those k bits — they're all 1 — so the filter correctly says "probably present". A false negative would require at least one of X's bits to be 0, which can never happen after insertion. False positives happen because bits are shared: if k bits that belong to X also happen to be set by other elements Y, Z, etc., then a query for X finds all k bits = 1 and says "probably present" even though X was never inserted. The probability of this decreases as m increases and as k is optimised.
QHow does Cassandra use Bloom filters, and what happens if you disable them?
A
Each Cassandra SSTable has a Bloom filter stored in a -Filter.db file, loaded into off-heap memory. On a read, before opening any SSTable, Cassandra checks its filter. If the filter says "definitely not present", the SSTable is skipped entirely — no disk I/O. With a typical 1% FP rate filter, 99% of irrelevant SSTables are skipped. If you disable Bloom filters (bloom_filter_fp_chance = 1.0), every read must check every SSTable that covers the token range, causing 10-50× more disk I/O for point reads, especially for non-existent keys. Conversely, lowering the FP chance (e.g. 0.001 = 0.1%) uses 50% more memory for filters but halves unnecessary I/O — useful for read-heavy workloads on SSD.
QGiven n=1 million elements and a desired 1% false positive rate, how large is the Bloom filter?
A
Using the formula m = -n × ln(p) / (ln2)²: m = -(1,000,000) × ln(0.01) / (0.693)² = 1,000,000 × 4.605 / 0.480 ≈ 9,585,059 bits ≈ 1.2 MB. Optimal k = (m/n) × ln2 ≈ 9.585 × 0.693 ≈ 6.64, round to 7. Compare to a hash set: 1M strings averaging 20 bytes each = 20MB plus hash table overhead ≈ 50MB+. Bloom filter is 40× more compact. The trade-off: the Bloom filter tells you "yes/no" — you still need the actual data structure for retrieval. It's a gatekeeping layer, not a replacement for storage.
QWhy can't you delete elements from a standard Bloom filter, and what's the solution?
A
In a standard bit-array Bloom filter, bits are shared across elements. If you delete element X by clearing its bits, you may clear bits that are also used by other elements Y and Z. Now queries for Y and Z return false negatives — correctness is broken. Solution 1: Counting Bloom filter — replace each bit with a small counter (typically 4 bits). Increment on insert, decrement on delete. Supports deletes at the cost of 4× memory. Solution 2: Cuckoo filter — a newer alternative using cuckoo hashing. Supports deletes, uses less memory than counting Bloom (≈7 bits/element for 1% FP vs 9.6), and has better cache performance. Preferred over counting Bloom filters in most modern systems. Solution 3: rebuild the filter from scratch periodically (Cassandra does this during compaction).
QYour system uses a Bloom filter but false positives are causing excessive disk I/O. How do you tune it?
A
Three levers: (1) Increase m (more bits) — halving the FP rate requires increasing m by about 44% (since p ≈ (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k, reducing p needs larger m). (2) Increase k with m — k is optimally tuned to m/n, so increasing m and recomputing k together gives the best FP rate for the memory budget. (3) Profile whether false positives are the actual bottleneck — in Cassandra, if your read pattern is mostly for keys that DO exist, false positives are irrelevant (filter says "probably present" which is correct). The real cost is reads for non-existent keys. If that's your bottleneck, either cache negative results explicitly (a "negative cache") or use a stricter FP rate. In RocksDB, bloom_bits_per_key=10 is the default; increasing to 14 or 20 halves/quarters the FP rate at proportional memory cost.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how does Cassandra handle reads efficiently with many SSTables?", say: "Cassandra uses per-SSTable Bloom filters loaded in off-heap memory. On a read, before touching any SSTable, it checks that file's filter — a ~1.2MB probabilistic structure for 1M keys. If the filter says 'definitely not present' (one of the k bit positions is 0), Cassandra skips that SSTable entirely. This eliminates 99% of disk I/O for non-existent keys at a 1% false positive rate. The filter uses about 10 bits per key with 7 hash functions (derived from just 2 hash evaluations via double hashing). Memory cost: ~1.2MB per million keys per SSTable level. The trade-off is controllable via bloom_filter_fp_chance — lower it (e.g. 0.001) for more memory but better read performance on SSDs; raise it for memory-constrained environments. Bloom filters never cause false negatives — if a key exists, it will always be found. False positives just mean occasional unnecessary SSTable checks."

Post 09

Distributed Locking

25 min deep dive Mutex · TTL · Fencing Tokens · GC Pause · Redlock · etcd · Correctness vs Safety
The Problem

How do you prevent two processes from modifying the same resource simultaneously?

You're running 10 replicas of a billing service. A cron job runs every hour to generate invoices. If all 10 replicas wake up simultaneously, 10 invoices get generated per customer. You need exactly one replica to run the job at a time. Within a single process, you'd use a mutex. Across 10 processes on different machines, you need a distributed lock.

A distributed lock sounds straightforward: store a key in Redis or etcd, whoever creates it first holds the lock, others wait or fail. But distributed locking is deceptively hard. Three problems that make it subtle:

The Three Hard Problems

1. TTL expiry and GC pauses: You hold a lock with a 30-second lease. Your JVM has a 45-second stop-the-world GC pause. The lock expires mid-pause. Another process acquires it. You resume and think you still hold the lock — two processes now hold the same lock simultaneously. This breaks mutual exclusion.

2. Network partitions: You're the lock holder. A network partition isolates you from the lock service. The lock expires. Another process acquires it. The partition heals. You still think you hold the lock.

3. Process crashes: If a lock holder crashes without releasing, the resource is permanently locked unless you have TTL-based auto-expiry. But TTL too long means long recovery. TTL too short causes false expirations.

Building Intuition — fencing tokens make locks safe

Why a lock alone isn't enough — you need fencing tokens

The Key Insight: Monotonically Increasing Fencing Tokens

Every time a lock is granted, the lock service increments a counter and returns a fencing token (a monotonically increasing integer). When you use the lock to write to a resource, you include the fencing token. The resource (storage server, database) only accepts writes with a token ≥ the highest token it has seen.

Now the GC pause scenario is safe: Client 1 holds token=1. GC pause occurs, lock expires. Client 2 acquires with token=2. Client 1 resumes and tries to write with token=1. The storage server has already seen token=2 — it rejects token=1. Mutual exclusion is restored even though both clients think they hold the lock simultaneously.

This is the design Martin Kleppmann proposed in his "How to do distributed locking" essay (2016) as a critique of Redlock. The fencing token externalises the safety check to the storage layer, making the lock's correctness independent of timing assumptions.

Diagram 1 · Lock Lifecycle — acquire, hold, release, and expiry paths

Every possible path through a distributed lock's state machine

lock-lifecycle.svg
Diagram 2 · The GC Pause Problem + Fencing Token Solution

Without fencing tokens, two clients can hold the lock simultaneously — this is the classic bug

gc-pause-fencing.svg
Animation · Acquire → GC Pause → Expiry → Fencing Token Rejection

Step-by-step: the full GC pause scenario with fencing token protection

distributed-lock-animation
Ready
Redlock — the algorithm, the controversy, and what to use instead

Redis's multi-node locking algorithm: is it safe?

Redlock is Redis's multi-node distributed lock algorithm. It tries to acquire a lock on N independent Redis nodes (typically N=5). If it acquires the lock on the majority (≥3), the lock is held. The idea: even if 2 nodes fail, the lock is still valid on 3 nodes.

The problem (Kleppmann's critique, 2016)

Redlock relies on timing assumptions. It measures the elapsed time to acquire across nodes and subtracts from the TTL. If a process experiences a GC pause longer than the remaining TTL — after acquiring the lock — it still resumes thinking it holds the lock. Since Redlock doesn't issue fencing tokens, there is no way for the storage layer to reject the stale write. Kleppmann's conclusion: for safety-critical mutual exclusion, don't use Redlock. Use a consensus-based system (etcd or Zookeeper) that provides linearisable compare-and-swap and issues incrementing version numbers you can use as fencing tokens.

What to use in practice

For strong correctness (financial, exactly-once execution): Use etcd's lease-based locking with CreateRevision as the fencing token. The etcd revision is a cluster-wide monotonically increasing integer — perfect as a fencing token. Every etcd write atomically checks the expected revision. For high availability with acceptable occasional duplicate execution: Redis-based locking (single node or Redlock) is fine — simpler, faster, lower operational cost. The key question: is your workload idempotent? If executing twice is acceptable (add to a set, increment a counter), Redis lock is fine. If executing twice causes data corruption (create an invoice, charge a credit card), use etcd.

Trade-offs — implementation options compared

Choosing the right distributed lock for your use case

lock-comparison.svg
Code · Python & Java — Redis lock + etcd lock with fencing token

Two implementations: Redis for simplicity, etcd for correctness

PYTHON
import uuid, time
from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import Optional

# ── Redis-based lock (simple, no fencing) ──────────────────
# pip install redis
import redis

class RedisLock:
    """
    Simple Redis distributed lock using SET NX EX.
    Safe for idempotent operations. NOT safe for
    critical sections that cannot tolerate duplicates.
    """
    def __init__(self, client: redis.Redis, key: str, ttl_seconds: int = 30):
        self.client = client
        self.key    = f"lock:{key}"
        self.ttl    = ttl_seconds
        self.token  = str(uuid.uuid4())   # unique per acquire attempt

    def acquire(self, retry_delay: float = 0.1, max_retries: int = 50) -> bool:
        for _ in range(max_retries):
            # SET key token NX EX ttl  — atomic: set only if not exists
            acquired = self.client.set(
                self.key, self.token,
                nx=True,       # Only set if Not eXists
                ex=self.ttl     # EXpire after ttl seconds
            )
            if acquired:
                return True
            time.sleep(retry_delay)
        return False

    def release(self) -> bool:
        """Only release if we still own the lock (compare token before delete)."""
        # Lua script for atomic check-and-delete
        script = """
            if redis.call("get", KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
                return redis.call("del", KEYS[1])
            else
                return 0
            end
        """
        result = self.client.eval(script, 1, self.key, self.token)
        return result == 1

    def extend(self, additional_seconds: int = 30) -> bool:
        """Refresh TTL if we still own the lock."""
        script = """
            if redis.call("get", KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
                return redis.call("expire", KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
            else
                return 0
            end
        """
        result = self.client.eval(script, 1, self.key, self.token, additional_seconds)
        return result == 1

    @contextmanager
    def acquired(self):
        if not self.acquire():
            raise RuntimeError(f"Could not acquire lock: {self.key}")
        try:
            yield self.token       # caller gets token for fencing
        finally:
            self.release()


# ── Simulated etcd-style lock with fencing token ───────────
# (In real code: use etcd3 library with leases and modRevision)
class FencedStorage:
    """
    Storage that enforces fencing tokens.
    Rejects writes with token ≤ max_seen_token.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self._data: dict = {}
        self._max_token: int = 0

    def write(self, key: str, value, token: int) -> bool:
        if token <= self._max_token:
            print(f"  REJECTED: token={token} ≤ max_seen={self._max_token} (stale write)")
            return False
        self._max_token = token
        self._data[key] = value
        print(f"  ACCEPTED: key={key} value={value} token={token}")
        return True


# ── Demonstrate GC pause scenario ─────────────────────────
storage = FencedStorage()

# C1 acquires with token=1, pauses (simulated), C2 steals with token=2
print("=== GC Pause Scenario ===")
c1_token = 1   # C1 received token=1 from lock service
c2_token = 2   # C2 acquired after C1's lease expired, gets token=2

print(f"C2 writes with token={c2_token}:")
storage.write("invoice:42", "generated", c2_token)  # accepted

print(f"C1 resumes from GC pause, tries with token={c1_token}:")
storage.write("invoice:42", "duplicate!", c1_token)  # REJECTED

# Redis lock context manager usage
print("\n=== Redis Lock Usage ===")
r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)
lock = RedisLock(r, "cron:invoice-job", ttl_seconds=60)
with lock.acquired() as token:
    print(f"Lock acquired, token={token}")
    print("Running exclusive job...")
# Lock auto-released on exit
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;

// ── Fenced Storage (enforces fencing tokens) ──────────────
class FencedStorage {
    private final Map<String, Object> data = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final AtomicLong maxToken = new AtomicLong(0);

    public boolean write(String key, Object value, long token) {
        long current = maxToken.get();
        if (token <= current) {
            System.out.printf("  REJECTED: token=%d ≤ max_seen=%d (stale)%n",
                token, current);
            return false;
        }
        // CAS: only update if maxToken hasn't changed
        if (maxToken.compareAndSet(current, token)) {
            data.put(key, value);
            System.out.printf("  ACCEPTED: key=%s token=%d%n", key, token);
            return true;
        }
        return false;  // concurrent update, retry
    }
}

// ── Redis-style lock simulation ────────────────────────────
// In production: use Jedis or Lettuce with SET NX EX + Lua script
class SimulatedLockService {
    private String currentHolder = null;
    private long expiryMs = 0;
    private long nextToken = 1;

    public synchronized Optional<Long> tryAcquire(
            String clientId, long ttlMs) {
        long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
        if (currentHolder != null && now < expiryMs) {
            return Optional.empty();   // lock held by another
        }
        // lock available (or expired) — grant to this client
        currentHolder = clientId;
        expiryMs = now + ttlMs;
        long token = nextToken++;
        System.out.printf("  [LockSvc] %s acquired, token=%d, TTL=%dms%n",
            clientId, token, ttlMs);
        return Optional.of(token);
    }

    public synchronized boolean release(String clientId, long token) {
        if (clientId.equals(currentHolder)) {
            currentHolder = null;
            System.out.printf("  [LockSvc] %s released%n", clientId);
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
}

// ── Demonstration ──────────────────────────────────────────
public class DistributedLockDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        SimulatedLockService svc = new SimulatedLockService();
        FencedStorage storage = new FencedStorage();

        System.out.println("=== GC Pause + Fencing Scenario ===");

        // C1 acquires
        var t1 = svc.tryAcquire("C1", 30_000).orElseThrow();
        System.out.println("C1 holds lock, token=" + t1);

        // Simulate C1 GC pause: TTL expires, C2 acquires
        // Hack expiry for demo:
        svc.expiryMs = System.currentTimeMillis() - 1;
        var t2 = svc.tryAcquire("C2", 30_000).orElseThrow();
        System.out.println("C2 holds lock, token=" + t2);

        // C2 writes (legitimate)
        storage.write("invoice:42", "generated", t2);   // ACCEPTED

        // C1 resumes from GC pause, tries stale write
        System.out.println("C1 resumes from GC, tries stale write:");
        storage.write("invoice:42", "duplicate!", t1);   // REJECTED
        // → invoice:42 = "generated" (C2's write protected)
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Distributed Locking

QExplain how a GC pause can break a distributed lock even with TTL.
A
Client C1 acquires a lock with TTL=30s. C1's JVM starts a stop-the-world garbage collection (common in CMS/G1 under heap pressure) that takes 45 seconds. During this pause, C1's process is completely frozen — it cannot send heartbeats or check the clock. The lock's TTL expires after 30 seconds. The lock service reclaims the key. Client C2 acquires the lock. C2 proceeds to write to the protected resource. After 45 seconds, C1's GC finishes. C1 resumes execution believing it still holds the lock (it never heard about the expiry). C1 proceeds to write to the protected resource. Now both C1 and C2 are writing concurrently — mutual exclusion is violated. This is not a bug in any specific implementation — it's a fundamental property of lock-based mutual exclusion under arbitrary process pauses.
QWhat is a fencing token and how does it make distributed locks safe?
A
A fencing token is a monotonically increasing integer returned by the lock service with every successful lock grant. Token N+1 is always higher than token N. When a client uses the lock to operate on a resource, it includes its fencing token in the request. The resource server maintains a "max token seen" counter and rejects any request with a token ≤ max seen. Now the GC pause scenario is safe: C1 gets token=33, pauses. Lock expires.
