C2 gets token=34, writes with token=34. Resource updates max_token=34. C1 resumes and tries to write with token=33. Resource rejects it (33 < 34). The lock's actual expiry becomes irrelevant to correctness — correctness is enforced by the storage layer, not by timing. This is the key insight: locks should be used for performance (reducing contention), not correctness. Fencing tokens provide correctness.
QWhy does the Redis SET NX EX command use a random UUID value, and why must release be a Lua script?
A
The random UUID as the lock value solves a "stale delete" problem. Without it: C1 acquires the lock, TTL expires (GC pause), C2 acquires the lock, C1 resumes and calls DEL — deleting C2's lock instead of its own. With UUID: each client stores its unique ID as the value. On release, the client first GETs the value to check it matches its UUID, then DELetes only if it matches. But GET then DEL is not atomic — C1 could GET (sees its ID), TTL expires, C2 acquires (overwrites with C2's ID), C1 DELetes (now deletes C2's lock). This is why release must be a Lua script: Lua scripts execute atomically in Redis, making the check-and-delete a single atomic operation. The script: if GET(key)==my_uuid then DEL(key) end.
QHow does etcd implement distributed locking, and why is it safer than Redis?
A
etcd provides linearisable reads and writes backed by Raft consensus. Lock implementation: (1) Client creates a lease with a TTL via LeaseGrant. (2) Client creates a key (e.g. /lock/my-job) with PutWithLease using a transaction that checks the key doesn't exist (CreateRevision == 0). If it does exist, the transaction fails. (3) On success, etcd returns the key's ModRevision — a cluster-wide monotonically increasing integer. This is the fencing token. (4) The client includes this revision in every storage operation. (5) When the lease TTL expires, etcd automatically deletes the key (unlike Redis where TTL expiry is approximate). The safety advantage: etcd's Raft-backed linearisability means the lock grant is globally ordered. Redis with a single node is fast but loses the lock on crash. Redis with replication risks a split-brain where both nodes grant the lock.
QWhen would you choose optimistic locking over a distributed lock?
A
Optimistic locking is better when: (1) Read >> Write contention — if most accesses are reads and writes are infrequent, holding a lock pessimistically wastes latency on uncontested reads. (2) Short critical sections — if the protected operation is a single CAS (compare-and-swap) on a database row (UPDATE WHERE version=N), the operation itself is atomic and no separate lock is needed. (3) Operations are retryable — on CAS failure (version mismatch), simply re-read and retry. This is how most databases implement optimistic concurrency control (MVCC). Distributed locks are better for: long-running exclusive operations (job scheduling, migrations), cases where you need to coordinate access across multiple resources that can't all be CAS'd atomically, and where retry under contention would be expensive (leader election).
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design a distributed lock for a cron job that must run exactly once across 10 replicas", say: "First, clarify 'exactly once' — do you mean at-most-once (skip if already running) or exactly-once with no skips? For at-most-once: a Redis SET NX EX lock with 60-second TTL plus an idempotency key in the job table. Simple, fast, acceptable if occasionally a job runs twice due to TTL edge cases. For true exactly-once: I'd use etcd leases. Each replica creates a key under /lock/cron-job with a lease. The winner (via Raft-ordered compare-and-swap) gets the lock and receives a CreateRevision as its fencing token. It includes this revision in all database writes — a PostgreSQL trigger rejects writes with revision ≤ max_seen. On GC pause: lease expires, another replica takes over, stale writes from the paused replica are rejected at the DB layer. For recovery: the job table tracks job state with the revision; on startup, any replica can check if a job completed (idempotency key present) or resume from last checkpoint."

Post 10

LSM Trees & SSTables

25 min deep dive Write Amplification · Read Amplification · MemTable · Compaction · Tombstones · RocksDB · Cassandra
The Problem

Why random disk writes are the bottleneck — and how LSM trees eliminate them

Traditional B-tree databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB) update records in place: a write to key user:42 seeks to the page containing that key on disk and overwrites it. For spinning disks, a random write involves a mechanical head seek — typically 5–10ms. At 200 writes/second, you're spending nearly all your time seeking. For SSDs, random writes cause write amplification and wear levelling overhead.

The fundamental insight behind LSM trees: sequential writes are orders of magnitude faster than random writes. A spinning disk doing sequential writes can sustain 100–200 MB/s; random writes peak at maybe 1–2 MB/s. SSDs show a similar (though smaller) gap. If you can convert all writes into sequential appends, you dramatically increase write throughput.

The LSM Trade-off in One Sentence

LSM trees sacrifice read performance and add background compaction cost in exchange for extremely high write throughput. Every write is a sequential append — the fastest possible disk operation. Reads must search multiple levels (but Bloom filters and sparse indexes make this fast). Compaction runs in the background merging levels, reclaiming space, and removing tombstones.

Building Intuition — write path from memory to disk

Think of it as: RAM buffer → immutable sorted files → periodic merges

The Three-Layer Mental Model

Layer 1 — MemTable (in RAM): Every write goes here first. It's a sorted in-memory data structure (usually a red-black tree or skip list). Writes are O(log n). Changes to the same key simply overwrite the entry. WAL provides durability. This is fast — pure RAM.

Layer 2 — L0 SSTables (on disk, recently flushed): When MemTable hits its size limit (typically 64MB), it's flushed to disk as an immutable sorted file called an SSTable. Multiple L0 files may contain overlapping key ranges (they're just sequential flushes, no merging yet). This is a sequential write — very fast.

Layer 3 — L1, L2, L3... (compacted levels): Background compaction merges L0 files into L1 (sorted, non-overlapping key ranges), then L1 into L2, etc. Each level is ~10× larger than the previous. Compaction removes tombstones (deleted keys), deduplicates, and sorts. A read searches MemTable, then L0 (all files), then L1, L2... in order, stopping when key is found.

Diagram 1 · LSM Tree Architecture — complete structure with all levels

Write path, read path, and compaction — all in one diagram

lsm-architecture.svg
Animation · Write → MemTable → Flush → Compaction — step by step

Watch a write flow from client through WAL, MemTable, SSTable flush, and compaction

lsm-write-animation
Ready
Compaction Strategies — the write amplification vs space amplification dial

Levelled vs Size-Tiered vs FIFO — choosing the right compaction for your workload

compaction-strategies.svg
Trade-offs — LSM vs B-tree and the RUM conjecture

No free lunch: every storage engine picks two of Read, Update, Memory

The RUM Conjecture

Harvard's 2016 RUM conjecture states: for any data structure, you can optimise at most two of: Read overhead (R), Update overhead (U), Memory overhead (M). LSM trees choose low U (writes) at the cost of higher R (reads). B-trees choose low R and moderate U at the cost of higher M (fragmented pages). Understanding this tells you why there's no "best" storage engine — it's always a workload-specific trade-off.

Write amplification — the hidden tax

Write amplification (WA) = bytes written to disk / bytes written by the application. For levelled LSM: WA ≈ 10-30×. For a 100 MB/s write workload, you're actually writing 1–3 GB/s to disk due to compaction. On SSDs with a write endurance of 300 TBW (terabytes written), high WA dramatically shortens disk lifetime. Production RocksDB tuning focuses heavily on reducing WA. Key knobs: increase L1 size ratio, use compression, tune compaction thread counts.

When to choose LSM vs B-tree

Choose LSM when: (1) Write throughput is the bottleneck (IoT ingestion, logging, metrics), (2) Keys are uniformly distributed (hot keys still cause issues), (3) Reads are mostly recent data (MemTable + L0 serve most reads). Choose B-tree when: (1) Read-write ratio is read-heavy (reporting, OLAP), (2) Point-in-time reads are common (MVCC), (3) Range scans are frequent (SQL WHERE key BETWEEN a AND b). In practice: Cassandra, RocksDB, LevelDB, ClickHouse use LSM. PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, SQLite use B-trees.

Code · Python & Java — LSM tree simulation

MemTable, SSTable flush, compaction, and read path with Bloom filter

PYTHON
from sortedcontainers import SortedDict   # pip install sortedcontainers
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, List, Dict
import heapq

TOMBSTONE = "__DELETED__"   # marker for deleted keys

# ── SSTable (immutable sorted file) ────────────────────────
@dataclass
class SSTable:
    data: SortedDict   # key → value (sorted)
    level: int = 0

    def get(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        return self.data.get(key)

    def might_contain(self, key: str) -> bool:
        # Real impl uses Bloom filter; simplified: check key range
        if not self.data: return False
        first, last = self.data.keys()[0], self.data.keys()[-1]
        return first <= key <= last


# ── MemTable (in-memory sorted write buffer) ──────────────
class MemTable:
    SIZE_LIMIT = 5   # flush after 5 entries (real: 64MB)

    def __init__(self):
        self._data = SortedDict()

    def put(self, key: str, value: str) -> None:
        self._data[key] = value

    def delete(self, key: str) -> None:
        self._data[key] = TOMBSTONE   # tombstone — not actual delete

    def get(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        return self._data.get(key)   # includes tombstones

    def is_full(self) -> bool:
        return len(self._data) >= self.SIZE_LIMIT

    def flush_to_sstable(self) -> 'SSTable':
        sst = SSTable(data=SortedDict(self._data), level=0)
        self._data.clear()
        return sst


# ── LSM Tree Engine ───────────────────────────────────────
class LSMTree:
    COMPACT_THRESHOLD = 3   # compact after 3 L0 SSTables

    def __init__(self):
        self.memtable = MemTable()
        self.l0: List[SSTable] = []   # may overlap
        self.l1: Optional[SSTable] = None  # single sorted SSTable

    def put(self, key: str, value: str) -> None:
        self.memtable.put(key, value)
        if self.memtable.is_full():
            self._flush()

    def delete(self, key: str) -> None:
        self.memtable.delete(key)   # tombstone in MemTable
        if self.memtable.is_full():
            self._flush()

    def get(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        # 1. Check MemTable first (most recent)
        val = self.memtable.get(key)
        if val == TOMBSTONE: return None
        if val is not None: return val

        # 2. Search L0 in reverse order (newest first)
        for sst in reversed(self.l0):
            if sst.might_contain(key):
                val = sst.get(key)
                if val == TOMBSTONE: return None
                if val is not None: return val

        # 3. Search L1
        if self.l1 and self.l1.might_contain(key):
            val = self.l1.get(key)
            if val == TOMBSTONE: return None
            return val

        return None   # not found

    def _flush(self) -> None:
        sst = self.memtable.flush_to_sstable()
        self.l0.append(sst)
        print(f"  Flushed MemTable → L0 SSTable [{list(sst.data.keys())}]")
        if len(self.l0) >= self.COMPACT_THRESHOLD:
            self._compact()

    def _compact(self) -> None:
        print("  Compacting L0 → L1...")
        # Merge all L0 + existing L1 into new L1
        merged = SortedDict()
        sources = list(self.l0)
        if self.l1: sources.append(self.l1)
        # Sort sources oldest → newest; newer overwrites older
        for sst in sources:
            for k, v in sst.data.items():
                merged[k] = v
        # Remove tombstones (no lower levels to propagate to)
        cleaned = SortedDict({k: v for k, v in merged.items() if v != TOMBSTONE})
        self.l1 = SSTable(data=cleaned, level=1)
        self.l0.clear()
        print(f"  L1 after compact: [{list(self.l1.data.keys())}]")


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
db = LSMTree()
# Insert 8 keys (triggers flush at 5)
for k, v in [('a',1),('c',3),('e',5),('h',8),('z',26)]:
    db.put(k, str(v))
db.delete('h')   # tombstone 'h'
db.put('b', '2')
db.put('d', '4')
db.put('f', '6')   # 3 L0 SSTables → compaction triggers

print(f"\nget('e') = {db.get('e')}")   # '5'
print(f"get('h') = {db.get('h')}")   # None (tombstone)
print(f"get('x') = {db.get('x')}")   # None (not inserted)
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.Collectors;

public class LSMTreeDemo {
    static final String TOMBSTONE = "__DELETED__";

    // ── SSTable ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    static class SSTable {
        final TreeMap<String, String> data;
        final int level;
        SSTable(TreeMap<String,String> d, int lvl) { data=d; level=lvl; }

        String get(String key) { return data.get(key); }

        boolean mightContain(String key) {
            if (data.isEmpty()) return false;
            return key.compareTo(data.firstKey()) >= 0
                && key.compareTo(data.lastKey())  <= 0;
        }
    }

    // ── MemTable ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    static class MemTable {
        static final int SIZE_LIMIT = 5;
        final TreeMap<String, String> data = new TreeMap<>();

        void    put(String k, String v)  { data.put(k, v); }
        void    delete(String k)          { data.put(k, TOMBSTONE); }
        String  get(String k)             { return data.get(k); }
        boolean isFull()                  { return data.size() >= SIZE_LIMIT; }

        SSTable flush() {
            SSTable sst = new SSTable(new TreeMap<>(data), 0);
            data.clear();
            return sst;
        }
    }

    // ── LSM Engine ────────────────────────────────────────────
    static class LSMTree {
        static final int COMPACT_THRESHOLD = 3;
        final MemTable memtable = new MemTable();
        final List<SSTable> l0 = new ArrayList<>();
        SSTable l1 = null;

        void put(String k, String v)    { memtable.put(k,v);        maybeFlush(); }
        void delete(String k)           { memtable.delete(k);      maybeFlush(); }

        Optional<String> get(String k) {
            // 1. MemTable
            String v = memtable.get(k);
            if (TOMBSTONE.equals(v)) return Optional.empty();
            if (v != null) return Optional.of(v);
            // 2. L0 (newest first)
            for (int i = l0.size()-1; i >= 0; i--) {
                SSTable sst = l0.get(i);
                if (sst.mightContain(k)) {
                    v = sst.get(k);
                    if (TOMBSTONE.equals(v)) return Optional.empty();
                    if (v != null) return Optional.of(v);
                }
            }
            // 3. L1
            if (l1 != null && l1.mightContain(k)) {
                v = l1.get(k);
                if (TOMBSTONE.equals(v)) return Optional.empty();
                if (v != null) return Optional.of(v);
            }
            return Optional.empty();
        }

        private void maybeFlush() {
            if (!memtable.isFull()) return;
            SSTable sst = memtable.flush();
            l0.add(sst);
            System.out.println("  Flushed → L0 " + sst.data.keySet());
            if (l0.size() >= COMPACT_THRESHOLD) compact();
        }

        private void compact() {
            TreeMap<String,String> merged = new TreeMap<>();
            if (l1 != null) merged.putAll(l1.data);
            l0.forEach(s -> merged.putAll(s.data));
            // Remove tombstones
            merged.entrySet().removeIf(e -> TOMBSTONE.equals(e.getValue()));
            l1 = new SSTable(merged, 1); l0.clear();
            System.out.println("  Compacted → L1 " + l1.data.keySet());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LSMTree db = new LSMTree();
        Map.of("a","1","c","3","e","5","h","8","z","26")
            .forEach(db::put);
        db.delete("h");
        db.put("b","2"); db.put("d","4"); db.put("f","6");

        System.out.println("get(e)=" + db.get("e"));  // Optional[5]
        System.out.println("get(h)=" + db.get("h"));  // Optional.empty
        System.out.println("get(x)=" + db.get("x"));  // Optional.empty
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about LSM Trees

QWalk me through exactly what happens when you write a key to an LSM-tree database.
A
Step 1: Write the record to the WAL (sequential append, fsync). This ensures durability — if we crash before the MemTable is flushed, we can replay the WAL on recovery. Step 2: Insert the key-value pair into the MemTable (an in-memory sorted data structure, typically a skip list or red-black tree). O(log n) insertion. Step 3: Acknowledge success to the client — we're done, the write is durable (WAL) and readable (MemTable). Step 4: When MemTable reaches its size threshold (e.g. 64MB in RocksDB), create a new empty MemTable for writes. The old MemTable becomes immutable. Step 5: A background thread flushes the immutable MemTable to disk as an SSTable — a sorted, immutable file. This is a sequential write — very fast. Step 6: Periodically, a background compaction job merges SSTables, removes tombstones, and deduplicates, maintaining the levelled structure.
QHow does an LSM tree handle deletes? What is a tombstone and why does it eventually disappear?
A
LSM trees are append-only — you cannot go back and erase a record from an immutable SSTable. To delete a key, you write a special tombstone record — a marker saying "this key is deleted". The tombstone is written to the MemTable and eventually flushed to an SSTable. When you read a key, if the most recent record for it is a tombstone, you return null (not found). The tombstone persists across levels until compaction reaches a point where there are no lower-level SSTables containing the original value. At that point, the compaction job can safely drop both the tombstone and the original record — they cancel out and the key truly disappears. This has an important implication: disk space is not immediately reclaimed on delete. A delete amplification can occur if tombstones accumulate faster than compaction processes them.
QWhat is write amplification and how does it affect SSD lifetime?
A
Write amplification (WA) = total bytes written to storage / bytes written by the application. For levelled LSM compaction, WA ≈ 10–30× because when you compact L0 (say 10MB) into L1 (100MB), you rewrite the entire L1 range that overlaps with L0 — potentially rewriting 100MB to accept 10MB of new data. For a 100MB/s application write rate with WA=10, the storage device sees 1GB/s of writes. SSDs have a finite write endurance (typically 300–600 TBW for enterprise SSDs). High WA shortens SSD lifetime and increases costs. Production systems tune WA by: increasing level size ratios (reducing compaction frequency), using compression to reduce SSTable sizes, and choosing size-tiered compaction for write-heavy workloads (lower WA at cost of higher read amp).
QWhy does Cassandra use LSM trees instead of B-trees for its storage engine?
A
Cassandra is designed for write-heavy workloads — IoT sensors, time-series, social media activity — where write throughput is the bottleneck. LSM trees give Cassandra three key advantages: (1) Extremely high write throughput — every write is a sequential append to the MemTable and WAL, then sequential flush to SSTable. No random I/O on writes. (2) No update-in-place — SSTables are immutable. No page fragmentation, no locking contention on shared pages. Multiple writers never conflict at the storage layer. (3) Efficient compaction on HDDs — Cassandra was designed to run on commodity spinning disks. Sequential compaction I/O is 50× faster than random reads on HDDs. The trade-off: reads on Cassandra may search multiple SSTables and MemTable, which is why Bloom filters, partition keys, and clustering columns are critical for performance — they eliminate unnecessary SSTable scans.
QHow do SSTables enable range scans efficiently despite being spread across multiple files?
A
Each SSTable is internally sorted by key. Each SSTable also has a sparse index — a small file (fits in memory) that maps every 4KB block's first key to its disk offset. For a range scan of keys a–z: (1) Binary search the sparse index to find the block offset where 'a' starts. (2) Sequentially read from that offset — sequential I/O, very fast. (3) Stop when you pass 'z'. For a multi-level LSM, you do a merge sort across the results from each level, using a priority queue (min-heap) keyed on the current smallest key from each SSTable iterator. The heap ensures keys are returned in sorted order. L1+ levels have non-overlapping key ranges, so each range scan touches at most one SSTable per level there. L0 may require scanning all files since they can overlap, which is why the number of L0 files is bounded (triggering compaction when exceeded).
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how does RocksDB/Cassandra achieve high write throughput?", say: "They use an LSM tree (Log-Structured Merge tree). Every write is a sequential operation: first to the WAL (for durability), then into the MemTable (in-memory sorted structure). Sequential writes are 50–100× faster than random writes on HDDs and significantly faster on SSDs too. When the MemTable fills up (64MB in RocksDB), it's flushed to disk as an immutable SSTable — another sequential write. No random I/O on the write path at all. Background compaction periodically merges SSTables, removing tombstones and maintaining sorted order, but this runs asynchronously without blocking writes. The trade-off is read amplification: a point read must check MemTable, then all L0 SSTables (which may overlap), then L1, L2... — mitigated by Bloom filters that skip most SSTables for non-existent keys. Write amplification (10–30× for levelled compaction) is the main operational concern — it drives storage bandwidth and SSD wear."

Post 11

Saga Pattern

25 min deep dive Distributed Transactions · Compensating Transactions · Choreography · Orchestration · Isolation · Idempotency
The Problem

How do you maintain data consistency across microservices without a distributed transaction?

You're building a travel booking system with three microservices: Hotel Service, Flight Service, and Payment Service. A customer books a trip: reserve hotel + reserve flight + charge card. All three must succeed, or none should persist. If the card charge fails after both reservations are made, you must cancel both reservations.

With a monolith you'd wrap it in a database transaction: BEGIN; reserve_hotel(); reserve_flight(); charge_card(); COMMIT;. One of them fails → automatic rollback. But with three separate microservices, each with its own database, there is no shared transaction manager. 2PC would work but creates tight coupling, blocking, and a coordinator SPOF. What do you do?

The Saga Solution

A Saga breaks the cross-service transaction into a sequence of local ACID transactions, each in one service, each publishing an event or message on success. If any step fails, the saga executes compensating transactions in reverse order to undo the previously completed steps. A compensating transaction is the semantic inverse of the original: "cancel hotel reservation" compensates "reserve hotel". The key insight: you don't need a global lock — you accept temporary inconsistency and fix it with compensation.

Building Intuition — semantic undo vs database rollback

Compensation is not rollback — it creates a new offsetting transaction

The Key Difference from Database Rollback

Database rollback: the original changes are erased as if they never happened. No trace remains. Atomic and instantaneous from the database's perspective.

Saga compensation: the original local transaction is already committed and visible to other services. Compensation creates a new transaction that logically undoes the effect. If you reserved a hotel room (transaction 1), the compensation is "cancel reservation" (transaction 2) — not an erase, but a new action. The reservation appears in the audit log as "created then cancelled". This is intentional — the system has a complete history.

The implication: between step N completing and its compensation running, other transactions can read the intermediate state. A saga lacks full ACID isolation. This is the fundamental trade-off you must design around.

Diagram 1 · Saga vs 2PC — side by side

Why Saga is preferred in microservices despite weaker guarantees

saga-vs-2pc.svg
Diagram 2 · Choreography vs Orchestration — two implementation styles

Same saga, two different coordination models — each with distinct trade-offs

choreography-vs-orchestration.svg
Animation · Orchestration Saga — Hotel → Flight → Card fails → compensate in reverse

Step-by-step: forward steps succeed, payment fails, compensations execute in LIFO order

saga-animation
Ready
The Isolation Problem — and how to handle it

Sagas lack ACID isolation — here are the four anomalies and their fixes

1. Dirty reads — reading intermediate saga state

After Hotel books but before Payment completes, another service reads the reservation and sees it as "confirmed". If Payment then fails and Hotel cancels, that other service acted on data that no longer exists. Fix: use a semantic lock — the Hotel service marks reservations as PENDING until the saga coordinator marks them CONFIRMED. Other services only process CONFIRMED records. This is the semantic lock countermeasure.

2. Lost updates — two sagas modify the same record

Saga A reads account balance $1000, Saga B reads account balance $1000. Both debit $200. One writes $800, the other also writes $800 — net effect: lost one debit. Fix: optimistic locking with version numbers. Each saga reads the version, includes it in its update: UPDATE WHERE version = N. One saga wins, the other retries. Or use commutative operations: instead of SET balance = 800, use DECREMENT balance BY 200 — safe to apply multiple times regardless of ordering.

3. Pivoting — what if compensation itself fails?

A compensating transaction can fail too — the Hotel service is down when you try to cancel. The saga must retry the compensation until it succeeds (with exponential backoff). This requires compensating transactions to be idempotent — calling "cancel reservation #42" twice must have the same effect as calling it once. Use idempotency keys: cancel_reservation(reservation_id, saga_id) — the first call cancels, subsequent calls with the same saga_id are no-ops.

4. Retriable vs compensatable transactions

Some transactions are retriable (they will eventually succeed if retried — a network timeout on a payment that hasn't been charged yet). Others are compensatable (they have already committed and must be undone). You must know which is which for each step. The pivot point is the "point of no return" — typically the payment step. Steps before it are compensatable. Steps after it are retriable. This is why payment always comes last in a booking saga: once payment succeeds, all remaining steps should retry indefinitely until they succeed.

Trade-offs — when to use Saga vs alternatives

Sagas are not always the answer

saga-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — Saga orchestrator with compensations

Complete orchestration saga: forward steps, failure detection, LIFO compensation

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, List, Optional
from enum import Enum, auto
import uuid

class SagaStatus(Enum):
    RUNNING      = auto()
    COMPLETED    = auto()
    COMPENSATING = auto()
    FAILED       = auto()

@dataclass
class SagaStep:
    name:        str
    action:      Callable[[str], bool]  # forward action: saga_id → success
    compensation: Callable[[str], bool] # undo action:    saga_id → success

class SagaOrchestrator:
    """
    Orchestration-based Saga with LIFO compensation.

    Executes steps in order. On failure, compensates
    all already-executed steps in reverse order.
    Compensations are retried until they succeed (must be idempotent).
    """

    def __init__(self, steps: List[SagaStep]):
        self.steps = steps

    def execute(self) -> SagaStatus:
        saga_id = str(uuid.uuid4()[:8])
        executed: List[SagaStep] = []   # tracks steps to compensate
        status = SagaStatus.RUNNING

        print(f"\n=== Saga {saga_id} started ===")

        # ── Forward phase ──────────────────────────────────────
        for step in self.steps:
            print(f"  → Executing: {step.name}")
            try:
                success = step.action(saga_id)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"    EXCEPTION: {e}")
                success = False

            if success:
                executed.append(step)
                print(f"    ✓ succeeded")
            else:
                print(f"    ✗ failed — starting compensation")
                status = SagaStatus.COMPENSATING
                break

        if status == SagaStatus.RUNNING:
            print(f"=== Saga {saga_id} COMPLETED ===")
            return SagaStatus.COMPLETED

        # ── Compensation phase (LIFO order) ───────────────────
        for step in reversed(executed):
            print(f"  ↩ Compensating: {step.name}")
            retries = 0
            while True:   # retry until success (idempotent)
                try:
                    ok = step.compensation(saga_id)
                    if ok:
                        print(f"    ✓ compensation succeeded")
                        break
                except Exception as e:
                    print(f"    retry {retries+1}: {e}")
                retries += 1
                if retries > 3:
                    print(f"    ESCALATE: manual intervention for {step.name}")
                    break

        print(f"=== Saga {saga_id} FAILED (compensated) ===")
        return SagaStatus.FAILED


# ── Concrete steps ─────────────────────────────────────────
reservations: dict = {}

def book_hotel(saga_id: str) -> bool:
    reservations[f"hotel:{saga_id}"] = "PENDING"   # semantic lock
    print("    Hotel: room 204 reserved (PENDING)")
    return True

def cancel_hotel(saga_id: str) -> bool:
    # Idempotent: safe to call multiple times
    reservations.pop(f"hotel:{saga_id}", None)
    print("    Hotel: reservation cancelled")
    return True

def book_flight(saga_id: str) -> bool:
    reservations[f"flight:{saga_id}"] = "PENDING"
    print("    Flight: seat 14B reserved (PENDING)")
    return True

def cancel_flight(saga_id: str) -> bool:
    reservations.pop(f"flight:{saga_id}", None)
    print("    Flight: seat cancelled")
    return True

def charge_card(saga_id: str) -> bool:
    print("    Payment: card declined!")
    return False   # payment always fails in this demo

def refund_card(saga_id: str) -> bool:
    print("    Payment: no charge to refund")
    return True


# ── Run saga ───────────────────────────────────────────────
saga = SagaOrchestrator([
    SagaStep("Book Hotel",  book_hotel,  cancel_hotel),
    SagaStep("Book Flight", book_flight, cancel_flight),
    SagaStep("Charge Card", charge_card, refund_card),
])
result = saga.execute()
print(f"\nFinal status: {result}")
print(f"Active reservations: {reservations}")  # should be empty
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.function.Predicate;

record SagaStep(String name,
                  Predicate<String> action,
                  Predicate<String> compensation) {}

enum SagaStatus { COMPLETED, FAILED }

class SagaOrchestrator {
    private final List<SagaStep> steps;

    public SagaOrchestrator(List<SagaStep> steps) { this.steps = steps; }

    public SagaStatus execute(String sagaId) {
        Deque<SagaStep> executed = new ArrayDeque<>();
        System.out.println("\n=== Saga " + sagaId + " started ===");

        // ── Forward phase ────────────────────────────────────
        for (SagaStep step : steps) {
            System.out.println("  → Executing: " + step.name());
            boolean ok = false;
            try { ok = step.action().test(sagaId); }
            catch (Exception e) { System.out.println("    EXCEPTION: " + e.getMessage()); }

            if (ok) {
                executed.push(step);   // LIFO stack
                System.out.println("    ✓ succeeded");
            } else {
                System.out.println("    ✗ failed — compensating");
                compensate(executed, sagaId);
                System.out.println("=== Saga FAILED (compensated) ===");
                return SagaStatus.FAILED;
            }
        }
        System.out.println("=== Saga COMPLETED ===");
        return SagaStatus.COMPLETED;
    }

    private void compensate(Deque<SagaStep> stack, String sagaId) {
        while (!stack.isEmpty()) {
            SagaStep step = stack.pop();   // reverse order
            System.out.println("  ↩ Compensating: " + step.name());
            for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
                try {
                    if (step.compensation().test(sagaId)) {
                        System.out.println("    ✓ compensated");
                        break;
                    }
                } catch (Exception e) {
                    System.out.println("    retry "+(i+1)+": "+e.getMessage());
                }
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Map<String, String> reservations = new HashMap<>();

        SagaOrchestrator saga = new SagaOrchestrator(List.of(
            new SagaStep("Book Hotel",
                id -> { reservations.put("hotel:"+id, "PENDING");
                        System.out.println("    Hotel: room 204 reserved");
                        return true; },
                id -> { reservations.remove("hotel:"+id);
                        System.out.println("    Hotel: cancelled");
                        return true; }),
            new SagaStep("Book Flight",
                id -> { reservations.put("flight:"+id, "PENDING");
                        System.out.println("    Flight: seat 14B reserved");
                        return true; },
                id -> { reservations.remove("flight:"+id);
                        System.out.println("    Flight: cancelled");
                        return true; }),
            new SagaStep("Charge Card",
                id -> { System.out.println("    Payment: card declined!");
                        return false; },   // fails
                id -> { System.out.println("    Payment: no charge to refund");
                        return true; })
        ));

        SagaStatus result = saga.execute(UUID.randomUUID().toString().substring(0,8));
        System.out.println("Status: " + result);
        System.out.println("Reservations: " + reservations);  // empty
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about the Saga Pattern

QWhat is the Saga pattern and why is it preferred over 2PC in microservices?
A
A Saga decomposes a multi-service transaction into a sequence of local ACID transactions, each within one service. If a step fails, compensating transactions run in reverse order to undo completed steps. 2PC is preferred over Saga only when strict ACID isolation is required. Saga is preferred in microservices because: (1) No blocking — there's no coordinator that can freeze all participants; (2) No tight coupling — services don't need to expose a prepare/commit interface; (3) No single point of failure — if the orchestrator crashes, it can resume from a durable state store; (4) Works across heterogeneous databases — each service uses its own DB, no distributed transaction protocol needed. The trade-off: Sagas have no isolation — intermediate states are visible, requiring semantic locks or idempotent operations to handle concurrent reads.
QWhat is the difference between choreography and orchestration sagas?
A
In choreography, there's no central coordinator. Each service listens for events on a message bus and reacts by executing its local step and publishing a new event. Hotel service listens for BookingStarted → reserves room → publishes HotelReserved → Flight service picks it up → etc. No service knows the overall saga flow. Pros: truly decoupled, no coordinator SPOF. Cons: hard to track overall state, risk of cyclic dependencies, debugging requires tracing events across services. In orchestration, a central Saga Orchestrator (a service or state machine) sends commands to each participant in order and waits for replies. It tracks the saga state explicitly. Pros: clear visibility, easy to monitor, compensation order is explicit. Cons: orchestrator is a central component that must be fault-tolerant; services are coupled to its API. In practice: use choreography for simple 2–3 step sagas; orchestration for complex sagas with many steps, timeouts, and parallel branches.
QWhat is a compensating transaction and why must it be idempotent?
A
A compensating transaction is the semantic inverse of a completed forward step. For "reserve hotel room", the compensation is "cancel reservation". It creates a new transaction that logically undoes the effect — unlike a database rollback which erases the change. Compensating transactions must be idempotent because: the saga orchestrator may retry a compensation if the service is temporarily unavailable or returns an error. Calling "cancel reservation #42" twice must produce the same result as calling it once — the second call finds the reservation already cancelled and returns success without side effects. Implementation pattern: include the saga ID in every compensation call. On first call: cancel and mark as CANCELLED. On subsequent calls: check if already CANCELLED, return success immediately (no-op). This makes compensations safe to retry without double-cancelling, double-refunding, etc.
QA payment step succeeds but the subsequent "create order" step fails. What do you do?
A
Payment is the "pivot point" — the point of no return. Once payment succeeds, the customer has been charged real money. You cannot simply refund as compensation because payment refunds have real consequences (processing fees, bank policies, customer experience). The correct design: steps after the pivot point should be retriable rather than compensatable. "Create order" should retry indefinitely (with exponential backoff) until it succeeds — a transient DB failure is not a reason to refund. If "create order" ultimately cannot succeed (e.g. schema migration bug), the saga enters a dead letter queue for manual intervention rather than automatically refunding. This is why in booking systems, payment always comes last: if the hotel or flight reservation can't be confirmed, you don't charge. If payment is the final step, you charge only when everything else is already confirmed.
QHow do you handle the "dirty read" problem where another service reads interim saga state?
A
The semantic lock countermeasure: mark records created during a saga with a status field (PENDING, CONFIRMED, CANCELLED). Other services only process records in CONFIRMED state. The saga coordinator marks records CONFIRMED only after all steps succeed. If the saga fails, records in PENDING state are marked CANCELLED by the compensation. This prevents other services from acting on data that might be rolled back. A related technique: the semantic lock asks other operations to wait or return an error if they encounter a PENDING record, similar to a mutex at the business logic level. For read-heavy workloads where waiting is unacceptable, use commutative operations: design your data model so that operations can be applied in any order without conflict (e.g. use event sourcing where the final balance is computed from all events, not stored as a mutable field).
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you handle a multi-step transaction across three microservices?", say: "I'd use a Saga with orchestration. The orchestrator is a state machine stored in a durable store (e.g. a sagas table in PostgreSQL or a workflow engine like Temporal). It tracks the current step, saga ID, and compensation history. Each step calls the downstream service and waits for a success/failure reply. On success: advance to next step, persist state. On failure: start compensation in LIFO order. Every compensation is idempotent (includes saga ID, checks for already-compensated state). I put payment last — it's the pivot point. Steps before it are compensatable, steps after it are retriable. For intermediate state isolation, services mark records PENDING until the saga completes, and only other CONFIRMED records are processed. I'd use the outbox pattern on each service to publish events transactionally, ensuring exactly-once delivery even if the service crashes mid-step."

Post 12

Replication Strategies

25 min deep dive Single-Leader · Multi-Leader · Leaderless · Replication Lag · Read-Your-Writes · Quorum · Split-Brain
The Problem

Why keep multiple copies of data — and what consistency guarantees do you sacrifice?

Replication means keeping copies of the same data on multiple nodes. The reasons: (1) High availability — if one node fails, others serve requests; (2) Read scalability — spread read load across multiple replicas; (3) Latency reduction — place replicas close to users in different geographies. But replication introduces a fundamental challenge: keeping all copies consistent when writes happen. How do you ensure every replica eventually has the same data? What happens during the lag?

The Three Core Questions for Any Replication Strategy

1. Where do writes go? One leader (single-leader), multiple leaders (multi-leader), or any node (leaderless).
2. When are replicas updated? Synchronously (block until all ack) or asynchronously (fire and forget).
3. What happens during failures? Automatic failover, manual promotion, or quorum-based acceptance.

Building Intuition — the replication lag problem

Async replication is fast but creates a window of inconsistency

Why async replication causes stale reads

User A posts a photo. The write goes to the primary (acknowledged immediately with async replication). User A refreshes — the request routes to a replica. The replica hasn't received the replication event yet (200ms lag). The photo is gone. User A thinks the post failed and tries again.

This is the classic read-your-writes anomaly. The user wrote data and then read it back, but got stale data because reads and writes went to different nodes. The solution isn't "make replication synchronous" (that kills write throughput) — it's to be clever about routing: route reads to the primary for 1–2 seconds after a write, or track the latest written LSN and only route to a replica that has caught up to that LSN.

Diagram 1 · Three Replication Strategies — complete comparison

Single-leader, Multi-leader, Leaderless — when to use each

replication-strategies.svg
Animation · Async Replication Lag → Stale Read → Read-Your-Writes Fix

Write to primary, read from stale replica, then fix with read-your-writes routing

replication-lag-animation
Ready
Failover — what happens when the primary dies

Automatic failover is tricky — split-brain and data loss are real risks

When the primary fails, a replica must be promoted. This seems simple but has nasty edge cases:

The split-brain problem

The primary is partitioned from the replicas. It's not dead — just unreachable. Replicas elect a new primary. Now you have TWO primaries accepting writes. When the partition heals, both have diverged writes — conflict. Solution: fencing (STONITH — Shoot The Other Node In The Head). Before promoting a replica, the system must ensure the old primary can no longer accept writes. Done via STONITH fencing commands, TTL-based leader leases (old primary stops accepting writes when its lease expires), or consensus-based leader election (only one leader can hold the Raft/Paxos term at a time).

Async replication and data loss on failover

If the primary dies before its latest writes were replicated, those writes are lost when a replica is promoted. This is the durability vs availability trade-off. With synchronous replication: write is acknowledged only after at least one replica acks → zero data loss on failover, but write latency = round-trip to replica. With semi-sync (MySQL's solution): at least one replica must ack before the primary acknowledges the client → usually very low additional latency (~1ms intra-DC). With fully async: fastest writes, but potential data loss on failover. PostgreSQL's synchronous_commit = remote_write is a good balance: the replica must receive the WAL before the client gets OK, but doesn't need to flush to disk.

Quorum Reads and Writes — leaderless consistency

W + R > N guarantees reading at least one up-to-date replica

In leaderless systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB), you send writes to W nodes and reads to R nodes. The quorum condition W + R > N guarantees that the read set and write set overlap by at least one node. That overlapping node has the latest value. With N=3: W=2, R=2 → W+R=4 > 3 → overlap = 1 node minimum → always read the latest write.

Cassandra's tunable consistency levels

ALL: W or R must ack from all N replicas. Highest consistency, lowest availability. QUORUM: ack from majority (floor(N/2)+1). Balanced. LOCAL_QUORUM: quorum within the local datacenter only (for multi-DC). ONE: ack from any 1 replica. Lowest consistency, highest availability and lowest latency. The combination WRITE=QUORUM, READ=QUORUM is linearisable for N=3. WRITE=ANY, READ=ONE is eventually consistent.

Trade-offs

Sync vs async, and the consistency anomalies each strategy creates

replication-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — single-leader replication with lag simulation

Primary-replica setup, async lag, read-your-writes routing

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Dict
import threading, time, queue

# ── Replication log entry ──────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class RepEvent:
    lsn: int        # Log Sequence Number (monotonically increasing)
    key: str
    value: str

# ── Primary node ───────────────────────────────────────────
class Primary:
    def __init__(self):
        self._data: Dict[str, str] = {}
        self._lsn = 0
        self._replication_queue: queue.Queue = queue.Queue()
        self._replicas: list = []

    def add_replica(self, replica: 'Replica') -> None:
        self._replicas.append(replica)
        replica._replication_queue = self._replication_queue

    def write(self, key: str, value: str) -> int:
        # Apply locally
        self._lsn += 1
        self._data[key] = value
        # Enqueue for async replication (fire and forget)
        event = RepEvent(lsn=self._lsn, key=key, value=value)
        self._replication_queue.put(event)
        print(f"  Primary: wrote {key}={value} (LSN={self._lsn})")
        return self._lsn   # return LSN to client for read-your-writes

    def read(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        return self._data.get(key)

    def current_lsn(self) -> int:
        return self._lsn


# ── Replica node ───────────────────────────────────────────
class Replica:
    def __init__(self, replica_id: str, lag_ms: float = 200):
        self.replica_id = replica_id
        self._data: Dict[str, str] = {}
        self._applied_lsn = 0
        self._lag_ms = lag_ms   # simulated replication lag
        self._replication_queue: Optional[queue.Queue] = None
        # Start background replication thread
        t = threading.Thread(target=self._apply_loop, daemon=True)
        t.start()

    def _apply_loop(self):
        while True:
            if self._replication_queue:
                try:
                    event = self._replication_queue.get(timeout=0.1)
                    time.sleep(self._lag_ms / 1000)   # simulate lag
                    self._data[event.key] = event.value
                    self._applied_lsn = event.lsn
                    print(f"  {self.replica_id}: applied {event.key}={event.value} (LSN={event.lsn})")
                except queue.Empty:
                    pass

    def read(self, key: str, min_lsn: int = 0) -> Optional[str]:
        # Read-your-writes: wait until replica has caught up
        deadline = time.time() + 2.0
        while self._applied_lsn < min_lsn:
            if time.time() > deadline:
                print(f"  {self.replica_id}: timeout waiting for LSN={min_lsn}")
                return None
            time.sleep(0.01)
        return self._data.get(key)

    def read_stale(self, key: str) -> Optional[str]:
        # Read without LSN check — may return stale data
        return self._data.get(key)


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
primary = Primary()
replica = Replica("replica-1", lag_ms=300)
primary.add_replica(replica)

# Write to primary, immediately read from replica (stale!)
print("\n=== Without Read-Your-Writes ===")
written_lsn = primary.write("user_photo", "photo_url")
time.sleep(0.01)   # 10ms — replica hasn't caught up yet
stale = replica.read_stale("user_photo")
print(f"  Stale read from replica: {stale}")   # None — stale!

# Read with LSN tracking — waits for replica to catch up
print("\n=== With Read-Your-Writes (LSN tracking) ===")
written_lsn = primary.write("account_balance", "$1000")
fresh = replica.read("account_balance", min_lsn=written_lsn)
print(f"  Fresh read: {fresh}")   # '$1000' — correct!
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;

record RepEvent(long lsn, String key, String value) {}

// ── Primary ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Primary {
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, String> data = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final AtomicLong lsn = new AtomicLong(0);
    private final BlockingQueue<RepEvent> repQueue = new LinkedBlockingQueue<>();
    private final List<Replica> replicas = new ArrayList<>();

    public void addReplica(Replica r) {
        replicas.add(r);
        r.setQueue(repQueue);
    }

    public long write(String key, String value) {
        long currentLsn = lsn.incrementAndGet();
        data.put(key, value);
        repQueue.offer(new RepEvent(currentLsn, key, value));
        System.out.printf("  Primary: wrote %s=%s (LSN=%d)%n", key, value, currentLsn);
        return currentLsn;
    }

    public String read(String key) { return data.get(key); }
}

// ── Replica ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Replica {
    private final String id;
    private final long lagMs;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, String> data = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final AtomicLong appliedLsn = new AtomicLong(0);
    private volatile BlockingQueue<RepEvent> queue;

    public Replica(String id, long lagMs) {
        this.id = id; this.lagMs = lagMs;
        Thread.ofVirtual().start(this::applyLoop);
    }

    public void setQueue(BlockingQueue<RepEvent> q) { queue = q; }

    private void applyLoop() {
        while (!Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()) {
            try {
                if (queue == null) { Thread.sleep(10); continue; }
                RepEvent e = queue.poll(100, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
                if (e == null) continue;
                Thread.sleep(lagMs);   // simulate lag
                data.put(e.key(), e.value());
                appliedLsn.set(e.lsn());
                System.out.printf("  %s: applied %s=%s (LSN=%d)%n",
                    id, e.key(), e.value(), e.lsn());
            } catch (InterruptedException ex) { break; }
        }
    }

    /** Read-your-writes: waits until appliedLsn >= minLsn */
    public Optional<String> read(String key, long minLsn)
            throws InterruptedException {
        long deadline = System.currentTimeMillis() + 2000;
        while (appliedLsn.get() < minLsn) {
            if (System.currentTimeMillis() > deadline)
                return Optional.empty();
            Thread.sleep(10);
        }
        return Optional.ofNullable(data.get(key));
    }

    public Optional<String> readStale(String key) {
        return Optional.ofNullable(data.get(key));
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Primary primary = new Primary();
        Replica replica = new Replica("replica-1", 300);
        primary.addReplica(replica);

        System.out.println("=== Stale Read (no RYW) ===");
        long lsn1 = primary.write("photo", "url");
        Thread.sleep(10);   // replica still lagging
        System.out.println("  Stale: " + replica.readStale("photo"));
        // → Optional.empty  (stale!)

        System.out.println("\n=== Read-Your-Writes (LSN tracking) ===");
        long lsn2 = primary.write("balance", "$1000");
        System.out.println("  Fresh: " + replica.read("balance", lsn2));
        // → Optional[$1000] after lag resolves
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Replication

QWhat is replication lag and what consistency anomalies does it cause?
A
Replication lag is the delay between when a write is acknowledged by the primary and when it's applied to all replicas. In async single-leader replication, this is typically milliseconds intra-DC, hundreds of milliseconds to seconds cross-DC. Three key anomalies: (1) Read-Your-Writes violation — user writes data, then reads it back from a lagging replica and gets the old value. Feels like the write was lost. Fix: route user's reads to primary for 1–2s after their write, or track the written LSN and wait for the replica to catch up. (2) Monotonic reads violation — user reads the same data twice, gets a newer value then an older value (different replicas at different lag levels). Fix: route all reads for a session to the same replica. (3) Consistent prefix reads violation — observer sees updates out of causal order. Fix: writes causally related must be replicated in order (causal consistency).
QWhat is split-brain in replication and how do you prevent it?
A
Split-brain occurs when network partition separates the primary from its replicas. The replica-side promotes a new primary (automatic failover). The partition heals — both nodes now think they're primary and have accepted divergent writes. Prevention: (1) Fencing/STONITH — before promoting a new primary, send a "STONITH" command to physically shut down or fence the old primary, preventing it from accepting writes. (2) Lease-based leadership — the primary holds a lease with a TTL. If it doesn't renew, it stops accepting writes when the lease expires. New primary only starts accepting writes after the old lease expires. (3) Consensus-based leader election (Raft/Paxos) — only one leader can hold a given term. Any write from a leader with a stale term is rejected by Raft followers. This is how etcd and CockroachDB prevent split-brain entirely.
QWhat is quorum in leaderless replication? Derive W + R > N.
A
In leaderless replication with N replicas, a write is sent to W nodes and a read is sent to R nodes. For a read to always include at least one node that has the latest write, the write set and read set must overlap: W + R > N guarantees their intersection is at least 1. With N=3, W=2, R=2: any 2-node write set and any 2-node read set must share at least 1 node out of 3 (by pigeonhole: 2+2=4 > 3). That overlapping node has the latest value. The read reconciles the R responses by taking the value with the highest version number (timestamp or vector clock). Common configurations: (W=1, R=3) = fast writes, slow reads; (W=3, R=1) = slow writes, fast reads; (W=2, R=2) = balanced. Note: quorum doesn't guarantee linearisability if sloppy quorums are used (allowing writes to "hint" nodes outside the replication set when the intended nodes are unavailable).
QWhy would you choose multi-leader replication over single-leader?
A
Two scenarios where multi-leader wins: (1) Multi-datacenter — with single-leader, all writes must go to the leader's DC, adding cross-DC latency (50–200ms) to every write. With multi-leader, each DC has a local leader accepting writes at local latency. DCs sync with each other asynchronously. Users get low write latency regardless of their DC. The cost: conflict resolution when the same key is written in two DCs before they sync. (2) Offline or occasionally connected clients — CouchDB and Google Docs use multi-leader to let clients operate offline. Each client is a leader. When reconnected, changes are merged using CRDT-based conflict resolution. This is the only practical option for offline-first applications. The conflict resolution challenge: you need a strategy (last-write-wins, CRDT merge, custom merge function) for every writable field in your schema. This complexity is why multi-leader is avoided when single-leader suffices.
QPostgreSQL has both synchronous_commit and synchronous_standby_names. How do they work together?
A
synchronous_commit controls when a write is acknowledged to the client: local (default) = fsync to primary WAL only; remote_write = wait for replica to receive WAL in its OS buffer (no disk flush — fast, minimal data loss risk); remote_apply = wait for replica to apply the WAL (replica can serve consistent reads immediately); on = wait for replica to flush to disk (zero data loss, highest latency). synchronous_standby_names specifies which standbys participate in synchronous ack. Format: FIRST 1 (standby1, standby2) means at least 1 of the listed standbys must ack. ANY 2 (s1, s2, s3) means any 2 of 3 must ack. Together: set synchronous_standby_names = 'FIRST 1 (replica1)' and synchronous_commit = remote_write for a balance of data safety (no more than OS buffer loss on failure) and low latency (no disk flush on replica required).
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you design the replication strategy for a financial database?", say: "Single-leader with synchronous replication to at least one replica in the same DC, and async replication to a secondary DC. The sync replica ensures zero data loss on primary failure — if the primary crashes, the sync replica is immediately promotable with no data loss. Failover uses lease-based leadership: the primary holds a 30-second renewable lease. On failure, replicas wait for the lease to expire before electing a new primary, preventing split-brain. For reads: most queries go to async replicas for scale. For balance reads and anything needing read-your-writes: route to primary or use LSN-tracking to route to the sync replica once it's caught up. Cross-DC: the async replica in DC2 gives disaster recovery with RPO of ~1 second. For higher RPO requirements, add a second sync replica in DC2, but accept the write latency penalty of a cross-DC round-trip (~50ms)."

Post 13

Rate Limiting

25 min deep dive Token Bucket · Leaky Bucket · Fixed Window · Sliding Window · Distributed Rate Limiting · Redis
The Problem

How do you protect your service from being overwhelmed by too many requests?

Your API serves 10,000 paying customers. One customer's misconfigured script starts making 50,000 requests per second — 5× your entire cluster's capacity. Without rate limiting, this one customer starves every other customer and crashes your service. With rate limiting, their excess requests are rejected (429 Too Many Requests) while legitimate customers continue to be served.

Rate limiting is not just about protection from abuse — it also enables fair resource allocation, cost control (preventing surprise API bills), SLA enforcement, and graceful degradation under load. Every production API has rate limiting. The question is which algorithm to use and how to implement it correctly at scale.

Four Properties of a Good Rate Limiter

1. Accurate — doesn't allow more requests than the limit, doesn't reject valid requests near the boundary.
2. Fast — rate limit checks must not add significant latency to every request.
3. Distributed — works correctly across multiple API server instances (counters must be shared).
4. Flexible — supports per-user, per-IP, per-endpoint, global limits simultaneously.

Building Intuition — four algorithms, four trade-offs

Each algorithm makes a different trade-off between burst tolerance, memory, and accuracy

The Core Analogy: A Water Tank

Token Bucket: A bucket that fills with tokens at a fixed rate (e.g. 10/second). Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, reject. The bucket has a maximum capacity (burst size). This allows bursts up to bucket capacity, then throttles to the refill rate. Used by: AWS API Gateway, Stripe, most production systems.

Leaky Bucket: Requests flow in at any rate but flow out at a fixed rate (like water leaking from a bucket). This smooths traffic — no bursts. If the bucket overflows (input rate > leak rate), excess is dropped. Used by: traffic shaping, network QoS.

Fixed Window Counter: Count requests in a fixed time window (e.g. this minute). Reject if count > limit. Simple but has a boundary problem: a burst spanning two windows can double the effective limit. Used by: simple APIs where burst at window boundary is acceptable.

Sliding Window Log/Counter: Track the exact time of recent requests (log) or use a weighted average across windows (counter). More accurate than fixed window, no boundary problem. Higher memory (log) or slight inaccuracy (counter). Used by: Cloudflare, Nginx.

Diagram 1 · Four Rate Limiting Algorithms — behaviour under burst

Same burst traffic, four different outcomes — visualised

rate-limit-algorithms.svg
Animation · Token Bucket — drain, reject, refill step by step

5 tokens, burst drains bucket, request rejected, 1 second later token refills

token-bucket-animation
Ready
Distributed Rate Limiting — the hard part

Local counters give each server its own limit — you need a shared counter

If you have 10 API servers and a 100 req/min limit, local rate limiting gives each server a 100 req/min limit — so 10 servers allow 1000 req/min total. The fix: use a shared counter in Redis. All servers increment the same Redis key. The rate limiter check becomes an atomic Redis operation.

Redis sliding window log — the correct implementation

Use a Redis Sorted Set (ZSET) where each member is a unique request ID and the score is the timestamp. For each request: (1) Remove all members with score < (now - window_size) — expire old requests. (2) Count remaining members — if count ≥ limit, reject. (3) Add new member with score = now. (4) Set TTL on the key = window_size. This is O(log N) per operation and works across multiple servers. Use a Lua script to make steps 1–4 atomic.

The race condition without atomicity

Without atomicity: Server A reads count=99 (under limit). Server B reads count=99 (under limit). Both write count=100. Both accept their request. Result: 101 requests allowed past a 100/min limit. Solution: Redis Lua scripts execute atomically — no other command can interleave. Alternatively, use Redis's INCR + EXPIRE for fixed window (atomic increment), or the token bucket Lua script pattern.

Trade-offs — algorithm comparison

Choosing the right algorithm for your use case

rate-limit-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — Token bucket and sliding window with Redis

Production-ready implementations: atomic Lua scripts, per-user limits

PYTHON
import time, redis
from typing import Optional
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RateLimitResult:
    allowed: bool
    remaining: int
    retry_after_seconds: Optional[float] = None   # for 429 response header


# ── Token Bucket (Redis, atomic Lua) ──────────────────────
class TokenBucketLimiter:
    """
    Token bucket rate limiter backed by Redis.
    State per user: {tokens: float, last_refill: timestamp}
    All operations are atomic via Lua script.
    """

    # Lua script: atomically check/consume a token
    _SCRIPT = """
    local key        = KEYS[1]
    local capacity   = tonumber(ARGV[1])
    local rate       = tonumber(ARGV[2])   -- tokens per second
    local now        = tonumber(ARGV[3])
    local ttl        = tonumber(ARGV[4])

    local state = redis.call('HMGET', key, 'tokens', 'last_refill')
    local tokens    = tonumber(state[1]) or capacity
    local last_refill = tonumber(state[2]) or now

    -- Refill tokens based on elapsed time
    local elapsed = math.max(0, now - last_refill)
    tokens = math.min(capacity, tokens + elapsed * rate)

    local allowed = 0
    local retry_after = 0
    if tokens >= 1 then
        tokens = tokens - 1
        allowed = 1
    else
        -- How long until next token arrives?
        retry_after = (1 - tokens) / rate
    end

    redis.call('HMSET', key, 'tokens', tokens, 'last_refill', now)
    redis.call('EXPIRE', key, ttl)
    return {allowed, math.floor(tokens), retry_after}
    """

    def __init__(self, redis_client: redis.Redis,
                 capacity: int = 100,
                 rate: float = 10.0):  # 10 tokens/second
        self.r = redis_client
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.rate = rate
        self._script = self.r.register_script(self._SCRIPT)

    def check(self, user_id: str) -> RateLimitResult:
        key = f"rl:tb:{user_id}"
        now = time.time()
        ttl = int(self.capacity / self.rate) + 60
        result = self._script(
            keys=[key],
            args=[self.capacity, self.rate, now, ttl]
        )
        allowed, remaining, retry_after = result
        return RateLimitResult(
            allowed=bool(allowed),
            remaining=int(remaining),
            retry_after_seconds=float(retry_after) if not allowed else None
        )


# ── Sliding Window Log (Redis ZADD) ───────────────────────
class SlidingWindowLogLimiter:
    """
    Sliding window log. Most accurate — no boundary spike.
    Memory: O(limit) per user per window.
    """

    def __init__(self, redis_client: redis.Redis,
                 limit: int = 100, window_seconds: int = 60):
        self.r = redis_client
        self.limit = limit
        self.window = window_seconds

    def check(self, user_id: str) -> RateLimitResult:
        key = f"rl:swl:{user_id}"
        now = time.time()
        window_start = now - self.window

        pipe = self.r.pipeline(transaction=True)
        # Remove expired entries
        pipe.zremrangebyscore(key, 0, window_start)
        # Count current window
        pipe.zcard(key)
        # Add this request (score=timestamp, member=unique request id)
        pipe.zadd(key, {f"{now}:{id(pipe)}": now})
        # Set TTL
        pipe.expire(key, self.window + 1)
        _, count, _, _ = pipe.execute()

        if count >= self.limit:
            # Undo the add we just did
            self.r.zremrangebyscore(key, now, now + 1)
            return RateLimitResult(allowed=False, remaining=0,
                                        retry_after_seconds=self.window / 10)
        return RateLimitResult(allowed=True, remaining=self.limit - count - 1)


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)
limiter = TokenBucketLimiter(r, capacity=5, rate=1.0)

print("=== Token Bucket Demo (capacity=5, rate=1/s) ===")
for i in range(8):
    result = limiter.check("user:42")
    status = "✓" if result.allowed else f"✗ retry_after={result.retry_after_seconds:.1f}s"
    print(f"  req {i+1}: {status} (remaining={result.remaining})")
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;

// ── Rate Limit Result ──────────────────────────────────────
record RateLimitResult(boolean allowed, int remaining,
                          double retryAfterSeconds) {
    static RateLimitResult ok(int rem) {
        return new RateLimitResult(true, rem, 0);
    }
    static RateLimitResult reject(double retryAfter) {
        return new RateLimitResult(false, 0, retryAfter);
    }
}

// ── In-memory Token Bucket (local, per-user) ─────────────
// In production: replace AtomicReference with Redis Lua script
class TokenBucketLimiter {
    private record BucketState(double tokens, long lastRefillNanos) {}

    private final int     capacity;
    private final double  ratePerSec;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, AtomicReference<BucketState>>
        buckets = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public TokenBucketLimiter(int capacity, double ratePerSec) {
        this.capacity = capacity; this.ratePerSec = ratePerSec;
    }

    public RateLimitResult check(String userId) {
        var ref = buckets.computeIfAbsent(userId, k ->
            new AtomicReference<>(
                new BucketState(capacity, System.nanoTime())));

        while (true) {
            BucketState curr = ref.get();
            long   now     = System.nanoTime();
            double elapsed = (now - curr.lastRefillNanos()) / 1e9;
            double tokens  = Math.min(capacity,
                                curr.tokens() + elapsed * ratePerSec);

            if (tokens < 1) {
                double retry = (1 - tokens) / ratePerSec;
                return RateLimitResult.reject(retry);
            }

            BucketState next = new BucketState(tokens - 1, now);
            if (ref.compareAndSet(curr, next))   // atomic CAS
                return RateLimitResult.ok((int)(tokens - 1));
            // CAS failed → another thread updated — retry
        }
    }
}

// ── Sliding Window Counter (O(1), ~0.3% error) ────────────
class SlidingWindowCounterLimiter {
    /**
     * Weighted combination of current and previous window counters.
     * current_window * (elapsed / window) + prev_window * (1 - elapsed/window)
     * Approximates a sliding window. Error < 0.3%.
     */
    private final int   limit;
    private final long  windowMs;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, long[]> counters
        = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    // counters[userId] = [prevCount, currCount, windowStartMs]

    public SlidingWindowCounterLimiter(int limit, long windowMs) {
        this.limit = limit; this.windowMs = windowMs;
    }

    public synchronized RateLimitResult check(String userId) {
        long[] s = counters.computeIfAbsent(userId,
            k -> new long[]{0, 0, System.currentTimeMillis()});
        long   now     = System.currentTimeMillis();
        long   elapsed = now - s[2];

        if (elapsed >= windowMs) {   // advance window
            s[0] = (elapsed >= 2 * windowMs) ? 0 : s[1];
            s[1] = 0;
            s[2] = now;
            elapsed = 0;
        }

        double fraction = (double) elapsed / windowMs;
        double estimate = s[0] * (1 - fraction) + s[1];

        if (estimate >= limit)
            return RateLimitResult.reject((windowMs - elapsed) / 1000.0);

        s[1]++;
        return RateLimitResult.ok((int)(limit - estimate - 1));
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        var tb = new TokenBucketLimiter(5, 1.0);
        System.out.println("=== Token Bucket (capacity=5, rate=1/s) ===");
        for (int i = 1; i <= 8; i++) {
            RateLimitResult r = tb.check("user:42");
            System.out.printf("  req %d: %s (remaining=%d%s)%n",
                i,
                r.allowed() ? "✓" : "✗ 429",
                r.remaining(),
                r.allowed() ? "" : " retry_after="+String.format("%.1f", r.retryAfterSeconds())+"s");
        }
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Rate Limiting

QExplain the fixed window boundary problem and how sliding window fixes it.
A
Fixed window: reset counter every minute at :00. With a 100 req/min limit, an attacker can send 100 requests at 12:00:59 (last second of window) and 100 at 12:01:01 (first second of next window) — 200 requests in 2 seconds, double the stated limit. The counter correctly shows 100 for each window, but the actual rate was 100x the limit. Sliding window fixes this by counting requests in the last 60 seconds from "now" — the window moves with time. If 100 requests were made at 12:00:59, any new request before 12:01:59 sees 100 already in the window and is rejected. The count accurately reflects the actual rate over any 60-second period.
QHow do you implement a distributed rate limiter that works correctly across 10 API servers?
A
Use Redis as the shared counter. For a token bucket: store {tokens, last_refill_ts} as a Redis HASH per user. Use a Lua script for atomic check-and-consume — Lua scripts in Redis are atomic, so no two servers can race. For sliding window log: use a sorted set (ZSET) with request timestamps as scores. Atomic pipeline: ZREMRANGEBYSCORE (expire old), ZCARD (count), ZADD (add new), EXPIRE. The critical requirement: the check-and-consume must be atomic. Without Lua, a gap between CHECK and CONSUME allows race conditions where both operations read "under limit" and both proceed. Alternative: use Redis's INCR+EXPIRE for fixed window (INCR is atomic, and you call EXPIRE only on first increment). Performance: Redis Lua operations are sub-millisecond — acceptable overhead for rate limit checks.
QToken bucket vs leaky bucket — when would you use each?
A
Token bucket allows bursts up to the bucket capacity, then throttles to the refill rate. Use for API rate limiting where legitimate users occasionally burst (loading a dashboard makes 20 requests at once, then nothing for 10 minutes) — you want to allow the burst and smooth out sustained overuse. Leaky bucket smooths all traffic to a constant output rate regardless of input bursts. Use for traffic shaping where you need constant, predictable output — e.g. controlling how fast you make outbound API calls to a third party that can't handle bursts, or smoothing traffic on a network link. The choice: if you're protecting a service from clients, token bucket. If you're controlling your own outbound traffic to protect a downstream service, leaky bucket.
QWhat should a 429 response include? What headers matter?
A
A 429 Too Many Requests response should always include: (1) Retry-After: N — the number of seconds until the client can retry. Without this, clients will immediately retry and create a thundering herd. For token bucket: N = time until next token arrives (1/refill_rate seconds if bucket is empty). (2) X-RateLimit-Limit: 100 — the limit. (3) X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 — tokens/requests remaining in current window. (4) X-RateLimit-Reset: 1716382800 — Unix timestamp when the window resets. Include these on ALL responses (2xx too), not just 429 — clients should know how much budget they have left before they hit the limit, so they can self-throttle. GitHub's API returns all four headers on every response.
QHow do you implement rate limiting without Redis — what are the trade-offs?
A
Three alternatives to Redis: (1) Local in-process counter — works for single-server. For N servers, each allows N× the limit. Acceptable if N is small and you can tolerate N× burst (e.g. 10 servers, 10× burst allowed occasionally). Very fast — no network round trip. (2) Nginx rate limiting (limit_req_zone) — the reverse proxy handles rate limiting before requests reach your service. Good for IP-based rate limiting at the network edge. No code changes. Limited to IP-level granularity (not per-user). (3) Sticky sessions / consistent hashing — route each user's requests to the same server (by user ID hash). Each server can use local rate limiting. Works well with consistent hashing + session affinity. Breaks if servers fail (user routes to new server with empty counter). In practice: Redis is the production standard for user-level distributed rate limiting. Nginx is used for IP-level protection at the edge.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design a rate limiter for an API that has 10 servers and 100 req/min per user limit", say: "I'd use a token bucket backed by Redis. Each user has a Redis HASH key storing tokens (float) and last_refill_timestamp. On each request, I run a Lua script atomically: (1) compute elapsed time since last refill, (2) add elapsed × rate tokens (capped at capacity), (3) if tokens ≥ 1: decrement and allow, return remaining; (4) else: reject, return retry-after = (1 - tokens) / rate. The Lua script is atomic — no race conditions across 10 servers. Token bucket allows legitimate users to burst (posting a photo triggers 5 API calls at once) then throttles to the sustained rate. I'd return X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and Retry-After headers on every response. For additional protection at the edge, I'd add Nginx rate limiting per IP to block obvious scraping before it even reaches my servers."

Post 14

CRDTs — Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types

25 min deep dive G-Counter · PN-Counter · OR-Set · LWW-Register · Convergence · Merge Function · Shopping Cart
The Problem

How do you merge concurrent edits without a coordinator and without conflicts?

You have a shopping cart stored in two datacenters. A customer adds "shoes" from their phone (DC1). At the same moment, they add "jacket" from their laptop (DC2). A network partition separates the datacenters for 30 seconds. Both carts diverge. When the partition heals, how do you merge them?

Last-write-wins loses one item. Manual conflict resolution requires the user to see a "conflict" screen. These are bad user experiences. What if the data type itself knew how to merge? What if "add item to cart" was designed so that adding the same item twice gave the same result as adding it once, and that any ordering of concurrent additions produced the same final state?

The CRDT Solution

CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) are data structures where every operation is designed to be commutative, associative, and idempotent. These three properties guarantee that no matter what order replicas receive updates, and no matter how many times they receive the same update, they always converge to the same final state — without any coordinator, without any conflict resolution logic, without any user-visible conflict. The merge function is part of the data type's definition.

Building Intuition — the three properties that make CRDTs work

Commutative + Associative + Idempotent = automatic convergence

Why these three properties matter

Commutative: order doesn't matter. merge(A, B) = merge(B, A). If node 1 hears A then B, and node 2 hears B then A, they both end up with the same state. Required because messages arrive in different orders in distributed systems.

Associative: grouping doesn't matter. merge(merge(A, B), C) = merge(A, merge(B, C)). Required because different subsets of nodes may sync at different times before joining into a larger sync.

Idempotent: applying the same update twice gives the same result as applying it once. merge(A, A) = A. Required because in distributed systems, messages can be delivered multiple times (at-least-once delivery). An idempotent merge means duplicates are harmless.

The simplest example: the max() function is commutative (max(3,5)=max(5,3)=5), associative (max(max(3,5),7)=max(3,max(5,7))=7), and idempotent (max(5,5)=5). This is why G-Counter uses max() per node slot in its merge function.

Diagram 1 · G-Counter — grow-only counter with vector structure

Each node owns one slot. Increment only your slot. Value = sum of all slots.

g-counter.svg
Diagram 2 · CRDT Taxonomy — key types and what they're used for

Five CRDTs every senior engineer should know cold

crdt-types.svg
