        System.out.println("\n--- Safe with FOR UPDATE at READ COMMITTED ---");
        try (Connection safe = DriverManager.getConnection(url)) {
            safe.setAutoCommit(false);
            goOffCallSafe(safe, "Alice");
        }
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Isolation Levels and MVCC

QWhat are the four SQL isolation levels and which anomaly does each prevent?
A
READ UNCOMMITTED: no protection — allows dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads, write skew. Almost never used. READ COMMITTED: prevents dirty reads (only see committed data). Still allows non-repeatable reads (same query twice in same transaction returns different values), phantom reads (new rows appear), and write skew. PostgreSQL default. REPEATABLE READ: prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads. In PostgreSQL's MVCC implementation, also prevents phantom reads (takes a snapshot at transaction start, sees the same rows throughout). Still allows write skew. MySQL default. SERIALIZABLE: prevents all anomalies including write skew. PostgreSQL uses SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation) which detects dangerous read-write dependency cycles and aborts conflicting transactions. The application must retry on serialization failure (SQLSTATE 40001). The key practical point: most applications run at READ COMMITTED and are unknowingly vulnerable to non-repeatable reads and write skew bugs.
QWhat is MVCC and why does it allow readers to not block writers?
A
MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) stores multiple versions of each row simultaneously, each tagged with the transaction ID that created it (xmin) and the transaction that deleted it (xmax=0 if alive). When a transaction starts, it takes a "snapshot" recording which transaction IDs are currently active. When reading a row, PostgreSQL checks whether the row version was created before the snapshot start (xmin committed and < snapshot's xid) and not yet deleted (xmax=0 or not committed before snapshot). If both conditions hold, this version is visible. Readers see a consistent point-in-time snapshot without blocking. Writers create new row versions without touching the old ones — the old version remains visible to ongoing transactions. This is why "readers never block writers and writers never block readers" in PostgreSQL. The downside: dead tuples (old versions no longer needed by any snapshot) accumulate and must be cleaned by VACUUM. PostgreSQL's autovacuum runs continuously to handle this.
QWhat is write skew and how do you prevent it?
A
Write skew occurs when two concurrent transactions each read overlapping data, each decide to make a write based on what they read, and both commit — but the combined result violates an invariant that neither transaction individually violated. Classic examples: (1) two doctors simultaneously going off-call (hospital example above), (2) double-spending (two transactions both see sufficient balance, both debit), (3) double-booking a meeting room (both see it as available, both book it). Prevention options: (1) SERIALIZABLE isolation (PostgreSQL SSI): detects the dependency cycle and aborts one transaction. Application must retry on SQLSTATE 40001. (2) SELECT FOR UPDATE: explicitly lock the rows you're making a decision based on. This serialises concurrent decisions at the database level. Works at READ COMMITTED. (3) Optimistic locking with version numbers: include the version number in the WHERE clause of the UPDATE — only succeeds if no one else updated it. Retry on 0 rows affected. The most important thing: be aware that REPEATABLE READ does NOT prevent write skew despite being a high isolation level. Only SERIALIZABLE does.
QWhen should you use SELECT FOR UPDATE vs SERIALIZABLE isolation?
A
SELECT FOR UPDATE (pessimistic locking): explicitly locks specific rows, preventing other transactions from reading them with a lock until your transaction commits. Use when: the set of rows to lock is known and small (lock the specific inventory item, the specific meeting room), the operation is simple (read then update), and you want to avoid the complexity of retry logic for serialization failures. Tradeoff: creates lock waits that reduce concurrency. Deadlock possible if two transactions lock rows in different orders. SERIALIZABLE isolation (SSI in PostgreSQL): no explicit locking needed — PostgreSQL detects conflicts automatically. Use when: the predicate used to make the decision is complex or involves aggregates ("at least one doctor on-call" requires locking the entire result set of an aggregate, which is impractical with FOR UPDATE), or when the code is too complex to identify all the rows to lock. Tradeoff: requires retry logic for SQLSTATE 40001 throughout the application layer, and slightly lower concurrency than RC with targeted locking. A practical heuristic: use FOR UPDATE for simple inventory/booking patterns. Use SERIALIZABLE for complex multi-entity invariants.
QPostgreSQL defaults to READ COMMITTED. What bugs does this allow that developers often don't expect?
A
At READ COMMITTED, each statement in a transaction sees a fresh snapshot of committed data — not the snapshot at transaction start. This means: (1) Non-repeatable reads: if transaction A reads balance=100, B commits a debit, A reads again in the same transaction — now sees 90. A's two reads return different values. Breaks assumptions in batch processing code that reads data multiple times. (2) Write skew (as detailed above). Common bugs that arise: (a) Counting with an aggregate for a business rule then acting on it — another transaction commits between your count and your update. (b) Double-booking: SELECT 1 to check availability, INSERT to book — another transaction books it between your check and insert. Fix: use SELECT FOR UPDATE to lock, or use INSERT with a unique constraint and catch the constraint violation. (c) Lost updates: two transactions read the same counter, both increment locally, both write — one update is lost. Fix: use UPDATE counter = counter + 1 (single atomic SQL) instead of read-modify-write in application code.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "a booking system sometimes double-sells seats — how would you fix it?", say: "Double-selling happens when two transactions both check availability (see 1 seat remaining), both decide to book, both commit — a write skew. Three solutions in order of preference: (1) Use a unique constraint as the last line of defence: on the bookings table, (event_id, seat_number) must be unique. Both transactions try to INSERT the same seat — the second gets a unique constraint violation (not a serialization failure), handled with a simple retry or error. No lock contention. Best option for most booking systems. (2) SELECT FOR UPDATE on the seat record: SELECT * FROM seats WHERE id=42 AND available=true FOR UPDATE. The lock ensures only one transaction can check and update at a time. Second transaction waits, then re-reads to find available=false and rolls back cleanly. Works at READ COMMITTED. (3) SERIALIZABLE isolation: SSI detects the conflict and aborts one transaction. Application retries. Most correct but requires retry logic throughout the codebase. The unique constraint approach (option 1) is simplest and most robust — it's idempotent and doesn't add lock contention."

Post 23

Paxos Consensus

25 min deep dive Prepare · Promise · Accept · Accepted · Quorum · Multi-Paxos · Chubby · ZooKeeper · vs Raft
The Problem

How do you get a majority of nodes to agree on a single value despite crashes and message loss?

Three nodes must agree on a value. Node 1 wants to store "A". Node 2 wants to store "B". Messages may be delayed or lost. Any node may crash. If you let each node just broadcast its value and accept the majority, you still have races — what if node 1 votes for "A" at the same time node 2 votes for "B" and node 3 hasn't heard from anyone? The agreed value might change if a node crashes mid-protocol. Consensus — getting a set of nodes to irrevocably agree on a single value — is genuinely hard.

Why Paxos Matters

Paxos, invented by Leslie Lamport in 1989 and published 1998, was the first provably correct consensus algorithm for asynchronous networks. It underpins Google Chubby (lock service), Apache ZooKeeper, and many distributed databases. Raft (Post 01) was designed to be more understandable, but knowing Paxos gives you the deeper intuition: any value that achieves a quorum of promises and acceptances is permanently decided — even if the proposer crashes after sending only some Accept messages.

Building Intuition — why two phases?

Phase 1 establishes authority. Phase 2 commits the value. Each phase needs a quorum.

Phase 1 — Prepare/Promise: "I'm going to propose. Please don't accept anything older."

A proposer picks a proposal number n (must be higher than any it has seen before). It sends Prepare(n) to all acceptors. An acceptor that hasn't seen any proposal ≥ n responds with Promise(n) — a commitment to not accept any proposal with number < n. If the acceptor previously accepted a value, it includes that (n_prev, value_prev) in its Promise so the proposer knows what was previously accepted. If a quorum (majority) of acceptors promise, the proposer can proceed to Phase 2. Critical: if any acceptor returns a previously accepted value, the proposer must use that value (to preserve any value that might already be decided).

Phase 2 — Accept/Accepted: "Please accept this value."

The proposer sends Accept(n, v) — where v is either the value it wants to propose (if no previous value was returned in Phase 1) or the highest-numbered previously-accepted value from Phase 1 (to preserve already-decided values). If a quorum of acceptors respond with Accepted(n, v), the value v is decided. The key safety property: once a value is decided (accepted by a quorum), any future proposer that runs Phase 1 and gets promises will discover the decided value and must re-propose it — ensuring only one value is ever decided.

Diagram 1 · Paxos Two Phases — complete message flow with one crashed acceptor

3 acceptors, 1 crashes — quorum of 2 still decides "X" safely

paxos-protocol.svg
Animation · Paxos with crashed node — two phases, quorum of 2 decides

Step-by-step: A3 crashes, proposer gets promises from A1+A2, value "X" decided

paxos-animation
Ready
Multi-Paxos and Paxos vs Raft

Single-decree Paxos decides one value. Multi-Paxos is the production algorithm.

Multi-Paxos — skip Phase 1 after leader election

Single-decree Paxos decides exactly one value. A real distributed log needs to decide thousands of values per second (one per log entry). Multi-Paxos: once a leader successfully completes Phase 1 and gets a quorum of promises, it skips Phase 1 for all subsequent log entries (as long as it remains leader). It just sends Phase 2 (Accept) directly. This requires a stable leader — a separate leader election protocol ensures only one proposer runs at a time. This is the basis for Google Chubby and ZooKeeper (which uses a Paxos variant called ZAB — ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast).

Paxos vs Raft — same safety, different design philosophy

Both guarantee the same safety properties. The differences: (1) Leader election: Raft has an explicit leader election (term-based, random timeout) baked into the protocol. Paxos leaves leader election as a separate concern. (2) Log holes: Paxos allows "holes" in the log (slot 5 decided before slot 4). Raft requires strict sequential log commitment. (3) Membership changes: Raft has a built-in joint consensus algorithm. Paxos requires careful protocol extensions. (4) Understandability: Raft was explicitly designed to be understandable — Diego Ongaro's thesis title was "In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm". Production choice: most new systems use Raft (etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV). Legacy systems built on Paxos (Chubby, ZooKeeper) remain in use.

Code · Python & Java — Single-decree Paxos simulation

Proposer, Acceptor, and Learner — the complete Paxos message exchange

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, List, Tuple
import random

# ── Messages ───────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Prepare:
    n: int                  # proposal number (ballot)

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Promise:
    n: int                  # the n we're promising to respect
    accepted_n: Optional[int]   # previously accepted proposal number
    accepted_v: Optional[str]   # previously accepted value (if any)

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Accept:
    n: int; v: str

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Accepted:
    n: int; v: str; acceptor_id: int


# ── Acceptor ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Acceptor:
    """
    An acceptor stores its promise (highest n promised) and
    the last accepted (n, v). Implements the two Paxos rules:
    1. Only accept Prepare(n) if n > highest_n_promised
    2. Only accept Accept(n, v) if n >= highest_n_promised
    """
    def __init__(self, aid: int, alive: bool = True):
        self.id               = aid
        self.alive            = alive
        self.highest_n        = 0   # highest proposal number promised
        self.accepted_n: Optional[int] = None
        self.accepted_v: Optional[str] = None

    def receive_prepare(self, msg: Prepare) -> Optional[Promise]:
        if not self.alive: return None   # crashed — no response
        if msg.n > self.highest_n:
            self.highest_n = msg.n
            print(f"  A{self.id}: promised n={msg.n} (prev_accepted: {self.accepted_n}, {self.accepted_v})")
            return Promise(n=msg.n, accepted_n=self.accepted_n, accepted_v=self.accepted_v)
        print(f"  A{self.id}: NACK prepare n={msg.n} (already promised {self.highest_n})")
        return None

    def receive_accept(self, msg: Accept) -> Optional[Accepted]:
        if not self.alive: return None
        if msg.n >= self.highest_n:
            self.highest_n  = msg.n
            self.accepted_n = msg.n
            self.accepted_v = msg.v
            print(f"  A{self.id}: accepted n={msg.n} v={msg.v!r}")
            return Accepted(n=msg.n, v=msg.v, acceptor_id=self.id)
        print(f"  A{self.id}: NACK accept n={msg.n} (promised {self.highest_n})")
        return None


# ── Proposer ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Proposer:
    def __init__(self, pid: int, acceptors: List[Acceptor]):
        self.id        = pid
        self.acceptors = acceptors
        self.n         = pid   # start with unique n based on proposer id

    def propose(self, value: str) -> Optional[str]:
        """
        Run single-decree Paxos to decide a value.
        Returns the decided value (may differ from proposed if
        a previous round already decided something).
        """
        quorum = len(self.acceptors) // 2 + 1

        # Phase 1: Prepare
        self.n += 10   # ensure n is higher than previous rounds
        print(f"\nP{self.id}: Phase 1 — Prepare(n={self.n})")
        prepare = Prepare(n=self.n)
        promises = [a.receive_prepare(prepare) for a in self.acceptors]
        promises = [p for p in promises if p is not None]

        if len(promises) < quorum:
            print(f"P{self.id}: failed to get quorum for Prepare ({len(promises)}/{quorum})")
            return None

        # If any promise contains a previously accepted value, use it
        prev_accepted = [p for p in promises if p.accepted_n is not None]
        if prev_accepted:
            # Use value from highest accepted proposal number
            best = max(prev_accepted, key=lambda p: p.accepted_n)
            value = best.accepted_v
            print(f"P{self.id}: found prev accepted v={value!r} — must use it")

        # Phase 2: Accept
        print(f"P{self.id}: Phase 2 — Accept(n={self.n}, v={value!r})")
        accept = Accept(n=self.n, v=value)
        accepted_msgs = [a.receive_accept(accept) for a in self.acceptors]
        accepted_msgs = [m for m in accepted_msgs if m is not None]

        if len(accepted_msgs) >= quorum:
            print(f"P{self.id}: DECIDED v={value!r} (accepted by {len(accepted_msgs)}/{len(self.acceptors)}) ✓")
            return value

        print(f"P{self.id}: failed to get quorum for Accept")
        return None


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=== Paxos: 3 acceptors, A3 crashed, Proposer wants v='X' ===")
acceptors = [Acceptor(1), Acceptor(2), Acceptor(3, alive=False)]
proposer  = Proposer(1, acceptors)
decided   = proposer.propose("X")
# → DECIDED v='X' (accepted by 2/3) — quorum achieved despite A3 crash ✓
JAVA
import java.util.*;

// ── Messages ───────────────────────────────────────────────
record Prepare(int n) {}
record Promise(int n, Integer prevN, String prevV) {}
record Accept(int n, String v) {}
record Accepted(int n, String v, int acceptorId) {}

// ── Acceptor ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Acceptor {
    final int      id;
    final boolean alive;
    int            highestN   = 0;
    Integer        acceptedN  = null;
    String         acceptedV  = null;

    Acceptor(int id, boolean alive) { this.id = id; this.alive = alive; }

    Optional<Promise> prepare(Prepare msg) {
        if (!alive) return Optional.empty();
        if (msg.n() > highestN) {
            highestN = msg.n();
            System.out.printf("  A%d: promised n=%d%n", id, msg.n());
            return Optional.of(new Promise(msg.n(), acceptedN, acceptedV));
        }
        System.out.printf("  A%d: NACK prepare%n", id);
        return Optional.empty();
    }

    Optional<Accepted> accept(Accept msg) {
        if (!alive) return Optional.empty();
        if (msg.n() >= highestN) {
            highestN = msg.n(); acceptedN = msg.n(); acceptedV = msg.v();
            System.out.printf("  A%d: accepted n=%d v=%s%n", id, msg.n(), msg.v());
            return Optional.of(new Accepted(msg.n(), msg.v(), id));
        }
        return Optional.empty();
    }
}

// ── Proposer ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Proposer {
    private int              n;
    private final List<Acceptor> acceptors;

    Proposer(int startN, List<Acceptor> acceptors) {
        this.n = startN; this.acceptors = acceptors;
    }

    Optional<String> propose(String value) {
        int quorum = acceptors.size() / 2 + 1;
        n += 10;

        // Phase 1: Prepare
        System.out.println("\nPhase 1 — Prepare(n=" + n + ")");
        List<Promise> promises = acceptors.stream()
            .map(a -> a.prepare(new Prepare(n)))
            .filter(Optional::isPresent).map(Optional::get)
            .toList();

        if (promises.size() < quorum) { System.out.println("No quorum"); return Optional.empty(); }

        // If any promise has prev accepted value, must use highest
        String v = promises.stream()
            .filter(p -> p.prevN() != null)
            .max(Comparator.comparingInt(Promise::prevN))
            .map(Promise::prevV)
            .orElse(value);  // use our value if no previous accepted

        // Phase 2: Accept
        System.out.println("Phase 2 — Accept(n=" + n + ", v=" + v + ")");
        long acceptedCount = acceptors.stream()
            .map(a -> a.accept(new Accept(n, v)))
            .filter(Optional::isPresent).count();

        if (acceptedCount >= quorum) {
            System.out.println("DECIDED: " + v + " ✓");
            return Optional.of(v);
        }
        return Optional.empty();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Acceptor> acceptors = List.of(
            new Acceptor(1, true), new Acceptor(2, true),
            new Acceptor(3, false)  // crashed
        );
        new Proposer(1, acceptors).propose("X");
        // → DECIDED: X ✓  (quorum of 2 from A1+A2)
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Paxos

QExplain the two phases of Paxos and why both are needed.
A
Phase 1 (Prepare/Promise) establishes the proposer's authority and discovers any previously decided value. The proposer sends Prepare(n) with a proposal number n higher than any it's seen. Acceptors that haven't promised a higher n respond with Promise(n) — a commitment not to accept any proposal with number < n. If any acceptor previously accepted a value, it includes (prev_n, prev_v) in its Promise. Phase 1 is needed because a previous proposer might have decided a value already. By collecting promises, the new proposer learns about any previously decided values and must honor them. Phase 2 (Accept/Accepted) actually commits the value. If any promise contained a previously accepted value, the proposer uses that value (not its own). Otherwise it uses its own proposed value. It sends Accept(n, v) to all acceptors. Acceptors that haven't promised a higher n respond with Accepted. If a quorum responds, the value is decided. Phase 2 is needed because promises alone don't commit — you need explicit acceptances to form the quorum that makes a value irrevocable. The two-phase structure ensures that a decided value survives any combination of node failures and message losses, as long as a majority of nodes eventually communicate.
QWhy must a proposer use a previously accepted value from Phase 1 instead of its own proposed value?
A
This is the core safety invariant of Paxos. If a proposer received a Promise containing (prev_n, prev_v), it means that prev_v was accepted by at least one acceptor in a previous round. That previous round may have achieved a quorum — the value prev_v may already be decided. If the new proposer were to use its own value and achieved a new quorum, two different values would both be decided, violating the safety property (consensus on a single value). By using the highest previously-accepted value instead of its own, the proposer ensures it's not breaking any existing decision. If prev_v was already decided, the new round just re-confirms the same decided value. If prev_v was not decided (the previous round failed before achieving a quorum), the new round either commits prev_v or is overridden by an even newer round that will do the same analysis. This "takeover" of a previous proposal is what allows Paxos to make progress even when proposers crash mid-protocol.
QWhat is the liveness problem with Paxos and how does Multi-Paxos solve it?
A
Liveness problem (dueling proposers): Proposer P1 completes Phase 1 with n=5. Before it can complete Phase 2, Proposer P2 runs Phase 1 with n=10. Now P1's Phase 2 is rejected (acceptors promised n=10). P1 retries with n=15. Before P2 completes its Phase 2, P1's n=15 round causes P2 to fail. This cycle can repeat indefinitely — two proposers can prevent each other from making progress, a livelock. Paxos provides safety (once decided, always decided) but not guaranteed liveness (might never decide anything). Solution: elect a single distinguished proposer (the leader). Only the leader runs Paxos. Competitors back off and wait. This requires a separate leader election mechanism. Multi-Paxos: once a leader successfully completes Phase 1 for a given epoch, it skips Phase 1 for all subsequent log entries — only Phase 2 for each new entry. This makes Multi-Paxos much more efficient: 1 round trip per log entry instead of 2, as long as the leader is stable. Leader changes restart the Phase 1 process for the next epoch.
QHow does Paxos differ from Raft? Which would you choose for a new system?
A
Both provide the same fundamental safety guarantees. Differences: Raft has a built-in leader election algorithm (randomised timeout, term-based). Paxos leaves leader election as a separate concern, which creates flexibility but also implementation complexity. Raft requires a strict sequential log — no gaps. Paxos allows "holes" in the log (entry 5 can be decided before entry 4), requiring a filling mechanism. This makes Paxos slightly more flexible but harder to implement correctly. Raft provides a clean algorithm for log compaction (snapshots) and cluster membership changes. Paxos requires careful extensions for both. Understandability: Raft was explicitly designed to be understood and implemented correctly — the paper includes a comprehensive correctness argument and a formal specification (TLA+). Paxos is notoriously hard to implement correctly from the paper alone. For a new system: use Raft. Libraries like etcd/raft (Go) and Apache Ratis (Java) provide production-grade Raft implementations. Use Paxos (via ZooKeeper or a custom implementation) only if you're integrating with an existing Paxos-based infrastructure.
QCan Paxos tolerate Byzantine faults (malicious/corrupted nodes)?
A
No. Standard Paxos (and Raft) assumes crash-fault tolerance (CFT): nodes either follow the protocol correctly or stop responding (crash). It cannot tolerate Byzantine faults where a node sends arbitrary or malicious messages — a Byzantine node could promise to multiple proposers simultaneously with different values, break the invariant that a Promise is a real commitment. For Byzantine fault tolerance you need BFT (Byzantine Fault Tolerant) consensus algorithms like PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance), Tendermint (used in blockchains), or Bullshark. BFT requires 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine nodes (vs 2f+1 for CFT). The overhead is much higher (more message rounds, more nodes required). BFT is used primarily in: public blockchains (where nodes are untrusted participants), financial systems where cryptographic verification of each step is required, and critical infrastructure requiring protection against insider threats. For most distributed databases and coordination services (where you control all nodes), CFT with crash fault tolerance is sufficient and far more performant.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "explain how Paxos achieves consensus", say: "Paxos runs in two phases. Phase 1: the proposer picks a ballot number n higher than any it's seen and broadcasts Prepare(n) to all acceptors. Acceptors that haven't promised a higher n respond with Promise(n) — a commitment not to accept anything with ballot < n. If any acceptor previously accepted a value, it includes it in the Promise. If the proposer gets a majority of Promises, it picks the value: either the highest-numbered previously-accepted value from the Promises (to preserve any existing decision) or its own value if no previous acceptances. Phase 2: the proposer sends Accept(n, chosen_value) to all acceptors. Acceptors that haven't promised a higher n respond with Accepted. If a majority Accepts, the value is decided. The key safety insight: once value V achieves a quorum of Accepted, any future proposer running Phase 1 will discover V from at least one Promise and must re-propose V. Two different values can never both achieve quorum decisions. Multi-Paxos skips Phase 1 while the leader is stable — just one round-trip per log entry. For new systems I'd use Raft, which makes all of this more prescriptive and easier to implement correctly."

Post 24

Leader Election Patterns

25 min deep dive Heartbeat · Bully Algorithm · ZooKeeper Ephemeral Nodes · Epoch · Split-Brain · Fencing · etcd
The Problem

How do you reliably elect exactly one leader — and ensure the old leader stops acting when a new one takes over?

Many distributed systems require exactly one active coordinator at a time: one Kafka partition leader accepting writes, one database primary, one cron job runner. When the current leader fails, the system must detect the failure, elect a new leader, and — critically — ensure the old leader can no longer act as leader even if it recovers from a partition.

Two problems are harder than they appear: (1) Failure detection: you can't distinguish a crashed node from a slow one or a network partition. Aggressive timeouts cause false positives and unnecessary re-elections. Slow timeouts mean long outages. (2) Split-brain prevention: during network partitions, both the isolated old leader and the newly elected leader may believe they're the true leader simultaneously. They both accept writes. When the partition heals, data is corrupted.

The Core Requirement

A correct leader election algorithm must provide: Safety — at most one leader at any time. Liveness — eventually a leader is elected. Validity — the elected leader is an alive node. Safety is more critical than liveness — a brief period with no leader (liveness failure) is recoverable. A period with two leaders (safety failure) corrupts data.

Building Intuition — three patterns

Heartbeat + timeout, consensus-based, and ZooKeeper ephemeral nodes

Pattern 1 — Heartbeat + Timeout (simple, split-brain risk)

Each leader sends heartbeats to followers every T seconds. If followers don't hear a heartbeat in T×k seconds, they assume the leader is dead and start an election. Simple but problematic: GC pauses, network congestion, or high CPU load can cause missed heartbeats without the leader actually being dead. Each false positive triggers an unnecessary re-election. More dangerously, if the leader is partitioned (not dead), it still believes it's leader while followers elect a new one — split-brain.

Pattern 2 — Consensus-based (etcd/Raft, safe, correct)

Use etcd leases: the leader writes a key with a TTL, renewing it periodically. Other nodes try to create the same key — only one succeeds (compare-and-swap). If the leader fails to renew before TTL, the key expires and another node immediately acquires it. Split-brain prevention: Raft's term mechanism ensures old leaders stop accepting writes when they discover a new term. The key is always held by exactly one node at a time — guaranteed by etcd's linearisable CAS.

Pattern 3 — ZooKeeper Ephemeral Nodes (elegant, battle-tested)

Each node creates an ephemeral sequential znode under /election. Ephemeral means ZooKeeper automatically deletes the node when the session disconnects. Each node watches the znode with the next-lower sequence number (not all znodes — this prevents herd effect). The node holding the lowest-numbered znode is the leader. When the leader dies, its session expires, ZooKeeper deletes its znode, and only the node watching it is notified — clean O(1) notification.

Diagram 1 · ZooKeeper Leader Election — ephemeral sequential znodes

Three nodes compete, lowest znode wins, each node watches its predecessor

zookeeper-leader-election.svg
Animation · ZooKeeper Leader Failover — session expires, watch fires, N2 elected

Step-by-step: N1 crashes, session times out, N2 notified via watch, promotes to leader

leader-election-animation
Ready
Split-Brain and Fencing — the hardest part

When two nodes think they're leader simultaneously — and how to prevent data corruption

The split-brain scenario

Leader L1 is partitioned from followers. After the timeout, followers elect L2. L1 is still running — it believes it's the leader and accepts writes. L2 also accepts writes. When the partition heals, the write logs have diverged. Without additional protection, last-writer-wins will silently drop one set of committed writes.

Fencing tokens — the correct solution

Every time a leader is elected, it receives a monotonically increasing fencing token (epoch number) from the election service. The leader includes this token in every storage operation. The storage layer tracks the maximum epoch it has seen and rejects any write with a lower epoch. If L1 (epoch=3) tries to write after L2 (epoch=4) is elected, the storage rejects L1's writes (epoch 3 < 4). No data corruption. This is the same mechanism described in Post 09 (Distributed Locking). The key: correctness is enforced by the storage layer, not by the lock/election mechanism's timing.

STONITH — Shoot The Other Node In The Head

Before a new leader starts serving, it must ensure the old leader is truly dead. STONITH (also called node fencing) sends a hardware-level kill command to the old leader via a separate out-of-band channel (IPMI/iDRAC, PDU power cut, hypervisor kill). Only after the STONITH command succeeds does the new leader start. Brutal but effective. Used in database HA solutions like Pacemaker+Corosync.

Trade-offs — comparing election approaches

Choose by your correctness requirements and infrastructure

leader-election-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — etcd lease-based leader election

Acquire leadership with TTL lease, renew heartbeat, release on shutdown

PYTHON
# pip install etcd3  (grpcio + etcd3gw)
import etcd3, threading, time, logging
from typing import Callable, Optional

class EtcdLeaderElection:
    """
    Leader election using etcd leases.
    Only one node can hold the leader key at a time (CAS).
    Leader must renew its lease before TTL expires.
    On failure, lease expires and another node wins.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        client: etcd3.Etcd3Client,
        election_key: str = "/election/leader",
        node_id: str = "node-1",
        ttl_seconds: int = 10,
        on_elected: Optional[Callable] = None,
        on_demoted: Optional[Callable] = None,
    ):
        self.client       = client
        self.election_key = election_key
        self.node_id      = node_id
        self.ttl          = ttl_seconds
        self.on_elected   = on_elected   # called when this node becomes leader
        self.on_demoted   = on_demoted   # called when this node loses leadership

        self._is_leader   = False
        self._lease       = None
        self._stop_event  = threading.Event()

    def start(self) -> None:
        """Start campaigning for leadership in a background thread."""
        t = threading.Thread(target=self._campaign_loop, daemon=True)
        t.start()

    def stop(self) -> None:
        self._stop_event.set()
        if self._lease:
            try: self._lease.revoke()   # immediately release leadership
            except: pass

    @property
    def is_leader(self) -> bool:
        return self._is_leader

    def _campaign_loop(self) -> None:
        while not self._stop_event.is_set():
            try:
                self._try_become_leader()
            except Exception as e:
                logging.warning(f"Election error: {e}")
                time.sleep(1)

    def _try_become_leader(self) -> None:
        # Grant a lease with TTL
        self._lease = self.client.lease(self.ttl)

        # Try to create the leader key (CAS: only if key doesn't exist)
        success = self.client.put_if_not_exists(
            self.election_key,
            self.node_id.encode(),
            lease=self._lease
        )

        if success:
            # Became leader!
            self._is_leader = True
            print(f"{self.node_id}: LEADER (epoch from lease ID: {self._lease.id})")
            if self.on_elected: self.on_elected()

            # Keep refreshing the lease while we're leader
            self._keep_alive_loop()

            # Reached here means lease expired or we stopped
            if self._is_leader:
                self._is_leader = False
                print(f"{self.node_id}: DEMOTED")
                if self.on_demoted: self.on_demoted()
        else:
            # Another node holds leadership. Watch for changes.
            print(f"{self.node_id}: follower — waiting for leader key to expire")
            self._lease.revoke()  # don't need a lease if not leader
            self._watch_leader_key()

    def _keep_alive_loop(self) -> None:
        """Renew lease periodically to maintain leadership."""
        renew_interval = self.ttl / 3   # renew at 1/3 of TTL for safety margin
        while not self._stop_event.is_set() and self._is_leader:
            try:
                self._lease.refresh()
                time.sleep(renew_interval)
            except Exception:
                # Renewal failed — lease may have expired
                self._is_leader = False
                break

    def _watch_leader_key(self) -> None:
        """Wait for the leader key to disappear, then re-campaign."""
        for event, _ in self.client.watch_prefix(self.election_key):
            if self._stop_event.is_set(): return
            if isinstance(event, etcd3.events.DeleteEvent):
                print(f"{self.node_id}: leader key deleted — campaigning...")
                return   # re-campaign


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
def run_demo():
    client = etcd3.client()

    election1 = EtcdLeaderElection(
        client, node_id="node-1", ttl_seconds=5,
        on_elected=lambda: print("node-1: starting work as leader"),
        on_demoted=lambda: print("node-1: stopping work")
    )
    election2 = EtcdLeaderElection(
        client, node_id="node-2", ttl_seconds=5,
        on_elected=lambda: print("node-2: taking over as leader")
    )

    election1.start()
    election2.start()
    time.sleep(3)

    print("\nSimulating node-1 failure...")
    election1.stop()   # revokes lease → node-2 wins on next election
    time.sleep(7)      # wait for TTL + node-2 to notice
    election2.stop()
JAVA
// Gradle: io.etcd:jetcd-core:0.7.7
import io.etcd.jetcd.*;
import io.etcd.jetcd.lease.*;
import io.etcd.jetcd.op.*;
import io.etcd.jetcd.options.*;
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;
import io.etcd.jetcd.ByteSequence;

class EtcdLeaderElection implements AutoCloseable {
    private final Client     etcd;
    private final String     nodeId;
    private final String     leaderKey;
    private final int        ttlSeconds;
    private final AtomicBoolean isLeader = new AtomicBoolean(false);
    private volatile long    leaseId;
    private volatile CloseableClient keepAliveClient;
    private final ScheduledExecutorService scheduler =
        Executors.newSingleThreadScheduledExecutor();

    public EtcdLeaderElection(Client etcd, String nodeId,
                               String leaderKey, int ttlSeconds) {
        this.etcd       = etcd;
        this.nodeId     = nodeId;
        this.leaderKey  = leaderKey;
        this.ttlSeconds = ttlSeconds;
    }

    public void campaign() {
        scheduler.scheduleWithFixedDelay(this::tryBecomeLeader,
            0, 1, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
    }

    private void tryBecomeLeader() {
        try {
            Lease lease = etcd.getLeaseClient();
            LeaseGrantResponse grant = lease.grant(ttlSeconds).get();
            leaseId = grant.getID();

            ByteSequence key = ByteSequence.from(leaderKey, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
            ByteSequence val = ByteSequence.from(nodeId,    StandardCharsets.UTF_8);

            // Atomic CAS: only PUT if key doesn't exist
            Txn txn = etcd.getKVClient().txn();
            TxnResponse txnResp = txn
                .If(CmpTarget.createRevision(key).equals(0))  // key doesn't exist
                .Then(Op.put(key, val, PutOption.newBuilder()
                    .withLeaseId(leaseId).build()))
                .commit().get();

            if (txnResp.isSucceeded()) {
                isLeader.set(true);
                System.out.println(nodeId + ": LEADER (leaseId=" + leaseId + ")");
                // Keep-alive: etcd auto-refreshes the lease
                keepAliveClient = lease.keepAlive(leaseId, new StreamObserver<>() {
                    public void onNext(LeaseKeepAliveResponse r)  {}
                    public void onError(Throwable t) { isLeader.set(false); }
                    public void onCompleted()           { isLeader.set(false); }
                });
                scheduler.shutdown();   // stop campaigning once leader
            } else {
                // Another node is leader — revoke unused lease
                lease.revoke(leaseId).get();
                System.out.println(nodeId + ": follower");
            }
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.err.println(nodeId + ": campaign error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }

    public boolean isLeader() { return isLeader.get(); }

    public void close() {
        isLeader.set(false);
        scheduler.shutdownNow();
        if (keepAliveClient != null) keepAliveClient.close();
        try { etcd.getLeaseClient().revoke(leaseId).get(); }
        catch (Exception ignored) {}
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Client etcd = Client.builder().endpoints("http://localhost:2379").build();
        var node1 = new EtcdLeaderElection(etcd, "node-1", "/election/leader", 5);
        var node2 = new EtcdLeaderElection(etcd, "node-2", "/election/leader", 5);
        node1.campaign(); node2.campaign();
        Thread.sleep(3000);
        System.out.println("Simulating node-1 crash");
        node1.close();     // revokes lease → node-2 wins
        Thread.sleep(7000);
        System.out.println("node-2 is leader: " + node2.isLeader());
        node2.close(); etcd.close();
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Leader Election

QWhat is split-brain and why is it dangerous?
A
Split-brain occurs when a network partition causes two nodes to simultaneously believe they are the leader. The original leader is partitioned from followers. After the timeout, followers elect a new leader. The original leader is still running, believes it's the leader, and accepts writes. The new leader also accepts writes. Both write to the same resource with diverging data. When the partition heals, both logs are inconsistent — merging them without data loss is impossible without additional state. This is dangerous because: it silently corrupts data (no error is raised during the partition), and depending on the reconciliation strategy, committed writes from one leader will be discarded. Prevention: fencing tokens (storage rejects writes with stale epoch), STONITH (kill old leader before new one starts), or consensus-based election (Raft terms ensure stale leaders stop accepting writes).
QHow does ZooKeeper ephemeral node-based election work, and why does each node watch only its predecessor?
A
Each node creates an ephemeral sequential znode under /election (e.g. n-0001, n-0002, n-0003). Ephemeral means ZooKeeper deletes the node when the creator's session disconnects (crash, network partition). Sequential means each node gets a unique monotonically increasing number. The node with the lowest sequence number is the leader. Each node watches the znode with the next-lower number, not all znodes. When the leader (n-0001) dies, only n-0002 is notified. n-0002 reads the children list, finds n-0002 is now lowest, and becomes leader. n-0003 continues watching n-0002. This watch-predecessor design is critical: with N nodes, a leader failure generates exactly 1 notification (to the next node) rather than N notifications (a "thundering herd"). If all N nodes watched the leader, a single failure would cause N-1 nodes to simultaneously wake up, all read the znode list, all discover they're not the new leader — pointless load on ZooKeeper.
QHow does etcd lease-based leader election prevent split-brain?
A
etcd provides linearisable reads and writes backed by Raft consensus. The leader election key is created with a lease TTL. The compare-and-swap (CAS) operation — "create this key only if it doesn't already exist" — is linearisable: at most one node can win the CAS at any moment, even with concurrent attempts from multiple nodes. The Raft log ensures all etcd nodes agree on who won the CAS. The leader must renew its lease by refreshing the TTL periodically. If the leader fails to renew (crash, GC pause, network partition), the lease expires, the key is deleted, and another node can win the CAS. Split-brain prevention: if the original leader is partitioned and can't renew the lease, its lease expires and it loses ownership. Even if it doesn't know this, the next leader wins the CAS and gets a new epoch. If the partitioned old leader comes back and tries to write to storage with its old epoch, the storage (or the application checking etcd) rejects the stale operation.
QHow do you tune the failure detection timeout?
A
The timeout must be larger than the maximum expected pause that is NOT a failure. Three sources of "false pause": (1) GC pauses — a JVM running G1GC may pause 200ms; CMS could pause seconds under pressure. Rule: heartbeat timeout ≥ 3× worst-case GC pause. (2) Network jitter — packet delays under load can be 50-200ms in a congested data center. (3) CPU starvation — under extreme load, a process may not run for several hundred milliseconds. In practice: etcd's default lease TTL is 5 seconds with heartbeat every 1.67 seconds (TTL/3). ZooKeeper's default session timeout is 30 seconds. For Kubernetes pod health checks: initialDelaySeconds + failureThreshold × periodSeconds determines when a pod is restarted. Setting these too aggressively causes "restart loops" under load. Setting them too conservatively causes long outages. The production rule: "set your timeout to 3× the worst-case latency you've ever observed in production, not the average."
QWhat is the Bully Algorithm and when would you use it?
A
The Bully Algorithm is a classic leader election algorithm: each node has a unique numeric ID. When any node detects the leader has failed, it sends Election messages to all nodes with a higher ID. Nodes with higher IDs respond "OK" and take over the election. If no higher-ID node responds, the initiating node becomes leader and announces itself to all lower-ID nodes. The "bully" is the highest-ID active node — it always wins. Properties: simple to understand, always elects the highest-ID alive node. Problems: O(N²) messages (each election involves many broadcasts), not safe against split-brain (no lease or fencing), and the initiator can't distinguish a crashed high-ID node from a slow one. Practically: the Bully Algorithm is taught in academic courses but rarely used in production. It's useful in small clusters where simplicity matters more than correctness, or as a building block for understanding election algorithms. Production systems use ZooKeeper, etcd, or consensus-based protocols instead.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design a leader election system for a distributed cron job that must run exactly once", say: "I'd use etcd's leader election with a lease. Each cron service instance attempts to create /cron/leader with a 10-second TTL using a CAS (put-if-not-exists) transaction. The winner writes its node ID and epoch (the lease ID, which is unique per grant). It renews the lease every 3 seconds. On each cron tick, the instance first checks it still holds the lease key with the correct epoch — if yes, execute; if no, skip. When the leader instance is shut down or crashes, the lease expires within 10 seconds and another instance wins the next CAS. Split-brain protection: each cron run is gated on the instance confirming leadership via an etcd read immediately before execution (not just 'do I think I'm the leader'). This adds one etcd round-trip but prevents the race where a lease expires between the renewal check and the job execution. Additionally, the cron job itself writes an idempotency key to the database — if a job somehow runs twice, the second execution is a no-op."

Post 25

Geo-Distributed Systems

25 min deep dive Multi-Region · Speed of Light · Anycast · Active-Active · Geo-Sharding · CRDTs · Global Load Balancing
The Problem

A user in Mumbai hitting us-east-1 gets 200ms of network latency before your app does anything

Speed of light across the Pacific: ~70ms one way, ~140ms round-trip. Add TLS handshake (1–2 RTTs), DNS lookup, and TCP slow-start, and a cold request from Mumbai to Virginia can easily take 500ms before your first byte of application logic runs. You cannot fix this with faster code. The only solution is to run compute and serve data from a region geographically close to the user. But this introduces a new constraint: you cannot have strong consistency and low latency across regions simultaneously — the speed of light prevents it.

The Fundamental Constraint

Light travels ~300,000 km/second in vacuum, ~200,000 km/second in fiber. New York to London = ~70ms minimum. US East to West Coast = ~70ms. Any synchronous operation that requires data to cross the Atlantic or Pacific adds at least 140ms round-trip. This is physics, not engineering — no amount of optimisation removes it. Geo-distributed system design is the art of making the right trade-offs around this constraint.

Building Intuition — four geo-distribution strategies

Active-Passive, Active-Active, Geo-Sharded, and Multi-Master — each with different consistency models

Active-Passive (Primary + Read Replicas)

One region owns all writes (the primary). Other regions have read replicas with async replication. Reads are served locally (fast). Writes must go to the primary (slow from far away, or proxied). Simplest model. Used by most databases in their default configuration. Problem: users far from the primary get write latency = their RTT to the primary region. Failover: if primary goes down, a replica is promoted (potential data loss in async replication gap).

Active-Active (Each region accepts writes)

Every region accepts both reads and writes. No round-trip to a remote primary. Problem: concurrent writes to the same data in different regions create conflicts. Two users in different regions simultaneously update their profile — which update wins? Solutions: (1) Last-write-wins (LWW) with vector clocks — simple but loses one update. (2) CRDTs — conflict-free data structures that merge automatically. (3) Application-level merge logic. Used by: Cassandra, DynamoDB global tables, Google Spanner (with TrueTime for clock synchronisation).

Geo-Sharding (Ownership by region)

Each user's data is owned by their "home" region. European users' data lives in EU-West, US users in US-East, Asian users in AP-Southeast. A user's write always goes to their home region (low latency). When a US user travels to Europe, their writes are forwarded to US-East (slightly higher latency, but transparent). No cross-region conflicts because each piece of data has exactly one owner. Used by: Facebook (region-affinity), gaming systems (player data stays in their region).

Multi-Master with Spanner-style Global Consistency

Google Spanner uses TrueTime (GPS/atomic clock-derived bounded uncertainty timestamps) to provide external consistency across regions. Writes are committed across replicas using Paxos. Reads can be served from any replica that has seen the relevant commit timestamp. This is the most correct approach — but requires specialised hardware (TrueTime GPS clocks) and careful engineering. Most teams don't need or can't afford Spanner-level global consistency.

Diagram 1 · Multi-Region Architecture — read vs write latency by strategy

EU user: 5ms local read vs 80ms cross-region write — the fundamental geo trade-off

geo-architecture.svg
Animation · Local Read vs Cross-Region Write — latency visualised

EU user: 5ms local cache read vs 95ms forwarded write to US primary

geo-latency-animation
Ready
Global Load Balancing and Anycast

How traffic finds the nearest datacenter — DNS-based and BGP Anycast

DNS-based global load balancing

The DNS resolver returns different IP addresses based on the requester's geographic location. A user in Frankfurt gets the Frankfurt datacenter IP. A user in Singapore gets the Singapore datacenter IP. All done via geo-aware DNS (AWS Route 53 geolocation, Cloudflare DNS). Round-trip to the nearest datacenter rather than always going to us-east-1. DNS TTL must be short (30–60s) for fast failover, but short TTL increases DNS query volume. Limitation: DNS-based routing can't account for real-time latency — a region might be geographically close but temporarily congested.

BGP Anycast — the same IP in every datacenter

The same IP address is advertised from multiple physical locations via BGP routing. The internet's routing protocol automatically sends each packet to the topologically closest announcement of that IP. Used by: Cloudflare (all their CDN nodes share the same IPs), Google (8.8.8.8 is anycast), Fastly. Benefit: transparent failover at the routing level — if a datacenter goes down, BGP reconverges and traffic goes to the next-nearest. Limitation: "topologically closest" in BGP routing is not always geographically closest (BGP path selection uses AS hop count and policies, not latency). Typically used for DNS, CDN edge nodes, and DDoS scrubbing.

Trade-offs — geo-distribution strategies

Choose by your consistency requirements and user distribution

geo-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — geo-aware routing and latency budget enforcement

Route reads to nearest region, measure latency budget, handle cross-region writes

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Optional
import time, logging

@dataclass
class Region:
    name:     str
    endpoint: str
    is_primary: bool = False
    is_healthy: bool = True

# ── Latency budget tracker ─────────────────────────────────
class LatencyBudget:
    """
    Track time spent across request phases.
    Alert when budget is exceeded.
    """
    def __init__(self, total_budget_ms: float = 150):
        self.budget   = total_budget_ms
        self.start    = time.perf_counter()
        self.phases: Dict[str, float] = {}

    def mark(self, phase: str) -> float:
        """Record duration of a phase. Returns elapsed ms."""
        elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - self.start) * 1000
        self.phases[phase] = elapsed
        return elapsed

    def remaining_ms(self) -> float:
        elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - self.start) * 1000
        return self.budget - elapsed

    def report(self) -> dict:
        total = (time.perf_counter() - self.start) * 1000
        over  = total > self.budget
        return {"total_ms": round(total, 1),
                "budget_ms": self.budget,
                "over_budget": over,
                "phases": {k: round(v, 1) for k, v in self.phases.items()}}


# ── Geo-aware router ───────────────────────────────────────
class GeoRouter:
    """
    Routes requests based on operation type and user region.
    - Reads → nearest healthy region
    - Writes → primary region (or user's home shard if geo-sharded)
    """

    def __init__(self, regions: Dict[str, Region]):
        self.regions = regions

    def route_read(self, user_region: str) -> Region:
        """Return the best region to serve a read request."""
        # Prefer the user's local region if healthy
        local = self.regions.get(user_region)
        if local and local.is_healthy:
            return local

        # Fall back to primary
        primary = next((r for r in self.regions.values()
                        if r.is_primary and r.is_healthy), None)
        if not primary:
            raise RuntimeError("No healthy region available")
        logging.warning(f"Local region {user_region} unhealthy, routing to {primary.name}")
        return primary

    def route_write(self, user_region: str, user_id: Optional[str] = None) -> Region:
        """
        Route a write to the correct region.
        - Geo-sharded: route to user's home region
        - Single primary: always route to primary
        """
        # If geo-sharded, user's home region owns their data
        if user_id:
            home = self._get_home_region(user_id)
            if home and home.is_healthy: return home

        # Otherwise route to primary
        return next(r for r in self.regions.values() if r.is_primary and r.is_healthy)

    def _get_home_region(self, user_id: str) -> Optional[Region]:
        """Determine user's home region from their ID (geo-sharding)."""
        # In practice: look up user's region from a routing table
        region_code = user_id.split("-")[0]
        region_map  = {"eu": "eu-west", "us": "us-east", "ap": "ap-southeast"}
        return self.regions.get(region_map.get(region_code, ""), None)


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
regions = {
    "us-east":     Region("us-east",     "us.db.example.com", is_primary=True),
    "eu-west":     Region("eu-west",     "eu.db.example.com"),
    "ap-southeast": Region("ap-southeast", "ap.db.example.com"),
}
router = GeoRouter(regions)
budget = LatencyBudget(150)

# EU user reads from local replica (5ms)
read_region = router.route_read("eu-west")
budget.mark("read_routing")
print(f"Read from: {read_region.name}")  # eu-west (local)

# EU user writes — must go to primary (80ms RTT)
write_region = router.route_write("eu-west")
budget.mark("write_routing")
print(f"Write to: {write_region.name}")   # us-east (primary)

# EU user with geo-sharded account → local write
write_region_geo = router.route_write("eu-west", user_id="eu-user-123")
print(f"Geo-sharded write: {write_region_geo.name}")  # eu-west (local!)
print(budget.report())
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap;

record Region(String name, String endpoint, boolean isPrimary, boolean isHealthy) {}

class LatencyBudget {
    private final double          budgetMs;
    private final long            startNs = System.nanoTime();
    private final Map<String,Double> phases = new LinkedHashMap<>();

    LatencyBudget(double budgetMs) { this.budgetMs = budgetMs; }

    public double mark(String phase) {
        double ms = (System.nanoTime() - startNs) / 1_000_000.0;
        phases.put(phase, ms);
        return ms;
    }

    public boolean isOverBudget() {
        return (System.nanoTime() - startNs) / 1_000_000.0 > budgetMs;
    }

    public String report() {
        double total = (System.nanoTime() - startNs) / 1_000_000.0;
        return String.format("total=%.1fms budget=%.0fms over=%b phases=%s",
            total, budgetMs, total > budgetMs, phases);
    }
}

class GeoRouter {
    private final Map<String, Region> regions;
    // user_id prefix → region name (geo-shard routing table)
    private final Map<String, String> regionMap = Map.of(
        "eu", "eu-west", "us", "us-east", "ap", "ap-southeast");

    GeoRouter(Map<String, Region> regions) { this.regions = regions; }

    public Region routeRead(String userRegion) {
        Region local = regions.get(userRegion);
        if (local != null && local.isHealthy()) return local;
        return regions.values().stream()
            .filter(r -> r.isPrimary() && r.isHealthy())
            .findFirst().orElseThrow();
    }

    public Region routeWrite(String userRegion, String userId) {
        // Geo-sharded: route to user's home region
        if (userId != null) {
            String prefix = userId.split("-")[0];
            String home   = regionMap.get(prefix);
            Region r      = regions.get(home);
            if (r != null && r.isHealthy()) return r;
        }
        return regions.values().stream()
            .filter(r -> r.isPrimary() && r.isHealthy())
            .findFirst().orElseThrow();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var regions = Map.of(
            "us-east",      new Region("us-east",      "us.db", true,  true),
            "eu-west",      new Region("eu-west",      "eu.db", false, true),
            "ap-southeast", new Region("ap-southeast", "ap.db", false, true)
        );
        GeoRouter    router = new GeoRouter(regions);
        LatencyBudget budget = new LatencyBudget(150);

        System.out.println("Read:  "  + router.routeRead("eu-west").name());         // eu-west
        System.out.println("Write: "  + router.routeWrite("eu-west", null).name()); // us-east
        System.out.println("Geo:   "  + router.routeWrite("eu-west", "eu-user-1").name()); // eu-west
        budget.mark("routing");
        System.out.println(budget.report());
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Geo-Distributed Systems

QWhy can't you have both low latency and strong consistency in a geo-distributed system?
A
This is a direct consequence of the CAP theorem (availability + partition tolerance required) and physics (the speed of light). For strong consistency, every read must return the most recently written value. In a geo-distributed system, to guarantee a read in EU sees a write just committed in US, you either need: (1) the read to wait for the write to propagate across the Atlantic (~140ms round-trip), OR (2) the write to wait for acknowledgment from EU before confirming (~140ms added to write latency). Either way, at least one operation pays the cross-region network cost, which is bounded below by the speed of light — you can't make light travel faster. The only alternative is to relax consistency: allow EU reads to see slightly stale data (eventual consistency), making reads fast while writes are acknowledged locally. The design choice is always: which operations must be fast (reads? writes?), and what consistency can you accept for the others?
QWhat is geo-sharding and how does it solve the write latency problem?
A
Geo-sharding assigns ownership of each piece of data to one specific region — the "home" region for that data. For user-centric applications, the shard key is often the user's home region: EU users' data lives in EU-West, US users in US-East, Asian users in AP-Southeast. Writes for a EU user always go to EU-West (their home region) — no cross-region forwarding, low write latency. Reads for that user's own data are also served from EU-West. Cross-region reads (e.g. US user's friend activity in EU) can be served from async read replicas with acceptable staleness. When a user travels (US user in Europe), their writes are still routed to US-East — slightly higher latency for the traveler, but this is an acceptable trade-off vs. the complexity of migrating data. Used by: Facebook (region-affinity routing), gaming systems (player data in their timezone). Main limitation: cross-shard queries (global leaderboards, global search) require scatter-gather across all shards.
QHow does Google Spanner achieve global external consistency?
A
Spanner uses TrueTime — a globally synchronised clock API backed by GPS receivers and atomic clocks in every datacenter. TrueTime doesn't give you a single precise timestamp; it gives you a time interval [earliest, latest] with guaranteed bounds (typically ±7ms). Two key mechanisms: (1) Commit wait: when a transaction commits, Spanner waits until the uncertainty interval from the commit timestamp has fully passed (up to ~14ms). This ensures no transaction can commit with a timestamp that might be in the past for another datacenter. (2) Timestamp ordering: transactions are ordered by their commit timestamps, which TrueTime guarantees are globally monotonically increasing. Result: reads can be served from any replica that has seen the relevant commit timestamp, and they're guaranteed to be consistent with the global transaction order. The 14ms commit wait adds to write latency but provides strong external consistency. This is why Spanner is used for globally distributed financial systems where consistency is non-negotiable.
QHow do you handle conflict resolution in an active-active multi-region setup?
A
Active-active means every region accepts writes for the same data. Concurrent writes in different regions create conflicts — two different values for the same key. Resolution strategies: (1) Last-write-wins (LWW): use physical timestamps (NTP-synchronised). Whichever write has the later timestamp wins. Simple but relies on clock accuracy and silently discards one write. Used by Cassandra (configurable). (2) Application-level merge: the application defines how to merge conflicting values. "Shopping cart" conflict: union of both carts rather than picking one. Requires domain-specific logic for each data type. (3) CRDTs: data types that are provably conflict-free — G-Counter, OR-Set, LWW-Register. No application merge logic needed; CRDTs merge automatically. Used by Riak, Redis (CRDT-based replication). (4) Operational transformation / CRDT hybrid: for collaborative editing (Google Docs uses OT internally). Choice depends on: Can you tolerate losing any write? If no, use CRDTs or application merge. If LWW is acceptable (metrics, counters), use LWW for simplicity.
QHow would you design a latency budget for a globally distributed API?
A
A latency budget allocates a maximum time to each phase of a request. For a 150ms total target: DNS resolution 5ms, TCP handshake 5ms, TLS 10ms, load balancer 2ms, API processing 20ms, database read 5ms (local replica), response serialization 3ms = 50ms used — 100ms remaining for contingency and additional hops. Steps: (1) Define the SLO (e.g. p99 < 150ms). (2) Instrument every phase with high-resolution timestamps. (3) Build a waterfall view of each request (like distributed tracing in Post 16). (4) Identify which phases consume the most budget. (5) For geo-distributed requests: measure actual cross-region latencies (not theoretical), they're often higher than speed-of-light due to BGP path asymmetry, peering decisions, and congestion. (6) Set phase-level SLOs: if the DB read consistently takes >20ms, the budget is at risk before the request even reaches the network. Alerting: alert when any single phase's p99 approaches its budget. Don't only alert on total latency — by then you've already violated SLO for affected users.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design a globally distributed user profile system with <50ms read latency worldwide", say: "Three-tier approach: (1) CDN edge cache: profile data cached at 300+ PoPs globally. Cache-hit latency <5ms. TTL=30s for active profiles. When a user updates their profile, the write invalidates their specific CDN cache key. This handles ~95% of profile reads. (2) Regional read replicas: five regions (US-East, US-West, EU-West, AP-Southeast, SA-East). User profile reads that miss CDN go to the nearest regional database replica. Async replication from the primary. Replication lag 50–200ms — acceptable for profile data. (3) Geo-sharded primary: each user has a 'home region' based on signup location, stored in a routing table. Profile writes go to the user's home region primary, never cross-region. When a user moves permanently, their home region is migrated. Total latency for a read: CDN hit <5ms, CDN miss → regional replica ~8ms, replica miss → local DB ~15ms. Write latency: local primary <10ms for EU users with EU home region. The only case with high write latency is a traveling user writing from outside their home region — acceptable trade-off."

Post 26

Kafka Internals

25 min deep dive Partitions · Offsets · ISR · Consumer Groups · Rebalance · Exactly-Once · Log Compaction · KRaft
The Problem

You need to stream 5 million events per second durably, replay them, and have multiple independent consumers

A traditional message queue like RabbitMQ gives each message to exactly one consumer and deletes it after acknowledgment. This works for task queues but fails for event streaming: you can't replay history, you can't add a new consumer that reads from the beginning, and you can't have multiple independent services consuming the same events at different rates. Kafka solves all three by storing events as an immutable, append-only log on disk. Consumers don't pop messages — they read from an offset and advance it independently. The log is retained for days or weeks.

Kafka's Core Model

A topic is a logical stream. A topic is split into partitions — each partition is an ordered, immutable sequence of records on disk. Each record has an offset — its position in the partition (monotonically increasing). Producers append to partitions. Consumers track their own offset per partition. Parallelism = number of partitions. Ordering is guaranteed within a partition but not across partitions.

Building Intuition — partitions, ISR, and consumer groups

Partitions give parallelism. ISR gives durability. Consumer groups give independent consumption.

Partitions and the partition key

The partition key determines which partition a record goes to: partition = hash(key) % num_partitions. Records with the same key always go to the same partition, guaranteeing ordering for that key. No key = round-robin assignment. Parallelism: each partition can be consumed by at most one consumer per consumer group. 12 partitions = up to 12 parallel consumers. Adding more consumers than partitions = idle consumers. Changing partition count after creation remaps key→partition assignments — avoid after data is written.

ISR — In-Sync Replicas

Each partition has one leader and N-1 followers (replicas). Producers always write to the leader. Followers continuously fetch from the leader and stay in the ISR (In-Sync Replicas) set. An ISR member must have fetched all messages within replica.lag.time.max.ms (default 10s). If a follower falls behind, it's removed from ISR. Producer acks setting controls durability: acks=0 (fire and forget), acks=1 (leader only), acks=all (all ISR must acknowledge — strongest durability). Leader failure: the controller elects a new leader from ISR members only, preventing data loss.

Consumer groups and rebalancing

A consumer group is a set of consumers that collectively read a topic. Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group at any time. This provides load balancing across consumers while ensuring ordered processing per partition. When a consumer joins or leaves, a rebalance is triggered: all partition assignments are recalculated and redistributed. During rebalance, all consumers in the group stop consuming — a "stop-the-world" pause. Eager rebalancing (classic): all partitions revoked, then reassigned. Cooperative rebalancing (Kafka 2.4+): only moved partitions are revoked — most consumers continue consuming during the rebalance.

Diagram 1 · Kafka Anatomy — partitions, offsets, ISR, and consumer groups

One topic, three partitions, two replicas, two consumer groups

kafka-anatomy.svg
Animation · Consumer Group Rebalance — stop-the-world pause when C3 joins

Watch all consumers pause during rebalance — and why cooperative rebalancing matters

kafka-rebalance-animation
Ready
Exactly-Once, Log Compaction, and KRaft

Three advanced Kafka features every senior engineer must know

Exactly-Once Semantics (EOS)

Kafka offers three delivery guarantees: at-most-once (fast, may lose messages), at-least-once (may duplicate — consumer re-processes on restart), exactly-once (no loss, no duplicates). EOS requires: (1) Idempotent producer: each message gets a sequence number; the broker deduplicates retries within a session. (2) Transactional API: producer begins a transaction, writes to multiple partitions, commits atomically. Consumers with isolation.level=read_committed only see committed transactions. Use EOS for financial systems, inventory updates — anywhere duplicates would cause real harm. Cost: ~20% throughput reduction vs at-least-once.

Log Compaction — retaining only the latest value per key

By default, Kafka retains all records for the configured retention period. Log compaction changes the model: for topics with a key, Kafka retains only the latest record for each key — older versions are removed. This turns a Kafka topic into a compacted key-value store. Used for: materialising current database state (the "changelog topic" pattern in Kafka Streams), storing the latest user preferences, feature flags. Delete a key: publish a tombstone (null value for that key). After compaction, the tombstone itself is eventually removed. Active segments are not compacted — only closed (full) segments are eligible.

KRaft — Kafka without ZooKeeper (Kafka 3.3+)

Kafka historically used ZooKeeper for metadata management (topic configs, partition assignments, leader elections). ZooKeeper was a separate cluster to operate, with its own operational complexity. KRaft (Kafka Raft Metadata) replaces ZooKeeper: metadata is stored in a special internal topic replicated via Raft. A subset of Kafka brokers are "controllers" that run the Raft consensus. Benefits: simpler operations (one system instead of two), faster metadata propagation, no ZooKeeper scaling limit on partition count. KRaft is production-ready since Kafka 3.3 (2022). ZooKeeper mode is deprecated and will be removed.

Code · Python & Java — producer with acks, consumer with offset management, exactly-once

Production-grade producer and consumer patterns

PYTHON
# pip install confluent-kafka
from confluent_kafka import Producer, Consumer, KafkaError
import json, logging

# ── Production-grade Producer ─────────────────────────────
def create_producer() -> Producer:
    return Producer({
        "bootstrap.servers":         "kafka:9092",
        "acks":                      "all",          # wait for all ISR
        "enable.idempotence":        "true",         # deduplicate retries
        "max.in.flight.requests.per.connection": 5,  # required for idempotence
        "retries":                   2147483647,     # retry forever (idempotent)
        "compression.type":          "lz4",          # compress batches
        "linger.ms":                 5,              # batch window 5ms
        "batch.size":                16384,          # 16KB batches
    })

def send_order(producer: Producer, order: dict) -> None:
    """
    Send an order event to Kafka.
    Key = user_id ensures all events for the same user go to the same partition.
    """
    def delivery_callback(err, msg):
        if err:
            logging.error(f"Delivery failed: {err}")
        else:
            print(f"Delivered offset={msg.offset()} partition={msg.partition()}")

    producer.produce(
        topic="orders",
        key=str(order["user_id"]).encode(),     # same user → same partition → ordering
        value=json.dumps(order).encode(),
        callback=delivery_callback
    )
    producer.poll(0)   # trigger delivery callbacks without blocking


# ── Production-grade Consumer ─────────────────────────────
def create_consumer(group_id: str) -> Consumer:
    return Consumer({
        "bootstrap.servers":    "kafka:9092",
        "group.id":             group_id,
        "auto.offset.reset":    "earliest",    # start from beginning if new group
        "enable.auto.commit":   "false",        # manual commit after processing
        "max.poll.interval.ms": 300000,         # 5min: increase for slow processing
        # Cooperative rebalancing: reduces stop-the-world pause
        "partition.assignment.strategy": "cooperative-sticky",
        # Static membership: restart without triggering rebalance
        "group.instance.id":    "fraud-consumer-1",
    })

def consume_orders(consumer: Consumer) -> None:
    consumer.subscribe(["orders"])
    try:
        while True:
            msg = consumer.poll(timeout=1.0)
            if msg is None: continue
            if msg.error():
                if msg.error().code() == KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF: continue
                raise Exception(msg.error())

            order = json.loads(msg.value())
            try:
                process_order(order)       # your business logic here
                # Commit AFTER successful processing (at-least-once)
                consumer.commit(msg)       # commit only this message's offset
            except Exception as e:
                logging.error(f"Failed to process offset {msg.offset()}: {e}")
                # Don't commit — will re-process on restart
    finally:
        consumer.close()  # always close to trigger rebalance

def process_order(order: dict) -> None:
    print(f"Processing order {order['id']} for user {order['user_id']}")
JAVA
import org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.*;
import org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.*;
import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.*;

class KafkaPatterns {

    // ── Producer ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    static KafkaProducer<String, String> createProducer() {
        Properties props = new Properties();
        props.put("bootstrap.servers",     "kafka:9092");
        props.put("acks",                  "all");          // wait for all ISR
        props.put("enable.idempotence",    "true");
        props.put("compression.type",      "lz4");
        props.put("linger.ms",             "5");
        props.put("batch.size",            "16384");
        props.put("key.serializer",        "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer");
        props.put("value.serializer",      "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer");
        return new KafkaProducer<>(props);
    }

    static void sendOrder(KafkaProducer<String,String> producer, String userId, String orderJson) {
        ProducerRecord<String,String> record =
            new ProducerRecord<>("orders", userId, orderJson);
        producer.send(record, (meta, ex) -> {
            if (ex != null) System.err.println("Delivery failed: " + ex);
            else System.out.printf("Delivered partition=%d offset=%d%n",
                    meta.partition(), meta.offset());
        });
    }

    // ── Consumer with manual commit ───────────────────────────
    static KafkaConsumer<String, String> createConsumer(String groupId) {
        Properties props = new Properties();
        props.put("bootstrap.servers",               "kafka:9092");
        props.put("group.id",                         groupId);
        props.put("auto.offset.reset",               "earliest");
        props.put("enable.auto.commit",              "false");
        props.put("max.poll.interval.ms",           "300000");
        props.put("partition.assignment.strategy",
            "org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CooperativeStickyAssignor");
        props.put("key.deserializer",   "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
        props.put("value.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
        return new KafkaConsumer<>(props);
    }

    static void consumeOrders(KafkaConsumer<String,String> consumer) {
        consumer.subscribe(List.of("orders"));
        try {
            while (true) {
                ConsumerRecords<String,String> records =
                    consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(1000));
                for (ConsumerRecord<String,String> r : records) {
                    try {
                        processOrder(r.key(), r.value());
                        // Commit this specific partition+offset after success
                        consumer.commitSync(Map.of(
                            new TopicPartition(r.topic(), r.partition()),
                            new OffsetAndMetadata(r.offset() + 1)
                        ));
                    } catch (Exception e) {
                        System.err.println("Failed at offset " + r.offset());
                        break;   // don't commit — retry from this offset
                    }
                }
            }
        } finally { consumer.close(); }
    }

    static void processOrder(String key, String json) {
        System.out.println("Processing user=" + key + " order=" + json);
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Kafka

QHow does Kafka guarantee ordering, and what are its limits?
A
Kafka guarantees ordering within a single partition: all records in a partition are stored in the order they were appended and consumed in that order. A producer with the same key (e.g. user_id) always maps to the same partition (via hash(key) % num_partitions), so all events for the same user are ordered. Limits: ordering is NOT guaranteed across partitions. Two records with different keys may be in different partitions, and the consumer may see them interleaved depending on how far ahead each partition is. If you need total ordering across all records in a topic: use 1 partition. Throughput is then limited to a single consumer and a single broker's write speed. For most use cases, per-key ordering within a partition is sufficient. Edge case: changing num_partitions remaps key→partition, breaking ordering for in-flight records during the migration.
