Add partitions only when the old partition count is being handled by the existing consumer group cleanly.
QWhat is the ISR and how does it protect against data loss on leader failure?
A
The ISR (In-Sync Replicas) is the set of replicas that are fully caught up with the partition leader within replica.lag.time.max.ms (default 10s). The leader tracks which replicas are in ISR. When a producer uses acks=all, the write is not acknowledged until all ISR replicas have written the record. This means every ISR member has a complete copy of all committed records. On leader failure: the controller elects a new leader from ISR members only. Since every ISR member has all committed records, the new leader can serve all committed writes without data loss. If acks=1 (leader only), a write acknowledged by the leader may not yet be replicated. If the leader crashes before a follower fetches it, that write is lost — the new leader (promoted from ISR) doesn't have it. The tradeoff: acks=all adds ~1 additional replication round-trip to write latency, but prevents data loss. Minimum ISR: configure min.insync.replicas=2 to prevent acknowledging writes when only one replica is in-sync (otherwise a broker failure could cause the last ISR member to be the leader with un-replicated data).
QWhat causes consumer rebalances and how do you minimise them?
A
Rebalances are triggered when: (1) a consumer joins the group, (2) a consumer leaves (shutdown or crash), (3) a consumer fails to call poll() within max.poll.interval.ms (default 5 minutes) — treated as a "consumer death", (4) new partitions are added to the topic, (5) the subscription pattern changes. Minimisation strategies: (1) Cooperative rebalancing (Kafka 2.4+): use CooperativeStickyAssignor — only partitions that need to move are revoked, most consumers keep consuming. (2) Static group membership (group.instance.id): give each consumer a stable ID. On restart, it re-joins with the same ID and gets back its previous partitions without triggering a full rebalance (within session.timeout.ms). (3) Increase max.poll.interval.ms: if processing is slow, increase this to prevent false "consumer death" detection. (4) Reduce processing time per batch: faster processing = less risk of exceeding max.poll.interval.ms. (5) Tune heartbeat vs session timeout: heartbeat.interval.ms should be 1/3 of session.timeout.ms. Short heartbeat = faster failure detection but more heartbeat traffic.
QHow does Kafka's exactly-once semantics work?
A
Exactly-once is achieved by combining two mechanisms: (1) Idempotent producer: the broker assigns each producer session a producer ID (PID) and sequence number per partition. If a producer retries after a network timeout, the broker detects the duplicate (same PID + sequence number) and ignores it rather than writing twice. This prevents duplicates from producer retries but only within a single session. (2) Transactions: the producer begins a transaction (beginTransaction()), writes to multiple partitions, and commits atomically (commitTransaction()). If the producer crashes mid-transaction, the coordinator aborts the partial transaction on the broker side. Consumers with isolation.level=read_committed only see records from committed transactions — they don't see any in-progress or aborted records. Together: idempotent producer prevents retry duplicates, transactions provide atomic multi-partition writes, and read_committed prevents consumers from seeing partial writes. The full EOS pipeline is: idempotent producer + transactional writes + consumer with read_committed. Cost: ~20% throughput reduction. Use EOS for: financial transfers, inventory deductions — anywhere a duplicate would cause real harm (double charges, negative inventory).
QWhen would you use log compaction instead of retention-based deletion?
A
Retention-based deletion removes all records older than the configured retention period (7 days by default). Log compaction removes older records for a key when a newer record for the same key exists, keeping only the latest value per key. Use compaction when: (1) The topic represents the current state of a database (changelog topic in Kafka Streams or Debezium CDC source) — you want to rebuild state by replaying the compacted topic from the beginning, getting only current values, not the full history. Without compaction, replaying 1 year of history to rebuild current state takes too long. (2) The topic stores configuration or feature flags — you only care about the current value, not every change. (3) Building a materialised view — join the compacted topic with a stream to reconstruct current state for any key. Compaction is not a substitute for a database: it doesn't provide random access (you must scan from the beginning to rebuild state), and compaction is asynchronous (the head of the topic is never compacted, only closed segments). In practice: use retention for event logs and audit trails; use compaction for state changelog topics that need to be replayable without full history.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you design a Kafka-based order processing pipeline with exactly-once guarantees?", say: "Producer side: enable idempotent producer (deduplicates retries) and transactional API. Order service wraps the Kafka write and the database update in a transaction: begin transaction → write to DB → produce to 'orders' topic → commit transaction. If the app crashes after the DB write but before Kafka commit, the transaction coordinator aborts the Kafka write on restart. Consumer side: fraud detection and fulfillment services use isolation.level=read_committed — they never see partial transactions. Both use manual offset commit (commitSync after successful processing). Enable cooperative sticky rebalancing to minimise rebalance pauses — each consumer instance uses group.instance.id for static membership so restarts don't trigger rebalance. Partition count: 12 (aligned with max expected parallelism). Partition key: user_id (all events for a user go to the same partition, preserving order). Replication factor: 3 with min.insync.replicas=2 and acks=all on the producer — loss-free even if one broker goes down."

Post 27

Database Indexing Deep Dive

25 min deep dive B-Tree · Hash · Composite · Covering · Partial · Index-Only Scan · EXPLAIN ANALYZE · Write Overhead
The Problem

A query on a 100M row table takes 30 seconds without an index — 0.1ms with one

A query like SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42 on a 100M row orders table with no index requires a full sequential scan — the database reads every row, one by one, comparing user_id. At ~10,000 rows/ms, that's 10 seconds. With a B-Tree index on user_id, the database does ~27 comparisons (log₂(100M) ≈ 27) to find the exact leaf node and then reads only the matching rows. The difference is not a constant factor improvement — it's algorithmic: O(N) vs O(log N). For 100M rows, that's a 3.7 million× speedup.

The Write Overhead Trade-Off

An index is a separate data structure maintained alongside the table. Every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also update every index on that table. A table with 8 indexes means every write touches 9 data structures (1 table + 8 indexes). This is why you don't index everything: each index trades write throughput and storage for read speed. The art is identifying which queries are on the hot path and deserve an index.

Building Intuition — four index types

B-Tree, Hash, GIN, and BRIN — each designed for a different access pattern

B-Tree — the default, works for range queries

A balanced tree where leaf nodes contain (key, heap_pointer) pairs, sorted by key. Supports equality (=), range (>, <, BETWEEN), prefix (LIKE 'abc%'), and ORDER BY (index already sorted). O(log N) lookup. Every Postgres index is B-Tree by default. The heap pointer is the physical location of the row in the table heap.

Hash index — equality only, faster than B-Tree for =

Stores hash(key) → heap_pointer. Supports ONLY equality (=). O(1) lookup for point queries. Faster than B-Tree for pure equality on high-cardinality columns. Does NOT support range queries, ORDER BY, or LIKE. In PostgreSQL, Hash indexes are now WAL-logged (crash-safe since PG 10). Use for: primary key lookups, UUID equality checks.

GIN (Generalised Inverted Index) — arrays, JSONB, full-text search

Maps each element to a list of rows containing it. Useful for: JSONB @> '{}' containment queries, array && overlap, full-text search (tsvector @@ tsquery). A single row with tags = ['python', 'kafka', 'redis'] contributes entries to three separate GIN keys. GIN indexes are large but enable sub-millisecond search over complex document structures.

BRIN (Block Range INdex) — huge tables with correlation

Stores the min/max value per physical disk block range (128 pages by default). Extremely small (orders of magnitude smaller than B-Tree). Only useful when data has high physical correlation with the indexed column — time-series data inserted in time order has high correlation between time column and physical location. For WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' on a 10TB table, BRIN eliminates entire block ranges instantly. Useless if data is inserted randomly.

Diagram 1 · B-Tree Index Anatomy — sequential scan vs index scan

O(N) full scan vs O(log N) B-Tree traversal — the same query, a million-fold difference

btree-vs-seqscan.svg
Animation · Sequential Scan → B-Tree Index → Comparison

Watch the database read every row, then skip to exactly one via B-Tree traversal

indexing-animation
Ready
Advanced Indexing Patterns

Composite, covering, partial, and expression indexes — for specific query patterns

Composite indexes — column order matters critically

A composite index on (a, b, c) can satisfy queries on a, (a, b), or (a, b, c) — but NOT on b alone or c alone. The leading column rule: the index is useful only if the leftmost columns are present in the WHERE clause. Put the most selective column first (highest cardinality). Put the column used in range queries LAST (range columns stop the index from being used for further columns to the right). Example: for WHERE status = 'active' AND created_at > '2024-01-01', use CREATE INDEX ON orders(status, created_at) — status is equality (high filter efficiency), created_at is range (last).

Covering indexes — eliminate heap fetches entirely

A covering index includes all columns needed by the query. When PostgreSQL can answer a query entirely from the index (no heap fetch needed), it performs an index-only scan — the fastest possible access. Syntax: CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(user_id) INCLUDE (product_name, amount). The INCLUDE columns are stored in the leaf nodes but not used for sorting/searching. Important for high-frequency read queries. Trade-off: the index is larger on disk.

Partial indexes — index only what you query

A partial index only indexes rows matching a WHERE clause. CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(user_id) WHERE status = 'pending' only indexes pending orders. If 99% of orders are 'completed' but all your hot queries are on 'pending', a partial index is 100× smaller and faster. PostgreSQL uses the partial index only when the query includes the index condition. Use for: soft-deleted rows (WHERE deleted_at IS NULL), status-filtered queries, recent data.

Trade-offs — index type selection

Match the index type to the query pattern

index-tradeoffs.svg
Code · SQL & Python — index creation, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, covering indexes

How to create the right index and verify it's being used

SQL
-- ── 1. Basic B-Tree index ─────────────────────────────────
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user
ON orders(user_id);
-- Good for: WHERE user_id = 42, WHERE user_id IN (1,2,3), ORDER BY user_id

-- ── 2. Composite index (order matters!) ───────────────────
-- Query: WHERE status = 'pending' AND created_at > '2024-01-01'
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_date
ON orders(status, created_at);
-- status = equality → goes first (filters most rows)
-- created_at = range → goes last (range stops leftmost-prefix rule)

-- ── 3. Covering index (eliminates heap fetch) ─────────────
-- Query: SELECT user_id, amount FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_covering
ON orders(user_id)
INCLUDE (amount, product_name);
-- If SELECT only needs (user_id, amount, product_name),
-- PostgreSQL uses Index-Only Scan — no heap fetch at all!

-- ── 4. Partial index (only indexes 'pending' rows) ─────────
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending
ON orders(user_id, created_at)
WHERE status = 'pending';
-- 10M total orders, 50K pending → index is 200× smaller
-- PG uses this index for: WHERE status='pending' AND user_id=42

-- ── 5. Expression index ───────────────────────────────────
-- Query: WHERE lower(email) = '[email protected]'
-- Normal index on email won't help (expression is computed)
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_lower
ON users(lower(email));
-- Now WHERE lower(email) = '[email protected]' uses the index

-- ── 6. EXPLAIN ANALYZE — verify the index is used ─────────
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT user_id, amount
FROM orders
WHERE user_id = 42
  AND status = 'pending';

-- Good output: "Index Only Scan using idx_orders_pending"
--   Rows Removed by Filter: 0
--   Buffers: shared hit=3  (only 3 pages read!)
--   Actual Rows: 5  Actual Loops: 1

-- Bad output: "Seq Scan on orders"
--   Rows Removed by Filter: 9999995
--   Buffers: shared hit=44248  (full table scan!)

-- ── 7. Find unused indexes ────────────────────────────────
SELECT
    schemaname,
    relname   AS table_name,
    indexrelname AS index_name,
    idx_scan  AS times_used,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0         -- never used!
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;
-- Drop indexes with idx_scan = 0 after a representative query period
-- They waste write overhead with zero read benefit
PYTHON
# ── SQLAlchemy index definitions ──────────────────────────
from sqlalchemy import (
    Column, Integer, String, DateTime, Numeric,
    Index, func, create_engine
)
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase

class Base(DeclarativeBase): pass

class Order(Base):
    __tablename__ = "orders"

    id         = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    user_id    = Column(Integer, nullable=False)
    status     = Column(String, nullable=False)
    created_at = Column(DateTime)
    amount     = Column(Numeric(10, 2))

    __table_args__ = (
        # Composite index: status (equality) → created_at (range)
        Index("idx_status_date", "status", "created_at"),

        # Covering index: user_id lookup, include amount
        Index("idx_user_covering", "user_id",
              postgresql_include=["amount"]),

        # Partial index: only pending orders
        Index("idx_user_pending", "user_id", "created_at",
              postgresql_where=(Column("status") == "pending")),
    )


# ── Index advisor: parse EXPLAIN output ────────────────────
import psycopg2

def explain_query(conn, query: str, params=()) -> dict:
    """Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and return key metrics."""
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(f"EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) {query}", params)
        plan = cur.fetchone()[0][0]  # JSON plan

        root = plan["Plan"]
        return {
            "node_type":   root.get("Node Type"),  # "Seq Scan" or "Index Scan"
            "actual_rows": root.get("Actual Rows"),
            "actual_ms":   root.get("Actual Total Time"),
            "buffers_hit": root.get("Shared Hit Blocks", 0),
            "index_name":  root.get("Index Name"),  # None for seq scan
        }

# ── Find unused indexes via pg_stat_user_indexes ──────────
def find_unused_indexes(conn) -> list:
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("""
            SELECT relname, indexrelname, idx_scan,
                   pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
            FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
            WHERE idx_scan = 0
              AND indexrelname NOT LIKE 'pg_%'
            ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC
            LIMIT 20
        """)
        return [
            {"table": r[0], "index": r[1], "scans": r[2], "size": r[3]}
            for r in cur.fetchall()
        ]

# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# conn = psycopg2.connect("dbname=prod")
# plan = explain_query(conn, "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id=%s", (42,))
# print(plan)
# → {'node_type': 'Index Scan', 'index_name': 'idx_user_covering', 'actual_ms': 0.12}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Database Indexing

QHow does a B-Tree index work internally, and why is it O(log N)?
A
A B-Tree (Balanced Tree) index is a self-balancing tree where all leaf nodes are at the same depth. Internal (non-leaf) nodes contain keys that act as routing guides — they tell you which subtree to descend into to find the target key. Leaf nodes contain (key, heap_pointer) pairs, where the heap pointer is the physical address of the row in the table's storage file. To find user_id=42: start at the root, compare 42 with root keys to choose the right child, recurse down until reaching a leaf, return the heap pointer, then do a second "random I/O" to fetch the actual row from the heap. For a 100M row table, the tree has about 3 levels (since each B-Tree node can hold hundreds of keys). Total lookups: 3 internal node reads + 1 leaf read + 1 heap fetch = 5 I/Os. Compare with sequential scan: 100M/100 ≈ 1M page reads. The O(log N) comes from the tree height being proportional to log of the number of rows, and B-Tree nodes are wide (hundreds of children per node), so the base of the logarithm is large — typically 3–5 levels for billion-row tables.
QWhy does column order matter in a composite index?
A
A composite index on (a, b, c) is physically sorted by a first, then b within each a value, then c within each b value. The database can only traverse the index from left to right using the sorted order. Queries are index-satisfiable if they provide equality conditions on leading columns. So (a=5, b>10) works (equality on a, then range on b). But (b=5) alone doesn't — the index is sorted by a first, so b values are scattered across all a values with no useful order. The leading column rule: use equality conditions on the leftmost columns, and if you need a range condition, put it on the last column (range stops further use of composite index for subsequent columns). Practical rule: put the most selective equality column first (highest cardinality, filters most rows), put range columns last, put sort/ORDER BY columns at the end if no range. For WHERE status='active' AND created_at > '2024-01-01': use (status, created_at) — status equality narrows to a subset, then created_at range is applied within that subset.
QWhat is a covering index and when does it enable an index-only scan?
A
A covering index includes all columns needed to satisfy a query — the query result can be returned entirely from the index without reading the actual table rows. A standard index only stores the indexed columns plus a heap pointer. When a query needs additional columns (in SELECT, WHERE, or ORDER BY), PostgreSQL must do a heap fetch for each matching index entry — a second random I/O. A covering index adds extra columns to the index leaf nodes using the INCLUDE clause: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id) INCLUDE (amount, email). Now SELECT amount, email FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42 can be satisfied entirely from the index — no heap fetch. This is an "Index-Only Scan" in EXPLAIN output, typically 2–5× faster than a regular index scan. Trade-off: the index is larger on disk. Use covering indexes for your highest-frequency read queries where every microsecond counts. Be careful: the included columns are not searchable and don't affect sort order — they're just stored in the leaf nodes for retrieval.
QA query has an index but EXPLAIN shows it's doing a sequential scan. Why?
A
PostgreSQL's query planner uses statistics to estimate costs. It chooses a seq scan when it estimates the index scan would be more expensive. Common reasons: (1) Low selectivity: the WHERE condition matches a large fraction of rows (e.g. WHERE status='active' and 80% of rows are active). A seq scan reads pages sequentially (fast); an index scan does random I/O per matching row (slow for high row counts). Rule of thumb: index scan is faster when matching <5–10% of rows. (2) Stale statistics: ANALYZE hasn't run since a large data load. Run ANALYZE on the table to refresh statistics. (3) Small table: for small tables, seq scan is often faster due to overhead of index traversal. (4) Wrong index: the indexed column isn't in the WHERE clause, or has a function applied (WHERE lower(email) = 'x' won't use an index on email — use a functional index). (5) planner cost settings: random_page_cost is set too high relative to seq_page_cost (common if your data is in RAM). Debug: set enable_seqscan=off to force an index scan and compare execution time.
QHow do you find and remove indexes that are hurting write performance without helping reads?
A
PostgreSQL's pg_stat_user_indexes tracks idx_scan (number of times the index has been used in a scan since last pg_stat_reset()). An index with idx_scan=0 over a representative time window (at least one week in production) has never been used for reads but is still slowing down every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Steps: (1) Query pg_stat_user_indexes for idx_scan=0 ordered by index size. (2) Verify the index name doesn't match any important query you just haven't run recently (check query patterns in pg_stat_statements). (3) Before dropping: create it as a CONCURRENTLY-droppable candidate — mark it in a maintenance table. (4) DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_name — concurrent drop doesn't lock the table. (5) Monitor for one week: if nothing breaks, the drop was safe. Additional heuristic: indexes with idx_scan < 100/day and size > 1GB are strong drop candidates. Also watch for duplicate indexes: two indexes covering the same columns — the planner will only ever use one, making the other pure write overhead.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "a query is slow — how do you investigate and fix it?", say: "Step 1: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) the query — look for Seq Scan on large tables, high Rows Removed by Filter, and large Buffers (pages read). Step 2: Identify the access pattern — is this a point lookup (equality), range query, or aggregate? Step 3: Create the right index. For WHERE a=x AND b>y: composite (a, b) with equality before range. For SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE a=x: covering index (a) INCLUDE (b) for an index-only scan. For WHERE status='pending' (1% of rows): partial index WHERE status='pending'. Step 4: EXPLAIN again to confirm 'Index Only Scan' or 'Index Scan' with expected row counts. Step 5: Check write impact — count total indexes on the table. More than 8 indexes on a write-heavy table may slow writes more than reads benefit. Use pg_stat_user_indexes to find unused indexes and drop them. Always create indexes CONCURRENTLY in production to avoid table locks."

Post 28

Service Discovery & Load Balancing

25 min deep dive DNS-based · Service Registry · Client-Side LB · Server-Side LB · Health Checks · Envoy · kube-proxy
The Problem

In a dynamic environment, where is the instance you need to call right now?

In a static world: hardcode payment-service.prod.internal:8080 in your config. Done. In modern cloud environments with Kubernetes, auto-scaling groups, and rolling deploys, service instances have ephemeral IPs that change constantly. A new pod gets a random IP when it starts. Spot instances are terminated and replaced. Rolling deploys bring up new pods while retiring old ones. If you hardcode IP addresses, your service will be calling dead endpoints within minutes.

Two Problems to Solve

Service Discovery: how does a caller find the current set of healthy IP:port pairs for a service? It must discover them dynamically from a registry, not from static config. Load Balancing: given a set of N healthy instances, which one does this specific request go to? The algorithm must distribute load evenly, avoid unhealthy instances, and ideally route to the instance most likely to respond quickly.

Building Intuition — four discovery and routing patterns

DNS, service registry, client-side, and server-side — each with different trade-offs

DNS-based discovery (simplest)

Each service has a DNS name (e.g. payment-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local). The DNS record returns all healthy IP addresses. Callers resolve DNS and get a random IP. Problem: DNS TTL caches the IP list. When an instance dies, the caller's DNS cache still points to it for up to TTL seconds. Low DNS TTL (e.g. 5s) reduces staleness but increases DNS query volume. Kubernetes DNS (CoreDNS) uses this pattern: kube-proxy keeps endpoints updated, CoreDNS returns ClusterIP (virtual IP) → iptables rules route to actual pod IPs.

Service registry (etcd, Consul, Eureka)

A dedicated service registry stores the list of healthy instances. Services self-register on startup and send heartbeats to keep their registration alive. On heartbeat failure, the registry deregisters them (after N failures). Callers watch the registry for changes and get notified immediately when instances appear/disappear. Much faster failure detection than DNS TTL. etcd: Kubernetes stores all service and endpoint state here. Consul: provides health checking, DNS interface, and key-value store. Eureka (Netflix OSS): client-side discovery with eventual consistency.

Client-side load balancing

The caller fetches the instance list from the registry, caches it locally, and picks an instance per request using its own load balancing algorithm. Algorithms: Round-Robin (rotate through the list), Random, Least-Connections (pick the instance with fewest active requests), Weighted (prefer faster instances). No extra network hop. The caller can implement sophisticated routing (zone-affinity, latency-based). Used by: Netflix Ribbon, gRPC with client-side LB, Kubernetes headless services. Downside: each service must implement LB logic; instance list can be stale if registry update is delayed.

Server-side load balancing (L4 and L7 proxies)

A proxy (L4: TCP/UDP routing; L7: HTTP-aware routing) sits between caller and service instances. The proxy handles discovery, health checking, and load balancing. Callers connect to a single VIP (Virtual IP). The proxy makes all routing decisions. L4 proxy: routes TCP connections based on IP/port. Fast, protocol-agnostic. L7 proxy: parses HTTP, can route by path, header, cookie. Supports retries, circuit breaking, rate limiting. Used by: Envoy (Kubernetes service mesh), AWS ALB/NLB, NGINX, HAProxy, kube-proxy (iptables/eBPF). The proxy is simpler for callers but adds a network hop and becomes a potential bottleneck.

Diagram 1 · Client-Side vs Server-Side Discovery — request routing flow

Client asks registry, picks instance (client-side) vs proxy receives request and routes (server-side)

service-discovery-patterns.svg
Animation · Health Check Failure → Registry Deregistration → Traffic Rerouted

Watch B:3 crash, health checks fail, registry removes it, traffic routes only to B:1 and B:2

service-discovery-animation
Ready
Load Balancing Algorithms and Health Checks

Choosing the right algorithm — and why least-connections beats round-robin for slow backends

Load balancing algorithms compared

Round-Robin: rotate through instances sequentially. Simple, even distribution for uniform request cost. Problem: if some requests take 10ms and others take 1000ms, some instances accumulate long queues while others are idle. Least-Connections: route to the instance with fewest active connections. Better for variable-duration requests — long-running requests don't pile up on one instance. Random: statistically converges to even distribution. No coordination needed. Weighted Round-Robin: assign weights to instances (faster hardware gets more traffic). IP Hash: hash(client_ip) → always same instance for same client. Useful for stateful sessions (but breaks if an instance is removed). Power of Two Choices: pick two random instances, send to the one with fewer connections. Near-optimal distribution with O(1) overhead. Used by Nginx, Envoy.

Health checks — active vs passive

Active health checks (Consul, Envoy): the load balancer/registry periodically probes each instance (GET /health). Detects failure within seconds. Tune: interval (how often to check), timeout (how long before declaring failure), failure_threshold (failures before deregistration). Kubernetes readinessProbe does this — controls whether the pod receives traffic. Passive health checks (outlier detection): the proxy observes actual request error rates and latency. If an instance returns 5xx for >20% of requests, it's ejected from the pool temporarily. Envoy outlier detection uses this. More accurate (based on real traffic) but detects failures only when requests are actively sent to the failing instance.

Code · Python & Java — client-side load balancer with health checking

Round-Robin, Least-Connections, and health-aware routing

PYTHON
import threading, time, random, requests
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional
from enum import Enum, auto

class LBAlgorithm(Enum):
    ROUND_ROBIN       = auto()
    LEAST_CONNECTIONS = auto()
    RANDOM            = auto()

@dataclass
class ServiceInstance:
    host:       str
    port:       int
    weight:     int = 1
    healthy:    bool = True
    active_connections: int = 0
    total_requests: int = 0
    failure_count:  int = 0

    @property
    def address(self) -> str:
        return f"{self.host}:{self.port}"


class LoadBalancer:
    """
    Client-side load balancer with:
    - Multiple LB algorithms
    - Active health checking
    - Automatic instance removal on failure
    """

    def __init__(self,
                 instances: List[ServiceInstance],
                 algorithm: LBAlgorithm = LBAlgorithm.ROUND_ROBIN,
                 health_check_path: str = "/health",
                 health_check_interval: float = 5.0,
                 failure_threshold: int = 3):
        self.instances        = instances
        self.algorithm        = algorithm
        self.health_check_path = health_check_path
        self.failure_threshold = failure_threshold
        self._lock            = threading.Lock()
        self._round_robin_idx = 0
        # Start background health check
        t = threading.Thread(
            target=self._health_check_loop,
            args=(health_check_interval,),
            daemon=True
        )
        t.start()

    def get_instance(self) -> Optional[ServiceInstance]:
        """Pick the next instance based on the LB algorithm."""
        with self._lock:
            healthy = [i for i in self.instances if i.healthy]
            if not healthy: raise RuntimeError("No healthy instances!")

            if self.algorithm == LBAlgorithm.ROUND_ROBIN:
                idx = self._round_robin_idx % len(healthy)
                self._round_robin_idx += 1
                return healthy[idx]

            elif self.algorithm == LBAlgorithm.LEAST_CONNECTIONS:
                return min(healthy, key=lambda i: i.active_connections)

            elif self.algorithm == LBAlgorithm.RANDOM:
                return random.choice(healthy)

    def _health_check_loop(self, interval: float) -> None:
        while True:
            for inst in self.instances:
                try:
                    url = f"http://{inst.address}{self.health_check_path}"
                    resp = requests.get(url, timeout=2)
                    if resp.status_code == 200:
                        with self._lock:
                            inst.failure_count = 0
                            if not inst.healthy:
                                inst.healthy = True
                                print(f"[LB] {inst.address} recovered → back in rotation")
                    else:
                        self._record_failure(inst)
                except Exception:
                    self._record_failure(inst)
            time.sleep(interval)

    def _record_failure(self, inst: ServiceInstance) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            inst.failure_count += 1
            if inst.failure_count >= self.failure_threshold and inst.healthy:
                inst.healthy = False
                print(f"[LB] {inst.address} FAILED ({inst.failure_count}x) → removed from rotation")


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
instances = [
    ServiceInstance("10.0.0.1", 8080),
    ServiceInstance("10.0.0.2", 8080),
    ServiceInstance("10.0.0.3", 8080),
]
lb = LoadBalancer(instances, algorithm=LBAlgorithm.LEAST_CONNECTIONS)

# Make 6 requests — should distribute evenly
for _ in range(6):
    inst = lb.get_instance()
    print(f"Routing to {inst.address}")
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;

record Instance(String host, int port,
                 AtomicBoolean healthy,
                 AtomicInteger activeConns,
                 AtomicInteger failures) {
    Instance(String h, int p) {
        this(h, p, new AtomicBoolean(true),
             new AtomicInteger(0), new AtomicInteger(0));
    }
    String address() { return host + ":" + port; }
}

class LoadBalancer {
    private final List<Instance>   instances;
    private final AtomicInteger     rrIdx = new AtomicInteger(0);
    private final int               failureThreshold;
    private final ScheduledExecutorService scheduler =
        Executors.newSingleThreadScheduledExecutor();

    public LoadBalancer(List<Instance> instances, int failureThreshold) {
        this.instances        = instances;
        this.failureThreshold = failureThreshold;
        // Background health checks every 5s
        scheduler.scheduleAtFixedRate(this::runHealthChecks, 5, 5, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
    }

    // ── Round-Robin ─────────────────────────────────────────
    public Instance getRoundRobin() {
        List<Instance> healthy = healthyInstances();
        if (healthy.isEmpty()) throw new IllegalStateException("No healthy instances");
        return healthy.get(rrIdx.getAndIncrement() % healthy.size());
    }

    // ── Least Connections ───────────────────────────────────
    public Instance getLeastConnections() {
        return healthyInstances().stream()
            .min(Comparator.comparingInt(i -> i.activeConns().get()))
            .orElseThrow(() -> new IllegalStateException("No healthy instances"));
    }

    // ── Power of Two Choices ────────────────────────────────
    public Instance getPowerOfTwo() {
        List<Instance> h = healthyInstances();
        if (h.size() < 2) return h.get(0);
        Random rng = new Random();
        Instance a = h.get(rng.nextInt(h.size()));
        Instance b = h.get(rng.nextInt(h.size()));
        return a.activeConns().get() <= b.activeConns().get() ? a : b;
    }

    private List<Instance> healthyInstances() {
        return instances.stream()
            .filter(i -> i.healthy().get()).toList();
    }

    private void runHealthChecks() {
        for (Instance inst : instances) {
            try {
                // Simplified: in prod use HttpClient.newHttpClient()
                boolean ok = pingHealth(inst);
                if (ok) {
                    inst.failures().set(0);
                    if (!inst.healthy().get()) {
                        inst.healthy().set(true);
                        System.out.println(inst.address() + " recovered");
                    }
                } else {
                    int fails = inst.failures().incrementAndGet();
                    if (fails >= failureThreshold && inst.healthy().compareAndSet(true, false))
                        System.out.println(inst.address() + " FAILED → removed");
                }
            } catch (Exception ignored) {}
        }
    }

    private boolean pingHealth(Instance inst) { return true; } // stub

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        var lb = new LoadBalancer(List.of(
            new Instance("10.0.0.1", 8080),
            new Instance("10.0.0.2", 8080),
            new Instance("10.0.0.3", 8080)
        ), 3);
        for (int i = 0; i < 6; i++)
            System.out.println("→ " + lb.getRoundRobin().address());
        // → 10.0.0.1:8080, 10.0.0.2:8080, 10.0.0.3:8080 (repeating)
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Service Discovery and Load Balancing

QWhat is the difference between client-side and server-side service discovery?
A
Client-side discovery: the caller queries the service registry directly (e.g. etcd, Consul) to get the list of healthy instance IPs. The caller then runs a load balancing algorithm locally to pick which instance to call. No extra network hop. The caller can implement sophisticated routing (zone affinity, latency-based). But each service must implement and maintain its own LB logic, and the registry client adds complexity. Examples: Netflix Ribbon, gRPC client-side LB, Kubernetes headless services. Server-side discovery: the caller sends all requests to a single virtual IP (VIP) or DNS name. A load balancer/proxy (Envoy, NGINX, AWS ALB, kube-proxy) sits in the middle, queries the registry on the caller's behalf, and routes the request to a specific backend instance. Callers don't know about individual instances — they just call the VIP. Centralises routing logic, supports retries and circuit breaking transparently. Adds one network hop and the proxy can be a bottleneck. Examples: Kubernetes ClusterIP service, AWS ALB, Envoy service mesh.
QHow does a service registry detect and remove unhealthy instances?
A
Two mechanisms: (1) Active health checks: the registry (Consul, Envoy) periodically probes each registered instance with an HTTP/TCP/script check. If the instance returns a non-200 response or times out for N consecutive checks, it's marked unhealthy and removed from the rotation. Configurable: interval (how often), timeout (response deadline), failure_threshold (how many failures before removing). Kubernetes readinessProbe implements this — a pod that fails its readinessProbe is removed from the Service's endpoint list. (2) TTL-based heartbeat: the instance itself sends periodic "I'm alive" messages to the registry. If the TTL expires without a renewal, the registry automatically removes the instance. This is how etcd leases work for service registration. Active checks are more reliable (detect network issues the instance can't report). TTL-based is better for detecting process deaths. Production pattern: use both — the instance sends heartbeats AND the registry runs active HTTP checks. The heartbeat detects the instance side; the HTTP check detects partial failures (port listening but returning errors).
QWhen would you use least-connections load balancing instead of round-robin?
A
Round-robin distributes requests evenly in count. Least-connections distributes load evenly in concurrent work. The difference matters when requests have variable duration. If some requests take 1ms (cache hit) and others take 500ms (DB query), round-robin will pile up 500ms requests on some instances while others are idle after their 1ms requests complete. Least-connections routes each new request to the instance with the fewest active connections — naturally avoiding overloaded instances. Use least-connections when: request latency has high variance (P99 >> P50), backend instances have different capacities, or you have long-lived connections (WebSocket, gRPC streaming). Round-robin is fine when: request durations are uniform (e.g. static file serving), instances are homogeneous, and simplicity matters. Envoy and NGINX both support least-connections. Kubernetes kube-proxy uses round-robin via iptables rules (no connection tracking). For Kubernetes workloads with variable request cost, consider an L7 proxy (like Envoy/Istio) with least-connections instead of relying on kube-proxy's round-robin.
QHow does Kubernetes service discovery work internally?
A
Kubernetes uses a combination of kube-proxy, CoreDNS, and etcd. When you create a Service object, Kubernetes assigns a ClusterIP (a virtual IP that doesn't map to any physical network interface). CoreDNS creates DNS records: my-svc.my-namespace.svc.cluster.local → ClusterIP. kube-proxy watches the Kubernetes API server for Service and Endpoint objects. When the endpoint list changes (pods start/stop), kube-proxy updates iptables (or eBPF) rules on every node to route traffic from ClusterIP → one of the healthy pod IPs using round-robin. readinessProbe controls whether a pod appears in the Endpoints list — if a pod fails its readinessProbe, it's removed from Endpoints and kube-proxy stops routing to it. Result: a calling service just hits the DNS name, which resolves to ClusterIP, which iptables (kube-proxy) routes to a healthy pod. The caller never sees individual pod IPs. Headless services (clusterIP: None): DNS returns the set of pod IPs directly — client-side discovery without kube-proxy routing.
QWhat is the "power of two choices" algorithm and why is it better than random?
A
Random load balancing picks a random instance per request with no awareness of current load. In theory this is fine — the law of large numbers ensures even distribution over time. In practice, with variable-duration requests, some instances can accumulate queues by chance. Power of Two Choices is a refinement: pick two random instances, then send to the one with fewer active connections. It sounds minor but the impact is significant: random selection gives O(log log N / log log log N) max load imbalance; Power of Two Choices gives O(log log N) — a dramatic improvement in tail latency. Intuition: you're not just picking randomly, you're doing one comparison and always avoiding the currently-overloaded option. This one comparison costs nearly nothing (just read two connection counters) but gives near-optimal load distribution. Used by Nginx (with "least_conn" + 2 candidates), Envoy outlier detection + round-robin combination, and Twitter's Finagle load balancer. The "best of N choices" generalises: sampling 3 or more candidates continues to improve but with diminishing returns. 2 is usually the sweet spot of performance vs overhead.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you design service discovery for a microservices platform that can handle 1,000 services and 50,000 instances?", say: "I'd use etcd as the service registry (built-in to Kubernetes) with watch-based propagation — callers don't poll, they receive push notifications when the endpoint list changes, making updates sub-second. For load balancing: deploy Envoy as a sidecar per service (Istio service mesh). Each Envoy sidecar watches the service registry via xDS API, maintains the endpoint list locally, and uses least-connections within the same availability zone (zone-affinity) to minimise cross-AZ traffic. For unhealthy instance detection: two layers — readinessProbe removes pods from the endpoint list immediately on failure, AND Envoy outlier detection ejects instances that have a high 5xx rate (passive health checking based on real traffic). This dual-layer detection handles both 'process died' (readinessProbe catches it) and 'process alive but returning errors' (outlier detection catches it). At 50,000 instances, the xDS control plane (Istiod) must efficiently push partial updates — only changed endpoints, not the full list — to avoid thundering herd when one service's endpoints change."

Post 29

Observability: SLOs & Alerting

25 min deep dive SLI · SLO · SLA · Error Budget · Burn Rate · Multi-window Alerting · RED Method · Alert Fatigue
The Problem

200 alerts per day — engineers are numb. A real outage is buried in noise.

Traditional monitoring: alert when CPU > 80%, alert when error rate > 1%, alert when latency > 500ms. Three problems: (1) These thresholds are arbitrary and uncalibrated. (2) A CPU spike that doesn't affect users fires an alert and wakes someone up at 3am for no reason. (3) A slow memory leak that gradually degrades user experience for days doesn't trigger any threshold until it's catastrophic. Alert fatigue sets in — engineers start ignoring alerts. When a real outage happens, it's buried in noise. The solution: measure what matters to users, not what's easy to instrument.

The SLO Framework

SLI (Service Level Indicator): a measured, quantitative signal that users actually care about. Request success rate, p99 latency, data freshness. SLO (Service Level Objective): a target for the SLI. "99.9% of requests succeed over a rolling 30-day window." Error Budget: the 0.1% of allowed failures = 43.8 minutes/month. Budget funds risk-taking (deploys, experiments). Burn Rate: how fast you're consuming the budget. A 14× burn rate means you'll exhaust the monthly budget in 2 days. Alert on burn rate, not raw metrics — this eliminates 95% of noise.

Building Intuition — error budget and burn rate

The budget is spent on every user-impacting failure. Burn rate is the velocity of spending.

Error budget calculation

SLO = 99.9% availability over 30 days. Error budget = (1 - 0.999) × 30 days × 24 hours × 60 min = 43.8 minutes of downtime allowed per month. If your error rate is 5% for 1 hour, you've consumed: 5% × 60 min = 3 minutes of budget... wait, that's not right. Actually: at 5% error rate, 5% of requests fail. SLO allows 0.1% to fail. Excess failure rate = 5% - 0.1% = 4.9%. If 5% error rate lasts for 10 minutes at normal traffic, and normal traffic = 1,000 req/s: failures = 5% × 1,000 × 600 = 30,000 failed requests. Budget allows 0.1% = 1,000 failed requests over 30 days. 30,000 / (budget of 1,000 × total requests over 30 days) = you've exhausted a large portion of the budget in 10 minutes.

Multi-window burn rate alerting

A 14× burn rate for 1 hour = exhausts the monthly budget in 2 days. A 1.1× burn rate sustained for a month silently exhausts the budget. Use two alert windows: Fast alert (1h window, high burn rate threshold e.g. 14×): catches fast-burning incidents. Slow alert (6h window, medium burn rate e.g. 6×): catches sustained moderate degradations. Critically: a short spike at 14× for 5 minutes doesn't page anyone — the 1-hour window smooths it out. A real outage that burns at 14× for 30+ minutes will hit the 1-hour window threshold. This is why burn-rate alerting has dramatically lower false-positive rates than threshold alerting.

Diagram 1 · Error Budget Anatomy — SLI, SLO, budget, burn rate

99.9% SLO = 43.8 min/month. Every incident burns the budget. Deploy freeze when empty.

error-budget-anatomy.svg
Animation · Error Budget Burning — incident spikes, warning fires, critical pages

Watch the budget drain during an incident and the multi-window burn rate alerts fire

error-budget-animation
Ready
The Three Pillars and the RED Method

Metrics, Logs, and Traces — and how RED gives you three SLI candidates for free

The Three Pillars of Observability

Metrics (Prometheus): time-series aggregates. Request count, error rate, CPU, memory. Cheap to store, fast to query, no per-request detail. Best for alerting and dashboards. Logs (ELK/Loki): structured event records. Every request, every error, every state change. Expensive to store, slow to query at scale, rich in detail. Best for debugging specific incidents. Traces (Jaeger, Post 16): distributed request flows. Shows which service took how long for a specific request. Critical for debugging latency in microservices. Use metrics to detect and alert, traces to diagnose, logs to understand details.

The RED Method — three SLIs every service should have

Rate: requests per second. Is the service receiving traffic? A rate drop to 0 often indicates a problem before error rate climbs. Errors: error rate (5xx, timeouts, business logic failures). The primary availability SLI. Duration: latency distribution — p50, p90, p99. P99 catches tail latency affecting the worst-off users. For databases, prefer p99 read/write latency over avg. The RED method gives you three SLI candidates that together detect virtually any user-impacting failure in a service. Complement with USE (Utilisation, Saturation, Errors) for infrastructure/databases.

Code · Python & Prometheus QL — SLI collection, error budget, burn rate alert

Instrument a service, compute error budget, write Prometheus burn rate alerts

PYTHON
# pip install prometheus-client fastapi
from prometheus_client import (
    Counter, Histogram, Gauge, start_http_server
)
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, Response
import time

# ── RED Metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) ──────────────────
REQUEST_COUNT = Counter(
    "http_requests_total",
    "Total HTTP requests",
    ["method", "endpoint", "status_code"]
)
REQUEST_LATENCY = Histogram(
    "http_request_duration_seconds",
    "HTTP request latency",
    ["method", "endpoint"],
    # Buckets tuned to your SLO latency target
    buckets=[0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0]
)
ERROR_BUDGET_REMAINING = Gauge(
    "slo_error_budget_remaining_ratio",
    "Fraction of error budget remaining (0-1)",
    ["service", "slo_name"]
)

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def metrics_middleware(request: Request, call_next) -> Response:
    """Instrument every request with RED metrics."""
    start = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    duration = time.perf_counter() - start
    endpoint = request.url.path

    REQUEST_COUNT.labels(
        method=request.method,
        endpoint=endpoint,
        status_code=response.status_code
    ).inc()

    REQUEST_LATENCY.labels(
        method=request.method,
        endpoint=endpoint
    ).observe(duration)

    return response


# ── SLO Tracker ───────────────────────────────────────────
class SLOTracker:
    """
    Computes error budget remaining and burn rate.
    Reads from Prometheus via its HTTP API in production.
    """

    def __init__(self, slo_target: float = 0.999,
                 window_days: int = 30):
        self.slo_target   = slo_target
        self.window_secs  = window_days * 86400
        self.allowed_fail = 1 - slo_target   # 0.001 for 99.9%

    def error_budget_remaining(self,
                                total_requests: int,
                                failed_requests: int) -> float:
        """
        Returns fraction of error budget remaining (0.0 to 1.0).
        < 0 means budget is exhausted.
        """
        allowed_failures = self.allowed_fail * total_requests
        actual_failures  = failed_requests
        remaining        = (allowed_failures - actual_failures) / allowed_failures
        return max(0.0, min(1.0, remaining))

    def burn_rate(self, actual_error_rate: float) -> float:
        """
        Burn rate = actual error rate / SLO error rate.
        1.0 = consuming budget at exactly the planned rate.
        14.0 = burning through monthly budget in 2 days.
        """
        if self.allowed_fail == 0: return float('inf')
        return actual_error_rate / self.allowed_fail

    def should_page(self, actual_error_rate: float,
                      window_burn_rate: float = 14.0) -> bool:
        """Alert if current error rate implies budget exhaustion within 2 days."""
        return self.burn_rate(actual_error_rate) > window_burn_rate


# ── Demo ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
tracker = SLOTracker(slo_target=0.999)

# During normal operation
print(tracker.burn_rate(0.0003))   # → 0.3× (healthily below budget)

# During incident
print(tracker.burn_rate(0.05))     # → 50× (depleting monthly budget in 14 hours!)
print(tracker.should_page(0.05))   # → True

budget = tracker.error_budget_remaining(
    total_requests=1_000_000,
    failed_requests=500     # 500 failures so far this month
)
print(f"Budget remaining: {budget:.1%}")  # → 50.0%

# Start Prometheus metrics server
# start_http_server(9090)  # scrape at :9090/metrics
PROMQL
-- ── Core SLI: Success Rate ────────────────────────────────
-- Fraction of successful requests over last 5 minutes
sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"5.."}[5m]))
  /
sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m]))

-- ── Burn Rate (1-hour window) ─────────────────────────────
-- How fast is the error budget burning?
-- SLO = 99.9%, so allowed error rate = 0.001
(
  1 - (
    sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"5.."}[1h]))
    /
    sum(rate(http_requests_total[1h]))
  )
) / 0.001
-- Result: 14 means burning at 14× normal rate

-- ── Burn Rate (6-hour window for slow-burn alert) ─────────
(
  1 - (
    sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"5.."}[6h]))
    /
    sum(rate(http_requests_total[6h]))
  )
) / 0.001

-- ── Error Budget Remaining (30-day window) ────────────────
1 - (
  (1 - sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code!~"5.."}[30d]))
       /
       sum(rate(http_requests_total[30d]))
  ) / 0.001
)
-- Result: 0.75 means 75% of budget remains

-- ── Alert Rule: Critical Burn Rate ───────────────────────
-- Fires if both 1h and 5min windows show burn_rate > 14
-- (Both windows required: reduces false positives)
--
-- YAML alert rule (Prometheus Alertmanager):
--
-- groups:
--   - name: slo_alerts
--     rules:
--       - alert: HighBurnRateCritical
--         expr: |
--           burn_rate_1h > 14
--           and
--           burn_rate_5m > 14
--         for: 2m
--         labels:
--           severity: critical
--           team: payments
--         annotations:
--           summary: "Payments SLO budget burning at {{ $value }}× rate"
--           description: "At current rate, 30-day budget exhausted in 2 days"
--           runbook: "https://runbooks.example.com/payments/high-error-rate"
--
--       - alert: HighBurnRateWarning
--         expr: |
--           burn_rate_6h > 6
--           and
--           burn_rate_30m > 6
--         for: 15m
--         labels:
--           severity: warning

-- ── P99 Latency SLO (availability ≥ 99% within 200ms) ─────
histogram_quantile(
  0.99,
  sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
)
-- Alert if P99 > 0.2s (your latency SLO)
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about SLOs and Observability

QWhat is the difference between SLI, SLO, SLA, and error budget?
A
SLI (Service Level Indicator): a precise quantitative measure of service behaviour — request success rate, P99 latency, data freshness. Something you can measure directly. SLO (Service Level Objective): a target for the SLI, measured over a time window. "99.9% of requests succeed over a rolling 30-day window." The SLO is an internal commitment — missing it signals a reliability problem but has no external consequence. SLA (Service Level Agreement): a contractual commitment to customers with financial consequences for breach. SLA target is always lower than SLO target (SLO=99.9%, SLA=99.5%) to provide a buffer. If you hit the SLO, you'll easily satisfy the SLA. Error budget: (1 - SLO) × window = the allowed failure allocation. At 99.9% over 30 days: 0.1% × 43,800 min = 43.8 minutes of downtime allowed. Budget quantifies how much unreliability you can absorb. It funds risk-taking: when budget is healthy, teams can deploy aggressively. When budget is low, they should freeze changes and focus on reliability.
QWhat is burn rate and why should you alert on it instead of raw error rate?
A
Burn rate = actual error rate / SLO error rate. If SLO allows 0.1% errors and your actual error rate is 1%, burn rate = 10× — meaning you'll exhaust the monthly budget in 3 days instead of 30. Alerting on raw error rate (e.g. "alert when error rate > 1%") has two failure modes: (1) False positives: 1% error rate during a 1-minute spike at 3am burns almost no budget and wakes someone up for nothing. (2) False negatives: a sustained 0.11% error rate (just above SLO) burns budget slowly over weeks without triggering a 1% threshold alert. Burn rate alerting solves both: a 1-minute spike at any rate barely affects the multi-hour burn rate window. A sustained 0.11% error rate shows up as a consistent 1.1× burn rate and fires the slow-burn alert. The multi-window approach (1h + 5min for critical, 6h + 30min for warning) combines fast detection with false-positive reduction: both windows must fire, preventing transient spikes from paging.
QWhat are the three pillars of observability and when do you use each?
A
Metrics: aggregated numerical time-series (Prometheus/OpenMetrics). Per-second aggregates of request counts, error rates, latency percentiles. Cheap to store, fast to query. Use for alerting (burn rate, SLO breach), dashboards (Grafana), capacity planning. Cannot answer "why is this specific request failing?" Traces: distributed request flows (Jaeger, Zipkin, Post 16). Show every service a request touched, timing per hop, error context. Expensive to store (especially at-scale). Use for diagnosing latency regressions, debugging inter-service failures, finding the critical path. Logs: structured event records (Elasticsearch, Loki). Every individual event with full context. Most expensive to store and query at scale. Use for debugging a specific error ("find all requests from user 42 that failed in the last hour"), auditing, security analysis. Workflow: metrics detect the problem (SLO burn rate fires alert), traces diagnose which service is responsible, logs explain the specific error messages causing the failures. All three are necessary — metrics alone can't explain root cause, traces alone can't scale to alert, logs alone can't provide real-time alerting.
QHow do you choose a good SLI?
A
A good SLI directly measures the user's experience, not an internal implementation detail. Tests: (1) If the SLI is good, are users happy? (2) If the SLI is bad, are users unhappy? Both should be yes. Bad SLI: CPU utilisation. CPU at 90% might be fine if users don't notice. CPU at 50% might be terrible if requests are failing. Good SLI: request success rate. Directly measures whether users are getting their requests fulfilled. Practical guidance for common services: HTTP APIs → success rate (non-5xx / total) and p99 latency. Data pipelines → freshness (age of latest processed record). Batch jobs → % successful completions within SLO deadline. Storage → durability (data not lost), read/write success rate. Avoid SLIs that measure system capacity (CPU, memory) — they're inputs, not outcomes. Measure outcomes. The RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration) gives you three good SLI candidates for any request-driven service.
QYour team is getting 200 alerts per day and people are ignoring them. How do you fix it?
A
Alert fatigue is a symptom of alerts that don't require action. Fix process: (1) Audit all existing alerts for the last 30 days. Categorise each alert as: "acted on and improved a user-impacting problem" vs "investigated and found no user impact" vs "ignored / silenced". Delete every alert in the second and third categories — they are false positives. (2) For remaining alerts: is the alert actionable? If you don't know what to do when it fires, it's not ready — write a runbook first. (3) Switch from threshold alerts to burn rate alerts for any availability/latency SLI. This typically eliminates 80-90% of noisy alerts. (4) Ensure every alert maps to user impact. "Cache hit rate dropped" is not a direct alert — the right alert is "request p99 latency exceeded SLO" which may be caused by cache issues but only alerts when users actually experience it. (5) Make silencing easy — if an alert fires for a known, non-urgent issue, make it trivial to snooze for 4 hours. Friction in silencing causes people to ignore instead of snooze. (6) Run a weekly alert review meeting for 30 days: was every paged alert justified? Delete what wasn't.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design an SLO framework for a payments API", say: "Two SLOs: (1) Availability SLO: 99.95% of payment requests succeed over a rolling 28-day window. Error budget = 0.05% × 28 days = 20.2 minutes. SLI = (requests returning 2xx) / total_requests. (2) Latency SLO: 99% of payment requests complete within 2 seconds (P99 < 2s). Error budget = 1% of requests may be slow. Alerts: two burn-rate alerts per SLO. Critical (14× burn rate on 1h + 5m windows) → page on-call. Warning (6× burn rate on 6h + 30m windows) → Slack notification, investigate next business hour. Prometheus metrics: http_requests_total with status_code label, http_request_duration_seconds histogram with 0.1s to 5s buckets. Grafana dashboard: error budget remaining gauge, burn rate time-series, error rate time-series, p99 latency. Error budget policy: if budget drops below 50%, require post-incident review before new deploys. Below 10%: freeze deploys until budget recovers or SLO is adjusted. This gives the team a precise, quantified way to make reliability vs velocity trade-offs."

Post 30

Chaos Engineering & Resilience Patterns

25 min deep dive Bulkhead · Timeout · Circuit Breaker · Graceful Degradation · Fault Injection · GameDay · Chaos Monkey
The Problem

A slow payment service doesn't just degrade payments — it takes down your entire API

Your API has a single thread pool of 20 threads handling all requests. The payment service starts responding slowly (200ms → 2000ms). Each payment request now holds a thread for 10× longer. Within seconds, all 20 threads are blocked waiting for payment responses. New requests for EVERY endpoint — even the unrelated health check — queue up and timeout. Total service outage. The payment issue caused a full cascade failure. This is the cascading failure problem, and it's the most common cause of large-scale outages.

Resilience is About Failing Safely

You cannot prevent all failures. A third-party API will go down. A network partition will happen. A dependent service will have a memory leak. The goal of resilience engineering is: when a component fails, only that component's functionality is degraded — not the entire system. Users get a degraded but functional experience. The system auto-recovers when the failed component comes back. Every resilience pattern is a specific mechanism to achieve this isolation.

Building Intuition — four essential resilience patterns

Bulkhead, Timeout, Circuit Breaker, and Graceful Degradation — each addresses a different failure mode

Bulkhead — isolate failure domains with separate thread pools

Named after watertight compartments in ships. Give each dependency its own bounded thread pool. Payment service gets 5 threads. Database gets 10. Email gets 2. If payment hangs and fills its 5 threads, the database pool (10 threads) is completely unaffected. Other endpoints keep serving. Bulkheads convert cascading failures into contained failures. Semaphore vs Thread-pool bulkhead: thread-pool uses a separate executor (true isolation), semaphore just limits concurrent calls (lighter weight). Hystrix/Resilience4j implement both.

Timeout — never wait forever

Every external call must have a deadline. Without timeouts, a hung dependency holds a thread forever. Timeout pyramid: client calls API (10s max) → API calls service B (5s max) → B calls database (1s max). Each layer's timeout must be shorter than the outer layer's — otherwise the inner service finishes before the outer caller gives up and retries, causing duplicate operations. Timeout budgets: track elapsed time at the request level and abort downstream calls if less than X ms remain. Pass deadlines via gRPC context or HTTP headers.

Circuit Breaker — stop calling failing services (Post 15)

CLOSED → OPEN → HALF-OPEN. In OPEN state, calls fail immediately without even trying the downstream service. Prevents timeout storms: when a service is known to be failing, fast-fail all calls and let it recover, rather than having every thread spend 5 seconds timing out. Key metrics: error rate threshold to open, timeout to try half-open, success count to close. Covered in depth in Post 15.

Graceful Degradation — serve partial results when components fail

When a dependency fails, return a degraded but useful response instead of an error. Product page without recommendation widget = degraded but functional. User feed without real-time updates = serve cached version. Search without personalization = return generic results. The pattern: try primary path → if it fails or times out, try fallback (cache, default value, previous result). Never let an optional feature cause a hard error on a core user journey. Prioritise which features are "must-have" vs "nice-to-have" and wrap the latter in fallback logic.

Diagram 1 · Bulkhead Pattern — without vs with isolation

Shared thread pool = single failure brings down all endpoints. Separate pools = contained failure

bulkhead-pattern.svg
Animation · Bulkhead — payment hangs, cascades without isolation, contained with bulkhead

Step-by-step: same payment failure, two outcomes

chaos-bulkhead-animation
Ready
Chaos Engineering Practice

Deliberately injecting failures to find weaknesses before production finds them

The Chaos Engineering process

Define the steady state (what does "normal" look like — request rate, error rate, latency). Form a hypothesis ("the system will continue to serve 99.9% of requests if we kill one instance of service B"). Run the experiment (inject the failure during low-traffic hours). Measure the result (did the system maintain the steady state?). Fix gaps (if it didn't, the resilience mechanism is missing or broken). The goal is not to break things — it's to verify that the resilience mechanisms you think are in place actually work. Netflix Chaos Monkey randomly terminates EC2 instances in production during business hours — forcing the team to build actual auto-recovery rather than relying on "it probably auto-restarts". GameDay: a scheduled chaos experiment involving the whole team, testing full failure scenarios.

Common chaos experiments

(1) Instance kill: terminate a random service instance. Does auto-scaling recover? Does load balancer stop routing to it? (2) Network latency injection: add 200ms to all calls to service B. Does the circuit breaker open? Does the timeout pyramid prevent cascade? (3) Disk full: fill the disk on a node. Does the service degrade gracefully or crash with data corruption? (4) Clock skew: set a node's clock 2 minutes ahead. Does distributed consensus (Raft, ZooKeeper) break? (5) Dependency failure: block all traffic to the payment service. Do fallbacks activate? Do users see a helpful error or a 500? Tools: Netflix Chaos Monkey, Gremlin, AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS), Chaos Toolkit, LitmusChaos (Kubernetes).

Code · Python & Java — bulkhead, timeout, and graceful degradation

Implementing the four resilience patterns with Resilience4j and Python

PYTHON
import asyncio, time, random
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from typing import Callable, Optional, TypeVar
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from collections import deque
from enum import Enum, auto

T = TypeVar('T')

# ── Bulkhead ───────────────────────────────────────────────
class Bulkhead:
    """
    Thread-pool bulkhead: separate bounded executor per dependency.
    Rejects calls when pool is full rather than queuing indefinitely.
    """
    def __init__(self, max_concurrent: int, max_wait: float = 0.5):
        self._semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)
        self._max_wait  = max_wait
        self._active    = 0
        self._rejected  = 0

    async def execute(self, coro_fn: Callable) -> T:
        """Execute coroutine within this bulkhead's capacity."""
        try:
            await asyncio.wait_for(self._semaphore.acquire(), timeout=self._max_wait)
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            self._rejected += 1
            raise RuntimeError(f"Bulkhead full — request rejected (rejected={self._rejected})")

        self._active += 1
        try:
            return await coro_fn()
        finally:
            self._active -= 1
            self._semaphore.release()


# ── Circuit Breaker ────────────────────────────────────────
class CircuitState(Enum):
    CLOSED    = auto()
    OPEN      = auto()
    HALF_OPEN = auto()

class CircuitBreaker:
    def __init__(self, failure_threshold: int = 5,
                 recovery_timeout: float = 30.0,
                 success_threshold: int = 2):
        self.state              = CircuitState.CLOSED
        self._failure_threshold = failure_threshold
        self._recovery_timeout  = recovery_timeout
        self._success_threshold = success_threshold
        self._failure_count     = 0
        self._success_count     = 0
        self._opened_at: Optional[float] = None

    async def execute(self, coro_fn: Callable, fallback: Callable = None) -> T:
        if self.state == CircuitState.OPEN:
            if time.time() - self._opened_at > self._recovery_timeout:
                self.state = CircuitState.HALF_OPEN
                self._success_count = 0
            elif fallback:
                return await fallback()
            else:
                raise RuntimeError("Circuit OPEN — fast failing")

        try:
            result = await coro_fn()
            self._on_success()
            return result
        except Exception as e:
            self._on_failure()
            if fallback: return await fallback()
            raise

    def _on_success(self):
        if self.state == CircuitState.HALF_OPEN:
            self._success_count += 1
            if self._success_count >= self._success_threshold:
                self.state = CircuitState.CLOSED
                self._failure_count = 0

    def _on_failure(self):
        self._failure_count += 1
        if self._failure_count >= self._failure_threshold:
            self.state = CircuitState.OPEN
            self._opened_at = time.time()


# ── Graceful Degradation wrapper ──────────────────────────
async def with_fallback(primary: Callable, fallback: Callable,
                        timeout_secs: float = 1.0) -> T:
    """
    Try primary. On timeout or error, use fallback.
    Never let an optional feature error the core response.
    """
    try:
        return await asyncio.wait_for(primary(), timeout=timeout_secs)
    except (asyncio.TimeoutError, Exception) as e:
        print(f"Primary failed ({e}), using fallback")
        return await fallback()


# ── Demo: product page with resilient dependencies ─────────
payment_bulkhead = Bulkhead(max_concurrent=5)
payment_cb       = CircuitBreaker(failure_threshold=5, recovery_timeout=30)

async def get_product_page(product_id: int) -> dict:
    # Core: product data (must succeed)
    product = await fetch_product(product_id)  # no fallback — core feature

    # Optional: recommendations (graceful degradation if slow)
    recommendations = await with_fallback(
        primary=lambda: fetch_recommendations(product_id),
        fallback=lambda: asyncio.coroutine(lambda: [])(),  # empty list
        timeout_secs=0.2   # 200ms max for optional feature
    )

    # Optional: payment availability (with bulkhead + circuit breaker)
    async def check_payment():
        return await payment_bulkhead.execute(
            lambda: payment_cb.execute(
                lambda: call_payment_service(),
                fallback=lambda: asyncio.coroutine(lambda: {"available": False})()
            )
        )

    payment = await with_fallback(
        primary=check_payment,
        fallback=lambda: asyncio.coroutine(lambda: {"available": False})(),
        timeout_secs=1.0
    )

    return {"product": product, "recommendations": recommendations, "payment": payment}
JAVA
// Gradle: io.github.resilience4j:resilience4j-all:2.1.0
import io.github.resilience4j.bulkhead.*;
import io.github.resilience4j.circuitbreaker.*;
import io.github.resilience4j.timelimiter.*;
import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.function.Supplier;

class ResiliencePatterns {

    // ── Thread-pool Bulkhead ──────────────────────────────────
    private final ThreadPoolBulkhead paymentBulkhead =
        ThreadPoolBulkhead.of("payment",
            ThreadPoolBulkheadConfig.custom()
                .maxThreadPoolSize(5)         // max concurrent payment calls
                .coreThreadPoolSize(5)
                .queueCapacity(10)             // small queue then reject
                .keepAliveDuration(Duration.ofSeconds(20))
                .build());

    // ── Circuit Breaker ───────────────────────────────────────
    private final CircuitBreaker paymentCB =
        CircuitBreaker.of("payment",
            CircuitBreakerConfig.custom()
                .failureRateThreshold(50)        // open at 50% failure
                .slowCallRateThreshold(50)         // open at 50% slow calls
                .slowCallDurationThreshold(Duration.ofSeconds(1))
                .waitDurationInOpenState(Duration.ofSeconds(30))
                .permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState(3)
                .slidingWindowSize(10)
                .build());

    // ── Time Limiter ──────────────────────────────────────────
    private final TimeLimiter timeLimiter =
        TimeLimiter.of("payment",
            TimeLimiterConfig.custom()
                .timeoutDuration(Duration.ofSeconds(1))
                .build());

    // ── Compose: bulkhead + CB + timeout ──────────────────────
    public Map<String, Object> getProductPage(int productId) {
        Map<String, Object> result = new HashMap<>();

        // Core: product data — no fallback, must succeed
        result.put("product", fetchProduct(productId));

        // Optional: recommendations with timeout + fallback
        result.put("recommendations", withFallback(
            () -> fetchRecommendations(productId),
            () -> List.of(),         // empty list on failure
            Duration.ofMillis(200)  // 200ms max
        ));

        // Payment: bulkhead + CB + timeout + fallback
        Supplier<Map<String, Object>> paymentCall = CircuitBreaker.decorateSupplier(
            paymentCB,
            () -> callPaymentService()
        );

        result.put("payment", withFallback(
            () -> paymentBulkhead.executeSupplier(paymentCall),
            () -> Map.of("available", false, "reason", "Payment temporarily unavailable"),
            Duration.ofSeconds(1)
        ));

        return result;
    }

    private <T> T withFallback(Supplier<T> primary, Supplier<T> fallback, Duration timeout) {
        try {
            CompletableFuture<T> future = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(primary);
            return future.get(timeout.toMillis(), TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.err.println("Primary failed: " + e.getMessage() + ", using fallback");
            return fallback.get();
        }
    }

    // Stubs
    private Map<?,?> fetchProduct(int id) { return Map.of("id", id); }
    private List<?>  fetchRecommendations(int id) { return List.of(); }
    private Map<String,Object> callPaymentService() { return Map.of("available", true); }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Chaos Engineering and Resilience

QWhat is a cascading failure and how do bulkheads prevent it?
A
A cascading failure occurs when a failure in one component propagates to cause failures in other, unrelated components. The most common mechanism: a shared thread pool. Service A has 20 threads handling all requests. Service B (a dependency) starts responding slowly. Requests to Service B now hold threads for 10× longer. All 20 threads eventually fill up with waiting-for-B requests. New requests to Service A — even those that don't involve Service B at all — queue up and time out because there are no free threads to handle them. The slow dependency caused a complete outage. Bulkheads prevent this by isolating dependencies in separate bounded thread pools (or semaphores). Service B gets its own pool of 5 threads. If B fills those 5 threads, calls to B fail fast (rejected, not queued) — but Service A's other 15 threads continue serving unrelated requests. The failure is "waterproofed" to just the B-dependent functionality. The name comes from ship design: watertight compartments ensure that flooding one compartment doesn't sink the whole ship.
QWhat is the timeout pyramid and why does the inner timeout have to be shorter than the outer?
A
The timeout pyramid ensures that each layer's timeout is shorter than the layer above it: API gateway (10s) → service A (5s) → service B (2s) → database (500ms). This is required for correctness in two ways: (1) If the inner timeout is longer, the outer layer gives up and returns an error to the user. But the inner operation continues running and may complete — leading to a committed database write without the user knowing about it. This is especially dangerous for payment operations (double charges). (2) If the outer layer retries before the inner completes, you get parallel operations that race each other. The general rule: each nested timeout should be short enough that the operation completes (succeed or fail) before the outer layer decides to retry. Add the deadline to the request context (gRPC has built-in deadline propagation, HTTP can use custom headers like X-Request-Deadline) so nested services can check "do I even have enough time to attempt this before my caller has given up?" and bail early if not.
QWhat is chaos engineering and how is it different from traditional testing?
A
Traditional testing (unit, integration, load) tests whether the system works as designed. Chaos engineering tests whether the system fails gracefully when components fail unexpectedly. Traditional tests verify specified behaviour; chaos engineering explores unspecified failure modes. Process: define the system's "steady state" (the observable metrics that represent normal operation — request rate, error rate, p99 latency). Hypothesize what should happen when a specific failure is injected ("the system should maintain 99.9% availability if we kill one instance of service B"). Inject the failure in production (or staging, but production is more accurate). Measure whether the steady state was maintained. If not, the hypothesis was wrong — you found a resilience gap. Fix it. The key principle: "the confidence you gain from the experiment is proportional to the blast radius you're willing to accept." Running chaos experiments only in staging gives you confidence only about staging. Netflix runs Chaos Monkey in production precisely because production has unique failure modes that staging doesn't reveal.
QHow do you implement graceful degradation for a microservices product page?
A
Identify which features on the page are "must-have" (the user can't do anything without them) vs "nice-to-have" (the page is less useful but functional without them). For a product page: must-have = product title, price, add-to-cart button. Nice-to-have = recommendations, reviews, personalisation, real-time inventory count. Design: (1) Fetch must-have data synchronously — if this fails, return an error. (2) Fetch nice-to-have features in parallel with short timeouts (200ms). If any fail or time out, use a fallback: cached version, empty result, or a "loading" placeholder. (3) Never let a nice-to-have feature's failure propagate to the must-have response. Implementation: wrap each nice-to-have call in a try/catch with a fallback value. Use bulkheads so that a stuck recommendations service doesn't block the core product data fetch. Use circuit breakers so that if recommendations fails 5 times in a row, you stop calling it entirely and return the empty list immediately, reducing latency for users. Feature flags can control which features are optional vs required.
QYour service is experiencing timeouts on calls to a downstream dependency. What do you investigate?
A
