Systematic investigation: (1) Is the downstream service actually slow? Check its p99 latency in its own metrics. If it's slow internally, the fix is on their side. (2) Is it a network issue? Is the latency at the TCP connection level or after the request is received? tcpdump/Wireshark can show. (3) Is the connection pool exhausted? If all connections are in use, new calls queue behind them. Check pool metrics (active, idle, waiting). (4) Is there a GC pause? JVM GC stops the world. Check GC logs for pause duration — if they exceed the timeout, that's the cause. (5) Is the timeout too short? If p99 latency is 800ms and timeout is 500ms, 1% of requests will always timeout even when the service is healthy. Tune the timeout to p99.9 × 1.5. (6) Are retries amplifying the problem? Three retries × 1s timeout = 3s total wait. Retries also increase load on the already-slow service. Add exponential backoff and jitter to retries. (7) Is there a circuit breaker? Without one, all callers continue timing out at 1s each. With a circuit breaker, after N failures, calls fast-fail immediately, reducing load on the struggling service and helping it recover.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "how would you make a payments service resilient to downstream failures?", say: "Five layers: (1) Timeouts everywhere: payment calls have 1s timeout. Database queries 500ms. No call ever waits indefinitely. Pass deadlines in the gRPC context so nested calls bail early if the budget is nearly exhausted. (2) Bulkheads: payment service gets its own thread pool of 10 threads. If the payment processor API hangs, only those 10 threads fill. The rest of the API (user profiles, order history) keeps serving. (3) Circuit Breaker: if error rate exceeds 50% over the last 10 calls, open the circuit. Calls fail-fast immediately with a cached 'payment service temporarily unavailable' response. Try half-open after 30s. (4) Graceful degradation: the product page shows 'Pay later' with a payment-pending status rather than a 500 error. The order is saved to a pending-payment queue for retry when the circuit closes. (5) Chaos testing: run a GameDay quarterly where we inject a 2-second latency into the payment processor mock and verify that: circuit opens within 30s, the product page degrades gracefully, and no cascading failure occurs in unrelated services."

Post 31

TCC & Distributed Transactions

25 min deep dive Try · Confirm · Cancel · Idempotent Operations · Seata · vs Saga · vs 2PC · Reservation Pattern
The Problem

Saga compensates after failure — but the partial state was visible. TCC prevents it from ever being visible.

Saga pattern (Post 11): Book Hotel → Book Flight → Charge Payment. If payment fails, a compensating transaction refunds and cancels. But between the hotel booking and the payment failure, the hotel was reserved — visible to other users and to the system. For most business domains this is acceptable. But for financial transactions — a fund transfer that either fully completes or never happened — Saga's "visible intermediate state" is unacceptable. You need a stronger guarantee.

TCC: Two-Phase Business Transactions Without Database Locks

TCC (Try-Confirm-Cancel) works like 2PC but at the business logic level, not the database level. Each service implements three methods: Try — check preconditions and reserve/freeze resources. Confirm — execute the real operation using the reserved resources. Cancel — release the reservation without executing. Resources are frozen after Try — visible only to this transaction, not to other users. Only after all services confirm successfully do the resources actually transfer. If any Try or Confirm fails, Cancel releases all reservations atomically.

Building Intuition — TCC vs Saga vs 2PC

TCC lives between 2PC (correct but DB-coupled) and Saga (flexible but visible intermediate state)

2PC (Two-Phase Commit)

Prepare → Commit/Rollback at the database level. True atomicity. Problems: blocking protocol (coordinator failure = all participants blocked indefinitely), requires XA-compatible databases, huge performance overhead. Impractical for microservices spanning multiple databases. Used for: same-database transactions across different schemas, legacy system integration where databases support XA.

Saga (Post 11)

Chain of local transactions + compensating transactions. No distributed lock. High availability. Problem: visible intermediate state — other requests can observe partially-complete sagas. Dirty reads of in-progress sagas require semantic locks, careful ordering, and idempotency. Works for: most microservice workflows where eventual consistency is acceptable.

TCC

Three business methods per participant. Try: reserves resources (balance frozen, not debited). Confirm: executes (debit the frozen amount). Cancel: releases reservation (unfreeze, no debit). The reserved amount is invisible to other transactions until Confirm. No database-level lock — the reservation is tracked in application state. Works for: financial transfers, inventory allocation, any domain where intermediate state must be hidden. Cost: more complex implementation — every service must implement Try/Confirm/Cancel with idempotency.

Diagram 1 · TCC Phase Flow — happy path and failure path

Try reserves without executing. Confirm executes. Cancel releases. No visible intermediate state.

tcc-flow.svg
Animation · TCC with Payment Failure — Try freezes, payment fails, Cancel restores

Step-by-step: Account Try succeeds, Payment Try fails, Cancel restores account cleanly

tcc-animation
Ready
The Hard Parts — Idempotency and the "Empty Cancel" Problem

Every TCC operation must be idempotent — and Cancel must handle the case where Try never ran

Idempotency requirement

If the coordinator crashes after sending Confirm to Service A but before sending Confirm to Service B, it must retry. Service A's Confirm may be called twice. Service A must be idempotent for Confirm: the second call must have the same effect as the first (not double-debit). Implementation: use the transaction ID as an idempotency key. Before executing Confirm, check if this transaction ID has already been confirmed. If yes, return success without executing. This requires each service to persist the transaction state (a TCC log table).

The "Empty Cancel" (null rollback) problem

The coordinator sends Cancel before Try has arrived at a service (network partition, message reordering). The service receives Cancel for a transaction it has no record of. If Cancel just returns "not found", the coordinator marks it as cancelled. Later, Try arrives and executes — the resources are now frozen with no Cancel coming. They stay frozen forever (resource leak). Solution: the Cancel implementation must check if a Try was executed. If no Try record exists, record "this transaction was cancelled" and succeed. When Try arrives, it checks for a cancel record and returns "already cancelled" without freezing resources. This defensive Cancel is called a "null rollback guard".

Trade-offs — 2PC vs Saga vs TCC
distributed-tx-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python & Java — TCC participant implementation with idempotency

Try, Confirm, Cancel — each idempotent, with null-rollback guard

PYTHON
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum, auto
import uuid

class TxState(Enum):
    TRYING   = auto()
    CONFIRMED = auto()
    CANCELLED = auto()

@dataclass
class TccRecord:
    tx_id:   str
    state:   TxState
    frozen_amount: float = 0.0


# ── Account Service TCC Participant ───────────────────────
class AccountServiceTCC:
    """
    Implements TCC protocol for the account service.
    All three methods must be idempotent.
    """

    def __init__(self, balance: float):
        self._balance = balance
        self._frozen  = 0.0
        self._tcc_log: dict[str, TccRecord] = {}  # persistent in prod (DB)

    def try_freeze(self, tx_id: str, amount: float) -> bool:
        """
        Phase 1: Check balance and freeze amount.
        Idempotent: same tx_id → same result.
        Null-cancel guard: if already cancelled, return False.
        """
        # Idempotency check
        if tx_id in self._tcc_log:
            rec = self._tcc_log[tx_id]
            if rec.state == TxState.CANCELLED: return False
            if rec.state == TxState.TRYING: return True  # already tried
            return False

        # Precondition check
        available = self._balance - self._frozen
        if available < amount:
            return False

        # Freeze (not debit)
        self._frozen += amount
        self._tcc_log[tx_id] = TccRecord(tx_id, TxState.TRYING, amount)
        print(f"  [Try] balance={self._balance}, frozen={self._frozen}")
        return True

    def confirm_debit(self, tx_id: str) -> bool:
        """
        Phase 2: Debit the frozen amount.
        Idempotent: second call returns True without re-debiting.
        """
        rec = self._tcc_log.get(tx_id)
        if not rec:
            return False  # Try never happened
        if rec.state == TxState.CONFIRMED:
            return True  # already confirmed — idempotent
        if rec.state == TxState.CANCELLED:
            return False

        self._balance -= rec.frozen_amount
        self._frozen  -= rec.frozen_amount
        rec.state = TxState.CONFIRMED
        print(f"  [Confirm] balance={self._balance}, frozen={self._frozen}")
        return True

    def cancel_freeze(self, tx_id: str) -> bool:
        """
        Phase 2 (failure): Release frozen amount.
        Null-rollback guard: if Try never ran, record cancellation anyway.
        """
        rec = self._tcc_log.get(tx_id)

        if not rec:
            # Null rollback: Try never arrived. Record cancellation to prevent future Try.
            self._tcc_log[tx_id] = TccRecord(tx_id, TxState.CANCELLED, 0.0)
            print(f"  [Cancel] null rollback — no Try found, recording cancel")
            return True

        if rec.state == TxState.CANCELLED:
            return True  # already cancelled — idempotent
        if rec.state == TxState.CONFIRMED:
            return False  # too late to cancel confirmed transaction

        self._frozen -= rec.frozen_amount  # unfreeze
        rec.state = TxState.CANCELLED
        print(f"  [Cancel] balance={self._balance}, frozen={self._frozen}")
        return True


# ── Demo: TCC with payment failure ────────────────────────
account  = AccountServiceTCC(balance=500.0)
tx_id    = str(uuid.uuid4())

print("=== TCC Transaction: transfer $100 ===")
ok = account.try_freeze(tx_id, 100)
print(f"Try result: {ok}")   # True

# Payment service TRY fails (simulate: slot at capacity)
payment_ok = False

if not payment_ok:
    ok = account.cancel_freeze(tx_id)
    print(f"Cancel result: {ok}")   # True
    print(f"Final balance: ${account._balance}")  # $500 — unchanged ✓

# Idempotency: second cancel is a no-op
ok2 = account.cancel_freeze(tx_id)
print(f"Duplicate cancel: {ok2}")  # True — idempotent
JAVA
import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;

enum TxState { TRYING, CONFIRMED, CANCELLED }

record TccRecord(String txId, TxState state, double frozenAmount) {
    TccRecord withState(TxState s) { return new TccRecord(txId, s, frozenAmount); }
}

class AccountServiceTCC {
    private volatile double            balance;
    private volatile double            frozen = 0;
    // In production: use DB table with unique(tx_id) constraint
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, TccRecord> tccLog = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    AccountServiceTCC(double initialBalance) { this.balance = initialBalance; }

    public synchronized boolean tryFreeze(String txId, double amount) {
        TccRecord rec = tccLog.get(txId);
        if (rec != null) {
            return rec.state() == TxState.TRYING;  // idempotent
        }
        if (balance - frozen < amount) return false;
        frozen += amount;
        tccLog.put(txId, new TccRecord(txId, TxState.TRYING, amount));
        System.out.println("  [Try] balance="+balance+" frozen="+frozen);
        return true;
    }

    public synchronized boolean confirmDebit(String txId) {
        TccRecord rec = tccLog.get(txId);
        if (rec == null || rec.state() == TxState.CANCELLED) return false;
        if (rec.state() == TxState.CONFIRMED) return true;  // idempotent
        balance -= rec.frozenAmount();
        frozen  -= rec.frozenAmount();
        tccLog.put(txId, rec.withState(TxState.CONFIRMED));
        System.out.println("  [Confirm] balance="+balance);
        return true;
    }

    public synchronized boolean cancelFreeze(String txId) {
        TccRecord rec = tccLog.get(txId);
        if (rec == null) {
            // Null rollback guard
            tccLog.put(txId, new TccRecord(txId, TxState.CANCELLED, 0));
            System.out.println("  [Cancel] null rollback recorded");
            return true;
        }
        if (rec.state() == TxState.CANCELLED) return true;  // idempotent
        if (rec.state() == TxState.CONFIRMED) return false;
        frozen -= rec.frozenAmount();
        tccLog.put(txId, rec.withState(TxState.CANCELLED));
        System.out.println("  [Cancel] balance="+balance+" (unfrozen)");
        return true;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AccountServiceTCC acct = new AccountServiceTCC(500);
        String txId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

        acct.tryFreeze(txId, 100);       // true — $100 frozen
        // Payment fails:
        acct.cancelFreeze(txId);        // unfreeze → $500 restored
        acct.cancelFreeze(txId);        // idempotent → no effect
        System.out.println("Balance after cancel: " + acct.balance); // 500.0
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about TCC and Distributed Transactions

QWhat is TCC and how does it differ from Saga?
A
TCC (Try-Confirm-Cancel) is a distributed transaction pattern where each participant service implements three business methods: Try (reserve/freeze resources without executing), Confirm (execute the operation using reserved resources), and Cancel (release the reservation). A coordinator calls Try on all participants — if all succeed, it calls Confirm on all; if any fail, it calls Cancel on all. Key difference from Saga: in Saga, each step fully executes (hotel is booked, money is debited) and if a later step fails, compensating transactions undo the earlier steps. The earlier steps are fully visible to other transactions while the saga is in progress. In TCC, resources are only frozen (not executed) during the Try phase. The frozen amount is invisible to other transactions. Only after all participants confirm is the operation fully executed. TCC provides a "hidden intermediate state" guarantee that Saga cannot. Cost: every service must implement Try, Confirm, and Cancel separately (3× implementation effort), each with idempotency and null-rollback guards.
QWhy must every TCC operation be idempotent?
A
The coordinator may crash and restart at any point. After recovering, it must re-attempt any in-progress phase to ensure completion. This means Confirm and Cancel may be called multiple times on the same participant. If Confirm isn't idempotent, calling it twice could double-debit an account. If Cancel isn't idempotent, calling it twice after already releasing the frozen amount could corrupt state. Implementation: use the transaction ID (txId) as an idempotency key in a TCC log table. Before executing Confirm, check if txId already exists with state=CONFIRMED. If yes, return success immediately without re-executing. This requires a persistent log per participant (typically a separate table: tcc_transactions(tx_id, state, frozen_amount, updated_at)). The same principle applies to Try: if Try is called twice with the same txId, the second call should return the same result as the first without freezing additional resources. This is critical because Try may fail and retry due to network timeouts.
QWhat is the "empty cancel" / "null rollback" problem and how do you solve it?
A
The null rollback problem: Cancel arrives at a participant before Try has arrived (network partition, message reordering, or the coordinator sends Cancel before Try is delivered). The participant has no record of this transaction. A naive Cancel implementation returns "transaction not found" and does nothing. The coordinator treats this as successful. Later, Try arrives and executes — the resources are frozen with no Cancel coming, leaking them indefinitely. Solution: the Cancel implementation must record "this transaction was cancelled" even if no Try was ever received. When the delayed Try eventually arrives, it checks for this cancel record and returns "already cancelled" without freezing resources. In practice: on Cancel, if txId is not found, insert a record with state=CANCELLED. On Try, if txId is found with state=CANCELLED, return false immediately without executing. This defensive pattern is called a "null rollback guard" or "suspend technique". The complementary "empty confirm" (Confirm before Try arrives) is handled similarly: the participant stores a "premature confirm" record, and when Try finally arrives, it immediately marks itself as confirmed without actually executing.
QWhen should you use TCC vs Saga vs a single database transaction?
A
The answer depends on your consistency requirements and whether you can avoid distributed transactions at all. In order of preference: (1) Single database transaction (best): if you can restructure your data model so that all the state changes needed for an operation live in a single database, use a normal ACID transaction. No coordination overhead. No distributed transaction complexity. Many apparent "distributed" operations can be solved this way. (2) Saga: for most microservice workflows where eventual consistency is acceptable and intermediate state is tolerable. Easiest to implement — just chain local transactions with compensation. Use for: order placement (hotel + flight + payment), multi-step approval workflows, background processing pipelines. (3) TCC: when you need "hidden intermediate state" — other transactions must not see partial results, and compensation is not sufficient. Use for: financial fund transfers across accounts in different services, inventory reservation with strict double-booking prevention, any "reservation then confirm" pattern in financial systems. (4) 2PC: only when all participants support XA transactions and availability is not the primary concern. Almost exclusively used for legacy system integration.
QWhat happens if the coordinator crashes after sending Confirm to some (but not all) participants?
A
This is the most dangerous failure scenario: some participants have confirmed (executed), others haven't. The transaction is partially applied. On coordinator recovery, it must complete the Confirm phase for all remaining participants. This requires the coordinator to persist the transaction state before sending any Confirms — the coordinator log must record: txId, phase (TRYING/CONFIRMING/CANCELLING), and which participants have already acknowledged each phase. On recovery, the coordinator reads the log, finds in-progress CONFIRMING transactions, and re-sends Confirm to participants that haven't yet acknowledged. Because each participant's Confirm is idempotent (already-confirmed txId is a no-op), re-sending is safe. The coordinator must implement a "confirm timeout" — if a participant doesn't respond to Confirm within a deadline, retry with exponential backoff. If retries are exhausted, the transaction is stuck in a partial state (a "heuristic hazard"). For financial systems: have a reconciliation job that detects stuck transactions and pages on-call. This is why production TCC frameworks (Seata, ByteTCC) include a distributed coordinator with its own persistence and recovery logic — the coordinator is as critical as the participants.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design a payment system that transfers money between two bank accounts in different microservices with strong consistency guarantees", say: "I'd use TCC. Three participants: Account Service (source), Account Service (destination), Transaction Service (audit log). Phase 1 TRY: source account checks balance and freezes the transfer amount (balance unchanged, frozen counter incremented). Destination account pre-registers the incoming transfer (marks a pending credit). Transaction service creates a pending transaction record. Phase 2 CONFIRM (if all TRY succeeded): source account debits the frozen amount. Destination account credits the pending amount. Transaction service marks completed. CANCEL (if any TRY failed): source account unfreezes. Destination cancels the pending credit. Transaction service marks failed. Each operation uses the transaction ID as an idempotency key — retries on network failure are safe. Coordinator persists transaction state before each phase to survive crashes. Null-rollback guards on every Cancel. Frozen amounts expire after 60s via a scheduled cleanup job — prevents resource leaks if coordinator crashes before Cancel. TCC gives us: no visible intermediate state (the frozen amount is not available to concurrent transfers), no database-level locking across services, and the ability to recover from any single node failure."

Post 32

Connection Pooling & Database Proxies

25 min deep dive PgBouncer · Transaction vs Session Pooling · HikariCP · RDS Proxy · ProxySQL · Pool Sizing
The Problem

50 pods × 20 connections = 1,000 connections. PostgreSQL supports ~300 before it thrashes.

A PostgreSQL connection is not cheap: ~5MB RAM, an OS socket, and a background worker process. At 1,000 connections, Postgres spends more time on context-switching and memory pressure than actually running queries. P99 latency spikes, queries queue behind lock contention, and the database becomes the bottleneck — not because queries are slow, but because there are simply too many connections holding resources. A connection pooler sits between your application and the database, multiplexing hundreds of app connections onto a small number of real database connections.

The Core Insight

Your application doesn't need a persistent connection to the database. It only needs a connection during the milliseconds it's actively executing a query. Between requests, the connection is idle but still consuming RAM and a background process on the DB server. A pooler exploits this: it reuses a small set of real connections across many application threads, returning connections to the pool when queries complete.

Building Intuition — three pooling modes

Session, Transaction, and Statement pooling — each with different compatibility trade-offs

Session Pooling (weakest multiplexing)

One real DB connection per client session for the duration of the client's connection. The pooler only helps by capping concurrent connections — it doesn't reuse connections within a session. Benefit: fully compatible with all PostgreSQL features (prepared statements, advisory locks, SET statements, LISTEN/NOTIFY). Use when: your application holds sessions open but not always active, or uses features that require persistent connection state.

Transaction Pooling (most efficient — PgBouncer default)

A real DB connection is assigned only while a transaction is active. The moment the transaction commits or rolls back, the connection returns to the pool. A pooler with 25 real connections can serve hundreds of apps as long as they don't all have active transactions simultaneously. Break: prepared statements, SET variables, advisory locks, and anything that requires persistent per-connection state. Fix: set prepareThreshold=0 in your JDBC driver to use protocol-level prepared statements instead of server-side named ones.

Statement Pooling (most aggressive, rarely used)

Connection returned to pool after every single statement, even within a transaction. Most efficient but breaks all multi-statement transactions. Only useful for analytics workloads where each request is a single read query. Rarely used in OLTP.

Diagram 1 · PgBouncer Architecture — 1,000 app connections → 25 real DB connections

The pooler multiplexes at the transaction boundary — real connections reused immediately after commit

pgbouncer-architecture.svg
Animation · Connection Pool — requests queue, connection reused after commit

Step-by-step: 5 requests share 2 real connections via transaction-mode pooling

connection-pool-animation
Ready
Pool Sizing and Database Proxies

The counter-intuitive sizing rule — and when to use RDS Proxy vs PgBouncer

The pool size anti-pattern: bigger is NOT better

Teams see high DB latency and increase pool_size from 10 to 50. Latency gets worse. Reason: more connections = more lock contention, more memory on the DB, more scheduler pressure. The HikariCP team proved empirically that for most OLTP workloads, 10 connections outperforms 50. The Hikari formula: pool_size = Tn × (Cm - 1) + 1 where Tn = max app threads, Cm = max concurrent queries per thread (usually 1 for OLTP). For a typical web server with 10 request threads: pool_size = 10. For 100 threads: still 10–20. Fix slow queries first; only increase pool size if you have measurable evidence of connection wait time.

PgBouncer vs RDS Proxy vs ProxySQL

PgBouncer: lightweight C proxy, transaction mode, 40× multiplexing. Self-hosted. Zero protocol overhead. Industry standard for PostgreSQL. Config: pool_mode=transaction, pool_size=25, max_client_conn=1000. RDS Proxy (AWS): managed, handles IAM auth, automatic failover reconnection (critical for Lambda — functions can't hold connections between invocations). Add ~1ms latency. Worth it for serverless. ProxySQL: MySQL-specific, adds query routing, read/write splitting, query rewriting. Use when you need to route reads to replicas automatically.

Code · Python & Java — connection pool configuration

SQLAlchemy and HikariCP pool configuration with correct sizing

PYTHON
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, event
from sqlalchemy.pool import QueuePool

# ── Connection pool via SQLAlchemy (with PgBouncer) ────────
# App connects to PgBouncer (port 6432), not Postgres directly
engine = create_engine(
    "postgresql://user:pass@pgbouncer-host:6432/mydb",

    # Pool config
    poolclass=QueuePool,
    pool_size=5,             # base connections kept alive
    max_overflow=5,          # extra connections allowed under burst
    pool_timeout=5,           # wait max 5s for a connection before error
    pool_pre_ping=True,       # send SELECT 1 before use (detects stale)
    pool_recycle=1800,         # recycle connections every 30min

    # CRITICAL for PgBouncer transaction mode:
    # Disable server-side prepared statements
    connect_args={
        "options": "-c statement_timeout=30000",
        "prepare_threshold": 0,  # disables named prepared statements
    }
)

# ── Monitor pool health ────────────────────────────────────
@event.listens_for(engine, "checkout")
def on_checkout(dbapi_conn, conn_record, conn_proxy):
    # Fires when connection is checked out from pool
    print(f"Pool: checked out. Size={engine.pool.size()}, "
          f"Checked out={engine.pool.checkedout()}, "
          f"Overflow={engine.pool.overflow()}")

@event.listens_for(engine, "checkin")
def on_checkin(dbapi_conn, conn_record):
    # Fires when connection returned to pool
    pass  # log metrics here

# ── Health check: detect pool exhaustion ──────────────────
def pool_health() -> dict:
    pool = engine.pool
    return {
        "size":       pool.size(),
        "checked_out": pool.checkedout(),
        "overflow":   pool.overflow(),
        "available":  pool.size() - pool.checkedout(),
    }


# ── Diagnose connection leaks ──────────────────────────────
# In PostgreSQL, run this to find idle-in-transaction connections:
IDLE_IN_TXN_QUERY = """
    SELECT pid, usename, application_name, state,
           now() - state_change AS duration,
           LEFT(query, 100) AS query
    FROM pg_stat_activity
    WHERE state = 'idle in transaction'
    ORDER BY duration DESC;
"""

# If you see long-running idle-in-transaction, your app is holding
# connections inside transactions without committing/rolling back.
# Fix: add idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '30s' to postgresql.conf
# This auto-terminates connections stuck in transactions for > 30s.
JAVA
// Gradle: com.zaxxer:HikariCP:5.1.0
import com.zaxxer.hikari.*;

class DatabaseConfig {

    static HikariDataSource createDataSource() {
        HikariConfig config = new HikariConfig();

        // Connect to PgBouncer, not Postgres directly
        config.setJdbcUrl("jdbc:postgresql://pgbouncer:6432/mydb");
        config.setUsername("app_user");
        config.setPassword("secret");

        // Pool sizing — Hikari recommends surprisingly small pools
        config.setMaximumPoolSize(10);         // default 10, rarely need more
        config.setMinimumIdle(5);              // keep 5 warm connections

        // Timeouts
        config.setConnectionTimeout(5_000);    // wait max 5s for conn
        config.setIdleTimeout(600_000);        // idle conn removed after 10min
        config.setMaxLifetime(1_800_000);      // recycle conn after 30min
        config.setKeepaliveTime(60_000);        // send keepalive every 60s

        // Validation (SELECT 1 before each checkout)
        config.setConnectionTestQuery("SELECT 1");

        // CRITICAL for PgBouncer transaction mode:
        // Disable server-side prepared statements
        config.addDataSourceProperty("prepareThreshold", "0");
        config.addDataSourceProperty("reWriteBatchedInserts", "true");

        // Pool name for JMX/metrics
        config.setPoolName("payments-pool");

        return new HikariDataSource(config);
    }

    // ── Pool sizing formula (from Hikari docs) ─────────────
    // pool_size = Tn × (Cm - 1) + 1
    // Tn = max threads in app
    // Cm = max concurrent queries per thread (1 for typical OLTP)
    //
    // For a Spring Boot app with 200 Tomcat threads:
    // pool_size = 200 × (1 - 1) + 1 = 1  ← that can't be right
    // Because: most threads don't ALL need DB simultaneously.
    // Hikari recommends empirically testing with 10, then 20, then 50.
    // Usually 10 is optimal for OLTP.

    // ── Monitor pool via JMX / Micrometer ─────────────────
    static void logPoolStats(HikariDataSource ds) {
        HikariPoolMXBean pool = ds.getHikariPoolMXBean();
        System.out.printf("Pool[%s]: active=%d idle=%d waiting=%d total=%d%n",
            ds.getPoolName(),
            pool.getActiveConnections(),
            pool.getIdleConnections(),
            pool.getThreadsAwaitingConnection(),
            pool.getTotalConnections()
        );
        // Alert: if ThreadsAwaitingConnection > 0 for > 1s, pool is too small
        // or queries are too slow. Don't blindly increase pool size.
    }

    // ── PgBouncer config snippet (pgbouncer.ini) ────────────
    // [databases]
    // mydb = host=postgres-primary port=5432 dbname=mydb
    //
    // [pgbouncer]
    // pool_mode = transaction
    // max_client_conn = 1000
    // default_pool_size = 25
    // min_pool_size = 5
    // reserve_pool_size = 5
    // server_idle_timeout = 600
    // listen_port = 6432
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Connection Pooling

QWhy does transaction pooling break prepared statements and how do you fix it?
A
Prepared statements in PostgreSQL are stored server-side, per-connection, referenced by name. When you execute PREPARE my_stmt AS SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1, the parsed plan is stored on the specific physical connection. In transaction pooling, your application's logical connection maps to a different physical DB connection on every transaction. The prepared statement "my_stmt" exists on physical connection A, but your next transaction might land on connection B — which has never seen "my_stmt". The query fails. Fix: (1) Set prepareThreshold=0 in your JDBC driver (Postgres JDBC) or prepare_threshold=0 in psycopg3. This disables named server-side prepared statements and uses protocol-level (unnamed) parameterised queries instead — still safe from SQL injection, still optimised, but not named on the server side. (2) Use session pooling instead of transaction pooling. (3) For SQLAlchemy, set connect_args={"prepare_threshold": 0}.
QHow do you diagnose a connection pool exhaustion problem?
A
Symptoms: requests fail with "connection pool exhausted" or "timeout waiting for connection" after a spike. Diagnosis steps: (1) Check pg_stat_activity — count connections by state: idle (fine), active (running queries), idle in transaction (holding a connection with an open transaction but not executing — the most dangerous). Large idle in transaction count means your code holds transactions open longer than needed. (2) Check connection wait time in your pool metrics: HikariCP exposes hikaricp.connections.pending. If it's non-zero for more than a second, the pool is saturated. (3) Check if pool_size is misconfigured. More is NOT better — profile with 10, then try 20, measuring P99 latency. (4) Fix long-running transactions: add idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = 30s in postgresql.conf to auto-terminate stuck transactions. (5) Ensure connections are always returned to the pool — wrap all DB operations in try-finally to guarantee connection.close() is called.
QWhen would you use RDS Proxy instead of PgBouncer?
A
RDS Proxy is the right choice for: (1) AWS Lambda workloads: Lambda functions can't hold persistent connections between invocations. Each invocation may create a new connection, potentially hitting the DB connection limit with thousands of concurrent Lambdas. RDS Proxy pools these connections server-side so the Lambda never touches the DB directly. (2) Managed failover: RDS Proxy maintains persistent connections to the DB and automatically reconnects during RDS failover. Your app's connections to the proxy stay alive — no reconnection logic needed. (3) IAM authentication: RDS Proxy supports IAM-based DB authentication — no DB passwords in your application code. Use PgBouncer instead when: you need self-hosted control, lower latency (PgBouncer adds ~0ms vs RDS Proxy adds ~1ms), or you're not on AWS. PgBouncer is also free vs RDS Proxy's ~$0.015/hour per vCPU of your DB.
QWhat is the ideal connection pool size for a typical web API?
A
Counter-intuitively: usually 10. The HikariCP documentation and empirical benchmarks show that for OLTP workloads, 10 connections consistently outperforms larger pools. The reasoning: database queries complete in milliseconds. A connection is only held while the query is executing. With 10 connections and 1ms queries at 1,000 req/s, you'd need at most 10 concurrent DB queries — and each is released almost immediately. Increasing to 50 connections means 50 simultaneous queries, more lock contention, more memory pressure on the DB, and worse cache locality. The situations where you genuinely need more connections: (1) queries take a long time (100ms+), making connections unavailable for longer — fix the slow queries first. (2) High concurrency with truly parallel DB-bound work — verify with your pool metrics that threads are actually waiting for connections before adding more. (3) Read replicas with separate pools — the primary and read replicas each get their own pools.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "your app is getting connection errors to the database under load — what do you do?", say: "Step 1: run SELECT state, count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state to see the breakdown. If I see many 'idle in transaction', the app has a connection leak — transactions being held open. I'd add idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = 30s to Postgres immediately to auto-kill stuck connections. Step 2: check current max_connections and how many connections exist. If we're at capacity, I'd deploy PgBouncer between the app and DB in transaction mode with pool_size=25. This immediately reduces DB connections from (pods × pool_size) to 25, regardless of app tier scale. Step 3: check the app-side pool metrics — is pool.threads_awaiting_connection non-zero? If so, the pool is too small OR queries are too slow. Profile query P99 before blindly increasing pool_size. Step 4: verify prepareThreshold=0 in the JDBC driver so transaction-mode pooling doesn't break prepared statements."

Post 33

API Design: REST vs gRPC vs GraphQL

25 min deep dive REST · gRPC · GraphQL · N+1 Problem · Pagination · Versioning · Idempotency · Over-fetching
The Problem

Your mobile app makes 12 API calls to render one screen. Your microservices waste 40% bandwidth on JSON overhead.

Two real problems. First: a product page needs user info, product data, reviews, inventory, and recommendations — each from a different service. The frontend makes 5 sequential REST calls, waiting for each before starting the next. Total latency = sum of all calls. Second: your internal services exchange millions of messages per second. Each JSON message is self-describing — field names repeated in every payload. A gRPC binary message encoding the same data is 5–10× smaller. These are the problems that drive real architectural decisions about API design.

The Right Mental Model

REST, gRPC, and GraphQL solve different problems. REST: human-readable, HTTP-native, great for public APIs and browser clients. gRPC: binary, typed, streaming-capable, 5–10× smaller payloads — for internal microservice communication. GraphQL: client-driven queries, eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching — for heterogeneous clients (web/mobile needing different data shapes). Production systems typically use all three.

Building Intuition — three paradigms

What each is optimised for, and where it breaks down

REST — Resources and HTTP semantics

REST models your API as resources (/users/42, /orders/99) acted on by HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). HTTP caching works natively (CDN-cacheable GET requests). Browser-native. Excellent tooling (Swagger/OpenAPI). Problems: over-fetching (GET /user returns 30 fields but you need 3), under-fetching (12 calls to build one screen), versioning is hard (/v1/ vs /v2/ proliferates), no schema enforcement.

gRPC — Binary, typed, streaming

Defines services in .proto files (Protobuf). Code is generated for client and server in any language. Binary encoding: a Protobuf message is 5–10× smaller than equivalent JSON. Strongly typed — the schema IS the contract. Streaming: server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional. HTTP/2 multiplexing means many concurrent RPC calls share one TCP connection. Problems: not browser-native (requires grpc-web proxy), binary messages are not human-readable, harder to debug with curl.

GraphQL — Client-driven queries

The client specifies exactly which fields it needs in a single query. One endpoint, infinite flexibility. Eliminates both over-fetching (get only the 3 fields you need) and under-fetching (get nested related data in one request). Problems: N+1 query problem (fetching 100 users each triggers a separate DB query for their orders — requires DataLoader batching), cache complexity (queries are POST by default — CDN doesn't cache POST), schema complexity grows fast in large orgs.

Diagram 1 · Side-by-side comparison — the same data request in all three paradigms

Getting a user's name and their last 3 orders: REST (2 calls) vs GraphQL (1 call) vs gRPC (1 typed call)

api-comparison.svg
Animation · REST N+1 vs GraphQL DataLoader batching

Fetching 3 users and their orders: REST makes 4 calls, GraphQL batches into 2 DB queries

api-n1-animation
Ready
REST Best Practices — Versioning, Idempotency, and Pagination

Three things that distinguish a well-designed REST API from one that breaks clients

API Versioning — URL vs header

URL versioning (/v1/users, /v2/users): explicit, easily testable with curl, cacheable. But creates URL proliferation and clients must update base URLs. Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2): URL stays clean. But less visible, harder to test, breaks CDN caching. No versioning (additive changes): never remove or rename fields — only add new optional fields. Mark deprecated fields with "_deprecated": true. Most resilient approach but requires long-term backward compatibility discipline. Recommendation: URL versioning for major breaking changes (/v1 → /v2), additive changes within a version.

Idempotency — safe retries for mutations

POST requests are not idempotent by default — calling POST /payments twice creates two payments. To make POST idempotent: accept an Idempotency-Key header (UUID generated by the client). On the server, check if this key has been processed before — if yes, return the cached response. If no, process and store the result keyed by the idempotency key. Stripe's API popularised this pattern. Critical for payment APIs where network retries could cause duplicate charges. TTL: store idempotency keys for 24 hours (long enough for client retry windows).

Cursor-based pagination — avoid OFFSET for large tables

OFFSET pagination (?page=5&size=20) requires the database to scan and discard the first N rows. For page 500 of a 10M row table: scan 10,000 rows to find the right position. Slow and unstable (rows added/deleted mid-pagination cause items to appear twice or be skipped). Cursor pagination (?after=eyJpZCI6MTIzfQ): encode the last item's ID in a cursor. The next query is WHERE id > cursor_id LIMIT 20 — index seek, not scan. Always fast. Used by all modern APIs at scale: Twitter, Facebook, Stripe. Tradeoff: can't jump to page 500.

Trade-offs
api-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python — REST idempotency key + cursor pagination + gRPC proto definition

Production patterns for all three paradigms

PYTHON / REST
from fastapi import FastAPI, Header, HTTPException, Query
from typing import Optional
import base64, json

app = FastAPI()
_idempotency_cache: dict = {}   # in prod: Redis with 24h TTL

# ── Idempotency key ────────────────────────────────────────
@app.post("/payments")
async def create_payment(
    body: dict,
    idempotency_key: Optional[str] = Header(default=None, alias="Idempotency-Key")
):
    """
    POST /payments with Idempotency-Key header.
    Same key = same result, no duplicate payment.
    """
    if idempotency_key:
        if idempotency_key in _idempotency_cache:
            print(f"Returning cached response for key: {idempotency_key}")
            return _idempotency_cache[idempotency_key]

    # Process payment
    result = {"payment_id": "pay_abc123", "status": "created"}

    if idempotency_key:
        _idempotency_cache[idempotency_key] = result
    return result


# ── Cursor-based pagination ────────────────────────────────
def encode_cursor(last_id: int) -> str:
    payload = json.dumps({"id": last_id})
    return base64.b64encode(payload.encode()).decode()

def decode_cursor(cursor: str) -> int:
    payload = json.loads(base64.b64decode(cursor))
    return payload["id"]

@app.get("/orders")
async def list_orders(
    after: Optional[str] = Query(default=None),
    limit: int = Query(default=20, le=100)
):
    """
    GET /orders?after=eyJpZCI6MTIzfQ&limit=20

    Cursor pagination: always O(log N) — no OFFSET scan.
    """
    cursor_id = decode_cursor(after) if after else None

    # SQL: WHERE id > cursor_id ORDER BY id LIMIT limit+1
    orders = await db.fetch(
        "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE (:cursor IS NULL OR id > :cursor) "
        "ORDER BY id LIMIT :limit",
        cursor=cursor_id, limit=limit + 1  # +1 to detect hasNextPage
    )

    has_next = len(orders) > limit
    items    = orders[:limit]

    return {
        "data":        items,
        "next_cursor": encode_cursor(items[-1]["id"]) if has_next else None,
        "has_next":    has_next,
    }
    # Client: GET /orders?after={next_cursor} to fetch next page
    # Always same speed regardless of how deep into the list you are
PROTO / gRPC
// ── user_service.proto ─────────────────────────────────────
syntax = "proto3";
package user.v1;

// Service definition — generates client + server stubs
service UserService {
  // Unary RPC: one request, one response
  rpc GetUser(GetUserRequest) returns (GetUserResponse);

  // Server-streaming: one request, stream of responses
  // E.g. real-time order updates
  rpc StreamOrders(StreamOrdersRequest) returns (stream Order);

  // Bidirectional streaming: e.g. chat, live auction
  rpc LiveBidding(stream Bid) returns (stream BidResult);
}

message GetUserRequest {
  int64 user_id    = 1;   // field numbers → binary encoding
  int32 order_limit = 2;
}

message GetUserResponse {
  int64  user_id = 1;
  string name    = 2;
  string email   = 3;
  repeated Order last_orders = 4;
}

message Order {
  int64  order_id = 1;
  double amount   = 2;
  string status   = 3;
  int64  created  = 4;  // unix timestamp
}

// ── Generated Python server (after protoc) ─────────────────
// class UserServiceServicer:
//   def GetUser(self, request, context):
//       user = db.get_user(request.user_id)
//       orders = db.get_orders(request.user_id, limit=request.order_limit)
//       return GetUserResponse(
//           user_id=user.id, name=user.name, email=user.email,
//           last_orders=[Order(order_id=o.id, amount=o.amount,
//                              status=o.status) for o in orders]
//       )
//
// Generated Python client:
// stub = UserServiceStub(grpc.insecure_channel('user-svc:50051'))
// resp = stub.GetUser(GetUserRequest(user_id=42, order_limit=3))
// print(resp.name, resp.last_orders[0].amount)
//
// JSON equivalent of same message: ~380 bytes
// Protobuf binary encoding: ~38 bytes (10× smaller)
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about API Design

QWhen would you choose gRPC over REST for internal services?
A
gRPC over REST for internal services when: (1) Payload efficiency matters — services exchanging millions of messages per second benefit from Protobuf's 5–10× smaller binary encoding vs JSON. (2) Strong typing is required — generated client/server stubs from .proto files eliminate entire classes of bugs. Schema changes that break clients are caught at compile time. (3) Streaming is needed — gRPC supports server-streaming (push updates), client-streaming (large uploads), and bidirectional streaming (real-time chat, live feeds). HTTP/1.1 + REST requires workarounds (SSE, WebSocket). (4) Polyglot services — Protobuf code generation in 10+ languages means Go service, Java service, and Python service all use the same typed interface with no manual client library maintenance. REST with REST: choose REST when the client is a browser, when you need CDN caching, when your API is public (developers expect HTTP semantics), or when debugging requires human-readable messages.
QWhat is the N+1 problem in GraphQL and how do you solve it?
A
N+1 occurs when a GraphQL query fetches a list of N objects, and each object's resolver triggers a separate database query. Example: query users → resolver fetches 100 users → for each user, the orders resolver calls SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = ? → 100 separate DB queries. Total: 1 (users) + 100 (orders) = 101 queries. Solution: DataLoader (Facebook's open-source batching library). How it works: DataLoader buffers all calls to the load function within a single event loop tick. Instead of firing 100 separate DB queries, it collects all 100 user IDs and fires one batched query: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id IN (1, 2, 3, ..., 100). Result: 2 DB queries total regardless of N. Implementation: create one DataLoader per request (not global — to avoid data leaking across requests). Register it in the GraphQL context. Resolvers call context.ordersLoader.load(userId) instead of calling the DB directly.
QHow do you version a REST API without breaking existing clients?
A
Three strategies: (1) URL versioning (/v1/users): most common, explicit. Old clients keep using /v1. New clients use /v2. Simple to implement, easy to test with curl, trivially CDN-cacheable. Downside: multiple versions must be maintained simultaneously — deprecation requires all clients to migrate before v1 can be removed. (2) Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.company.v2+json): same URL, version in header. URL stays clean but visibility is low, and CDN caching is complicated. (3) Additive-only evolution (no explicit versioning): never remove or rename fields. Add new optional fields. New clients use new fields; old clients ignore them. This is the most backward-compatible approach and avoids version proliferation. Mark deprecated fields but leave them in responses for a defined sunset period. Combine: URL versioning for major breaking changes (v1 → v2), additive evolution within a version.
QWhy should you use cursor-based pagination instead of offset-based?
A
Offset pagination (LIMIT 20 OFFSET 500) requires the database to read and discard 500 rows to return rows 501–520. For deeply nested pages on large tables, this is an O(N) scan — slow and getting slower as the dataset grows. Additionally, results are unstable: if a row is inserted before page 5 while you're on page 4, page 5 shifts and you see the same row twice, or you skip one. Cursor-based pagination uses the last item's unique identifier as a cursor. The next query is WHERE id > cursor_id ORDER BY id LIMIT 20 — a single index seek, O(log N) regardless of how deep into the dataset. Results are stable: insertions before the cursor don't affect subsequent pages. Implementation: encode the cursor as base64(json({id: lastId})) to make it opaque to clients (they shouldn't hardcode cursor structure). Include in the API response a next_cursor field that is null when no more pages exist. Tradeoff: you can't jump to "page 500" directly — cursors are sequential navigation only.
QWhat is the Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern and when do you use it?
A
BFF is an API gateway layer tailored to a specific frontend client. Instead of one general-purpose API consumed by web, iOS, Android, and internal tools all making multiple calls, you create separate BFFs for each: web-bff, mobile-bff, internal-bff. Each BFF aggregates calls to multiple downstream services, tailors the response shape to what its specific client needs, and handles authentication, caching, and error translation for that client's context. Why: mobile clients on 4G need smaller payloads than desktop on WiFi. Mobile might need fewer fields. The web dashboard might need different aggregations than the mobile app. Problems with a single API: it either over-fetches (always returns maximum fields any client might need) or the API becomes cluttered with hundreds of client-specific parameters. The BFF pattern is often implemented with GraphQL (flexible query shape) in front of gRPC services (efficient internal communication). Pattern: client → BFF (GraphQL/REST, one per client type) → microservices (gRPC internally).
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design an API layer for a platform with web, iOS, and Android clients hitting 12 microservices", say: "Three-tier API architecture: (1) Internal microservices talk gRPC: typed Protobuf contracts, 5–10× smaller payloads than JSON, streaming for real-time feeds, generated clients eliminate mismatches. (2) BFF (Backend for Frontend) per client type: a GraphQL gateway for web (needs rich queries), a REST gateway for mobile (mobile clients are simpler, offline-first, need small JSON). Each BFF aggregates from the gRPC microservices — one BFF call replaces 12 client-to-service calls. DataLoader handles N+1 in the GraphQL BFF. (3) Public REST API for third-party integrations: versioned at /v1/, cursor-based pagination, Idempotency-Key header on mutations, OpenAPI spec for documentation. This structure eliminates the '12 API calls per screen' problem (BFF aggregates), the bandwidth problem (gRPC internally), and the developer experience problem (typed GraphQL on the outside)."

Post 34

Concurrency Patterns

25 min deep dive Thread-per-Request · Event Loop · Goroutines · Virtual Threads · Optimistic Locking · Lock-Free · Actor Model
The Problem

10,000 threads at 2MB stack each = 20GB RAM. You run out of memory before you run out of CPU.

The traditional model: one OS thread per request. A thread blocks waiting for a database query (10ms). During those 10ms it holds 2MB of stack memory and an OS scheduler slot — doing nothing. At 10,000 concurrent requests, that's 20GB RAM consumed by idle threads. This is the C10K problem, and it's why most web servers historically couldn't handle 10,000 simultaneous connections without vertical scaling. The solution is to decouple execution units (what runs) from OS threads (what the kernel schedules).

The Core Question Every Model Answers Differently

While waiting for I/O (database, network, disk), what does this thread do? Thread-per-request: blocks and waits — wastes memory, limited concurrency. Event loop: registers a callback and moves on — high throughput but no blocking allowed. Goroutines/Virtual threads: parks on the heap and frees the OS thread — readability of blocking code + efficiency of async. The right model depends on your workload: CPU-bound vs I/O-bound, latency vs throughput.

Building Intuition — four concurrency models

Thread-per-request, event loop, goroutines, and virtual threads — each with different trade-offs

Thread-per-request (Java < 21, traditional servers)

One OS thread handles one request from start to finish. Blocking I/O is natural — db.query() just blocks. Simple to reason about. Limit: OS threads are expensive (2MB stack, kernel context-switch overhead). A 4-core machine with 8GB RAM can sustain ~4,000 concurrent threads before memory pressure degrades performance. Used by: traditional Java (Tomcat, Jetty), Ruby (Puma), Python (Gunicorn sync workers).

Event loop (Node.js, Nginx, Redis)

Single thread processes all events using OS I/O multiplexing (epoll/kqueue). When I/O is initiated, a callback is registered and the loop immediately moves to the next event. No blocking allowed — a single while(true){} freezes the entire server. Extremely high throughput for I/O-bound workloads (100K+ concurrent connections on one core). Terrible for CPU-bound work (one computation blocks everything). Used by: Node.js, Nginx, Redis, Python asyncio, Java Netty.

Goroutines (Go)

Lightweight execution units with 2–8KB initial stack (growing dynamically). Multiplexed onto OS threads by the Go runtime scheduler (GOMAXPROCS OS threads). Blocking a goroutine on I/O parks it — the OS thread picks up another runnable goroutine. Millions of goroutines possible on one machine. The programmer writes blocking code; the scheduler makes it async. Go's killer feature. Channels for communication eliminate shared-state concurrency bugs. Used by: all Go services.

Virtual Threads (Java 21 / Project Loom)

JVM-managed threads (~1KB on heap) multiplexed onto platform (OS) threads called "carrier threads". When a virtual thread blocks on I/O, it's unmounted from the carrier thread (which picks up another virtual thread). The programmer writes blocking code exactly as before — but the JVM makes it non-blocking under the hood. Enables Java to match Go's concurrency model without rewriting code to async/await. Caveat: synchronized blocks and native calls can "pin" a virtual thread to its carrier (blocking it), degrading to thread-per-request behaviour.

Diagram 1 · Three models — what happens while waiting for DB

Same 10ms DB query: thread blocks (wasted), callback fires later (efficient), virtual thread parks (clean)

concurrency-models.svg
Animation · Thread-per-request exhaustion vs virtual thread scaling

Watch OS threads fill up under load, then see virtual threads handle the same concurrency cheaply

concurrency-animation
Ready
Concurrency Control — Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking

Optimistic locking: assume no conflict, detect and retry. Pessimistic: lock first, then read.

Optimistic Locking (versioned writes)

Each row has a version column. Read: fetch row with version=5. Write: UPDATE SET balance=400, version=6 WHERE id=42 AND version=5. If another transaction updated the row first (version is now 6), your UPDATE affects 0 rows — conflict detected. Retry with the fresh row. No lock held during the read phase. Excellent for low-contention scenarios: most reads, few concurrent writes to the same row. Performance degrades under high contention — every operation retries repeatedly. Used by: Hibernate (optimistic) via @Version, JPA, most ORMs.

Pessimistic Locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE)

SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id=42 FOR UPDATE — acquires a row-level exclusive lock immediately. Other transactions trying to read or write this row block until the lock is released (on COMMIT or ROLLBACK). Guarantees no concurrent modification. Best for: high-contention scenarios (payment processing, inventory reservation, counter increments). Cost: reduces parallelism — only one transaction can proceed at a time for a given row. Deadlock risk: two transactions each holding a lock the other needs. Prevention: always acquire locks in the same order, keep transactions short.

Lock-free concurrency (CAS — Compare-And-Swap)

Hardware instruction: CAS(address, expected, new) atomically writes new only if current value equals expected. If not, it fails — the caller retries with the current value. No OS-level lock, no kernel context switch. Java: AtomicInteger.compareAndSet(expected, update). Go: atomic.CompareAndSwapInt64. Used for: counters, lock-free queues, reference counting. CAS does not compose — multi-step atomic operations still require locks. ABA problem: value changes from A → B → A between CAS reads; appears unchanged. Solution: version stamp (compare value + version).

Code · Python & Java — virtual threads, optimistic locking, CAS

Modern concurrency patterns in practice

PYTHON
import asyncio, threading
from dataclasses import dataclass

# ── asyncio event loop (Python's event-loop model) ─────────
async def fetch_user(user_id: int) -> dict:
    # This does NOT block the event loop — yields while waiting
    await asyncio.sleep(0.01)  # simulates DB query (10ms)
    return {"id": user_id, "name": f"User {user_id}"}

async def handle_100_concurrent():
    """Run 100 DB-bound tasks concurrently on ONE thread."""
    tasks = [fetch_user(i) for i in range(100)]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    print(f"Fetched {len(results)} users concurrently on 1 thread")
    # Total time ≈ 10ms (all run concurrently)
    # vs 1000ms if sequential


# ── Optimistic locking (version column) ───────────────────
@dataclass
class Account:
    id:      int
    balance: float
    version: int

def transfer_optimistic(conn, from_id: int, to_id: int,
                          amount: float, max_retries: int = 3) -> bool:
    """
    Transfer with optimistic locking.
    Retries if version conflict detected.
    """
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        # Read both accounts
        src = conn.execute("SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id=?", (from_id,))
        dst = conn.execute("SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id=?", (to_id,))

        if src.balance < amount:
            raise ValueError("Insufficient funds")

        # Conditional UPDATE — only succeeds if version unchanged
        rows = conn.execute(
            "UPDATE accounts SET balance=?, version=version+1 "
            "WHERE id=? AND version=?",
            (src.balance - amount, from_id, src.version)
        ).rowcount

        if rows == 0:
            # Version conflict — another transaction modified the row
            print(f"  Conflict on attempt {attempt+1}, retrying...")
            continue   # retry with fresh data

        # Update destination (no version check needed for credit)
        conn.execute(
            "UPDATE accounts SET balance=balance+? WHERE id=?",
            (amount, to_id)
        )
        conn.commit()
        return True

    return False  # all retries exhausted


# ── Lock-free counter with CAS semantics ──────────────────
import threading

class AtomicCounter:
    """Thread-safe counter without locks (uses GIL-protected int ops)."""
    def __init__(self): self._val = 0; self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def increment(self) -> int:
        with self._lock:
            self._val += 1
            return self._val

    def compare_and_set(self, expected: int, new: int) -> bool:
        with self._lock:
            if self._val == expected:
                self._val = new; return True
            return False
JAVA
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;
import java.util.concurrent.locks.*;
import java.util.List;

class ConcurrencyPatterns {

    // ── Virtual Threads (Java 21) ─────────────────────────────
    static void virtualThreadsDemo() throws Exception {
        // ExecutorService backed by virtual threads
        var exec = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor();

        // Submit 10,000 tasks — each blocks on I/O for 100ms
        // With platform threads: 10,000 × 2MB = 20GB RAM
        // With virtual threads: ~10,000 × 1KB = 10MB RAM
        List<Future<String>> futures = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 10_000; i++) {
            final int id = i;
            futures.add(exec.submit(() -> {
                Thread.sleep(100);     // blocks virtual thread, not carrier
                return "user-" + id;
            }));
        }

        long ok = futures.stream().mapToLong(f -> {
            try { f.get(); return 1; } catch (Exception e) { return 0; }
        }).sum();
        System.out.println("Completed: " + ok + " tasks in ~100ms");
        exec.shutdown();
    }

    // ── Optimistic locking with @Version (JPA) ───────────────
    // @Entity public class Account {
    //   @Id Long id;
    //   BigDecimal balance;
    //   @Version Long version;  // JPA auto-increments, checks on UPDATE
    // }
    // On conflict: OptimisticLockException thrown → retry in catch block

    // ── Pessimistic locking: SELECT FOR UPDATE ────────────────
    // @Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
    // @Query("SELECT a FROM Account a WHERE a.id = :id")
    // Account findByIdForUpdate(@Param("id") Long id);
    // → Holds row lock until transaction commits/rolls back

    // ── Lock-Free with CAS ────────────────────────────────────
    static AtomicLong counter = new AtomicLong(0);

    static void lockFreeIncrement() {
        long current, updated;
        do {
            current = counter.get();
            updated = current + 1;
        } while (!counter.compareAndSet(current, updated));
        // Retries if another thread modified counter between get() and CAS
        // No lock, no kernel syscall — just hardware atomic instruction
    }

    // ── Virtual thread pinning — avoid synchronized blocks ────
    private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();

    void safeForVirtualThreads() {
        lock.lock();   // ← ReentrantLock: VT parks, carrier is freed
        try { /* critical section */ }
        finally { lock.unlock(); }
    }

    synchronized void badForVirtualThreads() {
        // ← synchronized: PINS VT to carrier thread during block!
        // Carrier thread is blocked = wastes a platform thread
        // Replace synchronized{} with ReentrantLock in Java 21
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        virtualThreadsDemo();
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Concurrency

QWhat is the C10K problem and how do modern runtimes solve it?
A
The C10K problem: handling 10,000 concurrent connections on a single server. Thread-per-connection fails because each OS thread requires ~2MB of stack memory, plus kernel scheduler overhead for context-switching. 10K threads = 20GB RAM consumed just by idle threads — before any actual work is done. Modern solutions: (1) Event loops (Node.js, Nginx, Redis): one thread handles all I/O via OS-level I/O multiplexing (epoll on Linux, kqueue on BSD). No blocking allowed — a CPU-bound operation stalls all connections on that thread. (2) Goroutines (Go): 2KB initial stack, runtime scheduler multiplexes millions of goroutines onto a handful of OS threads. Goroutines park on I/O, freeing OS threads. Programmer writes blocking code. (3) Virtual threads (Java 21): same idea as goroutines — JVM manages thousands of virtual threads on a few platform threads. Each virtual thread uses ~1KB on the heap rather than 2MB in the stack. All three can handle 1M+ concurrent I/O-bound connections on commodity hardware.
QWhen does optimistic locking fail and what do you do?
A
Optimistic locking works well when conflicts are rare — most operations succeed on the first try. It degrades when the same rows are modified concurrently by many transactions (high contention). Symptoms: OptimisticLockException storms in logs, P99 latency spikes as operations retry repeatedly. Failure modes: (1) High contention on a hot row (e.g. a global counter, a popular product's inventory count). Every transaction reads version=N, every transaction tries to write version=N+1, all but one fail and retry. Effectively serialised with retry overhead added. (2) Long-running reads: a transaction reads a row and does a 30-second computation before writing. Version has changed 100 times by then — guaranteed conflict. Fixes: (1) Retry with exponential backoff (2–3 retries usually sufficient for low contention). (2) Switch to pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) for high-contention rows. (3) Redesign to reduce contention — use an atomic increment (UPDATE SET count = count + 1) instead of read-modify-write. (4) Partition the hot row into N separate rows (sharded counter) and aggregate periodically.
QWhat is the "pinning" problem with Java virtual threads?
A
A virtual thread is normally "unmounted" from its carrier (platform) thread when it blocks on I/O — the carrier picks up another runnable virtual thread. Pinning: a virtual thread becomes stuck to its carrier thread even while blocked, preventing the carrier from running other virtual threads. Two causes: (1) synchronized blocks and methods — Java's intrinsic lock (synchronized) holds the carrier thread even when the virtual thread blocks inside it. A virtual thread running inside a synchronized block that calls Thread.sleep() or does network I/O will pin its carrier for the duration. (2) Native method calls (JNI) — the carrier is held while native code runs. Detection: JVM flag -Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full prints a stack trace when pinning occurs. Fix: replace synchronized blocks with ReentrantLock, which is designed to work with virtual threads — a virtual thread can park on a ReentrantLock without pinning its carrier. Most modern Java frameworks (Tomcat, Jetty, Spring) have already migrated away from synchronized for this reason.
QHow would you handle 100,000 simultaneous WebSocket connections on one server?
A
Use an event-loop or virtual-thread model — not thread-per-connection. Each WebSocket connection is mostly idle (waiting for messages). The key insight: 100K idle connections consume almost no CPU. The challenge is memory and scheduler overhead. With Go: goroutines give this for free. Each WebSocket connection gets a goroutine — 100K goroutines × 2KB = 200MB RAM, on a single server. The Go scheduler handles the concurrency. With Java 21: virtual threads, same math. With Node.js: single-threaded event loop — 100K connections share one thread, zero overhead unless all send messages simultaneously. Implementation concerns: (1) Backpressure — if 100K connections all receive a broadcast simultaneously, the message fan-out can overwhelm the server. Use a queue or rate limiter for broadcasts. (2) Connection lifecycle — track idle-too-long connections and close them. (3) Sticky sessions or connection state — use Redis for shared session state if you need horizontal scaling across multiple servers.
QWhat is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
A
CAS (Compare-And-Swap) succeeds when the current value equals the expected value. The ABA problem: a value changes from A → B → A between when you read it (seeing A) and when you CAS it. Your CAS sees A = A and succeeds — but the value was changed twice in between. In many algorithms this doesn't matter (the value is back to A and you're adding to it). But in pointer-based data structures (lock-free stacks, queues), a pointer being back to A doesn't mean the node is the same node — it could be a new allocation at the same address. Classic example: a lock-free stack pop. Thread 1 reads top=A (A points to node with value 1). Thread 2 pops A, pops B, pushes A back (A now points to a different node). Thread 1's CAS(top, A, B) succeeds — but B was already popped by Thread 2. The stack is now corrupted. Solutions: (1) Stamped reference: pair the pointer with a version counter. CAS on (pointer + version) instead of just pointer. ABA would require the same address AND the same version — extremely unlikely. Java: AtomicStampedReference. (2) Hazard pointers: delay memory reclamation until all threads have left the pointer. (3) Use higher-level primitives (ConcurrentLinkedQueue) that already handle ABA internally.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "what concurrency model would you choose for a real-time chat server handling 500,000 simultaneous connections?", say: "Go or Java 21 virtual threads. Each connection is a long-lived WebSocket — mostly idle, occasionally active. Thread-per-connection in traditional Java would require 500K OS threads × 2MB = 1TB RAM — impossible. With Go goroutines or Java virtual threads, 500K connections × ~2KB = ~1GB RAM — easily fits on a single machine. The server is I/O-bound, not CPU-bound, so the async model is ideal. For message fan-out (broadcasting to rooms): implement a room registry as a concurrent hash map of room ID → list of connection goroutines/VTs. For broadcast, iterate and send to each — but rate-limit and use a buffered channel (Go) or bounded queue (Java) per connection so a slow receiver doesn't block the broadcaster. For horizontal scaling: each server handles its shard of connections; a Redis pub/sub layer routes room messages to the server that holds those connections."

Post 35

Network Fundamentals for Distributed Systems

25 min deep dive TCP · UDP · QUIC · Head-of-Line Blocking · TLS · WebSockets · SSE · Long Polling · HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3
The Problem

One lost TCP packet stalls every request on your HTTP/2 connection — even unrelated ones

HTTP/2 was supposed to eliminate the problem of one slow request blocking others. And it did — at the HTTP level. But TCP guarantees ordered delivery: if packet 47 in a stream is lost, TCP buffers everything that arrived after it and delivers nothing until packet 47 is retransmitted and received. All HTTP/2 streams share the same TCP connection, so one lost packet blocks every stream — all your requests, all your assets. This is TCP-level head-of-line blocking, and HTTP/2 cannot solve it. QUIC (HTTP/3) does — by moving to UDP and implementing per-stream reliability inside the protocol.

The Core TCP Trade-off

TCP gives you ordered, reliable, connection-oriented delivery. The cost: (1) 1-RTT connection establishment (or 1.5 RTT with TLS 1.2), (2) slow-start algorithm (congestion window starts small, ramps up), (3) head-of-line blocking (lost packet stalls all in-order delivery), (4) stateful connection (requires NAT binding, doesn't handle IP address changes). Every protocol above TCP inherits all these costs. UDP gives you none of TCP's guarantees — but lets you build exactly the guarantees you need.

Building Intuition — from TCP to QUIC

Each generation of HTTP solved the problem of the previous one — until QUIC moved the foundation

HTTP/1.1 — one request at a time per connection

Each HTTP/1.1 connection can have only one outstanding request at a time. Browser workaround: open 6 parallel connections per domain. Problems: TCP slow-start for each connection, high memory on servers, connection overhead. Connection: keep-alive reuses connections but still serialises requests.

HTTP/2 — multiplexing, but TCP head-of-line blocking

Multiple logical streams over one TCP connection. Removes the 6-parallel-connections hack. Header compression (HPACK). Server push. Solves application-level blocking. Doesn't solve TCP-level blocking: one lost packet stalls all streams. On reliable networks (data centres), this is rare. On mobile/WiFi with 1–2% packet loss, TCP blocking degrades P99 latency significantly.

QUIC / HTTP/3 — multiplexing over UDP with per-stream reliability

QUIC is a transport protocol built on UDP. Each stream has its own reliability layer — packet loss only stalls that stream, not others. 0-RTT connection establishment (on resumed sessions). Connection migration: if your IP changes (WiFi → cellular), the QUIC connection survives (identified by connection ID, not IP:port). TLS 1.3 mandatory, built into the handshake. Used by: Google, Cloudflare, most modern browsers. ~30–40% better tail latency on lossy networks.

Diagram 1 · TCP head-of-line blocking vs QUIC per-stream recovery

One lost packet: HTTP/2 stalls all 3 streams, QUIC only stalls the affected one

hol-blocking.svg
Animation · Real-time protocol selection — WebSocket vs SSE vs Long Polling

Each protocol's connection pattern, latency, and infrastructure requirements

realtime-protocols-animation
Ready
Real-Time Protocol Selection

Long Polling, SSE, WebSockets — use the simplest one that meets your requirements

realtime-tradeoffs.svg
Code · Python — SSE endpoint, WebSocket with reconnect, TLS connection timing

Production patterns for real-time connections

PYTHON
import asyncio
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from fastapi.websockets import WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

app = FastAPI()

# ── SSE (Server-Sent Events) ───────────────────────────────
@app.get("/events")
async def event_stream(user_id: str):
    """
    SSE endpoint — server pushes events, client reads.
    Client: const es = new EventSource('/events?user_id=42');
            es.onmessage = e => console.log(e.data);
    Auto-reconnects after network drop (built into EventSource).
    """
    async def generate():
        try:
            while True:
                event = await get_next_event_for_user(user_id)
                # SSE format: "data: ...\n\n"
                yield f"data: {event}\n\n"
                # Optional: send keep-alive comment every 15s
                # yield ": keep-alive\n\n"
        except asyncio.CancelledError:
            print(f"Client {user_id} disconnected")

    return StreamingResponse(
        generate(),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",  # disable Nginx buffering
        }
    )


# ── WebSocket ──────────────────────────────────────────────
@app.websocket("/ws/{user_id}")
async def websocket_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket, user_id: str):
    """
    WebSocket: full duplex — client and server both send.
    Client: const ws = new WebSocket('ws://localhost/ws/42');
    ws.onmessage = e => console.log(e.data);
    ws.send(JSON.stringify({type: 'message', text: 'hello'}));
    """
    await websocket.accept()
    print(f"WebSocket connected: {user_id}")
    try:
        while True:
            # Receive message from client
            data = await websocket.receive_text()
            # Echo back (in real app: broadcast to room, etc.)
            await websocket.send_text(f"Echo: {data}")
    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        print(f"WebSocket disconnected: {user_id}")
        # Note: no auto-reconnect — client must reconnect with backoff

async def get_next_event_for_user(user_id: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(1)  # simulate waiting for event
    return f'{{"type": "ping", "user": "{user_id}"}}'
CONNECTION TIMING
# ── TCP/TLS connection overhead breakdown ─────────────────
#
# New HTTPS connection (TLS 1.3, first request):
# DNS lookup:        5–50ms  (varies by resolver, TTL)
# TCP SYN/SYN-ACK:   1 RTT   (e.g. 20ms to nearby server)
# TLS 1.3 handshake: 1 RTT   (combined with TCP in TLS 1.3)
# HTTP request:      1 RTT
# Total: DNS + 3 RTT = 5ms + 60ms = 65ms before first byte
#
# TLS 1.3 0-RTT (resumed session):
# TCP SYN:           1 RTT
# TLS 0-RTT + HTTP:  0 RTT additional (data in first flight)
# Total: DNS + 2 RTT = 45ms — saves 20ms on resume
# Caveat: 0-RTT data is replay-vulnerable — don't use for
# state-mutating requests (POST/PUT/DELETE)
#
# QUIC (HTTP/3) resumed session:
# UDP + QUIC 0-RTT:  0 RTT for data
# Total: DNS + 1 RTT = 25ms — 40ms faster than TLS 1.3

# ── Measure connection phases with curl ───────────────────
# curl -w "@-" -o /dev/null -s https://example.com << 'EOF'
#    time_namelookup:   %{time_namelookup}s\n
#    time_connect:      %{time_connect}s\n
#    time_appconnect:   %{time_appconnect}s\n   (TLS done)
#    time_starttransfer: %{time_starttransfer}s\n (TTFB)
#    time_total:        %{time_total}s\n
# EOF

# ── TCP slow start — why the first request is slowest ─────
# congestion_window starts at ~10 MSS (14KB)
# doubles each RTT until loss or ssthresh:
# RTT 1: 14KB  RTT 2: 28KB  RTT 3: 56KB  ...
#
# Implication: a 100KB file needs 3+ RTTs even on a fast link
# because TCP starts conservatively.
# Fix: increase initial_cwnd on server:
# ip route change default via ... initcwnd 32
# (Linux, sets initial window to 32 MSS ≈ 46KB)

import time, socket, ssl

def measure_connection_phases(host: str, port: int = 443) -> dict:
    """Measure TCP + TLS handshake timing."""
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    sock = socket.create_connection((host, port), timeout=5)
    t_tcp = time.perf_counter()

    ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
    tls = ctx.wrap_socket(sock, server_hostname=host)
    t_tls = time.perf_counter()
    tls.close()

    return {
        "tcp_ms": round((t_tcp - t0) * 1000, 1),
        "tls_ms": round((t_tls - t_tcp) * 1000, 1),
        "total_ms": round((t_tls - t0) * 1000, 1),
    }

# result = measure_connection_phases("example.com")
# → {'tcp_ms': 18.2, 'tls_ms': 22.4, 'total_ms': 40.6}
# That's 40ms before you can send the first byte!
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Network Fundamentals

QWhat is TCP head-of-line blocking and why doesn't HTTP/2 fully solve it?
A
TCP guarantees ordered delivery. If packet N in a stream is lost, TCP buffers all packets that arrived after N and delivers nothing until N is retransmitted and received. HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple logical HTTP streams over a single TCP connection — but they all share the same underlying byte stream. One lost TCP packet stalls ALL HTTP/2 streams simultaneously, including those belonging to completely unrelated requests. HTTP/2 solved application-level blocking (HTTP/1.1's "one request at a time per connection") but cannot solve TCP-level blocking because TCP's ordering guarantee is at the transport layer, below where HTTP/2 operates. QUIC solves this by building the protocol on UDP. QUIC implements per-stream reliability — if a UDP packet for stream 2 is lost, only stream 2 stalls. Streams 1 and 3 keep receiving data. On reliable data-centre networks (0.01% packet loss), the difference is negligible. On mobile WiFi (1–2% loss), QUIC shows 30–40% better P99 latency because HOL blocking events are much more frequent.
QWhen would you use SSE instead of WebSockets?
A
Use SSE when traffic is unidirectional — the server pushes events and the client only reads. SSE is plain HTTP (GET request with text/event-stream content type). Benefits: (1) Works transparently through proxies, load balancers, CDNs with no special configuration. (2) Browser's EventSource API has built-in automatic reconnection with exponential backoff.
