(3) No protocol upgrade needed — simpler server implementation. (4) Compatible with HTTP/2 multiplexing. SSE is the right choice for: AI streaming responses (ChatGPT uses SSE), live notification feeds, dashboard real-time updates, stock price tickers. Use WebSockets when you need true bidirectional communication — the client needs to send messages as well as receive them. Examples: collaborative editing (cursor sharing, keystrokes), multiplayer games (player positions), live chat (client types and server responds in the same persistent connection). A common mistake: using WebSockets for notification systems that only push from server to client — SSE is simpler and more reliable for this use case.
QWhat is TCP slow start and how does it affect your API performance?
A
TCP slow start is a congestion control algorithm that begins every new connection conservatively. The initial congestion window (cwnd) is 10 MSS (Maximum Segment Size), approximately 14KB. After each successful RTT without loss, cwnd doubles: 14KB → 28KB → 56KB → 112KB. The sender can only have cwnd bytes "in flight" (sent but unacknowledged). Impact: a 100KB API response over a new connection cannot be sent in a single burst — it takes 3+ RTTs to ramp up the window. On a 50ms RTT connection: first 14KB arrives in 50ms, next 28KB arrives at 100ms, rest at 150ms = 150ms total vs 50ms if the window were already large. Mitigations: (1) HTTP keep-alive / connection reuse: established connections have large cwnd from prior transfers, no slow start. (2) Increase initial cwnd: Linux: ip route change default initcwnd 32 — sets initial window to ~46KB, enough for most API responses in one RTT. (3) HTTP/2 connection reuse: all requests share one connection, cwnd already large after the first request. (4) CDN edge nodes: short RTTs to CDN PoPs (5ms) make slow start much less impactful than 50ms RTT to origin.
QHow would you implement real-time notifications for 1 million users?
A
Architecture: SSE for client connections (not WebSockets — notifications are server-push only), horizontal scaling via Redis Pub/Sub for cross-server fanout. Step by step: (1) Each client opens a long-lived SSE connection to one of N notification servers. With virtual threads or an event loop, each server can hold 100K+ connections. 10 servers = 1M connections. (2) When an event needs to be delivered to user X, the publishing service publishes to Redis channel user:{X}. (3) The notification server holding user X's SSE connection subscribes to that Redis channel and receives the message within 1ms. (4) It writes data: {event}\n\n to the SSE stream. (5) For users who are offline: persist the notification to a notifications table. On reconnect, SSE includes a Last-Event-ID header — the server replays missed events since that ID. Key decisions: SSE over WebSockets (simpler, auto-reconnect, load balancer compatible), Redis Pub/Sub for fanout (not Kafka — notifications need sub-millisecond delivery, Kafka adds batching latency), connection affinity via consistent hashing on user_id to route to the right server.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "why is my API slow on mobile but fast on desktop?", say: "Three likely causes specific to mobile networks: (1) Higher RTT: mobile networks have 40–100ms RTT vs 5–20ms on WiFi. Every TCP round-trip (slow start, TLS handshake) costs more. Fix: reduce round trips — TLS 1.3 (1-RTT handshake), HTTP/2 or QUIC (multiplexing), connection reuse. (2) Packet loss and TCP head-of-line blocking: mobile networks have 1–3% packet loss vs <0.1% on wired. Each lost TCP packet stalls all HTTP/2 streams for a retransmit RTT. Fix: migrate to HTTP/3 / QUIC — per-stream recovery eliminates this cross-stream blocking. (3) Payload size: mobile data is metered and slower. Large JSON responses, un-compressed assets, high-resolution images slow downloads significantly. Fix: enable Brotli compression (30% smaller than gzip), serve mobile-specific image sizes, use gRPC or Protobuf binary encoding for API calls between app and server. Measure with curl's -w timing fields to confirm which phase is slow before optimising."

Post 36

Memory Management & GC Pressure in Distributed Systems

25 min deep dive Stop-the-World · G1GC · ZGC · Off-Heap · Arena Allocation · Memory Leaks · GC Tuning · Heap Sizing
The Problem

A 20-second GC pause loses the ZooKeeper session, triggers leader re-election, takes down the cluster

A Java service is the elected leader of a distributed system. It's processing a large batch, allocating millions of objects. The old generation fills up. The JVM triggers a full stop-the-world GC. For 20 seconds, every thread in the JVM stops — including the heartbeat thread. ZooKeeper's session timeout is 10 seconds. The session expires. A new leader is elected. When the GC finishes, the old leader wakes up and tries to continue as leader — split-brain. GC pauses are a distributed systems failure mode, not just a performance concern.

The Key Insight

Every garbage collector eventually does some form of stop-the-world pause — even concurrent collectors like G1GC and ZGC have short STW phases for root scanning. The question is: how long? G1GC targets <200ms (but can have multi-second full GCs under pressure). ZGC (Java 15+) targets sub-millisecond STW. For systems with ZooKeeper/Raft session timeouts of 5–30s, G1GC can cause leader re-elections; ZGC generally cannot.

Building Intuition — generational garbage collection

Most objects die young — the generational hypothesis and why it matters for GC design

Generational hypothesis — why GC is designed around generations

Empirical observation: the vast majority of objects die very quickly (within milliseconds of creation). A request handler creates 200 objects; 195 are garbage by the time the response is sent. Only a few "escape" to become long-lived (caches, connection pools, user sessions). This allows an efficient two-generation design: a small young generation (Eden + Survivor spaces) is collected very frequently at low cost (most objects are already dead), and a large old generation is collected infrequently. Minor GC (young gen) takes 1–50ms and happens many times per minute. Major GC (old gen) takes 100ms–20s and should happen rarely.

G1GC (Garbage First — Java 9+ default)

Divides the heap into equal-sized regions (1–32MB each). Collects the regions with the most garbage first ("Garbage First"). Concurrent marking phase runs alongside application threads. Has short STW phases for initial/remark marking and evacuation. Targets a configurable pause time goal (-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200). Can still trigger full GC under pressure (humongous objects, evacuation failure) with multi-second pauses. Best for: most JVM services, good balance of latency and throughput. Tuning: keep heap occupancy below 70%, avoid humongous allocations (>half a region size).

ZGC (Z Garbage Collector — Java 15+ production)

Designed for sub-millisecond STW pauses regardless of heap size. Uses load barriers on every pointer read to track object moves. Almost all GC work runs concurrently with application threads. STW phases: root scanning + relocation set selection (<1ms). Works on heaps from 8MB to 16TB. Cost: ~15% throughput reduction vs G1GC (load barriers on every read). Use when: your application has strict latency requirements and GC pauses must stay below your heartbeat/timeout threshold. Java 21: generational ZGC (ZGC with young/old generations) improves throughput significantly.

Diagram 1 · GC collector comparison — STW pause, throughput, use case

ZGC: sub-millisecond STW. G1GC: <200ms target. ParallelGC: highest throughput, for batch only.

gc-comparison.svg
Animation · GC pause causing distributed failure — and how ZGC prevents it

G1GC full GC: 15s STW → ZooKeeper session expires → leader re-election. ZGC: sub-ms, no impact.

gc-pause-animation
Ready
Off-Heap Memory and Memory Leak Patterns

Off-heap avoids GC pressure — but you own the lifecycle. Memory leaks have three distinct sources.

Off-heap (direct) memory — no GC pressure, manual lifecycle

Off-heap memory is allocated outside the JVM heap in native memory. Not tracked by GC — no GC pressure from large data. Accessed via ByteBuffer.allocateDirect(), Unsafe.allocateMemory(), or Project Panama MemorySegment. Used by: Kafka (log segment page cache — leverages OS page cache for zero-copy reads), Netty (pooled buffer allocator — avoids GC for network I/O), Apache Ignite/RocksDB (large data sets without GC overhead). Danger: off-heap memory is not automatically released. A bug that loses the reference means the memory leaks until the JVM exits. Monitor with: -XX:NativeMemoryTracking=summary and jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory summary.

Three common memory leak sources in Java services

Static collections: static Map<String, Object> cache with no eviction policy. Items added but never removed. Grows without bound. Diagnosis: heap dump shows one class with millions of instances. Fix: use Caffeine/Guava Cache with size limit and TTL. ThreadLocal without remove(): thread pools reuse threads. A ThreadLocal set on a pooled thread and never removed stays forever. Especially dangerous in application servers (Tomcat) where threads are reused across requests. Fix: always call threadLocal.remove() in a finally block. Unclosed resources: database connections, file handles, input streams held after exceptions. The object is referenced by an internal connection pool list and never GCed. Diagnosis: lsof shows thousands of open files. Fix: try-with-resources always.

Code · Java & Python — GC configuration, memory leak detection, off-heap

JVM flags, heap monitoring, and detecting leaks in production

JAVA
// ── JVM flags for low-latency distributed services ────────
// Java 21 recommended production flags:
//
// -XX:+UseZGC                    // Sub-ms STW (Java 15+)
// -XX:+ZGenerational             // Generational ZGC (Java 21, better throughput)
// -Xms4g -Xmx4g                  // Equal min/max — prevents heap resize pauses
// -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=2g     // Limit off-heap direct memory
// -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError
// -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/dumps/oom.hprof
// -Xlog:gc*:file=/var/log/gc.log:time,uptime:filecount=5,filesize=20m
// -XX:NativeMemoryTracking=summary  // track off-heap usage

// ── Monitor GC via JMX in code ────────────────────────────
import java.lang.management.*;
import java.util.List;

class GCMonitor {

    static void printGCStats() {
        List<GarbageCollectorMXBean> gcs =
            ManagementFactory.getGarbageCollectorMXBeans();

        for (GarbageCollectorMXBean gc : gcs) {
            System.out.printf("GC: %-30s  count=%d  time=%dms%n",
                gc.getName(),
                gc.getCollectionCount(),
                gc.getCollectionTime()
            );
        }
        // Alert if GC time > 5% of uptime (GC overhead limit warning)
    }

    // ── Heap occupancy check — prevent full GC ────────────────
    static double heapUsageRatio() {
        MemoryMXBean mem = ManagementFactory.getMemoryMXBean();
        MemoryUsage heap = mem.getHeapMemoryUsage();
        return (double) heap.getUsed() / heap.getMax();
        // Alert if > 0.80 — old gen filling → full GC imminent
    }

    // ── Off-heap direct buffer ────────────────────────────────
    static void offHeapDemo() {
        var buf = java.nio.ByteBuffer.allocateDirect(100 * 1024 * 1024); // 100MB
        // This 100MB does NOT appear in heap metrics
        // and does NOT increase GC pressure
        buf.putLong(0, 42L);
        System.out.println("Read: " + buf.getLong(0));
        // buf is released when GCed (via Cleaner) OR when JVM exits
        // To release explicitly: ((sun.nio.ch.DirectBuffer) buf).cleaner().clean()
    }

    // ── Detect memory leak: static cache without eviction ─────
    // BAD: unbounded growth
    // static final Map<String, byte[]> cache = new HashMap<>();
    // void process(String key) { cache.put(key, new byte[1024]); }

    // GOOD: bounded Caffeine cache with size + TTL
    import com.github.benmanes.caffeine.cache.Cache;
    import com.github.benmanes.caffeine.cache.Caffeine;
    import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

    static final Cache<String, byte[]> cache = Caffeine.newBuilder()
        .maximumSize(10_000)
        .expireAfterWrite(10, TimeUnit.MINUTES)
        .recordStats()   // enables hit/miss rate metrics
        .build();

    // ThreadLocal leak fix:
    static final ThreadLocal<String> requestId = new ThreadLocal<>();

    void handleRequest(String id) {
        requestId.set(id);
        try {
            doWork();
        } finally {
            requestId.remove(); // ALWAYS remove — thread pool reuses threads
        }
    }
    void doWork() {}
}
PYTHON
# Python memory management is reference counting + cyclic GC
# No stop-the-world, but cyclic GC runs periodically for cycles
import gc, tracemalloc, weakref

# ── Detect memory growth with tracemalloc ─────────────────
tracemalloc.start()
snapshot1 = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()

# ... run some work ...

snapshot2 = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
top_stats = snapshot2.compare_to(snapshot1, 'lineno')
for stat in top_stats[:5]:
    print(stat)  # shows which lines allocated the most memory growth


# ── Reference cycle prevention with weakref ───────────────
class Cache:
    """Weak-value cache: entries auto-removed when object GCed."""
    def __init__(self):
        self._data: weakref.WeakValueDictionary = weakref.WeakValueDictionary()

    def get(self, key: str):
        return self._data.get(key)

    def set(self, key: str, value) -> None:
        self._data[key] = value  # weak ref — won't prevent GC


# ── Monitor GC stats ──────────────────────────────────────
def gc_stats() -> dict:
    stats = gc.get_stats()   # per-generation stats
    return {
        f"gen{i}": {
            "collections": s["collections"],
            "collected":   s["collected"],
            "uncollectable": s["uncollectable"]  # uncollectable = leak!
        }
        for i, s in enumerate(stats)
    }

# Alert: if uncollectable > 0, you have reference cycles with __del__
# Fix: break cycles explicitly or avoid __del__ in cyclic references

# ── Force GC and check object counts ─────────────────────
import sys, objgraph

# Find top growing object types (pip install objgraph)
# objgraph.show_growth()          # prints what grew since last call
# objgraph.show_most_common_types()  # top 10 by count
# objgraph.show_backrefs(          # who holds a reference to a leaked obj
#     objgraph.by_type('MyClass')[0], max_depth=3)

# ── Python equivalent of off-heap: mmap for large buffers ─
import mmap, os

def shared_buffer(size_mb: int) -> mmap.mmap:
    """Allocate memory outside Python heap (mmap anonymous)."""
    return mmap.mmap(-1, size_mb * 1024 * 1024)
    # Not tracked by Python GC — no GC pressure
    # Must call buf.close() explicitly when done
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Memory Management and GC

QHow can a GC pause cause a distributed systems failure?
A
Stop-the-world GC pauses freeze the entire JVM — no threads run, no heartbeats sent, no network I/O processed. Distributed systems depend on heartbeats to detect node liveness. ZooKeeper sessions expire if no heartbeat is received within the session timeout (default: 10–30s in many systems). Raft election timeouts are similarly bounded. If a GC pause exceeds the timeout: ZooKeeper expires the session, a new leader is elected, and when the GC finishes, the old leader wakes up not knowing it was dethroned. Both nodes may now accept writes — split-brain. The fix ladder: (1) Switch to ZGC — sub-millisecond STW eliminates the problem. (2) Tune G1GC to stay within bounds: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200, monitor old-gen occupancy, ensure GC happens before old-gen pressure causes full GC. (3) Increase election/session timeout above your worst-case GC pause. (4) Add fencing tokens so only the true leader's writes are accepted even if two nodes think they're leader (the stale leader's epoch will be rejected).
QWhat is off-heap memory and why do some systems use it?
A
Off-heap (direct) memory is allocated outside the JVM heap, in native memory managed by the OS rather than the GC. It does not contribute to heap occupancy and therefore does not trigger GC cycles. Access via ByteBuffer.allocateDirect() or sun.misc.Unsafe.allocateMemory(). Systems that use it: Kafka stores log segments in direct byte buffers that map to OS page cache — reads go from disk → OS page cache → direct buffer, bypassing the JVM heap entirely (zero-copy transfer). Netty pools direct buffers to avoid allocating/collecting thousands of heap objects per network message. Apache Ignite and RocksDB store large datasets off-heap to avoid GC pressure on multi-GB data. The danger: off-heap memory is not automatically reclaimed by GC. A reference counted or manually freed buffer that leaks will grow until the process is killed. Monitor with jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory summary and -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize to cap it.
QHow do you diagnose a Java memory leak in production?
A
Step by step: (1) Confirm it's a leak: plot heap usage over time (Prometheus JVM metrics). If heap grows monotonically and GC doesn't bring it back to baseline, it's a leak. (2) Trigger a heap dump: jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=/tmp/heap.hprof <pid> or configure -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/dumps/ for automatic dumps. (3) Analyse with Eclipse MAT or VisualVM: look at "retained heap" — which objects hold the most memory including all objects they reference. The top object is usually the root of the leak. (4) Common culprits: static collections without eviction, ThreadLocal without remove() in thread pools, event listeners registered but never unregistered, unclosed resources in exception paths. (5) For subtle leaks: compare two heap dumps taken 30 minutes apart using MAT's "compare histograms" feature — find the class whose instance count grew most. (6) Use jcmd <pid> GC.run_finalization + GC.run before dumping to ensure only live objects remain.
QHow do you size the JVM heap for a distributed service?
A
Start with: -Xms = -Xmx (equal min and max) to prevent heap resize pauses. Heap size considerations: (1) Live data set: the minimum heap must be at least 2–3× your live data size. If your service holds 500MB of caches and session data, start with 2GB heap. (2) Allocation rate: high-throughput services create many short-lived objects. The young generation needs enough space to absorb allocation bursts without minor GCs becoming too frequent. Profile with jstat -gc <pid> 5000 — if Eden fills too quickly, increase young gen with -Xmn. (3) GC overhead: keep old-gen occupancy below 70–80% to prevent G1GC full GC. If old-gen consistently exceeds 80%, either the heap is too small or there's a leak. (4) Container awareness: -XX:+UseContainerSupport (enabled by default in Java 11+) reads container cgroup limits. Set -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 in containers to leave OS headroom. (5) Reserve: production heap should be at most 75% of available RAM — leaving room for OS page cache, direct buffers, and JVM overhead. A 4GB machine: -Xmx3g.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "your Java Kafka consumer keeps losing its consumer group after full GC — fix it", say: "Three-layer fix: (1) Switch to ZGC: add -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational (Java 21). Sub-millisecond STW eliminates the root cause — consumer group heartbeat never misses a deadline regardless of heap size. (2) Tune Kafka consumer timeout: set session.timeout.ms above worst-case G1GC pause if staying on G1. Check GC logs for pause duration, add 2× safety margin. Also increase heartbeat.interval.ms proportionally. (3) Fix the root cause of full GC: run jstat -gc <pid> 5000 to watch old-gen. If it's consistently above 80%, you have a leak or the heap is too small. Run jmap -histo:live <pid> to find the top classes by retained size. Common culprit: a static Map growing unboundedly. Replace with Caffeine cache with maximumSize() + expireAfterWrite(). Monitor GC pause time via Prometheus jvm_gc_pause_seconds_max and alert when it exceeds half the consumer session timeout."

Post 37

Query Optimization & Execution Plans

25 min deep dive EXPLAIN ANALYZE · Hash Join · Nested Loop · Merge Join · Planner Statistics · ANALYZE · Slow Query Log
The Problem

Your ORM-generated query worked at 10K rows. At 10M rows it takes 45 seconds. You never saw the SQL.

A query that joins orders with products runs in 2ms in development (50 rows) and 45 seconds in production (10M rows). The database chose a nested-loop join — fine for small tables, O(N×M) for large ones. When the planner chose this plan, the statistics table said orders had 1,000 rows. The statistics were stale — a 10M row load ran without ANALYZE. The planner made the rational decision with wrong inputs. This is the most common cause of sudden query regressions: not schema changes, not new code, but stale statistics causing the planner to choose a catastrophically wrong execution plan.

The Key Insight

The query planner never looks at your actual data — it reads statistics: row counts, column cardinality, null fraction, most common values, histogram buckets. When these are stale (after a large data load, bulk delete, or partition swap), the planner picks the wrong plan. Fix: ANALYZE table_name refreshes statistics. VACUUM ANALYZE does both. Autovacuum does this automatically, but it can lag behind bulk operations.

Building Intuition — three join algorithms

Nested Loop, Hash Join, Merge Join — the planner picks based on estimated row counts

Nested Loop Join — O(N×M), good for small inner tables

For each row in the outer table, scan the inner table to find matches. If the inner table has an index, each inner scan is O(log M). Total: O(N × log M). Best when: N (outer) is small (a few dozen rows from a highly selective WHERE), inner table has a usable index. Terrible when: both tables are large, outer table has thousands of rows, or the planner overestimates selectivity due to stale statistics. The plan will show Nested Loop with Index Scan on the inner side.

Hash Join — O(N+M), best for large unsorted tables

Build a hash table from the smaller table (the "build" side). Probe the hash table with each row from the larger table (the "probe" side). O(N+M) — scans each table exactly once. Requires memory proportional to the build side (work_mem). If the hash table doesn't fit in work_mem, it spills to disk (batched hash join, slower). Best for: large equi-joins where neither table is sorted. The planner prefers hash join when it estimates both sides have many rows. The plan shows Hash Join with Hash node on the build side.

Merge Join — O(N+M), requires sorted inputs

Both inputs must be sorted on the join key. Walk through both sorted lists simultaneously, matching rows. No memory proportional to table size (unlike hash join). Best for: joining two large tables that are already sorted (or can be sorted by an existing index without sorting overhead). If inputs aren't already sorted, PostgreSQL adds an explicit Sort node — which can be expensive. The plan shows Merge Join with potential Sort nodes feeding it.

Diagram 1 · Join algorithm selection — cost estimates drive planner decisions

Same query, same tables — different plans based on estimated row counts

join-algorithms.svg
Animation · Stale statistics → wrong plan → ANALYZE → correct plan

Planner thinks 1K rows → nested loop. ANALYZE shows 10M rows → hash join. 45s → 80ms.

query-plan-animation
Ready
Code · SQL — reading EXPLAIN ANALYZE, fixing stale stats, common plan patterns

How to diagnose and fix slow queries with execution plans

SQL
-- ── 1. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE (always use BUFFERS too) ───────
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT o.id, o.amount, p.name
FROM   orders o
JOIN   products p ON o.product_id = p.id
WHERE  o.status = 'pending';

-- ── Key things to look for in the output: ─────────────────
-- Node type:
--   "Seq Scan" on a large table → may need index
--   "Nested Loop" with large outer → should be Hash Join
--   "Hash" and "Buffers: written=N" → hash spilled to disk (increase work_mem)
--
-- Row estimate accuracy:
--   rows=1000 (estimated) vs rows=10000000 (actual) → stale stats → run ANALYZE
--   A 10x difference changes plan choice
--
-- Cost:
--   startup cost..total cost  (planner's estimate)
--   actual time=0.042..18432  (actual time in ms, startrow..lastrow)
--
-- Example bad output:
--   Nested Loop  (cost=0.43..847292.15 rows=91 width=56)
--                (actual time=0.089..44821.234 rows=91 loops=1)
--     -> Seq Scan on orders  (rows=91 estimated vs 10M actual!)
--     -> Index Scan on products using products_pkey
-- ← 10M index seeks instead of 1 hash join = 45 seconds

-- ── 2. Fix: refresh statistics ──────────────────────────────
ANALYZE orders;          -- refresh stats for one table (fast)
VACUUM ANALYZE orders;   -- remove dead rows AND refresh stats

-- Tune autovacuum for large tables (avoid stale stats):
ALTER TABLE orders
  SET (autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01);
-- Default: 0.2 (analyze after 20% of table changes)
-- For 10M row table: 0.01 = analyze after 100K changes
-- (instead of default 2M changes — much more responsive)

-- ── 3. Force a specific plan (for debugging only) ──────────
SET enable_nestloop = off;   -- force hash or merge join
SET enable_seqscan  = off;   -- force index scans
SET work_mem        = '256MB'; -- more memory for hash/sort

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT ...;
-- Reset after debugging:
RESET enable_nestloop;
RESET work_mem;

-- ── 4. Find slow queries in production ──────────────────────
-- Enable pg_stat_statements extension:
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'

SELECT
    ROUND(total_exec_time / 1000) AS total_sec,
    calls,
    ROUND(mean_exec_time)           AS avg_ms,
    ROUND(stddev_exec_time)         AS stddev_ms,
    LEFT(query, 80)               AS query_snippet
FROM   pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT  15;
-- Shows cumulative slow queries across all calls.
-- High stddev_ms = inconsistent plan (sometimes fast, sometimes slow).
-- High avg_ms × high calls = biggest total time drain.
PYTHON
import psycopg2, json, re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class PlanWarning:
    severity: str        # "critical" | "warning"
    message:  str
    node:     str

def diagnose_plan(conn, query: str) -> list[PlanWarning]:
    """
    Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and detect common anti-patterns.
    Returns a list of warnings with actionable advice.
    """
    warnings = []
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            f"EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) {query}"
        )
        plan = cur.fetchone()[0][0]["Plan"]

    def walk(node):
        node_type  = node.get("Node Type", "")
        est_rows   = node.get("Plan Rows", 1)
        act_rows   = node.get("Actual Rows", 1)
        buffers_w  = node.get("Shared Written Blocks", 0)
        actual_ms  = node.get("Actual Total Time", 0)

        # Check for row estimate mismatch (stale stats)
        if est_rows > 0:
            ratio = act_rows / est_rows
            if ratio > 100 or ratio < 0.01:
                warnings.append(PlanWarning(
                    "critical",
                    f"Estimate {est_rows:,} vs actual {act_rows:,} rows ({ratio:.0f}×). Run ANALYZE.",
                    node_type
                ))

        # Nested Loop on large outer tables
        if node_type == "Nested Loop" and act_rows > 10_000:
            warnings.append(PlanWarning(
                "critical",
                f"Nested Loop on {act_rows:,} rows — consider increasing work_mem for hash join",
                node_type
            ))

        # Hash spill to disk
        if node_type == "Hash" and buffers_w > 0:
            warnings.append(PlanWarning(
                "warning",
                f"Hash batch spilled {buffers_w} blocks to disk. SET work_mem='256MB';",
                node_type
            ))

        # Slow seq scan
        if node_type == "Seq Scan" and actual_ms > 1000:
            warnings.append(PlanWarning(
                "warning",
                f"Seq Scan took {actual_ms:.0f}ms on '{node.get('Relation Name')}'. Consider index.",
                node_type
            ))

        for child in node.get("Plans", []):
            walk(child)

    walk(plan)
    return warnings


# ── Usage ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# conn = psycopg2.connect("dbname=prod")
# issues = diagnose_plan(conn, "SELECT * FROM orders JOIN products ...")
# for w in issues:
#     print(f"[{w.severity.upper()}] {w.node}: {w.message}")
#
# [CRITICAL] Seq Scan: Estimate 1,000 vs actual 10,000,000 rows. Run ANALYZE.
# [CRITICAL] Nested Loop: Nested Loop on 10,000,000 rows. Increase work_mem.
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Query Optimization

QHow does the query planner decide which join algorithm to use?
A
The planner uses cost-based optimization: it estimates the cost of every feasible plan and picks the cheapest. Cost is a function of estimated row counts, I/O costs, and CPU costs. The estimated row counts come from pg_statistic (table row counts, column histograms, most common values). The planner compares: Nested Loop cost = N_outer × index_lookup_cost; Hash Join cost = build_cost(N_smaller) + probe_cost(N_larger); Merge Join cost = sort_cost(N1) + sort_cost(N2) + merge_cost(N1+N2). The critical dependency: all cost estimates depend on N_outer and N_inner being accurate. If statistics say N=1,000 but actual is 10,000,000, Nested Loop appears cheapest (small N assumption). When the query runs, 10M index seeks take 45 seconds. The planner wasn't wrong — it was working with wrong statistics. This is why ANALYZE and properly tuned autovacuum are not optional for production systems.
QWhat does "Buffers: shared written" in EXPLAIN ANALYZE mean and why is it bad?
A
"Shared written blocks" in the Hash node means the hash table didn't fit in memory (work_mem) and was written to temporary files on disk. This is called a "hash batch spill". Instead of one in-memory hash build + one probe pass, PostgreSQL splits the data into batches: it processes as much as fits in work_mem, writes the overflow to disk, and does multiple passes. Each disk pass adds I/O latency. A query that should take 100ms in memory can take 10+ seconds if the hash spills across 50 batches. Diagnosis: look for "Batches: N" in the Hash node. If N > 1, you had spills. Fix: increase work_mem for the session or globally (carefully — work_mem is per sort/hash operation per query, and a complex query can use multiple). SET work_mem = '256MB' before a known heavy query. Or tune the query to join smaller intermediate results. Also check: is the hash on the right side (the build side should be the smaller table)? If PostgreSQL chose the wrong build side, it may be a row estimate issue.
QA query was fast last week and now takes 45 seconds with no code changes. What do you investigate?
A
Systematic investigation: (1) Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) and look at the estimated vs actual row counts. A large discrepancy (10× or more) strongly indicates stale statistics — data volume changed significantly since last ANALYZE. Fix: run ANALYZE on the affected tables. (2) Check if the plan changed. Use pg_stat_statements to compare current query plans with historical. If the join algorithm switched from Hash Join to Nested Loop, a row count estimate changed. (3) Check for index bloat or table bloat. Over time, dead rows accumulate (from updates/deletes). Bloated tables take longer to seq scan. Fix: VACUUM on the table. (4) Check for lock contention. pg_stat_activity may show the query waiting on a lock held by another transaction. (5) Check for parameter sniffing issues. If the query uses a prepared statement, the plan was cached from the first execution with different parameters. Reload the plan with DEALLOCATE or restart the connection pool. (6) Check hardware changes — disk I/O saturation, memory pressure causing page cache evictions.
QHow do you find the slowest queries in PostgreSQL production?
A
Three tools: (1) pg_stat_statements: tracks cumulative execution statistics per query. Enabled via shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'. Key fields: total_exec_time (total CPU time ever), calls, mean_exec_time, stddev_exec_time. Sort by total_exec_time DESC to find the biggest time consumers. Sort by mean_exec_time DESC for the single slowest queries. High stddev means inconsistent — the query sometimes uses a different plan (parameter-dependent plan switching). (2) Slow query log: log_min_duration_statement = 1000 in postgresql.conf logs every query taking > 1 second. Lower the threshold during investigation. (3) pg_stat_activity: for currently running queries — shows pid, query, state, wait_event_type (Lock, IO, CPU). Use WHERE state = 'active' AND duration > '5 seconds' to find currently-hung queries. After finding slow queries: EXPLAIN them. Look for the row estimate issues, missing indexes, hash spills, and seq scans on large tables.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "a query that returns 10 rows is taking 45 seconds — diagnose it", say: "Step 1: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) the query. Look for: (a) Estimated rows vs actual rows — if the planner thought 100 rows but got 10M, statistics are stale. Run ANALYZE. (b) Nested Loop on a large outer table — should be Hash Join for big N. Verify stats first, then check work_mem. (c) 'Buffers: written' in a Hash node — hash spilled to disk. Increase work_mem. Step 2: Check pg_stat_statements for this query's history. Did mean_exec_time spike recently? If yes, what changed? Data volume? Index dropped? Step 3: If stats are fresh and plan looks correct, check for lock contention — pg_stat_activity for wait_event = 'Lock'. Step 4: If the query only returns 10 rows but scans 10M, the WHERE clause filtering is happening late (Filter node after a large scan rather than an index seek). Add the missing index. A correctly planned 10-row result query should take under 10ms — if it takes 45s something fundamental is wrong with either statistics, indexes, or plan choice."

Post 38

Idempotency & Exactly-Once Delivery

25 min deep dive Idempotency Keys · At-Least-Once · Exactly-Once · Deduplication · Outbox Pattern · Payment Safety
The Problem

The payment network timed out. You don't know if the charge succeeded. You retry. The customer gets charged twice.

A client sends a payment request. The server processes it and charges the card — but the response is lost in transit (network partition, server crash after processing but before responding). The client sees a timeout. Should it retry? If it does and the charge went through, the customer gets double-charged. If it doesn't retry and the charge failed, the order is never completed. This is the fundamental problem of distributed systems: you cannot distinguish between "request failed" and "response was lost". Idempotency is the solution: make every operation safe to execute multiple times with the same result.

The Core Principle

An operation is idempotent if executing it N times produces the same result as executing it once. PUT /users/42 {name: "Alice"} is idempotent — calling it 5 times leaves the same state as calling it once. POST /payments {amount: 100} is NOT idempotent by default — 5 calls create 5 charges. Making mutations idempotent requires the client to generate a unique key per logical operation and the server to deduplicate by that key.

Building Intuition — delivery guarantees

At-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once — and the cost of each

At-most-once (fire and forget)

Send the request. Don't retry on failure. The operation happens 0 or 1 times. Simplest implementation. Acceptable for: metrics/analytics events (losing 0.1% is fine), notifications (missing one push notification is OK), audit log entries in non-critical paths. Never acceptable for: payments, inventory deductions, user-visible mutations.

At-least-once (retry until acknowledged)

Retry on timeout or failure until you get a success response. The operation happens 1 or more times. Consumer must handle duplicates. This is the default for most production systems with retries enabled. The consumer must be idempotent — receiving the same message twice must produce the same final state as receiving it once. Implementation: deduplicate by message ID in the consumer. Used by: Kafka (at-least-once is the default), SQS, most HTTP clients with retry logic.

Exactly-once (hardest — usually implemented as idempotent at-least-once)

The operation happens exactly once despite retries. True exactly-once requires distributed transactions (expensive). Practical exactly-once = at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumer. The consumer processes each logical operation only once by tracking which operation IDs have been processed. Kafka EOS (exactly-once semantics, Post 26) implements this at the framework level. For HTTP APIs: Idempotency-Key header + server-side deduplication store achieves the same effect.

Diagram 1 · Idempotency key flow — safe retry without double-charge

Client generates UUID once. Server deduplicates by key. Retry returns cached result.

idempotency-flow.svg
Animation · Retry storm without idempotency vs safe retry with idempotency key

Without key: 3 retries = 3 charges. With key: 3 retries = 1 charge, 2 cache hits.

idempotency-animation
Ready
The Outbox Pattern — atomic DB write + event publish

Write to DB and publish to Kafka atomically — without a distributed transaction

The dual-write problem

Your order service needs to: (1) INSERT the order into the orders table, and (2) publish an OrderCreated event to Kafka. If you do both sequentially, one can fail after the other succeeds — the DB has the order but Kafka doesn't get the event, or vice versa. This is a dual-write inconsistency. The Outbox pattern solves it: write the event to an outbox table in the SAME database transaction as the order INSERT. A CDC process (Debezium, Post 20) reads the outbox table and publishes to Kafka. The database transaction is the atomic boundary — either both the order and the outbox row are committed, or neither is.

Outbox + Idempotent consumer = exactly-once semantics

The outbox guarantees at-least-once delivery (CDC may republish on restart). The consumer deduplicates by event ID (stored in the outbox row). Combined: each event is delivered at least once but processed exactly once. This is the standard pattern for reliable event-driven systems without Kafka EOS. Schema: outbox(id UUID, aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload JSONB, created_at, published_at). Poll-based alternative: a background job polls WHERE published_at IS NULL and publishes, then sets published_at.

Code · Python & Java — idempotency key server, outbox pattern
PYTHON
import redis, json, uuid
from fastapi import FastAPI, Header, HTTPException
from typing import Optional

app = FastAPI()
r   = redis.Redis()

# ── Idempotency key middleware ─────────────────────────────
@app.post("/payments")
async def create_payment(
    body: dict,
    idempotency_key: Optional[str] = Header(default=None,
                                             alias="Idempotency-Key")
) -> dict:
    if not idempotency_key:
        # Require idempotency key for all payment mutations
        raise HTTPException(400, "Idempotency-Key header required")

    redis_key = f"idem:{idempotency_key}"

    # 1. Check for existing result (any prior attempt)
    existing = r.get(redis_key)
    if existing:
        stored = json.loads(existing)
        if stored["status"] == "processing":
            raise HTTPException(409, "Request in progress — retry later")
        return stored["result"]  # cached result, no re-processing

    # 2. Mark as in-progress (SET NX — only succeeds if key absent)
    # Prevents two concurrent retries both processing
    acquired = r.set(
        redis_key,
        json.dumps({"status": "processing"}),
        nx=True,     # only set if not exists
        ex=300        # 5min TTL for in-progress (prevents stuck keys)
    )
    if not acquired:
        raise HTTPException(409, "Concurrent request with same key")

    try:
        # 3. Execute the actual operation
        result = await charge_card(body["amount"], body["card_token"])

        # 4. Store result with 24h TTL (covers all retry windows)
        r.set(redis_key,
             json.dumps({"status": "done", "result": result}),
             ex=86400)   # 24 hours
        return result

    except Exception as e:
        # 5. On failure: delete key so client can retry with same key
        r.delete(redis_key)
        raise HTTPException(500, str(e))


# ── Outbox pattern ─────────────────────────────────────────
async def create_order(conn, order_data: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Write order + outbox event in ONE transaction.
    Debezium CDC reads outbox table and publishes to Kafka.
    Guaranteed: if order is committed, event will be published.
    """
    async with conn.transaction():
        # 1. Insert the order
        order = await conn.fetchrow(
            "INSERT INTO orders(user_id, amount, status) VALUES($1,$2,'pending') RETURNING *",
            order_data["user_id"], order_data["amount"]
        )
        # 2. Write outbox event IN THE SAME TRANSACTION
        await conn.execute(
            """INSERT INTO outbox(id, aggregate_type, aggregate_id,
                               event_type, payload)
               VALUES($1, 'Order', $2, 'OrderCreated', $3)""",
            str(uuid.uuid4()),
            str(order["id"]),
            json.dumps({"order_id": order["id"],
                         "amount":   order["amount"],
                         "user_id":  order["user_id"]})
        )
        # Both committed or both rolled back — never half-written
        return dict(order)

async def charge_card(amount: float, token: str) -> dict:
    return {"payment_id": str(uuid.uuid4()), "status": "charged"}
JAVA
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.*;
import org.springframework.transaction.annotation.Transactional;
import java.util.*;

// ── Idempotency key with database deduplication ───────────
record IdempotencyRecord(String key, String status, String resultJson,
                          java.time.Instant createdAt) {}

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/payments")
class PaymentController {

    private final IdempotencyRepository idempRepo;
    private final PaymentService         paymentSvc;

    @PostMapping
    @Transactional
    public Map<String,Object> createPayment(
            @RequestHeader("Idempotency-Key") String key,
            @RequestBody Map<String,Object> body) {

        // 1. Try to insert with unique key (DB-level dedup)
        try {
            idempRepo.insertProcessing(key);  // throws on duplicate key
        } catch (DataIntegrityViolationException e) {
            // Key already exists — return cached or 409
            var existing = idempRepo.findByKey(key);
            if (existing.status().equals("done"))
                return parseResult(existing.resultJson());
            throw new ResponseStatusException(409, "In progress");
        }

        // 2. Process
        var result = paymentSvc.charge(body);

        // 3. Store result + mark done in same transaction
        idempRepo.markDone(key, toJson(result));
        return result;
    }

    // ── Outbox pattern (Spring + JPA) ─────────────────────────
    @Transactional
    public Order createOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        // 1. Save the order
        Order order = orderRepo.save(new Order(req.userId(), req.amount()));

        // 2. Write outbox event IN THE SAME TRANSACTION
        outboxRepo.save(new OutboxEvent(
            UUID.randomUUID().toString(),
            "Order", order.getId().toString(),
            "OrderCreated",
            toJson(Map.of("orderId", order.getId(),
                           "amount",  order.getAmount()))
        ));
        // @Transactional ensures both or neither committed
        // Debezium reads outbox table via Postgres WAL
        // and publishes to Kafka topic "order.OrderCreated"
        return order;
    }
}
Interview Q&A

Everything you will be asked about Idempotency

QWhat makes an HTTP method idempotent and why does it matter?
A
An HTTP method is idempotent if making the same request N times produces the same server state as making it once. GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD are idempotent by definition. GET retrieves without modifying. PUT sets a resource to a specific state — calling it twice leaves the same state as calling it once. DELETE removes a resource — the second call is a no-op (resource already gone). POST is explicitly NOT idempotent — it creates a new resource or triggers an action. Why it matters: HTTP clients, load balancers, and proxies automatically retry idempotent requests on failure (TCP reset, timeout, 5xx). They never auto-retry POST because it might double-submit. This is why "search" is often implemented as GET with query params rather than POST — search results are read-only and retries are safe. If you need a POST to be safely retryable, add Idempotency-Key header support on the server.
QHow do you design an idempotency key system that handles concurrent retries?
A
The dangerous race condition: two retries arrive simultaneously before either has completed. Without protection, both check "key not found", both start processing, and you get double-execution. Solution: use an atomic compare-and-set operation to claim the key before processing. Two approaches: (1) Redis SET NX (Set if Not eXists): SET idem:{key} "processing" NX EX 300. Only one caller succeeds. The loser gets a 409 response and retries after a delay. (2) Database UNIQUE constraint: INSERT INTO idempotency_keys(key, status) VALUES(?, 'processing') wrapped in a try-catch for duplicate key violation. Only one INSERT succeeds. The loser catches the exception, reads the existing record, and returns its cached result if done or 409 if still processing. Three states: PROCESSING (claimed, not done), DONE (result cached), not found (first attempt). Always set a TTL on DONE records (24 hours covers all realistic retry windows).
QWhat is the Outbox pattern and when do you need it?
A
The Outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem: you need to both update a database and publish an event to a message broker atomically. Without it, you might update the DB successfully but fail to publish the event (network failure), or publish the event but have the DB transaction roll back. The result: data inconsistency between your service's state and the event stream. Outbox approach: write the event to an outbox table as part of the same database transaction as the state change. A separate process (typically a CDC connector like Debezium) reads new outbox rows and publishes them to Kafka. This process retries on failure, guaranteeing at-least-once delivery. The database transaction provides atomicity — the state change and the outbox entry are always consistent. The consumer handles duplicates by deduplicating on the outbox event ID. Use Outbox when: (1) you need reliable event publication without distributed transactions, (2) your service uses a database and Kafka/message broker, (3) event loss would cause downstream inconsistency.
QHow do you design a message consumer to be idempotent?
A
An idempotent consumer produces the same final state when processing the same message multiple times. Design approaches in order of preference: (1) Natural idempotency — some operations are naturally idempotent. Setting a field to a specific value (UPDATE orders SET status='shipped' WHERE id=42) is idempotent — running it twice produces the same row. Incrementing is NOT: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 run twice doubles the credit. (2) Deduplication table — store processed message IDs in a processed_messages(message_id, processed_at) table. Before processing, check if message_id exists. If yes, skip. Use a DB unique constraint on message_id for concurrent safety. TTL: expire entries after the maximum expected redelivery window (24h for Kafka, longer for SQS). (3) Conditional update — embed the expected state in the update: UPDATE orders SET status='shipped' WHERE id=42 AND status='pending'. If the message is a duplicate, the status is already 'shipped' and the WHERE clause matches 0 rows — no harm. (4) Event sourcing — append-only log is inherently idempotent if events carry sequence numbers: skip events with sequence ≤ current sequence.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design a payment system that guarantees no duplicate charges under retries and failures", say: "Three layers: (1) Idempotency at the API layer: require Idempotency-Key: UUID on POST /payments. Server uses Redis SET NX to claim the key before processing. Result stored with 24h TTL. Retries with the same key get the cached result — zero re-processing. (2) Idempotency at the payment processor layer: when calling Stripe/Adyen, pass our idempotency key downstream. If our server crashes after charging but before returning, retrying to Stripe with the same key returns the original charge ID without double-charging. (3) Atomic state + event via Outbox: the payment record INSERT and the PaymentCompleted event are written in one DB transaction. Debezium publishes the event to Kafka reliably. Downstream services (order fulfilment, email) consume the event idempotently by checking if payment_id has already been processed. This gives us exactly-once semantics without distributed transactions: at-least-once delivery (Kafka + Outbox) + idempotent consumers."

Post 39

Timeouts, Retries & Exponential Backoff

20 min deep dive Deadline Propagation · Retry Storms · Exponential Backoff · Jitter · Token Bucket · Retry Budget
The Problem

Your retry logic just brought down the database you were trying to protect

Service A calls Service B. B is slow (overloaded). A times out after 5s and retries immediately. So do the other 200 instances of Service A — all retrying simultaneously, all adding more load to an already-struggling Service B. B gets 200× the normal traffic, all at once, all retrying every 5 seconds. A retry storm finishes what the original slowdown started. This is one of the most common causes of cascading failures in distributed systems — and it's caused entirely by retry logic that didn't account for the effect of simultaneous retries at scale.

The Three-Part Problem

Timeouts without deadlines: each layer times out independently. Inner service retries for 30s. Outer caller already gave up after 10s. The inner retry is wasted work. Retries without backoff: immediate retries pile on at the exact moment the downstream is struggling. Backoff without jitter: all callers backed off 2s — they all retry again simultaneously at t=2s. The thundering herd problem is just delayed, not solved.

Building Intuition — the right retry strategy

Exponential backoff + full jitter converts a thundering herd into a smooth arrival rate

Exponential backoff — grow the wait time geometrically

After each failure, wait twice as long before retrying: 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → 16s (capped at a max, typically 30–60s). The total retry window for 5 attempts with cap=30s: ~63s. Exponential backoff reduces the retry rate as the downstream stays degraded — callers naturally back off rather than maintaining constant pressure. AWS SDK, gRPC, and most production HTTP clients implement this.

Jitter — spread retries across time to prevent thundering herd

Without jitter: 200 clients all backed off for 4s. At t=4s, all 200 retry simultaneously — same problem as immediate retry, just delayed. With full jitter: each client waits a random amount between 0 and the backoff window (random(0, base * 2^attempt)). The 200 clients now spread their retries uniformly across a 4-second window — the downstream sees ~50 req/s instead of a 200 req spike. AWS research showed full jitter is the best strategy: lowest peak load on the server, fastest overall system recovery.

Deadline propagation — inner timeout must be shorter than outer

Every request should carry a deadline (the absolute time by which the caller needs a response). Each service checks the remaining deadline before attempting work. If less than X ms remain, abort immediately rather than doing work that will be thrown away. gRPC has built-in deadline propagation via context. HTTP: pass X-Request-Deadline header. Pattern: outer service allows 10s total. Inner service must complete within 8s (2s buffer). Database call must complete within 5s. This prevents the "retry on a timed-out caller" wasted work problem.

Diagram 1 · Retry storm vs exponential backoff + jitter

Immediate retry: 200 req/s spike every 5s. Full jitter: ~40 req/s spread evenly. Server recovers.

retry-comparison.svg
Animation · Retry storm failure vs backoff + jitter recovery
retry-animation
Ready
Code · Python & Java — exponential backoff with jitter and retry budget
PYTHON
import asyncio, random, time, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')

@dataclass
class RetryConfig:
    max_attempts:  int   = 4
    base_delay_s:  float = 0.5   # 500ms initial backoff
    max_delay_s:   float = 30.0  # cap at 30s
    jitter:        bool  = True   # ALWAYS use jitter
    deadline_s:    float = None   # optional absolute deadline

async def retry_with_backoff(fn: Callable, cfg: RetryConfig = RetryConfig()) -> T:
    """
    Retry fn with exponential backoff + full jitter.
    Respects deadline: aborts early if not enough time remains.
    """
    start    = time.monotonic()
    deadline = cfg.deadline_s if cfg.deadline_s else float('inf')

    for attempt in range(cfg.max_attempts):
        elapsed   = time.monotonic() - start
        remaining = deadline - elapsed

        if remaining <= 0.1:      # < 100ms left — abort
            raise TimeoutError(f"Deadline exceeded after {attempt} attempts")

        try:
            return await fn()

        except Exception as e:
            if attempt == cfg.max_attempts - 1:
                raise

            # Exponential backoff: base × 2^attempt, capped at max
            cap   = min(cfg.max_delay_s, cfg.base_delay_s * (2 ** attempt))

            # Full jitter: random in [0, cap]
            delay = random.uniform(0, cap) if cfg.jitter else cap

            # Don't wait longer than remaining deadline
            delay = min(delay, remaining - 0.1)

            logging.warning(f"Attempt {attempt+1} failed ({e}), retrying in {delay:.2f}s")
            await asyncio.sleep(delay)


# ── Retry budget — prevent cascading retries ──────────────
class RetryBudget:
    """
    Limit retries across all callers.
    If too many requests are retrying, stop retrying entirely.
    Google SRE recommendation: budget = 10% of requests may retry.
    """
    def __init__(self, rate: float = 0.1, window_s: float = 60.0):
        self._rate      = rate        # 10% retry rate limit
        self._requests  = 0
        self._retries   = 0
        self._window_s  = window_s
        self._window_start = time.monotonic()

    def should_retry(self) -> bool:
        if time.monotonic() - self._window_start > self._window_s:
            self._requests = self._retries = 0
            self._window_start = time.monotonic()
        self._requests += 1
        if self._requests == 0: return True
        retry_rate = self._retries / self._requests
        if retry_rate >= self._rate:
            return False   # budget exhausted — fail fast, don't retry
        self._retries += 1
        return True


# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
budget = RetryBudget(rate=0.1)  # max 10% of requests may retry
cfg    = RetryConfig(max_attempts=4, base_delay_s=0.5,
                      max_delay_s=30, deadline_s=10.0)

async def call_downstream():
    # raise on failure to trigger retry
    pass

async def main():
    if budget.should_retry():
        result = await retry_with_backoff(call_downstream, cfg)
    else:
        raise RuntimeError("Retry budget exhausted — fail fast")
JAVA
import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.function.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.*;

class RetryWithBackoff {

    // ── Exponential backoff + full jitter ────────────────────
    static <T> T retry(Supplier<T> fn, int maxAttempts,
                        long baseMs, long maxMs) throws Exception {
        for (int attempt = 0; attempt < maxAttempts; attempt++) {
            try {
                return fn.get();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                if (attempt == maxAttempts - 1) throw e;

                // Exponential cap
                long cap   = Math.min(maxMs, baseMs * (1L << attempt));
                // Full jitter: random in [0, cap]
                long delay = ThreadLocalRandom.current().nextLong(0, cap + 1);

                System.out.printf("Attempt %d failed, retrying in %dms%n", attempt+1, delay);
                Thread.sleep(delay);
            }
        }
        throw new IllegalStateException("unreachable");
    }

    // ── Retry budget ─────────────────────────────────────────
    static class RetryBudget {
        private final double            maxRate;
        private final AtomicLong        requests = new AtomicLong();
        private final AtomicLong        retries  = new AtomicLong();
        private volatile long            windowStart = System.nanoTime();
        private final long               windowNs;

        RetryBudget(double maxRate, long windowSeconds) {
            this.maxRate  = maxRate;
            this.windowNs = windowSeconds * 1_000_000_000L;
        }

        synchronized boolean shouldRetry() {
            if (System.nanoTime() - windowStart > windowNs) {
                requests.set(0); retries.set(0);
                windowStart = System.nanoTime();
            }
            long req = requests.incrementAndGet();
            if (req == 0) return true;
            if ((double) retries.get() / req >= maxRate) return false;
            retries.incrementAndGet();
            return true;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        var budget = new RetryBudget(0.1, 60);  // max 10% retry rate
        var result = new AtomicReference<String>();

        if (budget.shouldRetry()) {
            result.set(retry(() -> callService(), 4, 500L, 30_000L));
        } else {
            throw new RuntimeException("Retry budget exhausted — fail fast");
        }
    }

    static String callService() { return "ok"; } // stub
}
Interview Q&A
QWhat is a retry storm and how do you prevent it?
A
A retry storm occurs when a large number of clients all retry simultaneously after a timeout or failure, adding more load to an already-struggling downstream service. The retries amplify the problem rather than allowing recovery. Pattern: 200 clients hit a slow database, all time out at t=5s, all retry at t=5s — the database now receives 200× normal traffic at exactly the moment it's struggling. Prevention: (1) Exponential backoff: increase wait time geometrically after each failure, reducing retry rate as the outage continues. (2) Full jitter: randomise the backoff within the window so 200 clients don't all retry at the same moment. AWS research showed random(0, min(cap, base × 2^n)) achieves the best load distribution. (3) Retry budget: limit the fraction of requests that may retry (Google SRE recommends 10%). When the budget is exhausted, requests fail fast rather than retrying. (4) Circuit breaker: if error rate exceeds threshold, stop retrying entirely for a period. Let the downstream recover without retry pressure.
QWhat is the difference between a timeout and a deadline, and why does the distinction matter?
A
A timeout is a duration — "wait at most 5 seconds". A deadline is an absolute point in time — "this must complete by 14:30:05.250". The distinction matters for nested calls. If the outer service has a 10s timeout and the inner service has a 5s timeout, the inner service can retry 3 times (1s + 2s + 4s = 7s) — but the outer caller already gave up at 10s. The inner retries are wasted work. With deadlines: the outer service sets a deadline of now + 10s and passes it in the request context (gRPC deadline propagation, HTTP X-Request-Deadline header). Each inner service checks: "do I have enough time remaining to even attempt this?" If less than 500ms remains, abort immediately rather than trying and failing. This eliminates wasted retries on dead calls and prevents retry work from accumulating inside already-timed-out requests. gRPC has first-class deadline support built into the context. HTTP services should implement deadline-aware logic using a deadline header.
Senior-Level Power Answer

When asked "design the retry strategy for a payment service calling a card processor", say: "Three layers: (1) Idempotency first: every call to the processor carries an idempotency key (our payment ID + attempt number). If we retry and the processor already charged, it returns the cached result — no double charge. (2) Exponential backoff + jitter: max 3 retries. Wait random(0, 2^attempt × 500ms), capped at 10s. This spreads retries across time — 500 clients experiencing a processor blip retry at different times rather than all hammering at once. (3) Deadline propagation: the user's checkout request has a 10s total budget. The processor call gets a 6s deadline (leaving 4s for everything else). If 6s have passed and we haven't heard back, we don't retry — we fail fast with 'payment pending, check your bank'. (4) Retry budget: if retry rate exceeds 10% of payment requests in the last minute, stop retrying entirely and page on-call. This prevents a sustained processor outage from cascading into a retry storm."
